text: When you see anyone complaining of another's ill-nature and bad temper, know that the complainant is bad-tempered, for only he is truly good-tempered who quietly forbears towards the ill-tempered.
author: Rumi

text: If He who in Himself can lack nothing chooses to need us, it is because we need to be needed.
author: C. S. Lewis

text: Whoever enters the Way without a guide will take a hundred years to travel a two-day journey.
author: Rumi

text: To understand the heart and mind of a person, look not at what he has already achieved, but what he aspires to.
author: Khalil Gibran
source: As quoted in Become a Conscious Creator: A Return to Self-Empowerment (2007) by Lisa Ford, p. 44

text: In the long run the answer to all those who object to the doctrine of hell, is itself a question: What are you asking God to do? To wipe out their past sins and, at all costs, to give them a fresh start, smoothing every difficulty and offering every miraculous help? But He has done so, on Calvary. To forgive them? They will not be forgiven. To leave them alone? Alas, I am afraid that is what He does.
author: C. S. Lewis

text: Ah, me! so poor, can I declare that friend, who never had another friend his like,—none, therefore, who could know his soul?
author: Rumi
source_section: Pebbles, Pearls and Gems of the Orient (1882)

text: Perfect humility dispenses with modesty.
author: C. S. Lewis

text: Love may forgive all infirmities and love still in spite of them: but Love cannot cease to will their removal.
author: C. S. Lewis

text: You could send your soul after the good you had expected, instead of turning it to the good you had got. You could refuse the real good; you could make the real fruit taste insipid by thinking of the other.
author: C. S. Lewis

text: Courtship is the time for sowing those seeds which will grow up ten years into domestic hatred.
author: C. S. Lewis

text: We whose senses have been dulled, we gaze in full daylight and yet we do not see. We would cup our ears, but we do not hear; and stretch forth our hands, but we do not touch. And though all the incense of Arabia is burned, we go our way and do not smell.
author: Khalil Gibran
source: A Philosopher: On Wonder And Beauty

text: Friendship is always a sweet responsibility , never an opportunity .
author: Khalil Gibran

text: He was gentle, like a man mindful of his own strength.
author: Khalil Gibran
source: Mary Magdalen: His Mouth Was Like the Heart of a Pomegranate

text: Thou wilt never more endure without the flame, when thou hast known the rapture of burning. If the water of life should come to thee, it would not stir thee from the flame.
author: Rumi

text: Men would bless you or curse you; The curse, a protest against failure, The blessing, a hymn of the hunter Who comes back from the hills With provision for his mate.
author: Khalil Gibran
source: A Man From Lebanon: Nineteen Centuries Afterward

text: The mind weighs and measures but it is the spirit that reaches the heart of life and embraces the secret; and the seed of the spirit is deathless. The wind may blow and then cease, and the sea shall swell and then weary, but the heart of life is a sphere quiet and serene, and the star that shines therein is fixed for evermore.
author: Khalil Gibran
source: John The Beloved Disciple In His Old Age: On Jesus The Word

text: A truth is to be known always, to be uttered sometimes.
author: Khalil Gibran

text: My enemy said to me, "Love your enemy." And I obeyed him and loved myself.
author: Khalil Gibran

text: My thought is a tender leaf that sways in every direction and finds pleasure in its swaying. Your thought is an ancient dogma that cannot change you nor can you change it. My thought is new, and it tests me and I test it morn and eve.
author: Khalil Gibran

text: Say not, "I have found the path of the soul." Say rather, "I have found the soul walking upon my path." For the soul walks upon all paths. The soul walks not upon a line, neither does it grow like a reed. The soul unfolds itself, like a lotus of countless petals.
author: Khalil Gibran

text: My Soul gave me good counsel, teaching me that I am not exalted over the panhandler nor less than the mighty. Before, I thought people consisted of two types: the weak, whom I pitied and disregarded, and the powerful, whom I followed or against I rebelled. Now, I have discovered that I was formed as one individual from the same substance from which all human beings were created. My struggles are theirs, and my path is theirs.
author: Khalil Gibran
source_section: The Vision: Reflections on the Way of the Soul (1994)

text: Men reject their prophets and slay them, but they love their martyrs and honor those they have slain.
author: Fyodor Dostoevsky

text: When Life does not find a singer to sing her heart she produces a philosopher to speak her mind.
author: Khalil Gibran

text: My Soul taught me to love what others abhor and to show good will toward those they hate. It showed me that Love is a property not of the lover but of the beloved. Before, Love was a delicate thread; now it is a halo, encompassing every being, expanding to embrace all that ever will be.
author: Khalil Gibran
source_section: The Vision: Reflections on the Way of the Soul (1994)

text: Some charm but never convince, too vague, too distant and obscure to reach the mind. It is with eloquence, not with principles, that they hold your ear and your passing thought, but never the core of your heart.
author: Khalil Gibran
source: Manasseh: On the Speech and Gesture of Jesus

text: And God said, Love your enemy, & I obeyed Him & loved myself.
author: Khalil Gibran
source: Paraphrasing of Gibran's quote "My enemy said to me, 'Love your enemy.' And I obeyed him and loved myself.", misattributed on these websites: GaiamLife (blog) & Quote Corner - Khalil Gibran quotes Part 1

text: All men love you for themselves. I love you for yourself.
author: Khalil Gibran
source: Mary Magdalen: On Meeting Jesus For The First Time

text: All that you see was and is for your sake. The numerous books, uncanny markings, and beautiful thoughts are the ghosts of souls who preceded you. The speech they weave is a link between you and your human siblings. The consequences that cause sorrow and rapture are the seeds that the past has sown in the field of the soul, and by which the future shall profit.
author: Khalil Gibran
source_section: The Vision: Reflections on the Way of the Soul (1994)

text: Love is a gracious host to his guests though to the unbidden his house is a mirage and a mockery.
author: Khalil Gibran
source: John At Patmos: Jesus The Gracious

text: Starvation, and not sin, is the parent of modern crime.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: The Epigrams of Oscar Wilde , edited by Alvin Redman (1954)

text: Heaven is a despotism. I shall be at home there.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: I go — as others already crucified have gone. And think not we are weary of crucifixion. For we must be crucified by larger and yet larger men, between greater earths and greater heavens.
author: Khalil Gibran

text: You hate Jesus because someone from the North Country said He was the Son of God. But you hate one another because each of you deems himself too great to be the brother of the next man.
author: Khalil Gibran
source: Mary Magdalen (Thirty years later): On the Resurrection of the Spirit

text: In truth we gaze but do not see, and hearken but do not hear; we eat and drink but do not taste. And there lies the difference between Jesus of Nazareth and ourselves. His senses were all continually made new, and the world to Him was always a new world.
author: Khalil Gibran
source: A Philosopher: On Wonder And Beauty

text: O love, whose lordly hand Has bridled my desires, And raised my hunger and my thirst To dignity and pride , Let not the strong in me and the constant Eat the bread or drink the wine That tempt my weaker self. Let me rather starve, And let my heart parch with thirst, And let me die and perish, Ere I stretch my hand To a cup you did not fill, Or a bowl you did not bless.
author: Khalil Gibran

text: The ancient Romans erected architectural wonders, only to make beasts fight.
author: Voltaire
source: The ancient Romans built their greatest masterpieces of architecture for wild beasts to fight in. Letter addressed to "un premier commis" [name unknown] (20 June 1733), from Oeuvres Complètes de Voltaire: Correspondance [Garnier frères, Paris, 1880], vol. I, letter 343 (p. 354)

text: Reader, suppose you were an idiot. And suppose you were a member of Congress. But I repeat myself.
author: Mark Twain
source: Draft manuscript (c.1881), quoted by Albert Bigelow Paine in Mark Twain: A Biography (1912), p. 724

text: Adam, at Eve's grave: Wheresoever she was, THERE was Eden.
author: Mark Twain

text: Opinions have caused more ills upon this small globe than plague and earthquakes.
author: Voltaire
source: Opinions have caused more ills than the plague or earthquakes on this little globe of ours. Letter to Élie Bertrand (5 January 1759)

text: Every sensible, good man must hold the Christian sect in horror.
author: Voltaire
source: Every sensible man, every honorable man, must hold the Christian sect in horror. Examen important de milord Bolingbroke (1736) Conclusion

text: By giving us the opinions of the uneducated, it keeps us in touch with the ignorance of the community.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: In a word, we only pray to God because we have made Him in our image. We treat Him like a pasha, like a sultan one can irritate or appease.
author: Voltaire
source: The Eternal has his designs from all eternity. If prayer is in accord with his immutable wishes, it is quite useless to ask of him what he has resolved to do. If one prays to him to do the contrary of what he has resolved, it is praying that he be weak, frivolous, inconstant; it is believing that he is thus, it is to mock him. Either you ask him a just thing, in which case he must do it, the thing being done without your praying to him for it, and so to entreat him is then to distrust him; or the thing is unjust, and then you insult him. You are worthy or unworthy of the grace you implore: if worthy, he knows it better than you; if unworthy, you commit another crime by requesting what is undeserved. In a word, we only pray to God because we have made him in our image. We treat him like a pasha, like a sultan whom one may provoke or appease. "Prayers" (1770)

text: Most men are too concerned with themselves to be malicious.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: Laws should be clear, uniform, precise : to interpret them is nearly always to corrupt them.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: England has done one thing; it has invented and established Public Opinion, which is an attempt to organize the ignorance of the community, and to elevate it to the dignity of physical force.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Every concept arises from the equation of unequal things.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: Persons attempting to find a motive in this narrative will be prosecuted; persons attempting to find a moral in it will be banished; persons attempting to find a plot in it will be shot. BY ORDER OF THE AUTHOR.
author: Mark Twain

text: In Art, the public accept what has been, not because they appreciate it, but because they cannot alter it. They swallow their classics whole, and never taste them. A fresh mode of Beauty is absolutely distasteful to them, and whenever it appears, they declare it either grossly unintelligible or grossly immoral.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: A dreamer is one who can only find his way by moonlight, and his punishment is that he sees the dawn before the rest of the world.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: The surest way to corrupt a youth is to instruct him to hold in higher esteem those who think alike than those who think differently.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: The surest way to corrupt a youth is to instruct him to hold in higher esteem those who think alike than those who think differently.

text: Man must be content, they say; but with what?
author: Voltaire
source: Man ought to be content, it is said; but with what?

text: If Nature had been comfortable, mankind would never have invented architecture.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: There is only one class in the community that thinks more about money than the rich, and that is the poor.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: A circle is a round straight line with a hole in the middle.
author: Mark Twain
source: Quoting a schoolchild in "English as She Is Taught"

text: The pains which vivisection inflicts upon unconsenting animals is the basis of my enmity towards it, and it is to me sufficient justification of the enmity without looking further.
author: Mark Twain
source: Letter to Sidney G. Trist, Editor of the Animals' Friend Magazine , in his capacity as Secretary of the London Anti-Vivisection Society (26 May 1899), in Mark Twain's Notebooks , ed. Carlo De Vito (Black Dog & Leventhal, 2015)

text: The note of the perfect personality is not rebellion, but peace.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: There are truths not meant for all men, nor for all times.
author: Voltaire
source: There are truths which are not for all men, nor for all times. Letter to François-Joachim de Pierre, cardinal de Bernis (23 April 1764)

text: Civilisation requires slaves. Unless there are those to do the ugly, uninteresting work, culture and contemplation become almost impossible. Human slavery is wrong. On mechanical slavery, on the slavery of the machine, the future of the world depends.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: With such a love for glory, how can you persist in a venture that will surely cost you it?
author: Voltaire
source: While loving glory so much how can you persist in a plan which will cause you to lose it? Letters of Voltaire and Frederick the Great (New York: Brentano's, 1927), transl. Richard Aldington , letter 130 from Voltaire to Frederick II of Prussia, October 1757

text: The humorous story is told gravely; the teller does his best to conceal the fact that he even dimly suspects that there is anything funny about it.
author: Mark Twain

text: Superstition is to religion what astrology is to astronomy: a very stupid daughter of a very wise mother.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: Ages of restriction to the one tool which the law was not able to take from him--his brain--have made that tool singularly competent.
author: Mark Twain
source_section: Concerning the Jews ( Harper's Magazine , Sept. 1899)

text: What is tolerance? It is humanity's prerogative. We are all forged from weakness and error; let us forgive each other our follies, for that is nature's first law.
author: Voltaire
source: What is tolerance? It is the consequence of humanity. We are all formed of frailty and error; let us pardon reciprocally each other's folly — that is the first law of nature. "Tolerance" (1764)

text: Anybody can make history. Only a great man can write it.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: One may certainly admire man as a mighty genius of construction, who succeeds in piling an infinitely complicated dome of concepts upon an unstable foundation, and, as it were, on running water. Of course, in order to be supported by such a foundation, his construction must be like one constructed of spiders' webs: delicate enough to be carried along by the waves, strong enough not to be blown apart by every wind.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: Humor must not professedly teach, and it must not professedly preach, but it must do both if it would live forever. By forever, I mean thirty years.
author: Mark Twain
source: Mark Twain in eruption: hitherto unpublished pages about men and events , 1940, Mark Twain, Bernard Augustine De Voto, Harper & brothers. This appears to be the origin of the variant:

text: I have the simplest tastes. I am always satisfied with the best.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: As quoted in Oscar Wilde : An Idler's Impression (1917) by Edgar Saltus, p. 20

text: Our labour preserves us from three great evils -- weariness, vice, and want.
author: Voltaire

text: Although always prepared for martyrdom, I preferred that it should be postponed.
author: Winston Churchill
source_section: My Early Life: A Roving Commission (1930)

text: A people who traffic in their children are more culpable than the buyer; this trade reveals our supposed superiority, for he who yields to a master was born to be ruled.
author: Voltaire
source: A people that sells its own children is more condemnable than the buyer; this commerce demonstrates our superiority; he who gives himself a master was born to have one. Essai sur les Moeurs et l'Espit des Nations (1753), ch. CXCVII: Résumé de toute cette histoire jusqu'au temps où commence le beau siècle de Louis XIV

text: Barring that natural expression of villainy which we all have, the man looked honest enough.
author: Mark Twain
source: "A Mysterious Visit", Buffalo Express , 19 March 1870. Anthologized in Mark Twain's Sketches, New and Old‎ (1875)

text: It is a superstition of the human mind to have imagined virginity as a virtue.
author: Voltaire
source: It is one of the superstitions of the human mind to have imagined that virginity could be a virtue. Notebooks (c.1735-c.1750) Note: This quotation and the three that follow directly below are from the so-called Leningrad Notebook, also known as Le Sottisier; it is one of several posthumously published notebooks of Voltaire.

text: People who count their chickens before they are hatched act very wisely because chickens run about so absurdly that it's impossible to count them accurately...
author: Oscar Wilde
source: Written in a letter to Robert Ross from Paris (31 May 1898)

text: All charming people, I fancy, are spoiled. It is the secret of their attraction.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: "The Portrait of Mr. W. H.," Blackwood's Magazine , July 1889

text: Loyalty to petrified opinions never yet broke a chain or freed a human soul in this world — and never will .
author: Mark Twain
source: "Consistency" (5 December 1887). This quote is engraved on Twain's bust in the National Hall of Fame

text: That body, which was called and is still called the Holy Roman Empire, was in no way holy, nor Roman, nor an empire.
author: Voltaire
source: This body which called itself and which still calls itself the Holy Roman Empire was in no way holy, nor Roman, nor an empire. Essai sur l'histoire générale et sur les mœurs et l'esprit des nations, Chapter 70 (1756)

text: Indifference is the revenge the world takes on mediocrities.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Let us work without reasoning; it is the only way to make life endurable.
author: Voltaire

text: Wherever there is a man who exercises authority, there is a man who resists authority.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: Man thought that the important thing was to have, and did not know that the important thing is to be. The true perfection of man lies, not in what man has, but in what man is.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: The easy confidence with which I know another man's religion is folly teaches me to suspect that my own is also.
author: Mark Twain
source: marginal note in Moncure D. Conway's Sacred Anthology

text: If there were no God, it would be necessary to invent one.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: Get your facts first, and then you can distort them as much as you please.
author: Mark Twain
source: As quoted in "An Interview with Mark Twain" , From Sea to Sea: Letters of Travel (1899) by Rudyard Kipling , Ch. 37, p. 180

text: An injurious lie is an uncommendable thing; and so, also, and in the same degree, is an injurious truth—a fact that is recognized by the law of libel .
author: Mark Twain
source: On the Decay of the Art of Lying

text: He to whom the present is the only thing that is present, knows nothing of the age in which he lives.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Most writers regard truth as their most valuable possession and therefore are most economical in its use.
author: Mark Twain
source: Said to portrait painter Samuel Johnson Woolf, cited in Here am I (1941), Samuel Johnson Woolf; this has often been abbreviated: Most writers regard truth as their most valuable possession, and therefore are most economical in its use.

text: All ancient histories, as one of our great minds observed, are merely agreed-upon fables.
author: Voltaire
source: Ancient histories, as one of our wits has said, are but fables that have been agreed upon. Jeannot et Colin (1764)

text: Mankind has never before possessed the tools for its own unfailing extermination, without having improved in virtue or wisdom. If self-preservation still exists, the prevention of this supreme catastrophe must be humanity's paramount endeavor.
author: Winston Churchill

text: All things are mortal but the Jew; all other forces pass, but he remains. What is the secret of his immortality?
author: Mark Twain
source_section: Concerning the Jews ( Harper's Magazine , Sept. 1899)

text: The highest perfection of politeness is only a beautiful edifice, built, from the base to the dome, of ungraceful and gilded forms of charitable and unselfish lying.
author: Mark Twain
source: On the Decay of the Art of Lying , published in The Stolen White Elephant: Etc , Pages 220-221 (1882)

text: Can we not trace the lineage of superstition and fanaticism to those ancient villains who first seized the knife from the altar, making victims of all who refused to be their disciples?
author: Voltaire
source: May we not return to those scoundrels of old, the illustrious founders of superstition and fanaticism, who first took the knife from the altar to make victims of those who refused to be their disciples? Letter to Frederick II of Prussia (December 1740), published in Oeuvres complètes de Voltaire , Vol. 7 (1869), edited by Georges Avenel, p. 105; as translated by Richard Aldington

text: Nothing that grieves us can be called little: by the eternal laws of proportion a child's loss of a doll and a king's loss of a crown are events of the same size.
author: Mark Twain

text: Criticism will annihilate race-prejudices by insisting upon the unity of the human mind in the variety of its forms.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Man has an invincible inclination to be deceived, enchanted by epic fables and actors more royal than kings. So long as it deceives without injury, the intellect, that master of deception, is free, celebrating its Saturnalia, never more luxuriant, richer, prouder, cleverer, or more daring.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source_section: On Truth and Lie in an Extra-Moral Sense (1873)

text: To pray to God is to flatter oneself that words alone can alter the whole of nature.
author: Voltaire
source: To pray to God is to flatter oneself that with words one can alter nature. Notebooks (c.1735-c.1750)

text: A little sincerity is a dangerous thing, and a great deal of it is absolutely fatal.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Men can only bear light to come in upon them by degrees.
author: Voltaire
source: The critical review, or annals of literature, Volume XXVI , by A Society of Gentlemen (1768) p. 450

text: Man is least himself when he talks in his own person. Give him a mask, and he will tell you the truth.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: If you pick up a starving dog and make him prosperous, he will not bite you. This is the principal difference between a dog and a man.
author: Mark Twain
source: The Tragedy of Pudd'nhead Wilson (1894), p. 214.

text: Among the three or four million cradles now rocking in the land are some which this nation would preserve for ages as sacred things, if we could know which ones they are.
author: Mark Twain

text: It is well for his peace that the saint goes to his martyrdom. He is spared the sight of the horror of his harvest.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: There is much to be said in favor of modern journalism. By giving us the opinions of the uneducated, it keeps us in touch with the ignorance of the community. By carefully chronicling the current events of contemporary life, it shows us of what very little importance such events really are. By invariably discussing the unnecessary, it makes us understand what things are requisite for culture, and what are not.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Art finds her own perfection within, and not outside of herself. She is not to be judged by any external standard of resemblance. She is a veil, rather than a mirror.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: A powerful agent is the right word. Whenever we come upon one of those intensely right words in a book or a newspaper the resulting effect is physical as well as spiritual, and electrically prompt.
author: Mark Twain

text: Honesty is the best policy — when there is money in it.
author: Mark Twain

text: All religions are more or less mixed with superstitions. Man is not reasonable enough to content himself with a pure and sensible religion, worthy of the Deity.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: It is very strange that men should deny a creator and yet attribute to themselves the power of creating eels.
author: Voltaire
source: Quoted in Robert H. Murray, Science and Scientists in the Nineteenth Century (1925), "Pasteur and Microbes", p. 237

text: When the doctrine of allegiance to party can utterly up-end a man's moral constitution and make a temporary fool of him besides, what excuse are you going to offer for preaching it, teaching it, extending it, perpetuating it? Shall you say, the best good of the country demands allegiance to party? Shall you also say it demands that a man kick his truth and his conscience into the gutter, and become a mouthing lunatic, besides?
author: Mark Twain
source: "Consistency", paper read at the Hartford Monday Evening Club on 5 December 1887. The Complete Essays of Mark Twain , p. 582 (First published in the 1923 edition of Mark Twain's Speeches , ed. Albert Bigelow Paine, pp. 120-130, where it is incorrectly dated "following the Blaine-Cleveland campaign, 1884." (See Mark Twain's Notebooks & Journals (1979), ed. Frederick Anderson, Vol. 3, p. 41, footnote 92 ) Many reprints repeat Paine's dating.)

text: It is said that God is always with the largest battalions.
author: Voltaire
source: It is said that God is always on the side of the big battalions. Letter to François-Louis-Henri Leriche (6 February 1770) Note: In his Notebooks (c.1735-c.1750), Voltaire wrote: God is not on the side of the big battalions, but on the side of those who shoot best.

text: To be great is to be misunderstood.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: Letter to James McNeill Whistler (23 February 1885)

text: The only thing that one really knows about human nature is that it changes. Change is the one quality we can predicate of it. The systems that fail are those that rely on the permanency of human nature, and not on its growth and development.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: We all do no end of feeling, and we mistake it for thinking. And out of it we get an aggregation which we consider a boon. Its name is public opinion. It is held in reverence. Some think it the voice of God.
author: Mark Twain

text: Pleasure has its time; so, too, has wisdom. Make love in thy youth, and in old age, attend to thy salvation.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: The mystery of love is greater than the mystery of death.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: The mystery of love is greater than the mystery of death. Salomé (1893)

text: Where there is happiness, there is found pleasure in nonsense.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: All philosophical sects have foundered upon the reef of physical and moral evil. It remains only to confess that God, having acted for the best, could not have acted better.
author: Voltaire
source: All philosophical sects have run aground on the reef of moral and physical ill. It only remains for us to confess that God, having acted for the best, had not been able to do better. "Power, Omnipotence," Dictionnaire philosophique (ed. 1785-1789)

text: Belgium's royal palace is still what it has been for the last 14 years: a lair of a wild beast that for its money every year mutilates, murders and starves a half million helpless natives in the Congo Free State.
author: Mark Twain
source: Mark Twain about the infamous 1904 Casement report (detailing the abuses in the Congo Free State by Roger Casement) Quoted from the book "Zaïre, Ketens van Koper (translated: Zaire, Chains of Copper)" Chapter 2: From Leopold II to Bwana Kitoko, page 19. Leuven, Kritak Fonds, 1978. ISBN 9063030185

text: I haven't a particle of confidence in a man who has no redeeming petty vices whatsoever.
author: Mark Twain
source: "Answers to Correspondents", The Californian , 17 June 1865. Anthologized in The Celebrated Jumping Frog of Calaveras County, and Other Sketches (1867)

text: Art is the most intense mode of individualism the world has known. Alone, without reference to others, the artist fashions a beautiful thing; if not solely for his own pleasure, he is no artist at all.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: Haste is universal because everyone is in flight from himself.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: A work of art is the unique result of a unique temperament. The moment an artist takes notice of what others want, and tries to supply the demand, he ceases to be an artist.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: A council of stern tyrants remains impervious to all temptations.
author: Voltaire
source: A company of solemn tyrants is impervious to all seductions. "Tyranny" (1764)

text: I put all my genius into my life; I put only my talent into my works.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: J'ai mis tout mon génie dans ma vie; je n'ai mis que mon talent dans mes œuvres.

text: Doubt is not a pleasant state, but certainty is a ridiculous one.
author: Voltaire
source: Doubt is not a pleasant condition, but certainty is an absurd one. What is most repellent in the System of Nature [of d'Holbach ] — after the recipe for making eels from flour — is the audacity with which it decides that there is no God, without even having tried to prove the impossibility. Letter to Frederick William, Prince of Prussia (28 November 1770). English: in S. G. Tallentyre (ed.), Voltaire in His Letters (New York: G. P. Putnam's Sons, 1919) p. 232. French: Au prince royal de prusse, le 28 novembre , in M. Palissot (ed.), Oeuvres de Voltaire: Lettres Choisies du Roi de Prusse et de M. de Voltaire , vol. II (Paris: Chez Baudoiun, 1802) p. 419

text: The more we study Art, the less we care for Nature . What Art really reveals to us is Nature's lack of design, her curious crudities, her extraordinary monotony, her absolutely unfinished condition.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: We have lost as much as we gained by prying into that matter.
author: Mark Twain

text: To live is the rarest thing in the world. Most people exist, that is all.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: Cunning tyrants are never punished.
author: Voltaire
source: Clever tyrants are never punished. Mérope , act V, scene V (1743)

text: To be good, according to the vulgar standard of goodness, is obviously quite easy. It merely requires a certain amount of sordid terror, a certain lack of imaginative thought, and a certain low passion for middle-class respectability.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Men will always be mad; and those who believe they can cure them are the maddest of all.
author: Voltaire
source: Men will always be mad, and those who think they can cure them are the maddest of all. Letter to Louise Dorothea of Meiningen , duchess of Saxe-Gotha Madame (30 January 1762)

text: Society depends upon women. The nations who confine them are unsociable.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: I was sorry to have my name mentioned as one of the great authors, because they have a sad habit of dying off. Chaucer is dead, Spencer is dead, so is Milton, so is Shakespeare, and I'm not feeling so well myself.
author: Mark Twain
source: Speech to the Savage Club, 9 June 1899, in Mark Twain's Speeches (1910), ed. William Dean Howells, pp. 277–278 . (Possibly fabricated from a paraphrase in Aaron Watson, The Savage Club: a Medley of History, Anecdote, and Reminiscence (1907), pp. 126–129 )

text: Even in cities blessed with peace and flourishing arts, inhabitants are devoured by envy, cares, and anxieties—plagues greater than any siege.
author: Voltaire

text: To have good laws, burn your own and forge new.
author: Voltaire
source: If you want good laws, burn those you have and make new ones. "Laws" (1765)

text: In every province, the chief occupations, in order of importance, are lovemaking, malicious gossip, and talking nonsense.
author: Voltaire

text: It is difficult to free fools from the chains they revere.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: Life is fraught with thorns, and I know no other cure than to tend one's garden.
author: Voltaire
source: Life is bristling with thorns, and I know no other remedy than to cultivate one's garden. Letter to Pierre-Joseph Luneau de Boisjermain (21 October 1769), from Oeuvres Complètes de Voltaire: Correspondance [Garnier frères, Paris, 1882], vol. XIV, letter # 7692 (p. 478)

text: Adam's temperament was the first divine command, the only one he could never disobey. It dictated: 'Be weak, be water, be characterless, be cheaply persuadable.' The later command, to shun the fruit, was destined for disobedience, not by Adam, but by his inherent nature.
author: Mark Twain
source: "The Turning Point of my Life", §3, Harper's Bazar , February 1910, as reprinted in Essays and Sketches of Mark Twain (1995), ed. Stuart Miller, ISBN 1566198798

text: Always acknowledge a fault frankly. This will throw those in authority off their guard and give you opportunity to commit more.
author: Mark Twain
source: More Maxims of Mark (1927) edited by Merle Johnson

text: Meredith is a prose Browning , and so is Browning. He used poetry as a medium for writing in prose.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: It is better to have a permanent income than to be fascinating .
author: Oscar Wilde

text: He is a stranger to me, but he is a most remarkable man — and I am the other one. Between us, we cover all knowledge; he knows all that can be known, and I know the rest.
author: Mark Twain
source: Statement (1906) in Mark Twain in Eruption: Hitherto Unpublished Pages About Men and Events (1940) edited by Bernard DeVoto

text: Disobedience , in the eyes of any one who has read history, is man's original virtue. It is through disobedience that progress has been made, through disobedience and through rebellion .
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: Action ... is the last resource of those who know not how to dream.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: There isn't time--so brief is life--for bickerings, apologies, heartburnings, callings to account. There is only time for loving--and but an instant, so to speak, for that.
author: Mark Twain

text: No creature is more desolate than the man who has evaded his genius, now glancing everywhere but within. He is all exterior without a core, a damaged, painted, puffed-up garment.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
source: There exists no more repulsive and desolate creature in the world than the man who has evaded his genius and who now looks furtively to left and right, behind him and all about him. In the end such a man becomes impossible to get hold of, since he is wholly exterior, without kernel: a tattered, painted bag of clothes; a decked-out ghost that cannot inspire even fear and certainly not pity. “ Schopenhauer as educator,” § 3.1, R. Hollingdale , trans. (1983), p. 128

text: If this is the best of possible worlds, what then are the others?
author: Voltaire

text: If there were only one religion in England there would be danger of despotism, if there were two they would cut each other's throats, but there are thirty, and they live in peace and happiness.
author: Voltaire
source: Letters on England , letter 6, "On the Presbyterians", trans. Leonard Tancock (Penguin Books, 1980) p. 41, published first in English in 1733

text: My experience is that as soon as people are old enough to know better, they don't know anything at all.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Divorce is probably almost as old as marriage. Yet, I believe marriage is a few weeks older.
author: Voltaire
source: Divorce is probably of nearly the same age as marriage. I believe, however, that marriage is some weeks the more ancient. "Divorce" (1771)

text: Faith is believing what reason does not.
author: Voltaire
source: Faith consists in believing what reason cannot. "The Flood" (1764)

text: All you need in this life is ignorance and confidence, and then Success is sure.
author: Mark Twain

text: The form of government most suitable to the artist is no government at all. Authority over him and his art is ridiculous.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: He is really not so ugly after all, provided, of course, that one shuts one's eyes, and does not look at him.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: "The Birthday of the Infanta" , The House of Pomegranates (1892)

text: Experience is the name everyone gives to their mistakes.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Weather is a literary specialty, and no untrained hand can turn out a good article on it.
author: Mark Twain

text: [E]verybody who is incapable of learning has taken to teaching.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Clothes make the man. Naked people have little or no influence on society.
author: Mark Twain
source: More Maxims of Mark (1927) edited by Merle Johnson

text: Thunder is good, thunder is impressive; but it is lightning that does the work.
author: Mark Twain

text: By giving us the opinions of the uneducated, journalism keeps us in touch with the ignorance of the community. By carefully chronicling current events, it shows us of what very little importance such events really are. By invariably discussing the unnecessary, it makes us understand what things are requisite for culture, and what are not.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: The one duty we owe to history is to rewrite it.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Be respectful to your superiors, if you have any.
author: Mark Twain
source: "Advice to Youth", speech to The Saturday Morning Club, Boston, 15 April 1882. Mark Twain Speaking (1976), ed. Paul Fatout, p. 169

text: The institution of religion exists only to keep mankind in order, and to make men merit the goodness of God by their virtue. Everything in a religion which does not tend towards this goal must be considered foreign or dangerous.
author: Voltaire

text: We never live: we are always in expectation of living.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: Just as the worst slave-owners were those kind to their slaves, preventing the system's horror from being realized, so, in England, the people who do most harm are those who try to do most good.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: Love is of all the passions the strongest, for it attacks simultaneously the head, the heart, and the senses.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: Quotations, when engraved upon the memory, give you good thoughts and make you anxious to read the authors and look for more.
author: Winston Churchill
source_section: My Early Life: A Roving Commission (1930)

text: As for begging, it is safer to beg than to take, but it is finer to take than to beg.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: Prayer must never be answered: if it is, it ceases to be prayer and becomes correspondence.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: Quoted by Alvin Redman in The Epigrams of Oscar Wilde (1952)

text: A man who knows the price of everything and the value of nothing.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: The message of sunset is sadness; the message of dawn is hope.
author: Winston Churchill
source_section: My Early Life: A Roving Commission (1930)

text: The work of art is to dominate the spectator: the spectator is not to dominate the work of art. The spectator is to be receptive, the violin on which the master is to play.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: The things people say of a man do not alter a man. He is what he is. Public opinion is of no value whatsoever.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: Truth, in the matters of religion, is simply the opinion that has survived.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: If God made us in his image, we have certainly returned the favor.
author: Voltaire
source: If God has made us in his image, we have returned him the favor. Notebooks (c.1735-c.1750)

text: A thing is not necessarily true because a man dies for it.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: The Portrait of Mr. W. H. (1889)

text: What man has sought is not pain nor pleasure, but simply Life: to live intensely, fully, perfectly. Pleasure is Nature's test, her sign of approval.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: Life imitates art far more than art imitates Life.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Such is the human condition that to wish for the greatness of one's country is to wish ill upon one's neighbors.
author: Voltaire
source: Such then is the human condition, that to wish greatness for one's country is to wish harm to one's neighbors. "Fatherland" (1764)

text: Sometimes the poor are praised for being thrifty. But to recommend thrift to the poor is both grotesque and insulting. It is like advising a man who is starving to eat less.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: It is a strange thing that thought depends absolutely on the stomach, and yet the best stomachs are not the best thinkers.
author: Voltaire
source: Thought depends largely on the stomach. In spite of this, those with the best stomachs are not always the best thinkers. Letter to Jean le Rond d'Alembert (20 August 1770)

text: As I slowly grow wise I briskly grow cautious.
author: Mark Twain
source: "English as She Is Taught", The Century , Vol. 33, No. 6, April 1887 . A slightly abridged version was reprinted as Introduction to Caroline B. Le Row, English as She Is Taught: Genuine Answers to Some Examination Questions Asked in Our Public Schools (1901)

text: One is tempted to define man as a rational animal who always loses his temper when he is called upon to act in accordance with the dictates of reason.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: I have always offered God a very short prayer: 'My God, make our enemies utterly ridiculous!' God has granted it.
author: Voltaire
source: I always made one prayer to God, a very short one. Here it is: "O Lord, make our enemies quite ridiculous!" God granted it. Letter to Étienne Noël Damilaville (16 May 1767)

text: I do not know in the whole history of the world a hero, a worthy man, a prophet, a true Christian, who has not been the victim of the jealous, of a scamp, or of a sinister spirit.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: When it comes to money, everyone shares the same religion.
author: Voltaire
source: When it is a question of money, everybody is of the same religion. Letter to Mme. d'Épinal, Ferney (26 December 1760) from Oeuvres Complètes de Voltaire: Correspondance (Garnier frères, Paris, 1881), vol. IX, letter # 4390 (p. 124)

text: We can not always oblige, but we can always speak obligingly.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: It is immoral to use private property in order to alleviate the horrible evils that result from the institution of private property.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: When a man has no enemy left there must be something mean about him.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: It is forbidden to kill; every murderer is punished, unless he has killed in great company, and to the sound of trumpets.
author: Voltaire
source: It is forbidden to kill; therefore all murderers are punished unless they kill in large numbers and to the sound of trumpets. "Rights" (1771)

text: One of the brightest gems in the New England weather is the dazzling uncertainty of it.
author: Mark Twain
source_section: New England Weather , speech to the New England Society (December 22, 1876)

text: Nothing should harm a man but himself. What a man truly possesses is within him; what is outside should be of no importance.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: If you tell the truth you don't have to remember anything.
author: Mark Twain
source: Notebook entry, January or February 1894, Mark Twain's Notebook , ed. Albert Bigelow Paine (1935), p. 240

text: Experience, the name men give to their mistakes.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: All the passions die with the years; self-love alone never dies.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: Humor is the great thing, the saving thing. The minute it crops up, all our hardnesses yield, all our irritations and resentments flit away and a sunny spirit takes their place.
author: Mark Twain

text: I was a-trembling, because I'd got to decide, forever, betwixt two things, and I knowed it. I studied a minute, sort of holding my breath, and then says to myself, "All right, then, I'll go to hell."
author: Mark Twain

text: As long as war is regarded as wicked, it will always have its fascination. When it is looked upon as vulgar, it will cease to be popular.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Superstition is to religion what astrology is to astronomy: the very foolish daughter of a very wise mother. These two daughters have long held the entire world in thrall.
author: Voltaire
source: Superstition is to religion what astrology is to astronomy, the mad daughter of a wise mother. These daughters have too long dominated the earth. "Whether it is useful to maintain the people in superstition," Treatise on Toleration (1763)

text: A false science makes atheists, a true science prostrates men before the Deity
author: Voltaire
source: The critical review, or annals of literature, Volume XXVI , by A Society of Gentlemen (1768) p. 450

text: If as much care were taken to perpetuate a race of fine men as is done to prevent the mixture of ignoble blood in horses and dogs, the genealogy of every one would be written on his face and displayed in his manners.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: Between men and women there is no friendship possible. There is passion, enmity, worship, love, but no friendship.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Fool, nothing is impossible in Russia but reform.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: In old days men had the rack. Now they have the press.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: What a poverty of spirit to assert that beasts are machines deprived of sentiment. You discover in them all the same organs of feeling as in yourself. Has nature arranged all the springs of sentiment in an animal that it should not feel? Has it nerves to be incapable of suffering? Do not suppose this impertinent contradiction in nature.
author: Voltaire
source: " Beasts ", in A Philosophical Dictionary , Volume 2, J. and H. L. Hunt, 1824, p. 9

text: All men would necessarily be equal if they were without needs. The misery inherent in our species subordinates one man to another: it is not inequality that is a real misfortune, but dependence.
author: Voltaire
source: All men would then be necessarily equal, if they were without needs. It is the poverty connected with our species which subordinates one man to another. It is not inequality which is the real misfortune, it is dependence. "Equality" (1764)

text: He who thinks a great deal is not suited to be a party man: he thinks his way through the party and out the other side too soon.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: Money is always found for sending men to die on the frontier; there is none left when it comes to saving them.
author: Voltaire
source: Money is always to be found when men are to be sent to the frontiers to be destroyed: when the object is to preserve them, it is no longer so. "Charity" (1770)

text: In Boston they ask, How much does he know? In New York, How much is he worth? In Philadelphia, Who were his parents?
author: Mark Twain
source: "What Paul Bourget Thinks of Us?" , in How to Tell a Story and Other Essays (1897)

text: Active men often lack higher, individual activity. They are active as generic beings—officials, businessmen, scholars—but not as unique men. In this respect, they are lazy.
author: Friedrich Nietzsche

text: The public dislikes novelty, yet the vitality and progress of art depend on its continual extension. They fear novelty because it represents individualism, an assertion by the artist that he selects his own subject and treats it as he chooses.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: His style is chaos illumined by flashes of lightning.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: A reference to George Meredith 's style.

text: A man who does not think for himself does not think at all.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: The public have an insatiable curiosity to know everything, except what is worth knowing. Journalism, conscious of this, and having tradesmanlike habits, supplies their demands.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: Gossip is charming! History is merely gossip. But scandal is gossip made tedious by morality.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: It is better to risk saving a guilty man than to condemn an innocent one.
author: Voltaire
source: It is better to risk sparing a guilty person than to condemn an innocent one. Zadig (1747)

text: If Jesus had been damned for the race, that would have been an act of a size proper to a god, and would have saved the whole race. However, why should anybody want to save the human race, or damn it either?
author: Mark Twain

text: We owe consideration to the living; to the dead, we owe only the truth.
author: Voltaire
source: We should be considerate to the living; to the dead we owe only the truth. Letter to M. de Grenonville (1719)

text: Satire lies about men of letters during their life, and eulogy after their death.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: We have really everything in common with America nowadays, except, of course, language.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: The Canterville Ghost (1887). For history and analysis of the quote see Common Language .

text: It is very difficult to remove from fools the chains they revere.
author: Voltaire
source: It is difficult to free fools from the chains they revere. Le dîner du comte de Boulainvilliers (1767): Troisième Entretien

text: 'Know thyself' was written over the portal of the antique world. Over the portal of the new world, 'Be thyself' shall be written.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: Eaten bread is soon forgotten. Dangers which are warded off by effective precautions and foresight are never even remembered.
author: Winston Churchill
source: The World Crisis, 1911–1914 : Chapter XVII (The Grand Fleet and the Submarine Alarm), Churchill, Butterworth (1923), p. 399

text: In America, the President reigns for four years, and journalism governs for ever and ever.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: To promise not to do a thing is the surest way in the world to make a body want to go and do that very thing.
author: Mark Twain

text: All joys do not cause laughter; great pleasures are serious: pleasures of love do not make us laugh.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: All thinkers have about the same principles, and form but one republic.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: A husband who seeks to surprise is often greatly surprised himself.
author: Voltaire
source: Translation: The husband who decides to surprise his wife is often very much surprised himself.

text: An individual who has to make things for the use of others, and with reference to their wants and their wishes, does not work with interest, and consequently cannot put into his work what is best in him. Upon the other hand, whenever a community or a powerful section of a community, or a government of any kind, attempts to dictate to the artist what he is to do, Art either entirely vanishes, or becomes stereotyped, or degenerates into a low and ignoble form of craft.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: Enough, enough, enough! Say no more! Lump the whole thing! say that the Creator made Italy from designs by Michael Angelo!
author: Mark Twain

text: Death must be so beautiful. To lie in the soft brown earth, with the grasses waving above one's head, and listen to silence. To have no yesterday, and no tomorrow. To forget time, to forgive life, to be at peace.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Too often the strong, silent man is silent only because he does not know what to say, and is reputed strong only because he has remained silent.
author: Winston Churchill
source: Winston S. Churchill: His Complete Speeches (1974), Chelsea House, Volume IV: 1922–1928, p. 3462 ISBN 0835206939

text: He had only one vanity; he thought he could give advice better than any other person.
author: Mark Twain
source: " The Man That Corrupted Hadleyburg ", ch. I, in The Man That Corrupted Hadleyburg and Other Stories and Essays (1900)

text: Good kings are the enemies of democracy.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Always do right. This will gratify some people, and astonish the rest.
author: Mark Twain
source: To the Young People's Society, Greenpoint Presbyterian Church, Brooklyn (16 February 1901)

text: Nothing is so common as to read and converse unprofitably. Define your terms.
author: Voltaire

text: I have been complimented many times and they always embarrass me; I always feel that they have not said enough.
author: Mark Twain

text: Many a small thing has been made large by the right kind of advertising.
author: Mark Twain
source_section: A Connecticut Yankee in King Arthur's Court (1889)

text: Tell me, when you are alone with him Sphinx, does he take off his face and reveal his mask?
author: Oscar Wilde
source: In a letter to Ada Leverson [Sphinx] recorded in her book Letters To The Sphinx From Oscar Wilde and Reminiscences of the Author (1930)

text: He is now fast rising from affluence to poverty.
author: Mark Twain
source: "Rev. Henry Ward Beecher's Farm" (1869), anthologized in Mark Twain's Sketches (1872)

text: I love truth dearly, but I have no fondness for martyrdom.
author: Voltaire
source: I am very fond of truth, but not at all of martyrdom. Letter to Jean le Rond d'Alembert (8 February 1776)

text: Hard work is simply the refuge of people who have nothing whatever to do.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Happy Prince and Other Tales (1888)

text: A simile committing suicide is always a depressing spectacle.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: "The Poets' Corner III," The Pall Mall Gazette (May 30, 1887)

text: Thou hast tasted of prosperity and adversity: thou hast been driven out of the country over which thou now reignest, and from the throne on which thou sittest: thou hast groaned beneath the yoke of oppression; therefore hast thou reason to know how hateful the oppressor is both to God and man. If, after all these warnings and advertisements, thou dost not turn unto the Lord, with all thy heart; but forget Him who remembered thee in thy distress, and give thyself up to follow lust and vanity, surely great will be thy guilt, and bitter thy condemnation. Instead of listening to the flatterers about thee, hearken only to the voice that is within thee, which never flatters.
author: Voltaire

text: I cannot understand your nature. If my nature had been made to suit your comprehension rather than my own requirements, I am afraid I would have made a very poor figure in the world.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Virtue: a word easy to pronounce, difficult to understand.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: All modes of government are failures. Despotism is unjust to everybody, including the despot, who was probably made for better things. Oligarchies are unjust to the many, and ochlocracies are unjust to the few. High hopes were once formed of democracy; but democracy means simply the bludgeoning of the people by the people for the people.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: Nothing is more common than to imitate one's enemies, and to wield their very weapons.
author: Voltaire
source: Nothing is so common as to imitate one's enemies, and to use their weapons. "Oracles" (1770)

text: Principles have no real force except when one is well-fed.
author: Mark Twain

text: Anything serves as a pretext for the wicked.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: Laws are sand, customs are rock. Laws can be evaded and punishment escaped, but an openly transgressed custom brings sure punishment.
author: Mark Twain

text: I can resist everything except temptation.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: The first king was but a fortunate soldier; he who serves his country well needs no ancestors.
author: Voltaire
source: The first who was king was a fortunate soldier: Who serves his country well has no need of ancestors. Mérope , act I, scene III (1743). Borrowed from Lefranc de Pompignan's "Didon"

text: You can't depend on your eyes when your imagination is out of focus.
author: Mark Twain
source_section: A Connecticut Yankee in King Arthur's Court (1889)

text: A round man cannot be expected to fit in a square hole right away. He must have time to modify his shape.
author: Mark Twain

text: Virtue never has been as respectable as money.
author: Mark Twain

text: O unfortunates who sin without pleasure! in your errors be more reasonable; be, at least, fortunate sinners. Since you must be damned, be damned for amiable faults.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: One always speaks poorly when one has nothing to say.
author: Voltaire
source: One always speaks badly when one has nothing to say. "Commentaires sur Corneille", Oeuvres complètes de Voltaire (1827)

text: Virtue presupposes liberty. In constraint, there is no virtue, and without virtue, no religion. My thought is no more subject to authority than sickness or health.
author: Voltaire
source: Virtue supposes liberty, as the carrying of a burden supposes active force. Under coercion there is no virtue, and without virtue there is no religion. Make a slave of me, and I shall be no better for it. Even the sovereign has no right to use coercion to lead men to religion, which by its nature supposes choice and liberty. My thought is no more subject to authority than is sickness or health. "Canon Law: Ecclesiastical Ministry" (1771)

text: There was a good reason for removing that motto: it stated a lie. If this nation has ever trusted in God, that time has gone by; for nearly half a century its entire trust has been in the Republican party and the dollar—mainly the dollar. As for America being a Christian country? Why, so is hell.
author: Mark Twain
source: Statements ( c. December 1907), in Mark Twain In Eruption : Hitherto Unpublished Pages About Men And Events (1940) edited by Bernard Augustine De Voto

text: Formerly, if you killed a man, it was possible that you were insane—but now, if you, having friends and money, kill a man, it is evidence that you are a lunatic.
author: Mark Twain
source: "A New Crime", first published as "The New Crime" in the Buffalo Express , 16 April 1870. Anthologized in Mark Twain's Sketches, New and Old‎ (1875).

text: It is not worth while to try to keep history from repeating itself, for man's character will always make the preventing of the repetitions impossible.
author: Mark Twain
source: Mark Twain in Eruption: Hitherto Unpublished Pages About Men and Events (1940) edited by Bernard DeVoto

text: Agitators, by sowing discontent among the contented, are absolutely necessary, for without them, there would be no advance towards civilisation.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: There is always more brass than brains in an aristocracy.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Prosperity is the best protector of principle.
author: Mark Twain

text: Compliments make me vain; and when vain, I am insolent and overbearing. Yet I love them, even when untrue. I can live on a good compliment for two weeks with nothing else to eat.
author: Mark Twain

text: Use, but do not abuse; so the wise command. I shun both Epictetus and Petronius, for neither abstinence nor excess ever brought happiness.
author: Voltaire
source: Use, do not abuse; as the wise man commands. I flee Epictetus and Petronius alike. Neither abstinence nor excess ever renders man happy. "Cinquième discours: sur la nature de plaisir", Sept Discours en Vers sur l'Homme (1738)

text: Heaven made virtue; man, the appearance.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: Love is wiser than Philosophy, though she is wise, and mightier than Power, though he is mighty.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Happy Prince and Other Tales (1888)

text: Selfishness is not living as one wishes to live, it is asking others to live as one wishes to live.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: In America the President reigns for four years, and Journalism governs for ever and ever.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: They resolved that taking sweetmeats was merely 'hooking,' while taking bacon and hams was plain stealing, a crime forbidden by the Bible. Therefore, their piracies should never again be sullied by the crime of stealing.
author: Mark Twain

text: If it took Labouchere three columns to prove that I was forgotten, then there is no difference between fame and obscurity.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: As quoted in The New-York Herald (12 August 1883)

text: He was ignorant of the commonest accomplishments of youth. He could not even lie.
author: Mark Twain
source: "Brief Biographical Sketch of George Washington", The Celebrated Jumping Frog of Calaveras County, and Other Sketches (1867), ed. John Paul

text: Life is far too important a thing ever to talk seriously about it.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: It is always a silly thing to give advice, but to give good advice is absolutely fatal.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: The Portrait of Mr. W. H. (1889), p. 5

text: A single branch of physics consumes the lives of many, often leaving them to die in uncertainty.
author: Voltaire
source: A single part of physics occupies the lives of many men, and often leaves them dying in uncertainty. "A Madame la Marquise du Châtelet, Avant-Propos", Eléments de Philosophie de Newton (1738)

text: No great artist ever sees things as they really are. If he did, he would cease to be an artist.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: The kernel, the soul, the substance of all human utterances is plagiarism. For substantially all ideas are second-hand, drawn from a million outside sources, with no originality save the slight discoloration from one's own mental calibre and temperament.
author: Mark Twain
source: Letter to Helen Keller , after she had been accused of plagiarism for one of her early stories (17 March 1903), published in Mark Twain's Letters , Vol. 1 (1917) edited by Albert Bigelow Paine, p. 731

text: If they truly knew the philosopher, they would tremble. For he preached the great creed of Inaction, pointing out the uselessness of all things.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: Review of Herbert Giles translation of the works of Zhuangzi (Chuang Tsu) in The Speaker (8 February 1890)

text: Alas! how can we always resist? The devil tempts us, and the flesh is weak.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: An idea that is not dangerous is unworthy of being called an idea at all.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: The Epigrams of Oscar Wilde , edited by Alvin Redman (1954)

text: In general, the art of government consists of taking as much money as possible from one part of the citizens to give it to another.
author: Voltaire
source: In general, the art of government consists in taking as much money as possible from one party of the citizens to give to the other. "Money" (1770)

text: It is curious that physical courage should be so common in the world, and moral courage so rare.
author: Mark Twain
source: Mark Twain in Eruption: Hitherto Unpublished Pages About Men and Events (1940) edited by Bernard DeVoto

text: It is sometimes said that common sense is quite rare.
author: Voltaire
source: It is sometimes said, common sense is very rare. Philosophical Dictionary "Sens Commun" , Dictionnaire philosophique (ed. 1767)

text: Ah! Don't say you agree with me. When people agree with me I always feel I must be wrong.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: This also appears in Lady Windermere's Fan (1892), Act II

text: Shun idleness: it is the rust that attaches itself to the most brilliant metals.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: All authority is quite degrading. It degrades those who exercise it, and degrades those over whom it is exercised.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: When private property is abolished there will be no necessity for crime, no demand for it; it will cease to exist.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: Psychology is in its infancy, as a science. I hope in the interests of Art, it will always remain so.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: Oscar Wilde (1897), | Hart-Davis, ed., Letters of Wilde , p. 173

text: A gifted person ought to learn English (barring spelling and pronouncing) in thirty hours, French in thirty days, and German in thirty years.
author: Mark Twain

text: An experienced, industrious, ambitious, and often quite picturesque liar.
author: Mark Twain
source: " The Private History of a Campaign That Failed ", The Century , Vol. 31, No. 2, December 1885 . Anthologized in The American Claimant, and Other Stories and Sketches (1898)

text: Superstition sets the whole world aflame; philosophy extinguishes the fires.
author: Voltaire
source: Superstition sets the whole world in flames; philosophy quenches them. Dictionnaire philosophique (ed. 1822), "Superstition"

text: For my own part I have always felt that a politician is to be judged by the animosities which he excites among his opponents. I have always set myself not merely to relish but to deserve thoroughly their censure.
author: Winston Churchill
source: November 17, 1906, Institute of Journalists Dinner, London; in Churchill by Himself (2008), ed. Langworth, PublicAffairs, p. 392 ISBN 1586486381

text: One can survive everything nowadays except death.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: The true personality of man will grow naturally, flowerlike, having wisdom, not knowledge. It will have nothing, yet everything, so rich will it be. It will love others for their differences, helping all simply by being what it is.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: They are always asking a writer why he does not write like somebody else, or a painter why he does not paint like somebody else, quite oblivious of the fact that if either of them did anything of the kind he would cease to be an artist.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: It is absurd to divide people into good and bad. People are either charming or tedious.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: I die adoring God, loving my friends, not hating my enemies, and detesting superstition.
author: Voltaire
source: I die adoring God, loving my friends, not hating my enemies, and detesting superstition. Déclaration de Voltaire, note to his secretary, Jean-Louis Wagnière (28 February 1778)

text: The secret of being a bore is to tell everything.
author: Voltaire
source: The secret of being a bore is to tell everything. "Sixième discours: sur la nature de l'homme", Sept Discours en Vers sur l'Homme (1738)

text: Travel is fatal to prejudice, bigotry, and narrow-mindedness.
author: Mark Twain

text: Life is much too important a thing ever to talk seriously about it.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: A poet can survive everything but a misprint.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: "The Children of the Poets," The Pall Mall Gazette (October 14, 1886)

text: In matters of news, one must always await the sacrament of confirmation.
author: Voltaire
source: When we hear news, we should always wait for the sacrament of confirmation. Letter to Charles-Augustin Ferriol, comte d'Argental (28 August 1760]])

text: But when the toast works down to the babies, we stand on common ground.
author: Mark Twain
source: Answering a toast, "To the Babies," at a banquet in honor of General U.S. Grant (November 14, 1879).

text: A baby is an inestimable blessing and bother.
author: Mark Twain
source: Letter to Annie Moffett Webster (1 September 1876)

text: To create man was a fine and original idea; but to add the sheep was a tautology.
author: Mark Twain
source: St. Louis Post-Dispatch (30 May 1902); also in Mark Twain : A Life , p. 611

text: To drift with every passion till my soul Is a stringed lute on which all winds can play, Is it for this that I have given away Mine ancient wisdom, and austere control? Methinks my life is a twice-written scroll Scrawled over on some boyish holiday With idle songs for pipe and virelay , Which do but mar the secret of the whole.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: I have no special regard for Satan; but, I can at least claim that I have no prejudice against him. It may even be that I lean a little his way, on account of his not having a fair show. All religions issue bibles against him, and say the most injurious things about him, but we never hear his side. We have none but the evidence for the prosecution, and yet we have rendered the verdict. To my mind, this is irregular. It is un-English, it is un-American; it is French.
author: Mark Twain
source_section: Concerning the Jews ( Harper's Magazine , Sept. 1899)

text: He [George Washington Cable] has taught me to abhor and detest the Sabbath day and hunt up new and troublesome ways to dishonor it.
author: Mark Twain
source: Letter to William Dean Howells, 27 February 1885, in Albert Bigelow Paine, Mark Twain's letters: Arranged with Comment (1917), Vol. 2, p. 450

text: Really, what we want now, is not laws against crime, but a law against insanity.
author: Mark Twain

text: Man is free the moment he wills himself to be.
author: Voltaire
source: Man is free at the instant he wants to be. Brutus , act II, scene I (1730)

text: Misery and poverty are so absolutely degrading, and exercise such a paralysing effect over the nature of men, that no class is ever really conscious of its own suffering. They have to be told of it by other people, and they often entirely disbelieve them.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: And down the long and silent street, The dawn, with silver-sandalled feet, Crept like a frightened girl.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: For to disagree with three-fourths of the British public on all points is one of the first elements of sanity, one of the deepest consolations in all moments of spiritual doubt.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Everything tends towards catastrophe and collapse. I am interested, geared up and happy. Is it not horrible to be made like this?
author: Winston Churchill
source: In a letter to his wife Clemmie, during the build up to World War I.

text: Whose property is my body? Probably mine. I so regard it. If I experiment with it, who must be answerable? I, not the State. If I choose injudiciously, does the State die? Oh no.
author: Mark Twain

text: It is perilous to be right when those in power are wrong.
author: Voltaire
source: It is dangerous to be right in matters where established men are wrong. "Catalogue pour la plupart des écrivains français qui ont paru dans Le Siècle de Louis XIV, pour servir à l'histoire littéraire de ce temps," Le Siècle de Louis XIV (1752) Fontenelle Variant: It is dangerous to be right when the government is wrong.

text: There are few things easier than to live badly and to die well.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: It is the privilege of true genius, especially that which forges new paths, to err greatly with impunity.
author: Voltaire
source: It is the privilege of true genius , and certainly of the genius that opens a new road, to make without punishment great mistakes. "Siècle de Louis XIV", ch. 32 (1751), qtd. in Arthur Schopenhauer, The World as Will and Representation (1818), "Criticism of the Kantian philosophy"

text: Man is a Religious Animal. He is the only Religious Animal. He is the only animal that has the True Religion — several of them. He is the only animal that loves his neighbor as himself and cuts his throat if his theology isn't straight.
author: Mark Twain

text: The public is a ferocious beast: one must either chain it or flee.
author: Voltaire
source: The public is a ferocious beast: one must chain it up or flee from it. Letter to Mademoiselle Quinault, quoted in Charles Sainte-Beuve, "Lettres inédites de Voltaire," Causeries de Lundi (20 October 1856) ; an English translation can be found on this page:

text: Consistency is the last refuge of the unimaginative.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: "The Relation of Dress to Art," The Pall Mall Gazette (February 28, 1885)

text: A man's life must be nailed to a cross either of Thought or Action.
author: Winston Churchill
source_section: My Early Life: A Roving Commission (1930)

text: Love is the strongest of all passions, for it assails at once the head, the heart, and the body.
author: Voltaire
source: Love is of all the passions the strongest, for it attacks simultaneously the head, the heart, and the body. Le Dernier Volume Des Œuvres De Voltaire: Contes — Comédie — Pensées -— Poésies — Lettres (1862)

text: Art is Individualism, and Individualism is a disturbing and disintegrating force. Therein lies its immense value. For what it seeks to disturb is monotony of type, slavery of custom, tyranny of habit, and the reduction of man to the level of a machine.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: A republic is not founded on virtue, but on the ambition of its citizens.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: They use thought only to justify their injustices, and words only to disguise their thoughts.
author: Voltaire
source: Men use thought only to justify their wrongdoings, and speech only to conceal their thoughts. Dialogue 14 , Le Chapon et la Poularde (1766); reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: A minister is excusable for the harm he does when the helm of the state is forced into his hand by storms; but in calm, he is guilty of all the good he fails to do.
author: Voltaire
source: A minister of state is excusable for the harm he does when the helm of government has forced his hand in a storm; but in the calm he is guilty of all the good he does not do. Le Siècle de Louis XIV , ch. VI: "État de la France jusqu'à la mort du cardinal Mazarin en 1661" (1752) Unsourced paraphrase or variant translation: Every man is guilty of all the good he did not do.

text: It is always the unreadable that occurs.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: "You're a bitter man," said Candide. "That's because I've lived," said Martin.
author: Voltaire

text: Use, do not abuse : neither abstinence nor excess ever renders man happy.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: In the old days men had the rack. Now they have the Press. That is an improvement certainly, but still it is very bad, and wrong, and demoralising. We are dominated by journalism.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: There are three kinds of despots. There is the despot who tyrannises over the body. There is the despot who tyrannises over the soul. There is the despot who tyrannises over the soul and body alike. The first is called the Prince. The second is called the Pope. The third is called the People.
author: Oscar Wilde
source_section: The Soul of Man Under Socialism (1891)

text: Love truth, but forgive error.
author: Voltaire
source: Love truth , but pardon error . "Deuxième discours: de la liberté," Sept Discours en Vers sur l'Homme (1738)

text: My own business always bores me to death. I prefer other people's.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Love is a canvas furnished by Nature, and embroidered by imagination.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: An excellent man: he has no enemies, and none of his friends like him.
author: Oscar Wilde
source: Quoted by George Bernard Shaw in a letter to Ellen Terry (25 September 1896)

text: I am the only person in the world I should like to know thoroughly.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Let the punishments of criminals be useful. A hanged man is good for nothing, but a man condemned to public works still serves the nation and is a living lesson.
author: Voltaire
source: Let the punishments of criminals be useful. A hanged man is good for nothing; a man condemned to public works still serves the country, and is a living lesson. "Civil and Ecclesiastical Laws", Dictionnaire philosophique (1785-1789)

text: Paradise was made for tender hearts; hell, for loveless hearts.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)
