text: There is no mode of action, no form of emotion, that we do not share with the lower animals. It is only by language that we rise above them, or above each other—by language, which is the parent, and not the child, of thought.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: Whatever you do, crush the infamous, and love those who love you.
author: Voltaire
source: Whatever you do, crush the infamous thing, and love those who love you. Letter to Jean le Rond d'Alembert (28 November 1762); This was written in reference to crushing superstition, and the words " écrasez l'infâme " ("Crush the Infamy") became a motto strongly identified with Voltaire.

text: Define your terms, I say, or we shall never understand.
author: Voltaire
source: Define your terms, you will permit me again to say, or we shall never understand one another. "Miracles" (1764)

text: A man who, in a fit of melancholy, takes his life today, would wish to live had he but waited eight days.
author: Voltaire
source: The man, who in a fit of melancholy, kills himself today, would have wished to live had he waited a week. "Cato" (1764)

text: The best written book is a receipt for a pottage.
author: Voltaire
source_section: A Thousand Flashes of French Wit, Wisdom, and Wickedness (1902)

text: Indeed, history is but a canvas of crimes and misfortunes.
author: Voltaire
source: Indeed, history is nothing more than a tableau of crimes and misfortunes. L'Ingénu , ch.10 (1767) Quoted in The End , part 13 of A Series of Unfortunate Events

text: Almost everything is imitation. Even the most original minds borrow from one another. Books are like the fire in our hearths: we take it from a neighbor, light our own, share it, and it belongs to all.
author: Voltaire
source: Thus, almost everything is imitation. The idea of The Persian Letters was taken from The Turkish Spy . Boiardo imitated Pulci , Ariosto imitated Boiardo . The most original minds borrowed from one another. Miguel de Cervantes makes his Don Quixote a fool; but pray is Orlando any other? It would puzzle one to decide whether knight errantry has been made more ridiculous by the grotesque painting of Cervantes, than by the luxuriant imagination of Ariosto. Metastasio has taken the greatest part of his operas from our French tragedies. Several English writers have copied us without saying one word of the matter. It is with books as with the fire in our hearths; we go to a neighbor to get the embers and light it when we return home, pass it on to others, and it belongs to everyone "Lettre XII: sur M. Pope et quelques autres poètes fameux", Lettres philosophiques (1756 edition) Variants:

text: Headmasters have powers at their disposal with which Prime Ministers have never yet been invested.
author: Winston Churchill
source_section: My Early Life: A Roving Commission (1930)

text: His reputation will ever solidify, precisely because he is seldom read.
author: Voltaire
source: His reputation will go on increasing because scarcely anyone reads him. "Dante" (1765)

text: Mortals are equal; not by birth, but by virtue alone are they distinguished.
author: Voltaire
source: All mortals are equal; it is not their birth, But virtue itself that makes the difference.

text: Abandon the fallacy that limiting human enterprise through the shackles of a false equality will increase the world's well-being.
author: Winston Churchill
source: Winston S. Churchill, His Complete Speeches 1897-1963 , Vol. IV, p. 3821, (21 January 1926)

text: I cannot pretend to feel impartial about the colours. I rejoice with the brilliant ones, and am genuinely sorry for the poor browns.
author: Winston Churchill
source: In "Painting as a Pastime", first published in the Strand Magazine in two parts (December 1921/January 1922), cited in Churchill by Himself (2008), ed. Langworth, PublicAffairs, p. 456 ISBN 1586486381

text: There is always a strong case for doing nothing, especially for doing nothing yourself.
author: Winston Churchill
source: The World Crisis, 1911–1914 : Chapter XV (Antwerp), Churchill, Butterworth (1923), p. 340

text: God is sitting here, looking into my very soul to see if I think right thoughts . Yet I am not afraid, for I try to be right and good; and He knows every one of my struggles.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Letter to Abiah Root (29 January 1850); Mabel Loomis Todd (ed.) Letters of Emily Dickinson , vol. 1 (Boston: Roberts Bros, 1894) p. 39

text: Friday I tasted life. It was a vast morsel.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Letter to Mrs. J. G. Holland (c. 1857); Letters , vol. 1 (1894) p. 171

text: My friends are my "estate." Forgive me then the avarice to hoard them.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Letter to Samuel Bowles (August 1858 or 1859); Thomas H. Johnson (ed.) The Letters of Emily Dickinson (1958) p. 338, no. 193

text: If I feel physically as if the top of my head were taken off, I know that is poetry.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Letter to T. W. Higginson (1870); Letters (1958) p. 474, no. 342a

text: To live is so startling, it leaves but little room for other occupations.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Letter to T. W. Higginson (late 1872); Letters (1958) p. 380, no. 381

text: We turn not older with years, but newer every day.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Letter to Louise Norcross (late 1872); Letters (1958) p. 499, no. 379

text: A Grave – is a restricted Breadth – Yet ampler than the Sun –
author: Emily Dickinson
source: A Coffin – is a small Domain (J)

text: A death-blow is a life-blow to some Who, till they died, did not alive become; Who, had they lived, had died, but when They died, vitality begun.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: A Death blow is a Life blow to Some (B)

text: A little Madness in the Spring Is wholesome even for the King.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: A little Madness in the Spring (J)

text: After great pain, a formal feeling comes - The Nerves sit ceremonious, like Tombs - The stiff Heart questions 'was it He, that bore,' And 'Yesterday, or Centuries before'?
author: Emily Dickinson
source: After great pain, a formal feeling comes (Fr)

text: This is the Hour of Lead - Remembered, if outlived, As Freezing persons, recollect the Snow - First - Chill - then Stupor - then the letting go -
author: Emily Dickinson
source: After great pain, a formal feeling comes (Fr)

text: Because I could not stop for Death – He kindly stopped for me – The Carriage held but just Ourselves – And Immortality . We slowly drove – He knew no haste And I had put away My labor and my leisure too, For His Civility –
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Because I could not stop for Death (J)

text: Since then – 'tis Centuries – and yet Feels shorter than the Day I first surmised the Horses' Heads Were toward Eternity –
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Because I could not stop for Death (J)

text: Felled but by one assassin – Prosperity –
author: Emily Dickinson

text: Dreams – are well – but Waking's better, If One wake at Morn – If One wake at Midnight – better – Dreaming – of the Dawn –
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Dreams – are well – but Waking's better (J)

text: Drowning is not so pitiful As the attempt to rise.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: What fortitude the Soul contains, That it can so endure The accent of a coming Foot - The opening of a Door -
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Elysium is as far as to (Fr)

text: " Faith " is a fine invention When Gentlemen can see – But Microscopes are prudent In an Emergency.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: Fame is a fickle food Upon a shifting plate, Whose table once a Guest, but not The second time, is set. Whose crumbs the crows inspect, And with ironic caw Flap past it to the Farmer’s corn; Men eat of it and die.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: When her worst was done, and he, unmoved, regarded her, acknowledged him a man.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Fate slew Him, but He did not drop (B)

text: Then a silence suffuses the story, and a softness the teller's eye; and the children no further question, and only the waves reply.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Glee – The great storm is over (B)

text: Heart, we will forget him! You and I, to-night! You may forget the warmth he gave, I will forget the light. When you have done, pray tell me, That I my thoughts may dim; Haste! lest while you're lagging, I may remember him!
author: Emily Dickinson

text: " Hope " is the thing with feathers – That perches in the soul – And sings the tune without the words – And never stops – at all –
author: Emily Dickinson
source: "Hope" is the thing with feathers (J) Compare: Jasper Mayne

text: How happy is the little stone that rambles in the road alone, independent as the sun, fulfilling absolute decree in casual simplicity.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: How happy is the little Stone (B)

text: "And I – for Truth, – Themself are One – We Brethren, are", He said –
author: Emily Dickinson
source: I died for Beauty – but was scarce (J)

text: I heard a Fly buzz – when I died – The Stillness in the Room Was like the Stillness in the Air – Between the Heaves of Storm –
author: Emily Dickinson
source: I heard a Fly buzz – when I died (J)

text: There interposed a Fly – With Blue – uncertain – stumbling Buzz – Between the light – and me – And then the Windows failed – and then I could not see to see –
author: Emily Dickinson
source: I heard a Fly buzz – when I died (J)

text: I never hear the word "escape" Without a quicker blood, A sudden expectation, A flying attitude. I never hear of prisons broad By soldiers battered down, But I tug childish at my bars,— Only to fail again!
author: Emily Dickinson

text: I never saw a moor, I never saw the sea; Yet know I how the heather looks, And what a wave must be. I never spoke with God, Nor visited in heaven; Yet certain am I of the spot As if the chart were given.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: I reason that in heaven somehow, it will be even, some new equation given; but what of that?
author: Emily Dickinson

text: I taste a liquor never brewed - From Tankards scooped in Pearl - Not all the Frankfort Berries Yield such an Alcohol! Inebriate of air - am I - And Debauchee of Dew - Reeling - thro' endless summer days - From inns of molten Blue -
author: Emily Dickinson
source: I taste a liquor never brewed (Fr)

text: I took my power in my hand And went against the world; 'Twas not so much as David had, But I was twice as bold. I aimed my pebble, but myself Was all the one that fell. Was it Goliath was too large, Or only I too small?
author: Emily Dickinson
source: I took my Power in my Hand (B)

text: I took one Draught of Life – I'll tell you what I paid – Precisely an existence – The market price, they said.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: I took one Draught of Life (J)

text: I'm Nobody! Who are you? Are you – Nobody – Too?
author: Emily Dickinson

text: If I can stop one heart from breaking, I shall not live in vain; If I can ease one life the aching, Or cool one pain, Or help one fainting robin Unto his nest again, I shall not live in vain.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: If I can stop one Heart from breaking (B)

text: Except for its marauding hand, it had been heaven below.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: How dreary – to be – Somebody! How public – like a Frog – To tell one's name – the livelong June – To an admiring Bog!
author: Emily Dickinson
source: I'm Nobody! Who are you? (J) In some editions "June" altered to "day"

text: Auto-da-fé and judgment are nothing to the bee; his separation from his rose to him seems misery.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: It might be easier To fail with land in sight, Than gain my blue peninsula To perish of delight.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: It might be easier (B) J prints "lonelier" instead of "easier"

text: The ocean's heart too smooth, too blue, To break for you.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: It's such a little thing to weep , So short a thing to sigh ; And yet by trades the size of these We men and women die !
author: Emily Dickinson
source: It's such a little thing to weep (B)

text: Love is anterior to life, Posterior to death, Initial of creation, and The exponent of breath.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: Love – thou art Veiled – A few – behold thee – Smile – and alter – and prattle – and die – Bliss – were an Oddity – without thee – Nicknamed by God – Eternity –
author: Emily Dickinson

text: Mine enemy is growing old,— I have at last revenge. The palate of the hate departs; If any would avenge,— Let him be quick, the viand flits, It is a faded meat. Anger as soon as fed is dead; 'Tis starving makes it fat.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: More than the Grave is closed to me – The Grave and that Eternity To which the Grave adheres – I cling to nowhere till I fall – The Crash of nothing, yet of all – How similar appears –
author: Emily Dickinson
source: More than the Grave is closed to me (J)

text: Much madness is divinest sense To a discerning eye; Much sense the starkest madness. 'Tis the majority In this, as all, prevails. Assent, and you are sane; Demur,—you're straightway dangerous, And handled with a chain.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: My life closed twice before its close; It yet remains to see If Immortality unveil A third event to me, So huge, so hopeless to conceive As these that twice befell. Parting is all we know of heaven, And all we need of hell.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: My life closed twice before its close (Fr)

text: My Life had stood – a Loaded Gun – In Corners – till a Day The Owner passed – identified – And carried Me away – And now We roam in Sovereign Woods – And now We hunt the Doe – And every time I speak for Him – The Mountains straight reply –
author: Emily Dickinson
source: My Life had stood – a Loaded Gun (J)

text: Nature is what we know But have no art to say, So impotent our wisdom is To Her simplicity.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: New feet within my garden go, New fingers stir the sod; A troubadour upon the elm Betrays the solitude. New children play upon the green, New weary sleep below; And still the pensive spring returns, And still the punctual snow!
author: Emily Dickinson
source: New feet within my garden go (B)

text: Captivity is consciousness, So's liberty.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: Shame need not crouch in such an Earth as ours; stand erect, the Universe is yours.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Not with a Club, the Heart is broken (J)

text: One need not be a Chamber – to be Haunted – One need not be a House – The Brain has Corridors – surpassing Material Place –
author: Emily Dickinson
source: One need not be a Chamber – to be Haunted (J)

text: Retreat was out of hope; behind, a sealed route, Eternity's white flag before, and God at every gate.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: Presentiment is that long shadow on the lawn Indicative that suns go down; The notice to the startled grass That darkness is about to pass.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Presentiment – is that long Shadow – on the Lawn (B)

text: Soundless as Dots, On a Disk of Snow.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Safe in their Alabaster Chambers [third version] (Fr)

text: So proud she was to die It made us all ashamed That what we cherished, so unknown To her desire seemed. So satisfied to go Where none of us should be, Immediately, that anguish stooped Almost to jealousy.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: So proud she was to die (B)

text: Some Days retired from the rest In soft distinction lie, The Day that a companion came— Or was obliged to die.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Some Days retired from the rest (B)

text: Some keep the Sabbath going to Church – I keep it, staying at Home – With a Bobolink for a Chorister – And an Orchard, for a Dome –
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Some keep the Sabbath going to Church (J)

text: God preaches, a noted Clergyman – And the sermon is never long, So instead of getting to Heaven, at last – I'm going, all along.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Some keep the Sabbath going to Church (J)

text: Surgeons must be very careful When they take the knife! Underneath their fine incisions Stirs the culprit,—Life!
author: Emily Dickinson

text: The Bustle in a House The Morning after Death Is solemnest of industries Enacted upon Earth - The Sweeping up the Heart And putting Love away We shall not want to use again Until Eternity -
author: Emily Dickinson

text: Success is counted sweetest by those who ne'er succeed; to comprehend a nectar requires a sorest need.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: Tell all the Truth but tell it slant – The Truth must dazzle gradually Or every man be blind.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Tell all the Truth but tell it slant (J)

text: That such have died enables us The tranquiller to die; That such have lived, certificate For immortality.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: That Such have died enable Us (B)

text: The blunder is to estimate,— "Eternity is Then," We say, as of a station. Meanwhile he is so near, He joins me in my ramble, Divides abode with me, No friend have I that so persists As this Eternity.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: A Vastness, as a Neighbor, came, A Wisdom, without Face, or Name, A Peace, as Hemispheres at Home And so the Night became.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: The distance that the dead have gone does not at first appear; then, we more than half suspect we have followed them, so intimate have we become with their dear retrospect.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: The distance that the dead have gone (B)

text: The face we choose to miss, Be it but for a day— As absent as a hundred years When it has rode away.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: The Face we choose to miss (B)

text: If Aims impel these Astral Ones The ones allowed to know Know that which makes them as forgot As Dawn forgets them – now
author: Emily Dickinson
source: The Moon upon her fluent Route (J)

text: The pedigree of honey Does not concern the bee; A clover, any time, to him Is aristocracy.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: The Soul selects her own Society – Then – shuts the Door – To her divine Majority – Present no more –
author: Emily Dickinson
source: The Soul selects her own Society (J)

text: I've known her – from an ample nation – Choose One – Then – close the Valves of her attention – Like Stone –
author: Emily Dickinson
source: The Soul selects her own Society (J)

text: The sweets of Pillage can be known To no one but the Thief, Compassion for Integrity Is his divinest Grief.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: The Sweets of Pillage, can be known (B)

text: There is a solitude of space, A solitude of sea, A solitude of death, but these Society shall be, Compared with that profounder site, That polar privacy, A Soul admitted to Itself: Finite Infinity.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: There is a solitude of space (B)

text: Time's sublimest target Is a soul "forgot"!
author: Emily Dickinson

text: There's a certain Slant of light, Winter Afternoons - That oppresses, like the Heft Of Cathedral Tunes - Heavenly Hurt, it gives us - We can find no scar, But internal difference - Where the Meanings, are -
author: Emily Dickinson
source: There's a certain Slant of light (Fr)

text: They shut me up in Prose - As when a little Girl They put me in the Closet - Because they liked me "still" - Still! Could themself have peeped - And seen my Brain - go round - They might as wise have lodged a Bird For Treason - in the Pound -
author: Emily Dickinson
source: They shut me up in Prose (Fr)

text: This is my letter to the World That never wrote to Me – The simple News that Nature told – With tender Majesty Her Message is committed To Hands I cannot see – For love of Her – Sweet – countrymen – Judge tenderly – of Me
author: Emily Dickinson
source: This is my letter to the World (J)

text: This quiet dust was gentlemen and ladies And lads and girls; Was laughter and ability and sighing, And frocks and curls.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: This quiet Dust was Gentleman and Ladies (B)

text: To Whom the Mornings stand for Nights, What must the Midnights – be!
author: Emily Dickinson
source: To Whom the Mornings stand for Nights (J)

text: Truth – is as old as God – His Twin identity And will endure as long as He A Co-Eternity – And perish on the Day Himself is borne away From Mansion of the Universe A lifeless Deity.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Truth – is as old as God (J)

text: Upon the gallows hung a wretch, Too sullied for the hell To which the law entitled him. As nature’s curtain fell The one who bore him tottered in, For this was woman’s son. "'Twas all I had," she stricken gasped; Oh, what a livid boon!
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Upon the gallows hung a wretch (B)

text: We outgrow love, like other things And put it in the Drawer – Till it an Antique fashion shows – Like Costumes Grandsires wore.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: We outgrow love, like other things (J)

text: What Soft – Cherubic Creatures – These Gentlewomen are – One would as soon assault a Plush – Or violate a Star – Such Dimity Convictions – A Horror so refined Of freckled Human Nature – Of Deity – ashamed –
author: Emily Dickinson

text: Who has not found the Heaven – below – Will fail of it above – For Angels rent the House next ours, Wherever we remove –
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Who has not found the Heaven – below (J)

text: Who has not found the heaven below Will fail of it above. God's residence is next to mine, His furniture is love.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Who has not found the Heaven – below (B)

text: Wild nights - Wild nights! Were I with thee Wild nights should be Our luxury! Futile - the winds - To a Heart in port - Done with the Compass - Done with the Chart! Rowing in Eden - Ah - the Sea! Might I but moor - tonight - In thee!
author: Emily Dickinson

text: Whenever a thing is done for the first time, it releases a little demon.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Quoted on the web sans source. Not in any published edition of Dickinson's poems. Anthony Hernandez, The Enlightened Savage (2006) – a self-help book – attributes it verbatim to Dave Sim sans source. Compare a similar quote sourced to Sim in Cerebus no. 65 (1984): "Anything done for the first time unleashes a demon."

text: I never saw a moor, / I never saw the sea; / Yet know I how the heather looks, / And what a wave must be.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Rachel Carson , 1954 speech, in Lost Woods: The Discovered Writing of Rachel Carson (1998)

text: Separation is all the Hell we need.
author: Emily Dickinson
source: Rosario Castellanos , "In Praise of Friendship" (1964) in Another Way to Be: Selected Works of Rosario Castellanos

text: My spirit is too weak — mortality Weighs heavily on me like unwilling sleep.
author: John Keats

text: The feel of not to feel it, when there is none to heal it, was never said in rhyme.
author: John Keats

text: Then on the shore Of the wide world I stand alone, and think Till Love and Fame to nothingness do sink.
author: John Keats
source: " When I have fears that I may cease to be " (1817)

text: Thou wouldst wish thine own heart dry of blood so in my veins red life might stream again.
author: John Keats

text: Bright star! would I were stedfast as thou art.
author: John Keats

text: Making him see, where Learning hath no light.
author: John Keats

text: Here lies one whose name was writ in water.
author: John Keats

text: I am certain of nothing but the holiness of the heart's affections and the truth of imagination.
author: John Keats

text: The imagination may be compared to Adam's dream — he awoke and found it truth.
author: John Keats

text: Nothing startles me beyond the moment.
author: John Keats

text: The excellency of every art is its intensity.
author: John Keats

text: Negative capability is when a man is capable of being in uncertainties, mysteries, doubts, without any irritable reaching after fact and reason.
author: John Keats
source: To George and Thomas Keats (22 December 1817)

text: Nothing is finer for the purposes of great productions than a very gradual ripening of the intellectual powers.
author: John Keats

text: Poetry should be great and unobtrusive, a thing which enters into one's soul, and does not startle or amaze with itself, but with its subject.
author: John Keats

text: Many have original minds who do not think it — they are led away by custom — Now it appears to me that almost any man may like the spider spin from his own inwards his own citadel.
author: John Keats

text: If Poetry comes not as naturally as the leaves to a tree it had better not come at all.
author: John Keats

text: When I was a schoolboy I thought a fair woman a pure Goddess; my mind was a soft nest in which some one of them slept, though she knew it not.
author: John Keats

text: I begin to get a little acquainted with my own strength and weakness. Praise or blame has but a momentary effect on the man whose love of beauty in the abstract makes him a severe critic on his own works.
author: John Keats

text: I was never afraid of failure; for I would sooner fail than not be among the greatest.
author: John Keats

text: What shocks the virtuous philosopher, delights the chameleon poet.
author: John Keats

text: A man's life of any worth is a continual allegory — and very few eyes can see the mystery of life.
author: John Keats
source: To George and Georgiana Keats (14 February – 3 May 1819)

text: Nothing ever becomes real till it is experienced — Even a proverb is no proverb to you till your Life has illustrated it.
author: John Keats
source: To George and Georgiana Keats (14 February – 3 May 1819)

text: I strain at particles of light in a great darkness, without knowing the bearing of any truth.
author: John Keats
source: To George and Georgiana Keats (19 March 1819)

text: I have nothing to speak of but my self-and what can I say but what I feel
author: John Keats

text: Some think I have lost that poetical ardour and fire 'tis said I once had- the fact is, perhaps I have; but, instead of that, I hope I shall substitute a more thoughtful and quiet power.
author: John Keats

text: Love is my religion; I could die for it, I could die for you. My creed is Love, and you are its only tenet.
author: John Keats

text: I have loved the principle of beauty in all things.
author: John Keats

text: And then there crept A little noiseless noise among the leaves, Born of the very sigh that silence heaves.
author: John Keats

text: Then felt I like some watcher of the skies when a new planet swims into his ken.
author: John Keats

text: Spirits on the forehead of the age to come will give the world another heart, and other pulses.
author: John Keats
source: "Sonnet. Addressed to the Same" (Benjamin Robert Haydon)

text: Strength alone, like a fallen angel, feeds on life's thorns, forgetting poesy's true end: to soothe and lift the thoughts of man.
author: John Keats

text: Between the healthy imaginations of boy and man, the soul ferments, character undecided, ambition thicksighted, yielding mawkishness and a thousand bitters.
author: John Keats

text: A thing of beauty is a joy forever: Its loveliness increases; it will never Pass into nothingness.
author: John Keats

text: The passion poesy, glories infinite, haunt us till they become a cheering light unto our souls, bound so fast that, whether shine or gloom, they must be with us, or we die.
author: John Keats

text: Richer entanglements, enthralments far more self-destroying, lead to the chief intensity: the crown of these is made of love and friendship, and sits high upon the forehead of humanity.
author: John Keats

text: To Sorrow I bade good-morrow, but she loves me dearly; she is so constant to me, and so kind.
author: John Keats

text: I made a garland for her head, And bracelets too, and fragrant zone; She look'd at me as she did love, And made sweet moan.
author: John Keats

text: Sat gray-hair'd Saturn, quiet as a stone, still as the silence.
author: John Keats

text: Tall oaks, branch-charmed by the earnest stars, dream all night without a stir.
author: John Keats

text: The days of peace and slumberous calm are fled.
author: John Keats

text: And for her eyes: what could such eyes do there But weep, and weep, that they were born so fair?
author: John Keats

text: Philosophy will clip an Angel's wings, conquer all mysteries by rule and line, unweave a rainbow.
author: John Keats

text: “For cruel ’tis,” said she, “To steal my Basil-pot away from me.”
author: John Keats
source: " Isabella; or, The Pot of Basil ", st. 62

text: Season of mists and mellow fruitfulness, Close bosom-friend of the maturing sun.
author: John Keats

text: Or on a half-reap'd furrow sound asleep, Drows'd with the fume of poppies.
author: John Keats

text: You, beloved lost before arrival, never-come, I know not what sounds you love. No longer do I try to recognize you when the future surges.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: You who never arrived in my arms, Beloved, who were lost from the start, I don't even know what songs would please you. I have given up trying to recognize you in the surging wave of the next moment.

text: Ah, you are the gardens I saw with such hope; the merchants' mirrors still spun with your lingering presence.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: You, Beloved, who are all the gardens I have ever gazed at, longing. An open window in a country house —, and you almost stepped out, pensive, to meet me. Streets that I chanced upon,— you had just walked down them and vanished. And sometimes, in a shop, the mirrors were still dizzy with your presence and, startled, gave back my too-sudden image. Who knows? perhaps the same bird echoed through both of us yesterday, separate, in the evening...

text: What we cannot grasp transforms us, shaping us into who we are, even as we only feel the headwind.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Already my gaze is upon the hill, the sunny one, at the end of the path which I've only just begun. So we are grasped, by that which we could not grasp, at such great distance, so fully manifest— and it changes us, even when we do not reach it, into something that, hardly sensing it, we already are; a sign appears, echoing our own sign... But what we sense is the falling winds.

text: Rose, oh pure contradiction, joy, to be no one's sleep beneath so many lids.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Translation: Rose, oh pure contradiction, desire, To be no one's sleep under so many Lids.

text: The longer I live, the more I must transcribe existence to its end, for only the final word may reveal its glorious meaning.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: The longer I live, the more urgent it seems to me to endure and transcribe the whole dictation of existence up to its end, for it might just be the case that only the very last sentence contains that small and possibly inconspicuous word through which everything we had struggled to learn and everything we had failed to understand will be transformed suddenly into magnificent sense. Letter to Ilse Erdmann, 21 December 1913, in Letters on Life , U. Baer, trans. (2007)

text: An artist's insights should flow swiftly into the work, unrecognised in transition, lest they transform like gold that cannot remain gold.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Letter to his wife, reprinted in Rilke’s Letters on Cézanne (1952, trans. 1985). (October 21, 1907)

text: Painting is the intercourse of colors; leave them alone completely, for any human meddling disturbs and clouds their activity.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Letter to his wife, reprinted in Rilke’s Letters on Cézanne (1952, trans. 1985). (October 21, 1907)

text: Not since Moses has anyone seen a mountain so greatly.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Quoted in Rilke's Letters on Cézanne , foreword (1952, trans. 1985)

text: Love the difficult, for within its depths, our joys and dreams stand out, revealing their true beauty.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Selected Letters of Rainer Maria Rilke (1960)

text: From infinite longings rise finite deeds like weak fountains, which early and trembling bow. But our joyful forces, which otherwise conceal themselves from us—reveal themselves in these dancing tears.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Out of infinite longings rise finite deeds like weak fountains, falling back just in time and trembling. And yet, what otherwise remains silent, our happy energies—show themselves in these dancing tears.
source_section: Das Buch der Bilder (The Book of Images) (1902)

text: Lord: it is time. The summer was immense. Lay your shadow on the sundials, and on the fields, unleash the winds.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Lord: it is time. The summer was immense. Let thine shadows upon the sundials fall, and unleash the winds upon the open fields.
source_section: Das Buch der Bilder (The Book of Images) (1902)

text: The evening slowly changes its robes, held by a fringe of ancient trees.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Slowly the evening changes into the clothes held for it by a row of ancient trees.
source_section: Das Buch der Bilder (The Book of Images) (1902)

text: Put out my eyes: I can see you. Close my ears: I can hear you. Without feet, I can walk to you; without a mouth, I can conjure you.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: I am too alone in the world, yet not alone enough to consecrate every hour. I am too insignificant, yet not small enough to be before you like a thing, dark and wise.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: I am too alone in the world, and yet not alone enough to make every hour holy. I am too small in the world, and yet not tiny enough just to stand before you like a thing, dark and shrewd. I want my will, and I want to be with my will as it moves towards deed; and in those quiet, somehow hesitating times, when something is approaching, I want to be with those who are wise or else alone. Number 2 (as translated by Cliff Crego)

text: Everything that touches us, you and me, draws us together like a bow stroke that draws one voice from two strings. On what instrument are we strung? And what violinist holds us in hand? O sweet song.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: How shall I hold on to my soul, so that it does not touch yours? How shall I lift it gently up over you on to other things? I would so very much like to tuck it away among long lost objects in the dark, in some quiet, unknown place, somewhere which remains motionless when your depths resound. And yet everything which touches us, you and me, takes us together like a single bow, drawing out from two strings but one voice. On which instrument are we strung? And which violinist holds us in his hand? O sweetest of songs.

text: Dying is like the swan's anxious settling into waters that gently receive it, while it rests, infinitely still and sure, ever more kingly and serene.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: This difficult living, heavy and as if all tied up, moving through that which has been left undone, is like the not-quite-finished walk of the swan. And dying, this slipping away from the ground upon which we stand every day, is his anxious letting himself fall—: into the waters, which receive him gladly and which, as if happily already gone by, draw back under him, wave after wave; while the swan, infinitely calm and self-assured, opener and more magnificent and more serene, allows himself to be drawn on.

text: The small island out there has its eyes closed; its inhabitants are born into a sleep where they silently confuse many worlds, for they rarely speak, and every sentence is like an epitaph.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: The next tide will erase the way through the mudflats, and everything will be again equal on all sides; but the small, far-out island already has its eyes closed; bewildered, the dike draws a circle around its inhabitants who were born into a sleep in which many worlds are silently confused, for they rarely speak, and every phrase is like an epitaph.

text: His gaze has grown so weary from the passing of the bars that it holds nothing more. To him, it is as if there were a thousand bars and behind a thousand bars, no world.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: His tired gaze - from passing endless bars - has turned into a vacant stare which nothing holds. To him there seem to be a thousand bars, and out beyond these bars exists no world. His supple gait, the smoothness of strong strides that gently turn in ever smaller circles perform a dance of strength, centered deep within a will, stunned, but untamed, indomitable. But sometimes the curtains of his eyelids part, the pupils of his eyes dilate as images of past encounters enter while through his limbs a tension strains in silence only to cease to be, to die within his heart.

text: We cannot know his legendary head. Yet his torso is still suffused with brilliance from inside, like a lamp, in which his gaze, now turned to low, gleams in all its power.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Archaic Torso of Apollo · Full text online

text: For here there is no place that does not see you. You must change your life.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: I am so afraid of people's words. They describe everything so distinctly. I want to warn and object: Let the things be! But you always touch: and they hush and stand still. That's how you kill.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Is it possible that there are people who say 'God' and mean it is something common? Just as two identical knives become utterly different in different hands, can one have a God without using Him?
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Is it possible that there are people who say "God" and suppose they mean something shared by all? — Only consider two schoolboys: one of them buys a knife, and the other buys an identical one on the same day. And a week later, they show each other the two knives, and they turn out to be only remotely similar, so differently have they been shaped by different hands. ... Is it possible to believe we could have a god without making use of him?
source_section: The Notebooks of Malte Laurids Brigge (1910)

text: My God, so this is how you are. I have forgotten all proofs of your existence and never asked for any, for what an immense obligation would lie in your certainty. And yet, now it is shown to me.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: A thought struck me abruptly: My God, you do exist , then. There are proofs of Your existence. I had forgotten them all, and never demanded any either, for what an overwhelming obligation would come with this certainty. And yet that is what is now being shown to me.
source_section: The Notebooks of Malte Laurids Brigge (1910)

text: Who, if I cried out, would hear me among the orders of angels? I would perish from their stronger existence. For beauty is nothing but the beginning of terror, which we are just able to endure. Every angel is terrible.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Who, if I cried out, would hear me among the angels' hierarchies? and even if one of them pressed me against his heart: I would be consumed in that overwhelming existence. For beauty is nothing but the beginning of terror, which we still are just able to endure, and we are so awed because it serenely disdains to annihilate us. Every angel is terrifying.

text: Yes, the springs needed you. Some stars entrusted you to feel them. All this was a commission. But did you master it? Were you not always scattered by expectation, as if everything announced a beloved to you?
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Yes—the springtimes needed you. Often a star was waiting for you to notice it. A wave rolled toward you out of the distant past, or as you walked under an open window, a violin yielded itself to your hearing. All this was mission. But could you accomplish it? Weren't you always distracted by expectation, as if every event announced a beloved? (Where can you find a place to keep her, with all the huge strange thoughts inside you going and coming and often staying all night.)

text: The early departed no longer need us; one gently weans oneself from the earthly. But we, who need such great mysteries, for whom blessed progress so often springs from sorrow –: could we be without them?
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: In the end, those who were carried off early no longer need us: they are weaned from earth's sorrows and joys, and as gently as children outgrow the soft breasts of their mothers. But we, who do need such great mysteries, we for whom grief is so often the source of our spirit's growth — : could we exist without them ?

text: Lovers... when one lifts and sets the other to their mouth —: drink to drink: oh, how strangely then the drinker escapes the act.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Lovers ... when you raise yourselves and press your mouths together—drink upon drink: strange how each of you drinks your way past the other.

text: When you go to bed, don't leave bread or milk on the table: it attracts the dead.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Nothing touches a work of art so little as words of criticism, for most experiences are unsayable, and art's mysterious life endures beyond our transitory words.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Go into yourself; find the reason that commands you to write, see if its roots reach your heart's depths, and confess if you would die forbidden to write.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: If your daily life seems poor, blame yourself, for you are not poet enough to call forth its riches; to the creator, there is no poverty or indifferent place.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Letter One (17 February 1903) as translated by M. D. Herter Norton (1993)

text: No experience is unimportant; the smallest event unfolds like fate, a wonderful fabric where every thread is guided by an infinitely tender hand.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Read as little as possible of literary criticism, for works of art are of an infinite solitude, and no means of approach is so useless as criticism.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Be patient with all that is unresolved in your heart; love the questions themselves like locked rooms, for you cannot live the answers yet. Live the questions now, and perhaps one day you will live your way into the answer.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Be patient toward all that is unsolved in your heart and try to love the questions themselves, like locked rooms and like books that are now written in a very foreign tongue. Do not now seek the answers, which cannot be given you because you would not be able to live them. And the point is, to live everything. Live the questions now. Perhaps you will then gradually, without noticing it, live along some distant day into the answer.

text: If you trust in Nature, in the small things that become immeasurable, and love what is humble, everything will become easier, more coherent, and reconciling in your innermost awareness.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Sex is difficult, like all serious tasks; achieve a wholly individual relation to it, uninfluenced by convention, and you will no longer fear losing yourself.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Physical pleasure is a great, unending knowing of the world; the bad thing is that most people misuse and squander this experience as a stimulant or distraction, instead of a rallying toward exalted moments.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Through such impressions, one gathers oneself from exacting multiplicity, learning to recognize the few eternal, solitary things one can love and gently take part in.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: As bees gather honey, we collect what is sweetest to build Him, even from the trivial, through solitary acts of love, just as our ancestors exist in us, hoping we too will exist in Him.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Love is difficult, for in other conflicts nature enjoins us to collect ourselves, but in love, the impulse is to give oneself wholly away.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: We must hold to what is difficult; everything alive holds to it. To love is good, too: love being difficult. For one human being to love another: that is perhaps the most difficult of all our tasks, the ultimate test.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Young people, who are beginners in everything, cannot yet know love: they have to learn it.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Love is not merging, but a high inducement for the individual to ripen, to become a world for himself for another's sake, a great exacting claim calling him to vast things.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Love's demands are immense, and we, as beginners, are not up to them; yet, embracing it as burden and apprenticeship may yield progress for future generations.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Young people, as apprentices in love, must learn it with peace, patience, and composure, growing slowly towards happiness.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: On young couples who have not yet matured enough to recognize and respect each other's solitude

text: Sadness is a transformative transition where the future enters us; be solitary and attentive, for in quiet patience, this new presence becomes our fate.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Find in yourself enough patience to endure and enough simplicity to have faith; gain confidence in what is difficult and in your solitude. Let life happen to you: life is in the right, always.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: All feelings that concentrate and lift you are pure; only that which distorts you is impure. Embrace what makes you more, what is good, and joy you can see clear to the bottom.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Let magnificent solitude work upon you trustingly and patiently, for this solitude, like ancestral blood, will form the unique being you are at every turning of your life.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Art is a way of living, prepared for in everything real, not in half-artistic professions like journalism or criticism that deny its existence.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Juxtaposing Rilke's elegant language with Chinese American language calls into question what is beautiful, understandable, and what one aspires to.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: 1989 interview in Conversations with Maxine Hong Kingston (1998)

text: As a minority, I appropriate language and culture, claiming the Rilkean poetic soul as mine; my allusions are meant for fun, not trouble.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: 1989 interview in Conversations with Maxine Hong Kingston (1998)

text: Translating can evoke a 'joyous rapture,' a heightened state where meaning reveals itself, as Rilke experienced when translating Valéry.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Shirley Kumove , Drunk from the Bitter Truth: The Poems of Anna Margolin (2005)

text: True myth is an inexhaustible source, not destroyed by reason; it looks back at you, demanding, 'You must change your life.'
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Ursula Le Guin , "Myth and Archetype in Science Fiction" (1976)

text: Rilke teaches patience in the pursuit of excellence, awakening us to the seriousness of living deeply, working steadily, and finding joy, for 'Everything serious is difficult, and everything is serious.'
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Alexandra Stoddard in Roxanne J. Coady and Joy Johannessen (eds.) The Book That Changed My Life (2006), ISBN 1-592-40210-0 , p. 166

text: Do you know the streets are empty, and houses too? Only eyes in windows. If you knock, they'll open, but it's cold inside, the house empty, wanting nothing from you. Insist with tenderness, and the dog and cat will bite.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Don't you know there is no one in the streets and no one in the houses? There are only eyes in the windows. If you don't have a place to sleep, knock on a door and it will open, open up to a certain point and you will see that it is cold inside, and that that house is empty and wants nothing to do with you, your stories mean nothing, and if you insist on being gentle, the dog and the cat will bite you.

text: Something beat in my soul, fever or lost wings; I became alone, deciphering that burning, and wrote the first vague line, pure foolishness, wisdom of one who knows nothing, then saw the sky unhusked and open.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: And something started in my soul, fever or forgotten wings, and I made my own way, deciphering that fire, and I wrote the first faint line, faint, without substance, pure nonsense, pure wisdom of someone who knows nothing, and I suddenly saw the heavens unfastened and open.

text: All gods, from alabaster whales to golden spikes, from coiled serpents to smiling Buddhas and Christ on his cross, were poised to impose their heaven, with wounds or pistols to buy piety or burn our blood.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: There in Rangoon I realized that the gods were enemies, just like God, of the poor human being. Gods in alabaster extended like white whales, gods gilded like spikes, serpent gods entwining the crime of being born, naked and elegant buddhas smiling at the cocktail party of empty eternity like Christ on his horrible cross, all of them capable of anything, of imposing on us their heaven, all with torture or pistol to purchase piety or burn our blood, fierce gods made by men to conceal their cowardice, and there it was all like that, the whole earth reeking of heaven, and heavenly merchandise.

text: It is time, my love, to set aside this somber rose, close the stars, bury the ash: and, in light's insurrection, awaken with the awakened, or dream on, reaching the other shore of the sea that has no other shore.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: It is time, love, to break off that sombre rose, shut up the stars and bury the ash in the earth; and, in the rising of the light, wake with those who awoke or go on in the dream, reaching the other shore of the sea which has no other shore.

text: Only with burning patience shall we conquer the splendid city that will give light, justice, and dignity to all men. Thus, poetry will not have sung in vain.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Only with a burning patience can we conquer the splendid City which will give light, justice and dignity to all mankind. In this way the song will not have been sung in vain.

text: Who writes your name in smoke among the southern stars? Ah, let me remember how you were then, when you did not yet exist.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Who writes your name in letters of smoke among the stars of the south? Oh let me remember you as you were before you existed.
source_section: Veinte Poemas de Amor y una Canción Desesperada (Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair) (1924)

text: I want to do with you what spring does with the cherry trees.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: I want to do with you what spring does with cherry trees.
source_section: Veinte Poemas de Amor y una Canción Desesperada (Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair) (1924)

text: I like you when you are silent because you are as if absent, and you hear me from afar, and my voice does not touch you.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: I like for you to be still: it is as though you were absent, and you hear me from far away and my voice does not touch you.
source_section: Veinte Poemas de Amor y una Canción Desesperada (Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair) (1924)

text: I can write the saddest verses tonight.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Tonight I can write the saddest lines.
source_section: Veinte Poemas de Amor y una Canción Desesperada (Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair) (1924)

text: Love is so short, forgetting so long.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Love is so short and forgetting is so long.
source_section: Veinte Poemas de Amor y una Canción Desesperada (Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair) (1924)

text: I am alone among dilapidated things; the rain falls on me, and it resembles me, it resembles me in its delirium, solitary in the dead world, rejected as it falls, and stubbornly formless.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: I am alone with rickety materials, the rain falls on me, and it is like me, it is like me in its raving, alone in the dead world, repulsed as it falls, and with no persistent form.
source_section: Residencia en la Tierra (Residence on Earth) (1933)

text: Under the earth's dampness, among deaf roots, of human tongues, the poor would only know your name, and the thick earth cannot comprehend your name, made of impenetrable and divine substances.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Later on you will find buried near the coconut tree the knife which I hid there for fear you would kill me, and now suddenly I would be glad to smell its kitchen steel used to the weight of your hand, the shine of your foot: under the dampness of the ground, among the deaf roots, in all the languages of the men only the poor will know your name, and the dense earth does not understand your name made of impenetrable divine substances.
source_section: Residencia en la Tierra (Residence on Earth) (1933)

text: I do not want so many misfortunes for myself. I do not want to continue as root and tomb, as a solitary underground, a cellar with frozen dead, dying of sorrow.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: I do not want to be the inheritor of so many misfortunes. I do not want to continue as a root and as a tomb, as a solitary tunnel, as a cellar full of corpses, stiff with cold, dying with pain.
source_section: Residencia en la Tierra (Residence on Earth) (1933)

text: If you ask me where I've been, I must say 'It happens.' I know only what birds lose, the sea left behind, or my sister weeping. Why so many regions, why a black night in the mouth? Why dead?
author: Pablo Neruda
source: If you should ask me where I've been all this time I have to say "Things happen." I have to dwell on stones darkening the earth, on the river ruined in its own duration: I know nothing save things the birds have lost, the sea I left behind, or my sister crying. Why this abundance of places? Why does day lock with day? Why the dark night swilling round in our mouths? And why the dead?
source_section: Residencia en la Tierra (Residence on Earth) (1933)

text: You will ask: And where are the lilacs? And the metaphysics covered in poppies? And the rain that often struck their words, filling them with holes and birds?
author: Pablo Neruda
source: You are going to ask: and where are the lilacs? and the poppy-petalled metaphysics? and the rain repeatedly spattering its words and drilling them full of apertures and birds.
source_section: Residencia en la Tierra (Residence on Earth) (1933)

text: You will ask: why does his poetry not speak of dreams, of leaves, of the great volcanoes of his native land? Come and see the blood in the streets, come and see the blood in the streets, come and see the blood in the streets!
author: Pablo Neruda
source: And you will ask: why doesn't his poetry speak of dreams and leaves and the great volcanoes of his native land? Come and see the blood in the streets. Come and see the bloods in the streets. Come and see the blood in the streets!
source_section: Residencia en la Tierra (Residence on Earth) (1933)

text: He dies slowly who does not travel, who does not read, who does not listen to music, who does not find grace in himself. He dies slowly who destroys his self-esteem, who does not allow himself to be helped...
author: Pablo Neruda
source: He who does not travel, who does not read, who does not listen to music, who does not find grace in himself, she who does not find grace in herself, dies slowly. He who slowly destroys his own self-esteem, who does not allow himself to be helped, [dies slowly...]

text: They may cut all the flowers, but they cannot keep spring from coming.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: They may mow down all the flowers, but they can't stop spring.

text: Neruda died as the military junta took power, becoming a symbol of Chilean resistance, his writings a dialogue reaching beyond death.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Adrienne Rich What Is Found There (1993)

text: Pablo Neruda of Chile, one of the world’s greatest poets
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Paul Robeson , "Thoughts on Winning the Stalin Peace Prize" (January 1953)

text: Reading poetry keeps my sense of what words can do wide open and my sense of beauty awake.
author: Pablo Neruda

text: Neruda adored life, wild about everything from art to food, finding mystical experience in eating and drinking. Literature, for him, was generated by a sensual experience of life.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Mario Vargas Llosa interview with The Paris Review (1990)

text: Some say cavalry, some say ships, some say infantry is the fairest sight on earth. I say it is the one you love.
author: Sappho
source: Fr. 16 Voigt (tr. Willis Barnstone, 1965) Some there are who say that the fairest thing seen on the black earth is an array of horsemen; some, men marching; some would say ships; but I say she whom one loves best. St. 1 (tr. Richmond Lattimore, 1960) Some men say an army of horse and some men say an army on foot and some men say an army of ships is the most beautiful thing on the black earth. But I say it is what you love. St. 1 (tr. Anne Carson, 2002)

text: One glance at you, and my voice cracks, a thin flame runs under my skin, my eyes go blind, my ears ring, a cold sweat pours down my body, I tremble all over, turn paler than grass.
author: Sappho
source: Fr. 31 Voigt (tr. Stanley Lombardo, 2002) My muse, what ails this ardour? Mine eys be dym, my lymns shake, My voice is hoarse, my throte scorcht, My tong to this roofe cleaves, My fancy amazde, my thoughtes dull’d, My head doth ake, my life faints My sowle begins to take leave, So greate a passion all feele, To think a soare so deadly I should so rashly ripp up. (tr. Philip Sidney, 1593) Compare: Catullus , no. 51

text: The stars hide away their shining form around the lovely moon when in all her fullness she shines over all the earth.
author: Sappho
source: Fr. 34 Voigt (tr. David A. Campbell, 1982) The stars about the lovely moon Fade back and vanish very soon, When, round and full, her silver face Swims into sight, and lights all space. (tr. Edwin Arnold, 1869)

text: Eros has shaken my mind, wind sweeping down the mountain on oaks.
author: Sappho

text: I loved you, Atthis, once, long, long ago... You seemed to me a small, ungainly child.
author: Sappho

text: A handsome man guards his image a while; a good man will one day take on beauty.
author: Sappho

text: But thou shalt ever lie dead, nor shall there be any remembrance of thee then or thereafter, for thou hast not of the roses of Pieria; but thou shalt wander obscure even in the house of Hades, flitting among the shadowy dead.
author: Sappho
source: Fr. 55 Voigt (tr. H. T. Wharton, 1887) Dead shalt thou lie; and nought Be told of thee or thought, For thou hast plucked not of the Muses’ tree: And even in Hades’ halls Amidst thy fellow-thralls No friendly shade thy shade shall company! (tr. Thomas Hardy, 1901)

text: I am downcast and tremble with pity for my state when old age and wrinkles cover me, when Eros flies about and I pursue the glorious young.
author: Sappho

text: O dream on your black wings you come when I am sleeping. Sweet is the god but still I am in agony and far from my strength.
author: Sappho

text: Do thou, O Dica, set garlands upon thy lovely hair, weaving sprigs of dill with thy delicate hands; for those who wear fair blossoms may surely stand first.
author: Sappho
source: Fr. 81 Voigt (tr. E. M. Cox, 1924) Weave garlands, maiden, from the strands Of dill, and with soft gentle hands Set the delicious leafage round your head. The Goddess and the happy Graces Love to look on flower-crown’d faces, But turn aside from the ungarlanded. (tr. C. M. Bowra, 1938)

text: Truly, I wish I were dead. She was weeping when she left me, and said many things to me, and said this: "How much we have suffered, Sappho. Truly, I don't want to leave you."
author: Sappho

text: Sweet mother, I truly cannot weave my web; for I am o’erwhelmed through Aphrodite with love of a slender youth.
author: Sappho
source: Fr. 102 Voigt (tr. J. M. Edmonds, 1922) Oh, my sweet Mother—'tis in vain— I cannot weave, as once I wove— So wildered is my heart and brain With thinking of that youth I love! (tr. Thomas Moore, 1826) Compare: Landor , Simonidea (1806)

text: Like the sweet apple reddening on the topmost branch, the topmost apple on the tip of the branch, and the pickers forgot it, well, no, they didn't forget, they just couldn't reach it.
author: Sappho
source: Fr. 105a Voigt (tr. Stanley Lombardo, 2002) Like the sweet apple which reddens upon the topmost bough, A-top on the topmost twig,—which the pluckers forgot, somehow,— Forgot it not, nay, but got it not, for none could get it till now. (tr. D. G. Rossetti, 1870) Like the pippin blushing high On the tree-top beneath the sky, Where the pickers forgot it—nay, Could not reach it so far away. (tr. J. M. Edmonds, 1922)

text: As the hyacinth which the shepherd tramples on the hill Lies upon the ground and lying bloometh purple still.
author: Sappho
source: Fr. 105b Voigt (tr. J. M. Edmonds, 1922) Like the wild hyacinth flower, which on the hills is found, Which the passing feet of the shepherds for ever tear and wound, Until the purple blossom is trodden into the ground. (tr. D. G. Rossetti, 1870) Like a hyacinth in the mountains, trampled by shepherds until only a purple stain remains on the ground. (tr. Mary Barnard, 1958)

text: Virginity, virginity, when you leave me, where do you go? I am gone and never come back to you. I never return.
author: Sappho

text: Now Love masters my limbs and shakes me, fatal creature, bitter-sweet.
author: Sappho
source: Fr. 130 Voigt (tr. H. T. Wharton, 1887) Lo, Love once more, the limb-dissolving King, The bitter-sweet impracticable thing, Wild-beast-like rends me with fierce quivering. (tr. J. A. Symonds, 1883) Love has unbound my limbs and set me shaking, A monster bitter-sweet and my unmaking. (tr. C. M. Bowra, 1938) Compare: Tennyson, "Fatima", l. 1 ( Poems , 1843) · Swinburne , "Fragoletta", st. 14 ( Poems and Ballads , 1866); Bothwell , act 1, sc. 1 (1874); Tristram of Lyonesse , sec. 6 (1882)

text: I have a fair daughter with a form like a golden flower, Cleïs the belovedest, above whom I prize nor all Lydia nor lovely Lesbos.
author: Sappho
source: Fr. 132 Voigt (tr. H. T. Wharton, 1887) I have a child; so fair As golden flowers is she, My Cleïs, all my care. I’d not give her away For Lydia’s wide sway Nor lands men long to see. (tr. C. M. Bowra, 1938)

text: The lovely-voiced harbinger of Spring, the nightingale.
author: Sappho
source: Fr. 136 Voigt (tr. J. M. Edmonds, 1922) The dear good angel of the spring, The nightingale. (tr. Ben Jonson, 1641)

text: If you are my friend, stand up before me and scatter the grace that's in your eyes.
author: Sappho

text: Evening, thou that bringest all that bright morning scattered; thou bringest the sheep, the goat, the child back to her mother.
author: Sappho
source: Fr. 140 Voigt (tr. H. T. Wharton, 1887) Thou, Hesper, bringest homeward all That radiant dawn sped far and wide: The sheep to fold, the goat to stall, The children to their mother’s side. (tr. Rennell Rodd, 1919) All whom morning sends to roam, Hesper loves to lead them home. Home return who him behold, Child to mother, sheep to fold. (tr. A. E. Housman, 1922) Hesperus, you herd homeward whatever Dawn’s light dispersed you herd sheep—herd goats—herd children home to their mothers. (tr. Mary Barnard, 1958) Compare: Byron, Don Juan , canto 3 (1819) l. 107 · Tennyson , Leonine Elegiacs (1830–1884) and Locksley Hall: Sixty Years After (1886)

text: When wrath runs rampage in your heart you must hold still that rambunctious tongue!
author: Sappho

text: The moon has set, And the Pleiades. Midnight . The hour has gone by. I sleep alone.
author: Sappho

text: To have beauty is to have only that, but to have goodness is to be beautiful too.
author: Sappho

text: The Muse who died, yet with the deathless Muses, hand in hand, sang side by side.
author: Sappho
source: Antipater of Sidon , in Anthologia Palatina , bk. VII, no. 14; tr. Andrew Lang , Grass of Parnassus (2nd ed., 1892), p. 173

text: I wanted to hear Sappho’s laughter and the speech of her stringed shell. What I heard was whiskered mumble- ment of grammarians: Greek pterodactyls and Victorian dodos.
author: Sappho

text: And of the flowers of Sappho few, but roses .
author: Sappho
source: Meleager , Garland ; tr. J. M. Edmonds, Lyra Graeca , vol. 1 (1922) p. 165

text: Of Sappho few flowers, but they are roses.
author: Sappho
source: Meleager, in Anthologia Palatina , bk. IV, no. 1; tr. W. R. Paton, Greek Anthology , vol. 1 (1916) p. 111

text: Sappho speaks words mingled truly with fire; through her song she communicates the heat of her heart.
author: Sappho
source: Plutarch , Moralia , Dialogue on Love , 762, translated by Edwin L. Minar, F. H. Sandbach and W. C. Helmbold, Vol. IX (1961) p. 389

text: Living unloved, to die unknown, Unwept, untended and alone.
author: Sappho
source: Christina Rossetti , "Sappho", in Dinah Roe (ed.) Selected Poems (Penguin Books, 2008) p. 6

text: The small dark body's Lesbian loveliness That held the fire eternal.
author: Sappho
source: Algernon Charles Swinburne , "On the Cliffs", Songs of the Springtides (1880)

text: Thou large-brained woman and large-hearted man.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Or from Browning some "Pomegranate," which, if cut deep down the middle, shows a heart within blood-tinctured of a veined humanity.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Do ye hear the children weeping, O my brothers, Ere the sorrow comes with years? They are leaning their young heads against their mothers— And that cannot stop their tears.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning
source: The Cry of the Children , st. 1 (1844)

text: I tell you, hopeless grief is passionless.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Therefore to this dog will I, Tenderly not scornfully, Render praise and favor: With my hand upon his head, Is my benediction said Therefore and for ever.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: "Yes," I answered you last night; "No," this morning, Sir, I say. Colours seen by candlelight, Will not look the same by day.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: What was he doing, the great god Pan , Down in the reeds by the river? Spreading ruin and scattering ban, Splashing and paddling with hoofs of a goat, And breaking the golden lilies afloat With the dragon-fly on the river.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: That night we felt our love would hold.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: But so fair, She takes the breath of men away Who gaze upon her unaware.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Speak low to me, my Saviour, low and sweet, From out the hallelujahs, sweet and low, Lest I should fear, and fall, and miss Thee so, Who art not missed by any that entreat.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning
source: Reported in Josiah Hotchkiss Gilbert, Dictionary of Burning Words of Brilliant Writers (1895), p. 247

text: And I said in underbreath — All our life is mixed with death, — And who knoweth which is best? And I smiled to think God's greatness Flowed around our incompleteness, — Round our restlessness, His rest.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning
source: Rhyme of the Duchess ; reported in Josiah Hotchkiss Gilbert, Dictionary of Burning Words of Brilliant Writers (1895), p. 514

text: Though tears fall and clouds conceal the sun, I know God lives and loves, and say, 'Thy will be done.'
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning
source: Reported in Josiah Hotchkiss Gilbert, Dictionary of Burning Words of Brilliant Writers (1895), p. 514

text: But since he had The genius to be loved, why let him have The justice to be honoured in his grave.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning
source: Crowned and Buried , xxvii reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: And that dismal cry rose slowly And sank slowly through the air, Full of spirit's melancholy And eternity's despair; And they heard the words it said,— "Pan is dead! great Pan is dead! Pan, Pan is dead!"
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning
source: The Dead Pan ; reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: She has seen the mystery hid Under Egypt's pyramid: By those eyelids pale and close Now she knows what Rhamses knows.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning
source: Little Mattie , Stanza ii; reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: There Shakespeare , on whose forehead climb The crowns o’ the world; oh, eyes sublime With tears and laughter for all time!
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: And Chaucer, with his infantine Familiar clasp of things divine.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: And Marlowe, Webster, Fletcher, Ben, Whose fire-hearts sowed our furrows when The world was worthy of such men.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Knowledge by suffering entereth, And life is perfected by death.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: "Guess now who holds thee?"—"Death," I said. But there The silver answer rang—" Not Death, but Love. "
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Hush, call no echo up in further proof Of desolation! there's a voice within That weeps . . . as thou must sing . . . alone, aloof.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Go from me. Yet I feel that I shall stand Henceforward in thy shadow.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: If you must love me, let it be for love's sake only, not for fleeting qualities or pity, that our love may endure through eternity.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: When our two souls stand up erect and strong, Face to face, silent, drawing nigh and nigher, Until the lengthening wings break into fire At either curvèd point, — what bitter wrong Can the earth do to us, that we should not long Be here contented?
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: God only, who made us rich, can make us poor.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Because God's gifts put man's best dreams to shame.'
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Instruct me how to thank thee! Oh, to shoot My soul's full meaning into future years, That they should lend it utterance, and salute Love that endures, from life that disappears!
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: I seek no copy now of life's first half: Leave here the pages with long musing curled, And write me new my future's epigraph, New angel mine, unhoped for in the world!
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: I love thee to the depth and breadth and height my soul can reach, and, if God choose, I shall but love thee better after death.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Here's ivy! — take them, as I used to do Thy flowers, and keep them where they shall not pine. Instruct thine eyes to keep their colours true, And tell thy soul, their roots are left in mine.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: I will write my story for my better self, as one paints a portrait for a friend, to hold together what he was and is.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Life, struck sharp on death, Makes awful lightning. His last word was, 'Love–' 'Love, my child, love, love!'–(then he had done with grief) 'Love, my child.' Ere I answered he was gone, And none was left to love in all the world.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Dreams of doing good For good-for-nothing people.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: The beautiful seems right By force of Beauty, and the feeble wrong Because of weakness.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: If I married him, I would not dare to call my soul my own, for he had bought and paid for every thought and heart-beat.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: God answers sharp and sudden on some prayers, And thrusts the thing we have prayed for in our face, A gauntlet with a gift in't.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Good critics, who have stamped out poets' hope, Good statesmen, who pulled ruin on the state, Good patriots, who for a theory risked a cause.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: That he, in his developed manhood, stood A little sunburnt by the glare of life; While I . . it seemed no sun had shone on me.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: The growing drama may outgrow its simulated stage and take for a worthier stage the soul itself, with its shifting fancies and celestial lights.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: The sole work of poets is to represent their own live, throbbing age, which spends more passion than Roland with his knights.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Since when was genius found respectable?
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Man, the two-fold creature, apprehends the two-fold manner, and nothing in the world comes single to him, but builds up to eterne significance through God.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Earth's crammed with heaven, And every common bush afire with God: But only he who sees, takes off his shoes.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: The face, which, duly as the sun, Rose up for me with life begun, To mark all bright hours of the day With hourly love, is dimmed away — And yet my days go on, go on.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: The heart I leaned upon is caught away, and cold before my summer's done, yet my days go on, go on.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: By anguish which made pale the sun, I hear Him charge his saints that none Among his creatures anywhere Blaspheme against Him with despair, However darkly days go on.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: No mortal grief deserves the thorn-wreath crown; O supreme Love, the sharp regalia are for Thee. Grief may be joy misunderstood; I trust Thee while my days go on.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Whatever's lost, it first was won; We will not struggle nor impugn. Perhaps the cup was broken here, That Heaven's new wine might show more clear. I praise Thee while my days go on.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Being and suffering are one, and I praise Thee while my days go on.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Occupy their minds with predictable patterns while you wait for the extraordinary.
author: Sun Tzu

text: For Sunzi, the greatest general was the one who could win a war, through manoeuvre or trickery, without fighting a battle.
author: Sun Tzu
source: Margaret MacMillan , War: How Conflict Shaped Us (2020)

text: I say further, and lay my saying to your heart, you too shall live but for a little season; death and the day of your doom are close upon you, and they will lay you low by the hand of Achilles son of Aiakos.
author: Homer
source: XVI (tr. S. Butler); Patroclus to Hector.

text: A blind man; he dwells on the rocky Chios; and his songs are the first among men.
author: Homer
source: Homeric "Hymn to Delian Apollo", cited by Thucydides , iii.104, as quoted in Arnold H. L. Heeren 's Reflections on the Politics of Ancient Greece , trans. George Bancroft (1824), footnote on p. 119

text: O fortunate youth, to have found Homer as the herald of your valor!
author: Homer
source: O happy youth! in having found a Homer to celebrate thy virtues!

text: Homer's existence has been a serious misfortune to the human race, for he gave military life a charm that debases men to brutes under the pretext of exalting them to gods.
author: Homer
source: Joel Barlow , Advice to the Privileged Orders in the Several States of Europe, Resulting from the Necessity and Propriety of a General Revolution in the Principle of Government. Part I. (1792), pp. 76-77

text: Sappho's poetry, with its fervid feeling for love as a saving grace, rivaled Homer's hymnology of war, hatred, and revenge.
author: Homer
source: Mary Ritter Beard , Woman as force in history (1946)

text: Homer is the most simple of all great poets; Pope's translation, though excellent, modernizes him, losing the old bard's simplicity.
author: Homer
source: Hugh Blair , Lectures on Rhetoric and Belles Lettres (1784), Lecture XLIII: 'Homer's Iliad and Odyssey—Virgil's Aeneid', p. 407.

text: They say no woman could possibly have written the Odyssey. To me, on the other hand, it seems even less possible that a man could have done so. As for its being by a practised and elderly writer, nothing but youth and inexperience could produce anything so naïve and so lovely.
author: Homer
source: Samuel Butler , The Note-Books of Samuel Butler (1912), Part XII, 'Translating the Odyssey '.

text: Homer's poems sprang from a free fury and a full soul, imitating none, nor ever worthily imitated; Virgil's were but courtly echoes.
author: Homer
source: George Chapman , dedication to Achilles' Shield (1598).

text: Virgil was of a quiet, sedate temper, with propriety of thoughts and ornament of words; Homer was violent, impetuous, and full of fire, rapid in his thoughts and free in expression.
author: Homer
source: John Dryden , Preface to Fables, Ancient and Modern (1700).

text: The Iliad demonstrated that the fall of an enemy is tragic, not comic, introducing an objective vision that gave poetry its enduring authority.
author: Homer
source: Northrop Frye , Anatomy of Criticism (1957), p. 319.

text: Even great Homer sometimes nods.
author: Homer
source: I'm aggrieved when sometimes even excellent Homer nods.

text: The Iliad and the Odyssey belong to the end, not the beginning, of a poetical epoch, marking its highest point.
author: Homer
source: Richard Claverhouse Jebb , Greek Literature (1877), p. 36

text: Homer's excellence is elevation of thought, Virgil's is splendor of diction; thus, Homer's beauties are difficult to lose, Virgil's difficult to retain.
author: Homer
source: Samuel Johnson , The Lives of the Most Eminent English Poets; with Critical Observations on their Works. Volume II (1781), pp. 164-165

text: I was so strongly moved by the Iliad's genius that I blubbered for imaginary beings, a power far surpassing the glory of Caesar and Alexander.
author: Homer
source: Thomas Babington Macaulay to his niece Margaret (August 1851), quoted in George Otto Trevelyan, The Life and Letters of Lord Macaulay, Volume II (1876), pp. 186-187

text: The Iliad is a dangerous book, for it raises unholy passions, created models of conflict that have troubled the world, and taught bad historians to attribute silly causes to earnest wars.
author: Homer
source: Arnaldo Momigliano , 'Some Observations on Causes of War in Ancient Historiography', Acta Congressus Madvigiant , Proceedings of the Second International Congress of Classical Studies 1954, vol. I (1958), pp. 199-211, quoted in A. D. Momigliano, Studies in Historiography (1966; 1969), pp. 112-113

text: Homer defied nature by making the childhood of poetry adult, complete, and mature, earning him the title 'the first poet and the last.'
author: Homer
source: Michel de Montaigne , 'On the most excellent of men', The Complete Essays , translated by M. A. Screech (1991; 2003), pp. 851-852

text: What renown can compare with Homer's? Nothing lives like his fame and works on the lips of men, a noble farce where kings and empires play their parts.
author: Homer
source: Michel de Montaigne , 'On the most excellent of men', The Complete Essays , translated by M. A. Screech (1991; 2003), pp. 852-853

text: Poetry changes but does not progress; we still enjoy Sappho and Homer, who are today's news as much as when they were written.
author: Homer
source: Marge Piercy "WHY SPECULATE ON THE FUTURE?" in My Life, My Body (2015)

text: You were born of no mortal mother, but of Calliope.
author: Homer
source: Pseudo-Plutarch , On Homer , in Homeric Hymns, Homeric Apocrypha, Lives of Homer , edited and translated by Martin L. West (2003), p. 413
