text: Each time I put down The Iliad, I am convinced that I have been face to face with the meaning of existence, an insight no other work gives so powerfully.
author: Homer

text: Homer embodied the ideal perfection of his age in human character, awakening an ambition in his readers to become like Achilles, Hector, and Ulysses.
author: Homer
source: Percy Bysshe Shelley , A Defence of Poetry (1821)

text: Homer was central to the basic education of the Greeks, at least as familiar as Shakespeare is to us, if not more so.
author: Homer

text: It is easier to steal Hercules' club than a verse from Homer.
author: Homer
source: It is easier to steal the club of Hercules than a line from Homer.

text: Know the smallest things and the biggest things, the shallowest things and the deepest things.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: The principle of strategy is having one thing, to know ten thousand things.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: The essence of this book is that you must train day and night in order to make quick decisions.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: It is difficult to know yourself if you do not know others.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: The Way of strategy is the Way of nature.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: I have been many years training in the Way of strategy, called Ni Ten Ichi Ryu, and now I think I will explain it in writing for the first time.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: From youth my heart has been inclined toward the Way of strategy.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: After that I studied morning and evening searching for the principle, and came to realise the Way of strategy when I was fifty.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: There is no warrior in the world today who really understands the Way of strategy.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Generally speaking, the Way of the warrior is resolute acceptance of death.
author: Miyamoto Musashi
source: Variant translation: First, as is often said, a samurai must have both literary and martial skills: to be versed in the two is his duty. Even if he has no natural ability, a samurai must train assiduously in both skills to a degree appropriate to his status. On the whole, if you are to assess the samurai's mind, you may think it is simply attentiveness to the manner of dying.

text: When you sacrifice your life, you must make fullest use of your weaponry.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: The Way of the Ichi school is the spirit of winning, whatever the weapon and whatever its size.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: These things cannot be explained in detail. From one thing, know ten thousand things. When you attain the Way of strategy there will not be one thing you cannot see. You must study hard.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: If you know the Way broadly, you will see it in everything; men must polish their particular Way.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: If you learn "indoor" techniques, you will think narrowly and forget the true Way. Thus you will have difficulty in actual encounters.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Weapons should be hardy rather than decorative.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: There is timing in everything. Timing in strategy cannot be mastered without a great deal of practice.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: All things entail rising and falling timing. You must be able to discern this.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Victory comes from knowing the enemy's timing and striking when least expected.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: The Way is in training: perceive those things which cannot be seen, and do nothing which is of no use.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: To master strategy, look at things on a large scale; through training, you will conquer men with your body and beat ten with your spirit.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Language does not extend to explaining the Way in detail, but it can be grasped intuitively.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Think broadly to understand strategy for all battles; for if you mistake the Way even a little, you will become bewildered.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Do not merely read, memorise, or imitate; absorb these principles into your body, realizing the Way from within your own heart.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: In fighting and in everyday life, be determined yet calm; let not your spirit slacken when your body relaxes, nor your body relax when your spirit is calm.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Be neither insufficiently spirited nor over spirited. An elevated spirit is weak and a low spirit is weak. Do not let the enemy see your spirit.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Cultivate wisdom and spirit: look at things from a high point of view, and ceaselessly research strategy to develop a steady spirit, even when hard-pressed.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: In all forms of strategy, it is necessary to maintain the combat stance in everyday life and to make your everyday stance your combat stance. You must research this well.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: The gaze should be large and broad: see distant things as if close, and take a distanced view of close things.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Generally, I dislike fixedness in both long swords and hands. Fixedness means a dead hand. Pliability is a living hand. You must bear this in mind.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Whatever attitude you are in, do not be conscious of making the attitude; think only of cutting.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: To wield the long sword well, you must wield it calmly; haste will only lead to error.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Beyond fixed stances, the sword's true attitude is to adapt, cutting with ease according to the moment, place, and foe.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: With sword in hand, your sole intent must be to cut the enemy; every action, from parry to strike, must culminate in that single, decisive cut.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Fixed formation is bad. Study this well.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: The smacking parry is not a block, but a precise deflection, timed to instantly cut the enemy without losing control of your blade.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: When outnumbered, drive your enemies together like a line of fishes, then cut them down without quarter.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: You can know how to win through strategy with the long sword, but it cannot be clearly explained in writing. You must practise diligently in order to understand how to win.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Through diligent training in the Way, strategy will flow from your heart, granting you the certainty of a single, decisive cut and victory at will.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Step by step walk the thousand-mile road.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Today is victory over yourself of yesterday; tomorrow is your victory over lesser men.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: The Way of the sword is learned through countless contests, fighting for survival, and discovering the meaning of life and death.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: You cannot profit from small techniques particularly when full armor is worn. My Way of strategy is the sure method to win when fighting for your life one man against five or ten.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Always drive the enemy into disadvantageous positions, using the terrain to establish your predominant fighting stance.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: To win quickly, seize the lead by understanding the enemy's spirit and grasping his strategy.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: To 'know the times' is to discern the enemy's disposition—flourishing or waning—and position yourself to exploit it.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: To 'tread down the sword' means to defeat the enemy at the very start of his attack, crushing him so he cannot rise again.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: True 'treading' is a total commitment of body, spirit, and sword, ensuring the enemy cannot attack a second time.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Everything collapses when its rhythm deranges. When the enemy falters, pursue without hesitation, lest they recover.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: When locked in an indecisive struggle, abandon the current spirit and win with an unexpected technique.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: When the enemy's position is hidden, attack strongly to reveal their resources, then defeat them with a different method.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: When the enemy is agitated, meet them with complete calmness; they will relax, and then you can strike with a Void spirit.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Cause loss of balance: attack without warning where the enemy least expects it, then exploit their undecided spirit to defeat them.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Frighten the enemy not by sight, but by sound or unseen threats, then win by exploiting their frightened rhythm.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: When unable to advance, 'soak in' and become one with the enemy; win by applying a suitable technique while mutually entangled.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: In large-scale strategy, it is beneficial to strike at the corners of the enemy's force, If the corners are overthrown, the spirit of the whole body will be overthrown.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: In combat, confuse the enemy with varied techniques and feints; when they are bewildered, you can easily win.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: In battle, attack strong points, then shift like a winding mountain path to other strong points, especially when outnumbered.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: What is meant by 'mingling' is the spirit of advancing and becoming engaged with the enemy, and not withdrawing even one step. You must understand this.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: In large-scale strategy, when we see that the enemy has few men, or if he has many men but his spirit is weak and disordered, we knock the hat over his eyes, crushing him utterly. If we crush lightly, he may recover.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: The 'mountain-sea' spirit means never repeating a failed attack; do not try the same approach a third time.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: To truly win, 'penetrate the depths' and destroy the enemy's spirit, for a superficially beaten foe with an unbroken spirit is still undefeated.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: When deadlocked, 'renew' by changing your spirit and technique, not your circumstance, to find a new rhythm and win.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: There are various kinds of spirit involved in letting go the hilt. There is the spirit of winning without a sword. There is also the spirit of holding the long sword but not winning. The various methods cannot be expressed in writing. You must train well.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: When you have mastered the Way of strategy you can suddenly make your body like a rock, and ten thousand things cannot touch you. This is the body of a rock. You will not be moved.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: True mastery of the Way is spiritual, not mere technique; many schools, despite their skill, lack the slightest true spirit.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: The true Way of sword fencing is the craft of defeating the enemy in a fight, and nothing other than this.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Dexterity alone is not sufficient to win; it is not the essence of the Way.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Those who rely on a sword's length for advantage reveal a weak strategy; true victory is found in principle, not distance.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Reject a preconceived, narrow spirit, for it renders any strategy useless.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: When crossing swords, be intent solely on killing the enemy, without thought of strength or weakness in the cut.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Victory is not won by strength alone, but by the wisdom of strategy and correct principle.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Speed is not part of the true Way of strategy. Speed implies that things seem fast or slow, according to whether or not they are in rhythm. Whatever the Way, the master of strategy does not appear fast.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Really skilful people never get out of time, and are always deliberate, and never appear busy. From this example, the principle can be seen.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: When your opponent is hurrying recklessly, you must act contrarily and keep calm. You must not be influenced by the opponent. Train diligently to attain this spirit.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: What is called the spirit of the void is where there is nothing. It is not included in man's knowledge. Of course the void is nothingness. By knowing things that exist, you can know that which does not exist. That is the void.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: People in this world look at things mistakenly, and think that what they do not understand must be the void. This is not the true void. It is bewilderment.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: When your spirit is not in the least clouded, when the clouds of bewilderment clear away, there is the true void.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: In the void is virtue, and no evil. Wisdom has existence, principle has existence, the Way has existence, spirit is nothingness.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Do not, under any circumstances, depend on a partial feeling.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Think lightly of yourself and deeply of the world.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Be detached from desire your whole life long.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Never let yourself be saddened by a separation.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Resentment and complaint are appropriate neither for oneself or others.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Do not let yourself be guided by the feeling of lust or love.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Respect Buddha and the gods without counting on their help.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: You may abandon your own body but you must preserve your honour.
author: Miyamoto Musashi

text: Fortune, mighty in all things, but most in war, turns great affairs with but small moments.
author: Julius Caesar
source: Fortune , which has a great deal of power in other matters but especially in war , can bring about great changes in a situation through very slight forces. The Civil War , Book III, 68; variant translation: "In war, events of importance are the result of trivial causes."

text: I assure you I had rather be the first man here than the second man in Rome .
author: Julius Caesar
source: On passing through a village in the Alps, as attributed in Parallel Lives , by Plutarch , as translated by John Langhorne and William Langhorne (1836), p. 499

text: It is not the well-fed long-haired man I fear, but the pale and the hungry looking.
author: Julius Caesar
source: As reported in Plutarch 's Anthony'; William Shakespeare adapted this in having Caesar declare Cassius as having "a lean and hungry look."

text: The gods grant the wicked prosperity and long impunity, only to deepen their eventual suffering.
author: Julius Caesar
source: The immortal gods are wont to allow those persons whom they wish to punish for their guilt sometimes a greater prosperity and longer impunity, in order that they may suffer the more severely from a reverse of circumstances. Book I, Ch. 14, translated by W. A. McDevitte and W. S. Bohn

text: Men readily believe what they wish to be true.
author: Julius Caesar
source: In most cases men willingly believe what they wish. Book III, Chapter 18

text: Impulsive, inexperienced people, terrified by false gossip, often make ill-considered decisions on matters of state.
author: Julius Caesar

text: I undertook this war for general freedom; yielding to fortune, I offer myself for your atonement to Rome, by death or surrender.
author: Julius Caesar

text: Men are more readily found who will offer themselves to death than who will patiently endure pain.
author: Julius Caesar
source: It is easier to find men who will volunteer to die than to find those who are willing to endure pain with patience. Book VII, Ch. 77

text: I desire nothing more than to be true to myself, and for others to be true to themselves.
author: Julius Caesar
source: I prefer nothing but that they act like themselves, and I like myself.

text: Beware the leader who bangs the drums of war; when patriotism blinds, citizens will gladly surrender all rights. How do I know? For this is what I have done. And I am Caesar.
author: Julius Caesar
source: This statement by an unknown author has also been wrongly attributed to William Shakespeare , but there are no records of it prior to late 2001. It has been debunked at Snopes.com

text: I'd rather ten guilty persons should escape, than one innocent should suffer.
author: Julius Caesar
source: Attributed by Edward Seymour in 1696 during the parliamentary proceedings against John Fenwick ( "I am of the same opinion with the Roman, who, in the case of Catiline, declared, he had rather ten guilty persons should escape, than one innocent should suffer" ), to which Lieutenant General Harry Mordaunt replied "The worthy member who spoke last seems to have forgot, that the Roman who made that declaration was suspected of being a conspirator himself" (Caesar was the only one who spoke in the Senate against executing Catiline's co-conspirators and was indeed suspected by some to be involved in the plot). However, the Caesar's corresponding speech as transmitted by Sallust contains no such phrase, even though it appears to be somewhat similar in spirit ("Whatever befalls these prisoners will be well deserved; but you, Fathers of the Senate, are called upon to consider how your action will affect other criminals. All bad precedents have originated in cases which were good; but when the control of the government falls into the hands of men who are incompetent or bad, your new precedent is transferred from those who well deserve and merit such punishment to the undeserving and blameless.") The first person to undoubtedly utter such a dictum was in fact John Fortescue ("It is better to allow twenty criminals to mercifully avoid death than to unjustly condemn one innocent person"). It should also be noted that whether the exchange between Seymour and Mordaunt even happened is itself not clearly established .

text: It was already apparent that he would become the unattainable model for every dictator.
author: Julius Caesar
source: Bertolt Brecht , The Business Affairs of Mr Julius Caesar (2016), p. 23; quoted in Long Live Latin: The Pleasures of a Useless Language (2019) by Nicola Gardini, p. 72

text: Caesar was the only man who undertook to overthrow the state when sober.
author: Julius Caesar
source: Saying of Marcus Cato , reported by Suetonius , Life of Julius Caesar , 53 (J. C. Rolfe, ed. 1913)

text: He had already brought a free city, partly by fear, partly by patience, into a habit of slavery.
author: Julius Caesar

text: Wherever you are, remember that you are equally within the power of the conqueror.
author: Julius Caesar
source: Cicero in a letter to Marcus Marcellus on his Exile from Rome by Julius Caesar

text: The greatest man that ever lived was Julius Caesar.
author: Julius Caesar
source: Alexander Hamilton , quoted by Thomas Jefferson in a letter to Dr. Benjamin Rush (1811)

text: Constant exercise of power gave Caesar a love for it.
author: Julius Caesar
source: Dan Jones , Powers and Thrones: A New History of the Middle Ages (2021), pp. 12-13

text: Fierce and untamed, he swept aside whatever stood in his path to supreme power, rejoicing to have made his way through ruin.
author: Julius Caesar
source: Undaunted, keen: where Hope or Passion called He'd fight, nor ever sheathe the murderous sword. Pressing advantage, following up his star, And sweeping all between him and his prize, He hailed the ruin that bestrew’d his way. Lucan , 1, 146 Reported in Classical and Foreign Quotations (1904), no. 15

text: Jesus , not Cæsar , I repeat,—this is the meaning of our history and democracy .
author: Julius Caesar
source: Masaryk, Tomáš Garrigue (c1921), The Religious Conditions in Czechoslovakia , p. 7

text: Caesar's campaigns showed more restraint and reflection than Alexander's, who charged like a rushing torrent, indiscriminately battering all.
author: Julius Caesar
source: Michel de Montaigne , Essays , "Observations on Julius Caesar's methods of waging war" as translated by M. A. Screech (1993)

text: The ills of the state required a monarchy, and Caesar, like a most gentle physician, had been assigned to them by Heaven itself.
author: Julius Caesar

text: He persuaded the Roman people that one-person rule could work, and this new model of the Roman Caesar would change Roman history forever.
author: Julius Caesar

text: The die is cast.
author: Julius Caesar

text: Brutus, for casting out kings, became the first consul; This man, for casting out consuls, at last became king.
author: Julius Caesar
source: Brutus was elected consul, when he sent the kings away; Caesar sent the consuls packing, Caesar is our king today. Note left on a statue of Caesar in Rome, prior to the Ides of March, as reported in Suetonius , in The Twelve Caesars , as translated by Robert Graves (1957), Divus Iulius ¶ 80

text: Caesar was a logical man; and the heir of Caesar displayed coherence in thought and act when he inaugurated the proscriptions and when he sanctioned clemency, when he seized power by force, and when he based authority upon law and consent.
author: Julius Caesar

text: Though not lovable, Caesar was and is attractive, indeed fascinating.
author: Julius Caesar
source: Arnold Toynbee , as quoted in the preface of American Caesar: Douglas MacArthur 1880-1964 (1978)

text: If the deliverance of my fellow-countrymen depended upon the death of a single man, I would go immediately and plunge the sword which would avenge my country and its violated laws into the breast of tyrants.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: 'On Suicide' (May 1786), quoted in Oscar Browning, The Boyhood and Youth of Napoleon: Some Chapters on the Life of Bonaparte, 1769–1793 (1906), p. 284

text: Sweet and incomparable Josephine, what an extraordinary influence you have over my heart.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Letter to Joséphine de Beauharnais (February 1796), as translated in Napoleon's Letters to Josephine 1796-1812 (1901) edited by Henry Foljambe Hall

text: All great events hang by a hair.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Letter to Minister of Foreign Affairs, Passariano (26 September 1797), as quoted in Napoleon as a General (1902) by Maximilian Yorck von Wartenburg, p. 269

text: From the heights of these pyramids , forty centuries look down on us.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Speech to his troops in Egypt (21 July 1798) Variant translation: "Soldiers, from the summit of yonder pyramids forty centuries look down upon you...". Published in the autobiography of French general Eugène de Beauharnais .

text: What I have done up to this is nothing. I am only at the beginning of the course I must run.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: As quoted in Memoirs of Count Miot de Melito (1788 - 1815) as translated by Frances Cashel Hoey and John Lillie (1881), Vol. II, p. 94

text: I have tasted command, and I cannot give it up; if I cannot be master, I shall leave France.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Conversation at Turin, as quoted in Memoirs of Count Miot de Melito (1788 - 1815) as translated by Frances Cashel Hoey and John Lillie (1881), Vol. II, p. 113

text: I hope to unite all wise men under the true principles of the Quran, establishing a regime of happiness.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Letter to Sheikh El-Messiri (28 August 1798); published in Correspondance Napoleon edited by Henri Plon (1861), Vol.4, No. 3148, p. 420

text: Torture produces nothing worthwhile; the poor wretches say anything the interrogator wishes to know.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: On the subject of torture, in a letter to Louis Alexandre Berthier (11 November 1798), published in Correspendance Napoleon edited by Henri Plon (1861), Vol. V, No. 3605, p. 128

text: We have need to impose on our friends and foes by deeds of glory gained only by war.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Remarks to Antoine Claire Thibaudeau during the Peace of Amiens (1802), quoted in Antoine Claire Thibaudeau, Bonaparte and the Consulate , translated and edited by G. K. Fortescue (1908), pp. 119-120

text: A form of government that is not the result of a long sequence of shared experiences, efforts, and endeavors can never take root.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Statement (1803) as quoted in The Mind of Napoleon (1955) by J. Christopher Herold

text: Anarchy is the stepping stone to absolute power.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Napoleon Bonaparte, Napoleon's War Maxims: With His Social and Political Thoughts (1804-15), Gale & Polden, (1899) p. 148

text: Commerce unites men and make them; therefore it is fatal to despotic power.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Napoleon Bonaparte, Napoleon's War Maxims: With His Social and Political Thoughts (1804-15), Gale & Polden, (1899) p. 150

text: Religion is a kind of vaccine, which, by satisfying our natural love for the marvellous, keeps us out of the hands of charlatans.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Napoleon in council, or, The opinions delivered by Bonaparte in the Council of state. Translated by Capt. Basil Hall. p. 258 . Quoted in Andrew Roberts: Napoleon: A Life

text: I will bring the nobles of this Court down so low that they shall be obliged to beg their bread.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Said to "Count Nesch" [ sic , probably Cardinal Fesch] on entry into Berlin, 26 October 1806. Quoted in "Memoirs of the Empress Josephine" by Madame de Rémusat, Volume 2

text: An impressive example is needed to contain the hatred of the peasantry and soldiery.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Letter to the commander-in-chief on the revolt in Hesse (8 January 1807), quoted in Pieter Geyl, Napoleon For and Against (1949), p. 172

text: If you hang a few, they get tired of the game, and become as submissive and humble as they ought to be.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Letter to Joseph Bonaparte , King of Spain (10 January 1809), quoted in The Confidential Correspondence of Napoleon Bonaparte with His Brother Joseph, Sometime King of Spain: Selected and Translated, with Explanatory Notes, from the "Mémoires Du Roi Joseph". Vol. II (1855), p. 15

text: So…Bessieres lived like Bayard ; he died like Turenne . We should all be envious of his fate.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: During the early phase of the Battle of Bautzen

text: Conscripts, for shame! It was on you that I was basing my hopes. I expected much from your young courage, and you are running away!
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: Where do you think you are going? Can’t you see that the battle is won? Come on, stand firm!
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: During the final stages of the Battle of Lutzen

text: I have gained the battle of Lutzen like the general-in-chief of the Army of Italy and of the Army of Egypt !
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: During the final push of Battle of Lutzen .

text: From the sublime to the ridiculous is but a step.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Speaking about troubles in the invasions of Russia (10 December 1812), as recorded by Abbé du Pradt, and quoted in History of Europe from the Commencement of the French Revolution in 1789, to the Restoration of the Bourbons in 1815 , Vol. 3 (1842) by Sir Archibald Alison, p. 593

text: If the art of war were nothing but the art of avoiding risks, glory would become the prey of mediocre minds.... I have made all the calculations; fate will do the rest.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Statement at the beginning of the 1813 campaign, as quoted in The Mind of Napoleon (1955) by J. Christopher Herold, p. 45

text: All authority is in the throne; and what is the throne? This wooden frame covered with velvet? No, I am the throne.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Address to the Legislative Body (December 1813) ; he here echoes the remark attributed to Louis XIV L'état c'est moi ( "The State is I" or more commonly: "I am the State.")

text: France is invaded. I go to command my troops; with God's help, we will drive the enemy beyond the frontier.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Statement at Paris (23 January 1814) [ specific citation needed ]

text: The bullet that will kill me is not yet cast.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Statement at Montereau (17 February 1814) [ specific citation needed ]

text: Proclaimed the sole obstacle to peace, I am ready to relinquish throne, France, and even life, for my country's good.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: England will repent of bringing the Russians so far: they will deprive her of India.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Comment made to Lord John Russell , Elba 1814, as recorded in Russell's journal and quoted in Lady John Russell: A Memoir (1910) edited by Desmond McCarthy and Agatha Russell. p. 56

text: Unite for the public safety, if you would remain an independent nation.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Proclamation to the French People (22 June 1815)

text: Wherever wood can swim, there I am sure to find this flag of England.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Statement at Rochefort (July 1815) [ specific citation needed ]

text: Work is the scythe of time.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: On board H.M.S. Bellerophon (August 1815) [ specific citation needed ]

text: The Princes of this house have abandoned their capital, not like soldiers of honour who cede to the circumstances and setbacks of the war, but like the perjured who are pursued by their own remorse.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: J. David Markham: "Imperial Glory: The Bulletins of Napoleon's Grande Armee", p. 199. Quoted in Andrew Roberts: Napoleon The Great.

text: I did not usurp the crown. I picked it up from the gutter; the people placed it on my head. I was king of the people as the Bourbons are kings of the nobles
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: 3 September 1816. Quoted in Napoleon at St Helena , John Stevens Cabot Abbott, pg 375 .

text: I never was truly my own master but was always ruled by circumstances.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Conversation with Emmanuel, comte de Las Cases (11 November 1816), Mémorial de Sainte Hélène , v. 4, p. 133 .

text: What then is, generally speaking, the truth of history ? A fable agreed upon.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Conversation with Emmanuel, comte de Las Cases (20 November 1816), Mémorial de Sainte Hélène , v. 4, p. 251 . However, the phrase predates Napoleon. Helvétius attributes it to Bernard le Bovier de Fontenelle , De l'esprit (1758), p. 443

text: Women are nothing but machines for producing children.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: The St. Helena Journal of General Baron Gourgaud (9 January 1817); as quoted in The St. Helena Journal of General Baron Gourgaud, 1815-1818 : Being a Diary written at St. Helena during a part of Napoleon's Captivity (1932) as translated by Norman Edwards, a translation of Journal de Sainte-Hélène 1815-1818 by General Gaspard Gourgaud

text: My maxim: talent paves the way, regardless of birth or wealth.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: as quoted in Barry Edward O'Meara's Napoleon in Exile, or, A voice from St. Helena p. 405

text: Religions are founded on incomprehensible miracles. Mahomet's is less ridiculous than ours.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Letter from St. Helena (28 August 1817); as quoted in The St. Helena Journal of General Baron Gourgaud, 1815-1818 : Being a Diary written at St. Helena during a part of Napoleon's Captivity (1932) as translated by Norman Edwards, a translation of Journal de Sainte-Hélène 1815-1818 by General Gaspard Gourgaud , t.2, p. 226

text: Mahomet, an intrepid soldier and statesman, revived his fatherland and forged a new power.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Napoleon I of France in Précis des guerres de César , Gosselin, 1836, edited by Comte Marchand , p. 237. This work was written by Napoleon during his exile on St. Helena. Translated by Ziad Elmarsafy in The Enlightenment Qur'an .

text: The Mohammedan religion is the finest of all
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Talks of Napoleon at St. Helena with General Baron Gourgaud , together with the journal kept by Gourgaud on their journey from Waterloo to St Helena (1903) , pp. 279–280

text: Muhammad, an intrepid soldier and statesman, revived his fatherland and forged a new power.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Statement of 1817 quoted in Précis des guerres de César, écrit à Sainte-Hélène sous la dictée de l'empereur (1836) edited by Comte Marchand , p. 237. This work was written by Napoleon during his exile on St. Helena. Translated by Ziad Elmarsafy in The Enlightenment Qur'an .

text: Muhammad was a great man. He would have been a god, had his revolution not been prepared by circumstances.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Campagnes d'Egypte et Syrie , Paris, Imprimerie Nationale, 1998, p. 275. Translated by John Tolan in European Accounts of Muhammad's Life . Napoleon wrote his memoirs on the island of Saint Helena. It is here he develops his portrait of Muhammad as a model lawmaker and conqueror.

text: I have fought sixty battles and I have learned nothing which I did not know at the beginning.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Napoleon: A Biography by Frank McLynn, p. 145. Translated from Gourgaud's Journal de Sainte-Hélène 1815-1818 , entry of 25 December 1817.

text: The Italians will soon be recognized as Europe's finest soldiers. I am very proud of my brave Italian army.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Cited in De Laugier, Vicissitudes of the Italian people from 1801 to 1815 , to. X, Firenze, 1836, p. 43 – Aless. Zanoli, About the Italian army, historical-statistical outline from 1796 to 1814 , vol. II, Milano, 1845, p. 145.

text: Our hour is marked, and no one can claim a moment of life beyond what fate has predestined.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: To Dr. Arnott (April 1821) [ specific citation needed ]

text: Ordinary men died, men of iron were taken prisoner: I only brought back with me men of bronze.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Statement of 1812, quoted in Napoleon's Cavalry and its Leaders (1978) by David Johnson

text: Ordinary men succumbed; men of iron were taken prisoner; I bring back only men of bronze.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: A great captain supplies all deficiencies by his courage, marching boldly to meet the attack and seizing victory from any adversary's irresolution.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: Since one must take sides, choose the victorious: it is better to eat than to be eaten.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Letter to his brother, as quoted in The Age of Napoleon (2002) by J. Christopher Herold, p. 8

text: From first day to last, he remains the same: majestic and simple, infinitely severe and infinitely gentle. His prudent conduct, a blend of strength and sweetness, commands admiration.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Sentiment de Napoléon sur la divinité de Jésus-Christ (1841), p. 59. Translated: "From first day to the last, he is the same, always the same, majestic and simple, extremely severe and extremely mild in the business of public life, so to speak, Jesus does not hold to any criticism, his prudent manner so delighted admiration by a mixture of strength and gentleness".

text: I am a monarch of God 's creation, and you reptiles of the earth dare not oppose me. I render an account of my government to none save God and Jesus Christ .
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Addressing members of the Catholic clergy assembled during 'Bonaparte's Conference with the Catholic and Protestant clergy at Breda ,' May 1, 1810 (originally reported in the Gazette of Dorpt ), as quoted in The life of Napoleon Bonaparte, emperor of the French: with a preliminary view of the French revolution , Sir Walter Scott , Philadelphia: Leary & Getz, 1857, p. 91

text: A cowardly act! What do I care about that? You may be sure that I should never fear to commit one if it were to my advantage.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Quoted by George Gordon Andrews in Napoleon in Review (1939)

text: The true conquests, the only ones that cause no regret, are those made over ignorance.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: (November 26, 1797) as quoted in Andrew Roberts Napoleon: A Life p. 157

text: A journalist is a grumbler, a censurer, a giver of advice, a regent of sovereigns, a tutor of nations. Four hostile newspapers are more to be feared than an hundred thousand bayonets.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Appears to be fabricated as it is not known from surviving print before 19 March 1845 when it appeared in Mississippi Democrat in an article titled 'The Press' and in The North-Carolina Standard on its own as a column filler. On April 17, 1845 a longer version of "The Press" article appeared in The Guard (Holly Springs, Miss.) crediting the article to the New Orleans Jeffersonian , a newspaper edited by Col J F H Claiborne (Life of Col. J.F.H. Claiborne by Franklin L. Riley, P. 232). Claiborne was also a US congressman and "ranked among the most eloquent orators of the House of Representatives" (ibid P.221). It is known he was in agreement with the sentiment of the quote having said, "The journalist has a grander mission, and if conscientiously pursued, it is the highest and noblest of all avocations" (ibid P. 228). The relevant issue of the Jeffersonian does not appear to have survived but is possibly the original source of the invented quote. Claiborne again uses the "quote" in 1846 in a tribute to Professor Thomas R. Dew ( Richmond Enquirer (Richmond, Va.), October 20, 1846). The "quote" then quickly spreads across the english-speaking world, arriving in England by 26 April and appearing in The Sentinel (Sydney, Australia) on 5 November 1845. It appears to be unknown in french.

text: Immortality is the best recollection one leaves.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: Kiss the feet of Popes provided their hands are tied.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: Malice delights to blacken the characters of prominent men.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: More glorious to merit a sceptre than to possess one.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: Those who are free from common prejudices acquire others.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: I am the Revolution, I myself!
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source: Pg 427. Thomas W. Redhead translation, Volume II.

text: When you have an enemy in your power, deprive him of the means of ever injuring you.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: Political Aphorisms, Moral and Philosophical Thoughts (1848)

text: He who fears being conquered is certain of defeat.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: Political Aphorisms, Moral and Philosophical Thoughts (1848)

text: The greater the man, the less is he opinionative, he depends upon events and circumstances.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: Political Aphorisms, Moral and Philosophical Thoughts (1848)

text: What is the government ? nothing , unless supported by opinion .
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: Political Aphorisms, Moral and Philosophical Thoughts (1848)

text: A constitution should be framed so as not to impede the action of government, nor force the government to its violation.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: Political Aphorisms, Moral and Philosophical Thoughts (1848)

text: The people must not be counted upon; they cry indifferently : "Long live the King!" and "Long live the Conspirators!" a proper direction must be given to them, and proper instruments employed to effect it.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: Political Aphorisms, Moral and Philosophical Thoughts (1848)

text: Hereditary succession to the magistracy is absurd, as it tends to make a property of it; it is incompatible with the sovereignty of the people.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: Political Aphorisms, Moral and Philosophical Thoughts (1848)

text: Orders and decorations are necessary in order to dazzle the people.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: Political Aphorisms, Moral and Philosophical Thoughts (1848)

text: Sometimes a great example is necessary to all the public functionaries of the state.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: Political Aphorisms, Moral and Philosophical Thoughts (1848)

text: A Government protected by foreigners will never be accepted by a free people.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: Political Aphorisms, Moral and Philosophical Thoughts (1848)

text: A great people may be killed, but they cannot be intimidated.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: Political Aphorisms, Moral and Philosophical Thoughts (1848)

text: A great reserve and severity of manners are necessary for the command of those who are older than ourselves.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: Political Aphorisms, Moral and Philosophical Thoughts (1848)

text: A king is sometimes obliged to commit crimes; but they are the crimes of his position.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: Political Aphorisms, Moral and Philosophical Thoughts (1848)

text: A King should sacrifice the best affections of his heart for the good of his country; no sacrifice should be above his determination.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: Political Aphorisms, Moral and Philosophical Thoughts (1848)

text: Greatness is nothing unless it be lasting.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: Political Aphorisms, Moral and Philosophical Thoughts (1848)

text: Man seldom acts upon natural impulse, but from some secret passion of the moment.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: Political Aphorisms, Moral and Philosophical Thoughts (1848)

text: The life of a citizen is the property of his country.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: Political Aphorisms, Moral and Philosophical Thoughts (1848)

text: You cannot treat with all the world at once.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: Political Aphorisms, Moral and Philosophical Thoughts (1848)

text: If you make everything difficult, the really hard things seem less so.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte
source_section: With Napoleon in Russia: The Memoirs of General De Caulaincourt, Duke of Vicenza (1933)

text: There are only two forces that unite men — fear and interest. All great revolutions originate in fear, for the play of interests does not lead to accomplishment.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: Audacity succeeds as often as it fails; in life it has an even chance.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: The superior man is never in anyone's way.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: There are so many laws that no one is safe from hanging.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: Success is the most convincing talker in the world.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: As a rule it is circumstances that make men.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: Impatience is a great obstacle to success; he who treats everything with brusqueness gathers nothing, or only immature fruit which will never ripen.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: Forms are for mediocrity; ability takes its flight unhindered.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: Never depend on the multitude, full of instability and whims; always take precautions against it.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: From triumph to downfall is but a step. I have seen a trifle decide the most important issues in the gravest affairs.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: It is only by prudence, wisdom, and dexterity, that great ends are attained and obstacles overcome. Without these qualities nothing succeeds.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: The man fitted for affairs and authority never considers individuals, but things and their consequences.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: A congress of the powers is deceit agreed on between diplomats — it is the pen of Machiavelli combined with the scimitar of Mahomet .
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: Destiny urges me to a goal of which I am ignorant. Until that goal is attained I am invulnerable, unassailable. When Destiny has accomplished her purpose in me, a fly may suffice to destroy me.
author: Napoleon Bonaparte

text: For the love of gain would reconcile the weaker to the dominion of the stronger, and the possession of capital enabled the more powerful to reduce the smaller cities to subjection.
author: Thucydides

text: But as the power of Hellas grew, and the acquisition of wealth became more an objective, the revenues of the states increasing, tyrannies were established almost everywhere...
author: Thucydides
source: Book I, 1.13- See also: Karl Marx , Grundrisse , Introduction p. 7

text: So little pains do the vulgar take in the investigation of truth, accepting readily the first story that comes to hand.
author: Thucydides
source: Variant translation: "...the search for truth strains the patience of most people, who would rather believe the first things that come to hand." Translation by Paul Woodruff.

text: In fine, I have written my work, not as an essay which is to win the applause of the moment, but as a possession for all time.
author: Thucydides

text: Concessions to adversaries only end in self reproach, and the more strictly they are avoided the greater will be the chance of security.
author: Thucydides

text: Abstinence from all injustice to other first-rate powers is a greater tower of strength than anything that can be gained by the sacrifice of permanent tranquillity for an apparent temporary advantage.
author: Thucydides

text: For the true author of the subjugation of a people is not so much the immediate agent, as the power which permits it having the means to prevent it.
author: Thucydides

text: To describe their character in a word, one might truly say that they were born into the world to take no rest themselves and to give none to others.
author: Thucydides

text: It is a common mistake in going to war to begin at the wrong end, to act first, and wait for disaster to discuss the matter.
author: Thucydides

text: War is a matter not so much of arms as of money.
author: Thucydides

text: Self-control contains honour as a chief constituent, and honour bravery.
author: Thucydides
source: Book I, 1.84; "self-control is the chief element in self-respect, and respect of self, in turn, is the chief element in courage" ( trans. Charles Forster Smith )

text: The freaks of chance are not determinable by calculation.
author: Thucydides

text: Superiority lies with him who is reared in the severest school.
author: Thucydides
source: Variant translation: "Instead, we think the plans of our neighbors are as good as our own, and we can't work out whose chances at war are better in a speech. So we always make our preparations in action, on the assumption that our enemies know what they are doing. We should not build our hopes on the belief that they will make mistakes, but on our own careful foresight. And we should not think there is much difference between one man and another, except that the winner will be the one whose education was the most severe." Translation by Paul Woodruff.

text: Speculation is carried on in safety, but, when it comes to action, fear causes failure.
author: Thucydides

text: It is not right that what was won in want should be lost in plenty.
author: Thucydides

text: If you give way, you will instantly have to meet some greater demand, as having been frightened into obedience in the first instance; while a firm refusal will make them clearly understand that they must treat you more as equals.
author: Thucydides

text: I am more afraid of our own blunders than of the enemy's devices.
author: Thucydides

text: It must be thoroughly understood that war is a necessity, and that the more readily we accept it, the less will be the ardor of our opponents, and that out of the greatest dangers communities and individuals acquire the greatest glory.
author: Thucydides

text: For men can endure to hear others praised only so long as they can severally persuade themselves of their own ability to equal the actions recounted: when this point is passed, envy comes in and with it incredulity.
author: Thucydides

text: But the palm of courage will surely be adjudged most justly to those, who best know the difference between hardship and pleasure and yet are never tempted to shrink from danger.
author: Thucydides

text: But the prize for courage will surely be awarded most justly to those who best know the difference between hardship and pleasure and yet are never tempted to shrink from danger.
author: Thucydides

text: The bravest are surely those who have the clearest vision of what is before them, glory and danger alike, and yet notwithstanding, go out to meet it.
author: Thucydides
source: Variant translations: But the palm of courage will surely be adjudged most justly to those, who best know the difference between hardship and pleasure and yet are never tempted to shrink from danger. And they are most rightly reputed valiant, who though they perfectly apprehend both what is dangerous and what is easy, are never the more thereby diverted from adventuring. (translation by Thomas Hobbes )

text: The secret of happiness is freedom and the secret of freedom is courage.
author: Thucydides

text: Hatred also is short lived; but that which makes the splendor of the present and the glory of the future remains forever unforgotten
author: Thucydides

text: He who voluntarily confronts tremendous odds must have very great internal resources to draw upon.
author: Thucydides

text: We know that there can never be any solid friendship between individuals, or union between communities that is worth the name, unless the parties be persuaded of each other's honesty.
author: Thucydides

text: Now the only sure basis of an alliance is for each party to be equally afraid of the other.
author: Thucydides

text: I have often before now been convinced that a democracy is incapable of empire.
author: Thucydides

text: It is easier for mankind to stave off adversity than to preserve prosperity.
author: Thucydides

text: I think the two things most opposed to good counsel are haste and passion; haste usually goes hand in hand with folly, passion with coarseness and narrowness of mind.
author: Thucydides

text: Still hope leads men to venture; and no one ever yet put himself in peril without the inward conviction that he would succeed in his design.
author: Thucydides

text: We must make up our minds to look for our protection not to legal terrors but to careful administration.
author: Thucydides

text: Words had to change their ordinary meaning and to take that which was now given them.
author: Thucydides

text: Do not let unexpected success lead you to grasp continually at something further.
author: Thucydides

text: The incalculable future is the most treacherous, yet most useful, for it frightens us into caution.
author: Thucydides

text: We fight for our liberty, never to enslave others, and will not yield without a struggle.
author: Thucydides

text: They stood where they stood by the power of the sword.
author: Thucydides

text: And their judgment was based more upon blind wishing than upon any sound prediction; for it is a habit of mankind to entrust to careless hope what they long for, and to use sovereign reason to thrust aside what they do not desire.
author: Thucydides

text: The long training of action was of more use for saving lives than any brief verbal exhortation.
author: Thucydides

text: Right, as the world goes, is only in question between equals in power, while the strong do what they can and the weak suffer what they must.
author: Thucydides
source: A summary of Athenian statements to the Melians , Book V, 5.89-

text: Of the gods we believe, and of men we know, that by a necessary law of their nature they rule wherever they can.
author: Thucydides

text: [H]ere we bless your simplicity but do not envy your folly.
author: Thucydides

text: And it is certain that those who do not yield to their equals, who keep terms with their superiors, and are moderate towards their inferiors, on the whole succeed best.
author: Thucydides

text: Men do not rest content with parrying the attacks of a superior, but often strike the first blow to prevent the attack being made.
author: Thucydides

text: We must not disguise from ourselves that we go to found a city among strangers and enemies, and he who undertakes such an enterprise should be prepared to become master of the country the first day he lands, or failing in this find everything hostile to him.
author: Thucydides

text: [T]hey possess most gold and silver, by which war, like everything else, flourishes.
author: Thucydides
source: Book VI, 6.34; "they have abundance of gold and silver, and these make war, like other things, go smoothly" ( trans. Benjamin Jowett )

text: Contempt for an assailant is best shown by bravery in action.
author: Thucydides
source: Book VI, 6.34-; "the true contempt of an invader is shown by deeds of valour in the field" ( trans. Benjamin Jowett )

text: [T]he Thracian people, like the bloodiest of the barbarians, being ever most murderous when it has nothing to fear.
author: Thucydides

text: Right or community of blood was not the bond of union between them, so much as interest or compulsion as the case may be.
author: Thucydides

text: When men are checked in their special excellence, the unexpected shock to their pride causes them to give way more than their real strength warrants.
author: Thucydides

text: And the rarest dangers are those in which failure brings little loss and success the greatest advantage.
author: Thucydides

text: [T]heir swaying bodies reflected the agitation of their minds, and they suffered the worst agony of all, ever just within the reach of safety or just on the point of destruction.
author: Thucydides
source: Book VII, 7.71- (See also: Fog of war ).

text: The dead lay unburied, and each man as he recognized a friend among them shuddered with grief and horror; while the living whom they were leaving behind, wounded or sick, were to the living far more shocking than the dead, and more to be pitied than those who had perished.
author: Thucydides

text: Peace is better than war, not only in being more agreeable but also in being very much more predictable.
author: Thucydides
source: Bernard Brodie , Strategy in the Missile Age (1959), pp. 408-409

text: Thucydides: the eternal manual of statesmen.
author: Thucydides
source: Edward Bulwer-Lytton , 'Pitt and Fox', The Quarterly Review (September 1855), quoted in Edward Bulwer-Lytton, Miscellaneous Prose Works, Vol. I (1868), p. 214

text: Power confers the right to exercise it over the weak; that is the way the world is.
author: Thucydides
source: J. W. Burrow , A History of Histories: Epics, Chronicles, Romances and Inquiries from Herodotus and Thucydides to the Twentieth Century (2007), p. 35

text: Might is right: a preoccupation of Thucydides, countered only by fortune's fickle hand.
author: Thucydides
source: J. W. Burrow , A History of Histories: Epics, Chronicles, Romances and Inquiries from Herodotus and Thucydides to the Twentieth Century (2007), p. 43

text: Surely no more lucid, unillusioned intelligence has ever applied itself to the writing of history.
author: Thucydides
source: J. W. Burrow , A History of Histories: Epics, Chronicles, Romances and Inquiries from Herodotus and Thucydides to the Twentieth Century (2007), p. 51

text: Thucydides recreates the pathos of events, leading readers to participate vicariously in the sufferings of war.
author: Thucydides

text: Human nature is the eternal enemy of anything superior. Words shift meaning, opinions turn in a blink. Nothing changes.
author: Thucydides
source: Bob Dylan , Chronicles, Volume One (2004), p. 36

text: Power, or wealth, creates the desire for more. Nations fight not for difference, but for alike hunger. Power, whoever wielded it, was evil, the corrupter of men.
author: Thucydides
source: Edith Hamilton , The Greek Way to Western Civilization (1930; 1942), p. 187

text: Thucydides saw morality crumble: states, unlike individuals, seize every opportunity for self-advantage. 'A city that rules an empire holds nothing which is to its own interest as contrary to right and reason.'
author: Thucydides
source: Edith Hamilton , The Greek Way to Western Civilization (1930; 1942), pp. 194-195

text: Thucydides: a strictly scientific historian, a profound philosopher. His history, a memorable and truthful portrait of an age.
author: Thucydides
source: Frederic Harrison , The Meaning of History and Other Historical Pieces (1894), p. 96

text: For international aggression—continuous, calculated, pathologically inspired—Thucydides is still the author of the best book.
author: Thucydides
source: F. H. Hinsley , Power and the Pursuit of Peace: Theory and Practice in the History of Relations between States (1963), p. 275

text: Thucydides' account of the Athenian expedition against Syracuse was the most dramatic and overwhelming thing I have ever read.
author: Thucydides

text: The anxiety regarding the balance of power was most apparent in Greek politics; Thucydides attributed the Peloponnesian War entirely to this principle.
author: Thucydides
source: David Hume , 'Discourse VI. Of the Balance of Power', Political Discourses (1752), p. 101

text: The first page of Thucydides is the commencement of real history. All preceding narrations are intermixed with fable, best left to poets.
author: Thucydides
source: David Hume , 'Discourse X. Of the Populousness of antient Nations', Political Discourses (1752), pp. 212-213

text: No greater master of stern pathos than Thucydides ever lived, for he never says too much. He was a political philosopher and an artist, feeling the tragic force of his story.
author: Thucydides
source: Richard Claverhouse Jebb , Greek Literature (1877), p. 109

text: Reading the other day...in Thucydides the sad story of the destruction of the Athenian Expeditionary Force, the A.E.F., in Sicily, which after so many centuries still fills one's eyes with tears.
author: Thucydides
source: Dimitrios Kaklamanos , letter to The Times (10 June 1940), p. 4

text: There is no prose composition in the world I place so high as the seventh book of Thucydides; it is the ne plus ultra of human art.
author: Thucydides
source: Thomas Babington Macaulay to Thomas Flower Ellis (25 August 1835), quoted in George Otto Trevelyan, The Life and Letters of Lord Macaulay, Vol. I (1876), p. 440

text: Thucydides' history is a study in political and military leadership which after 2400 years still defies comparison.
author: Thucydides
source: Arnaldo Momigliano , 'Some Observations on Causes of War in Ancient Historiography', Acta Congressus Madvigiant , Proceedings of the Second International Congress of Classical Studies 1954, vol. I (1958), pp. 199-211, quoted in A. D. Momigliano, Studies in Historiography (1966; 1969), p. 117

text: By understanding the political life of the present, and its military consequences, Thucydides believed he had understood the nature of man in its perennial elements.
author: Thucydides
source: Arnaldo Momigliano , 'Historiography on Written Tradition and Historiography on Oral Tradition', Atti della Accademia delle Scienze di Torino , Vol. 96 (1961–1962), pp. 1-12, quoted in A. D. Momigliano, Studies in Historiography (1966; 1969), pp. 214-215

text: It is the sagacious sentences in the speeches that give Thucydides his perpetuity of interest, making him as modern as if he had written yesterday.
author: Thucydides
source: John Morley , Aphorisms: An Address delivered before the Edinburgh Philosophical Institution, November 11, 1887 (1887), pp. 15-16

text: It is courage in the face of reality that distinguishes Thucydides from Plato: Plato flees into the ideal, while Thucydides maintains control of things.
author: Thucydides
source: Friedrich Nietzsche , Twilight of the Idols (1889), in The Portable Nietzsche , translated by Walter Kaufmann (1954), pp. 558-559

text: Thucydides' account of the Peloponnesian War exemplifies the strengths and weaknesses of democracy, a truth every succeeding age has seized on.
author: Thucydides
source: Alan Ryan , On Politics: A History of Political Thought: From Herodotus to the Present (2012), Ch. 1 : Why Herodotus?

text: In peace and prosperity both states and individuals are activated by higher motives because they do not fall under the dominion of imperious necessities. But war, which takes away the comfortable provision of daily life, is a violent teacher and assimilates men's characters to their conditions...
author: Thucydides

text: Those about whom you inquire have moulded with their bones into dust. Nothing but their words remain.
author: Lao Tzu
source: Attributed to Laozi. Laozi speaking to Confucius. Quoted in James Legge, Texts of Taoism, 34; Quoted from Will Durant , Our Oriental Heritage .

text: The Tao that can be expressed is not the eternal Tao; The name that can be defined is not the unchanging name.
author: Lao Tzu

text: The Tao that can be told is not the eternal Tao; The name that can be named is not the eternal name.
author: Lao Tzu

text: The tao that can be told is not the eternal Tao. The name that can be named is not the eternal Name.
author: Lao Tzu

text: The tao that can be described is not the eternal Tao. The name that can be spoken is not the eternal Name.
author: Lao Tzu

text: Heaven and earth begin in the unnamed: name's the mother of the ten thousand things.
author: Lao Tzu

text: The mystery of mysteries is the gateway of marvels.
author: Lao Tzu

text: The Tao is like a well: used but never used up. It is like the eternal void: filled with infinite possibilities. It is hidden but always present. I don't know who gave birth to it. It is older than God .
author: Lao Tzu

text: It blunts the sharpness of the powerful, untangle the knot of the powerless; softens the glare of the noble, and stays with the humble.
author: Lao Tzu

text: The Tao is like a bellows: it is empty yet infinitely capable. The more you use it, the more it produces; the more you talk of it, the less you understand.
author: Lao Tzu

text: The Tao is called the Great Mother : empty yet inexhaustible, it gives birth to infinite worlds .
author: Lao Tzu

text: By never being an end in himself he endlessly becomes himself.
author: Lao Tzu

text: Thirty spokes unite at the single hub; It is the empty space which makes the wheel useful. Mold clay to form a bowl; It is the empty space which makes the bowl useful. Cut out windows and doors; It is the empty space which makes the room useful.
author: Lao Tzu

text: For to know what once there was, in the Beginning, This is called the essence of the Way.
author: Lao Tzu

text: But of a good leader, who talks little, when his work is done, his aims fulfilled, they will all say, "We did this ourselves."
author: Lao Tzu

text: Existing before time and space, the Tao is, beyond 'is' and 'is not'.
author: Lao Tzu

text: It is because he does not contend That no one in the world can contend against him.
author: Lao Tzu

text: A violent wind does not outlast the morning; a squall of rain does not outlast the day. Such is the course of Nature. And if Nature herself cannot sustain her efforts long, how much less can man!
author: Lao Tzu

text: If I am forced to give it a name, I call it Tao, and I name it as supreme.
author: Lao Tzu

text: This is called embodying the light.
author: Lao Tzu

text: Knowing others is intelligence ; knowing yourself is true wisdom . Mastering others is strength ; mastering yourself is true power .
author: Lao Tzu

text: Without the laughter , there would be no Tao.
author: Lao Tzu

text: He who knows that enough is enough will always have enough.
author: Lao Tzu

text: By letting it go it all gets done. The world is won by those who let it go. But when you try and try, the world is beyond the winning.
author: Lao Tzu

text: To attain knowledge, add things every day. To attain wisdom, remove things every day.
author: Lao Tzu

text: Block the passages, shut the doors, and your strength shall not fail. Open them, and no help shall come.
author: Lao Tzu

text: He who knows does not speak; he who speaks does not know.
author: Lao Tzu

text: The more laws, the poorer the people; the more order, the more thieves.
author: Lao Tzu
source: Variant translation: The more prohibitions there are, the poorer the people will be.

text: Governing a large country is like frying a small fish.
author: Lao Tzu

text: When men lack a sense of awe, there will be disaster.
author: Lao Tzu

text: People starved because the ruler taxed too heavily.
author: Lao Tzu

text: People are difficult to be ruled, Because the ruler governs with personal desire and establishes too many laws to confuse the people.
author: Lao Tzu

text: Let people return to simple knots, content with their food, clothing, and homes, living out their lives without desire for what lies beyond.
author: Lao Tzu

text: A moderate man is free from his own ideas, tolerant like the sky, and supple like a tree in the wind.
author: Lao Tzu
source: Ch. 59 as interpreted by Stephen Mitchell (1992)

text: Nothing that can be said in words is worth saying.
author: Lao Tzu

text: I am not at all interested in immortality, only in the taste of tea.
author: Lao Tzu
source: From Lu Tong (also spelled as Lu Tung)

text: Kindness in words creates confidence. Kindness in thinking creates profoundness. Kindness in giving creates love.
author: Lao Tzu
source: Attributed to Laozi in self-help books and on social media, this quotation is of unknown origin and date.

text: What I hear, I forget. What I say, I remember. What I do, I understand.
author: Lao Tzu
source: This quotation has also been misattributed to Confucius. Tell me and I [will] forget. Show me and I [will] remember. Involve me and I [will] understand. 不聞不若聞之，聞之不若見之，見之不若知之，知之不若行之；學至於行之而止矣 From Xun Zi 荀子

text: When the center does not hold, the circle falls apart.
author: Lao Tzu
source: This is a paraphrase of lines in " The Second Coming " by William Butler Yeats .

text: Life is a series of natural changes; don't resist them, for that only creates sorrow. Let things flow naturally.
author: Lao Tzu
source: This quotation's origin is actually unknown, however it is not found in the Dao De Jing. 生命是一连串的自发的自然变化。逆流而动只会徒增伤悲。接受现实，万物自然循着规律发展。

text: Care about people's approval and you will be their prisoner.
author: Lao Tzu
source: Also: "Care about what other people think and you will always be their prisoner"

text: When I am anxious it is because I am living in the future. When I am depressed it is because I am living in the past.
author: Lao Tzu
source: Attributed to "Jimmy R." in Days of Healing, Days of Joy (1987) [ 2 ]

text: Go to the people. Live with them. Learn from them. Love them. Start with what they know. Build with what they have. With the best leaders when the work is done, the task accomplished, the people will say, "We have done this ourselves."
author: Lao Tzu
source: Only the final bold section is connected to Laozi (see Ch. 17 of Tao Te Ching above). The origin of the added first section is unclear.

text: An ant on the move does more than a dozing ox
author: Lao Tzu
source: This is actually a Mexican proverb [ 3 ]

text: Water your dreams with worry and fear, and you will grow weeds; water them with optimism and solutions, and you will cultivate success.
author: Lao Tzu
source: This is actually a quote from a 2004 book by Aileen Miga and Janice Hughes called Inspired Wealth: Financial Leadership for the 21st Century , page 44 . The misattribution has been attested since at least 2006. [ 4 ]

text: Political leaders are never leaders. For leaders we have to look to the Awakeners! Lao Tse, Buddha, Socrates, Jesus, Milarepa, Gurdjiev, Krishnamurti.
author: Lao Tzu
source: Henry Miller , in My Bike & Other Friends (1977), p. 12

text: Truth is something so noble that if God could turn aside from it, I could keep the truth and let God go.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Meister Eckhart: A Modern Translation (1941) by Raymond Bernard Blakney, p. 240

text: To the quiet mind all things are possible.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: As translated in A Dazzling Darkness: An Anthology of Western Mysticism (1985) by Patrick Grant

text: God wants nothing of you but the gift of a peaceful heart .
author: Meister Eckhart
source: As translated in The Enlightened Mind: An Anthology of Sacred Prose (1991) edited by Stephen Mitchell , p. 115

text: To be full of things is to be empty of God; to be empty of things is to be full of God.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: As quoted in Men Who Have Walked with God (1992) by Sheldon Cheney, p. 198

text: We are all meant to be mothers of God , for God is always needing to be born .
author: Meister Eckhart
source: As quoted in Christianity (1995) by Joe Jenkins, p. 27

text: Knowledge is the key that unlocks the door, presses forward and breaks in, to discover God as he is.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Sermon 9, as translated in The Reading and Preaching of the Scriptures in the Worship of the Christian Church (1999) by Hughes Oliphant Old, Ch. 9: The German Mystics, p. 448

text: The soul is never at rest until it comes to the original idea, in which all things are one, and there it comes to rest in God.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Sermon 9, as translated in The Reading and Preaching of the Scriptures in the Worship of the Christian Church (1999) by Hughes Oliphant Old, Ch. 9: The German Mystics, p. 449

text: We shall find God in everything alike, and find God always alike in everything.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Quoted in Geary's Guide to the World's Great Aphorists (2007) by James Geary, p. 232

text: Only those who have dared to let go can dare to reenter.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Quoted in Geary's Guide to the World's Great Aphorists (2007) by James Geary, p. 232

text: God needs us as much as we need Him.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: God can dispense with us just as little as we can dispense with him. Sermon 49

text: Do not be content with a God merely thought; for when the thought fades, so too does that God.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: A person should never be satisfied with a thought God, for when the thought perishes, the God also perishes. Deutsche Predigten und Traktate (1963), p. 60

text: The more God is in all things, the more He is outside them. The more He is within, the more without.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Quoted by Aldous Huxley , in The Perennial Philosophy (1945)

text: God needs the sinner's help for conversion, for He converts thee not without thy help.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Sermon I : The Attractive Power of God

text: He knows God rightly who knows Him everywhere.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Sermon II : The Nearness of the Kingdom

text: Whoever thinks to escape God, flies into His bosom, for every corner is open to Him.
author: Meister Eckhart

text: Whoso will hear the wisdom of the Father must dwell deep, and abide at home, and be at unity with himself.
author: Meister Eckhart

text: All that the Eternal Father teaches and reveals is His being, His nature, and His Godhead , which He manifests to us in His Son, and teaches us that we are also His Son.
author: Meister Eckhart

text: If God told an angel to go to a tree and pluck caterpillars off it, the angel would be quite ready to do so, and it would be his happiness, if it were the will of God.
author: Meister Eckhart

text: The eye with which I see God is the same with which God sees me. My eye and God's eye is one eye, and one sight , and one knowledge , and one love .
author: Meister Eckhart

text: Now rejoice, all ye powers of my soul, that you are so united with God that no one may separate you from Him. I cannot fully praise nor love Him therefore must I die, and cast myself into the divine void, till I rise from non-existence to existence.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Sermon V : The Self-Communication of God

text: The Father and the Son have one Will, and that Will is the Holy Ghost, Who gives Himself to the soul so that the Divine Nature permeates the powers of the soul so that it can only do God-like works.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Sermon V : The Self-Communication of God

text: Nothing is sharper than suffering, nothing is sweeter than to have suffered.
author: Meister Eckhart

text: The peace, freedom and blessedness of all souls consist in their abiding in God's will.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Sermon VII : Outward and Inward Morality

text: As long as man loves something else than God, or outside God, he is not free, because he has not love.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Sermon VII : Outward and Inward Morality

text: When the Divine Light penetrates the soul, it is united with God as light with light.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Sermon VII : Outward and Inward Morality

text: Through this faculty the soul acquires such great confidence that she deems nothing in the Divine Nature beyond her reach.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Sermon VII : Outward and Inward Morality

text: As God can only be seen by His own light, so He can only be loved by His own love.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Sermon VII : Outward and Inward Morality

text: The aim of man is beyond the temporal — in the serene region of the everlasting Present.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Sermon VII : Outward and Inward Morality

text: Wouldst thou be free from all grief and trouble, abide and walk in God, and to God alone.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Sermon VII : Outward and Inward Morality

text: Through the higher love the whole life of man is to be elevated from temporal selfishness to the spring of all love, to God: man will again be master over nature by abiding in God and lifting her up to God.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Sermon VII : Outward and Inward Morality

text: If the only prayer you said in your whole life was, "Thank You," that would suffice.
author: Meister Eckhart

text: Variant: If "thank you" is the only prayer you can utter in your lifetime, that would be enough.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Very commonly attributed to Eckhart on the internet and some publications, the source of the first formulation however is: A Bucket of Surprises‎ (2002) by J. John and Mark Stibbe.

text: And suddenly you know: It's time to start something new and trust the magic of beginnings.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Widely circulated on the internet, but no actual text to tie it back to Eckhart, as of yet.

text: The knower and the known are one. God and I, we are one in knowledge.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Circulated on the Internet, this is an amended version of a quote from Eckhart's sermon iusti vivent in aeternum : There are simple people who imagine they are going to see God as if He were standing here and they there. This is not true. God and I are one.

text: God without them would not be God, for God is love, and must objectify Himself.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: William Ralph Inge , in Light, Life, and Love: Selections from the German Mystics of the Middle Ages (1904), p. xx

text: It is a harder and nobler task to preserve detachment in a crowd than in a cell.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: William Ralph Inge , in Light, Life, and Love: Selections from the German Mystics of the Middle Ages (1904), p. xxx; Inge quotes Eckhart quoting or paraphrasing Augustine of Hippo : "Love, and do as you will."

text: All that God asks you most pressingly is to go out of yourself and let God be God in you.
author: Meister Eckhart
source: Pope Saint John Paul II , Speech (in French) to participants in the Convention on Adrienne von Speyr (September 28, 1985)

text: Mindfulness must be engaged. Once there is seeing, there must be acting.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Peace is every step: The path of mindfulness in everyday life. (1991)

text: The trees, the water, and the air don't ask anything of us; they just love us. By destroying them, we are destroying ourselves.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Love in Action: Writings on Nonviolent Social Change (1993), p. 131

text: If you need to use force, use it out of compassion and a willingness to protect, not out of anger.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: When you understand the roots of anger in yourself and in the other, your mind will enjoy true peace, joy and lightness
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Teachings on Love (2005) Full Circle Publishing ISBN 81-7621-167-2

text: When anger arises, return to your breathing and follow it.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Teachings on Love (2005) Full Circle Publishing ISBN 81-7621-167-2

text: Your first love has no beginning or end. Your first love is not your first love, and it is not your last. It is just love. It is one with everything.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Cultivating the Mind of Love (2005) Full Circle Publishing ISBN 81-216-0676-4

text: The quality of our life depends on the quality of the seeds that lie deep in our consciousness.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Understanding Our Mind (2006) Parallax Press ISBN 978-81-7223-796-7

text: The present moment contains past and future. The secret of transformation, is in the way we handle this very moment.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Understanding Our Mind (2006) Parallax Press ISBN 978-81-7223-796-7

text: It is very difficult to go against the system. What must we do to prevent ourselves from being taken over by it?
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: One included all, and all were contained in one.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Old Path White Clouds : Walking in the Footsteps of the Buddha (1991) Parallax Press ISBN 81-216-0675-6

text: The leaf and his body were one. Neither possessed a separate permanent self. Neither could exist independently from the rest of the universe.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Old Path White Clouds : Walking in the Footsteps of the Buddha (1991)

text: Contemplating the bowl, it is possible to see the interdependent elements which give rise to the bowl.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Old Path White Clouds : Walking in the Footsteps of the Buddha (1991) Parallax Press ISBN 81-216-0675-6

text: Freedom from suffering is a great happiness.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Old Path White Clouds : Walking in the Footsteps of the Buddha (1991) Parallax Press ISBN 81-216-0675-6

text: The same clouds that Buddha had seen were in the sky. Each serene step brought to life the old path and white clouds of the Buddha. The path of Buddha was beneath his very feet.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Old Path White Clouds : Walking in the Footsteps of the Buddha (1991) Parallax Press ISBN 81-216-0675-6

text: Venerable Svasti and the young buffalo boys were rivers that flowed from that source. Wherever the rivers flowed, the Buddha would be there.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Old Path White Clouds : Walking in the Footsteps of the Buddha (1991) Parallax Press ISBN 81-216-0675-6

text: The source of a true smile is an awakened mind.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Peace Is Every Step : The Path of Mindfulness in Everyday Life (1992) Bantam reissue ISBN 0553351397

text: The miracle is not to walk on water. The miracle is to walk on the green earth in the present moment , to appreciate the peace and beauty that are available now.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Touching Peace (1992), p. 1. Parallax Press ISBN 0-938077-57-0

text: In order to rally people, governments need enemies. They want us to be afraid, to hate, so we will rally behind them. And if they do not have a real enemy, they will invent one in order to mobilize us.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
