source: Quoted in Engaged Buddhist Reader: Ten Years of Engaged Buddhist Publishing (1996) by Arnold Kotler, p. 106

text: The problem is to see reality as it is.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: Reality is reality. It transcends every concept. There is no concept which can adequately describe it, not even the concept of interdependence.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: Drink your tea slowly and reverently, as if it is the axis on which the whole earth revolves —slowly, evenly, without rushing toward the future. Live the actual moment. Only this actual moment is life.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: It's wonderful to be alive and to walk on earth.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: You are a miracle, and everything you touch could be a miracle.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Episode of the National Public Radio program Speaking of Faith : "Brother Thay: A Radio Pilgrimage with Thich Nhat Hanh" (2003)

text: If you look deeply into the palm of your hand, you will see your parents and all generations of your ancestors. All of them are alive in this moment. Each is present in your body. You are the continuation of each of these people.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Quoted in A Lifetime of Peace : Essential Writings by and About Thich Nhat Hanh (2003) edited by Jennifer Schwamm Willis, p. 141

text: It is with our capacity of smiling, breathing, and being peace that we can make peace.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: No one can take away your true home and your freedom.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: We will learn to help others let go of and transform fanaticism and narrowness through loving speech and compassionate dialogue.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: Going vegetarian may be the most effective way to fight global warming, nourishing compassion towards animals and protecting the earth.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: "Letter from Thay", Blue Cliff Monastery (12 October 2007)

text: Love is the capacity to take care, protect, and nourish; without self-care, it is difficult to care for another.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Quoted on his official website (30 December 2008)

text: Life is filled with suffering, but also with wonders; to suffer is not enough, we must also touch the wonders within and around us.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: If we are not peaceful, we cannot share peace; but if we are, we can smile and blossom like a flower, benefiting all of society.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: We are so busy we hardly have time to look at ourselves, constantly escaping from ourselves even in leisure.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: Meditation is awareness of life's dreadful and wonderful aspects; to meditate well, we must smile a lot.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: If we can smile, be peaceful and happy in our daily life, everyone will profit; this is the most basic kind of peace work.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: To remind ourselves to relax and be peaceful, set aside time for mindfulness: this is not a retreat, it is a treat.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: Practice smiling all day long; it means we are ourselves, sovereign, not drowned in forgetfulness, like Buddhas.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: Breathing in, I calm my body; breathing out, I smile. A smile makes you master of yourself, realizing its wonder.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: We tend to postpone being alive to the future; the only moment to be truly alive is the present moment, here and now.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: Even when life is hard, we must smile to our sorrow, for we are more than our sorrow, like a television with millions of channels.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: If you cling to your truth, you will not open the door when truth knocks. Understanding means letting go of knowledge to transcend.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: Forests are our lungs outside our bodies; yet, imprisoned in our small selves, we destroy them, thus destroying our large self.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: The sun is my heart outside this body; to be our true self is to be the river, the forest, a citizen of the world—the nondualistic way of seeing.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: During the Vietnam War, we understood the suffering of both sides, refusing to take a side, even when the world couldn't hear our plea for peace.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: The peace movement can write protest letters, but not yet love letters; we must learn to speak with understanding, not anger, so our message is heard.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: If you seek a Buddha statue, be choosy; it should be smiling, happy, beautiful. If not, have a flower instead, for a flower is a Buddha, possessing Buddha nature.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: To begin the day as a Buddha is a beautiful practice.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: When agitated, follow your breath into your inner Buddha Land.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: The quiet practice of mindfulness paints a picture more beautiful than any masterpiece.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: To sit and breathe, returning to our true self, is the best education for peace.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: Simple mindful practices can change our civilization.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: The sun is our second heart, our heart outside of our body.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: All phenomena are interdependent; unity is diversity, and diversity is unity.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: Forests are our lungs outside of our bodies; to save them is to save ourselves.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: Respect for all species reveals the noblest nature within us.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: The future of all life, including our own, depends on our mindful steps.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: The statement " The future of all life, including our own, depends on our mindful steps. " and much of the theme of this essay also occur later in his writings, including The World We Have : A Buddhist Approach to Peace and Ecology (2008), Ch. 1 : The Bells of Mindfulness, p. 3

text: Protect the ecology of Earth and mind, for by destroying one, we destroy ourselves.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: The best way to take care of the environment is to take care of the environmentalist.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: What we call a self is made only of non-self elements.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: Life is made of death, and death is made of life.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: When we realize our nature of interbeing, we stop blaming and killing.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Thích Nhất Hạnh here quotes and interprets the "Ten Penetrations" of the Avatamsaka Sutra

text: Discovering the true nature of interbeing through mindfulness brings peace and the strength to love and care endlessly.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: The real miracle is not walking on water, but walking on Earth.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source_section: The Miracle of Mindfulness: An Introduction to the Practice of Meditation (1999)

text: Breath is the bridge which connects life to consciousness, which unites your body to your thoughts.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source_section: The Miracle of Mindfulness: An Introduction to the Practice of Meditation (1999)

text: In mindfulness, all feelings and all things are equally sacred.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source_section: The Miracle of Mindfulness: An Introduction to the Practice of Meditation (1999)

text: When a sage is there and you sit near him, you feel light, you feel peace.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: bell hooks , Teaching to Transgress: Education as the Practice of Freedom (1994)

text: Our actions are our only true belongings.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Cherríe Moraga A Xicana Codex of Changing Consciousness (2011)

text: Let sadness be the mud for the lotus, transforming into love.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh
source: Ocean Vuong attributed in Not Too Late: Changing the Climate Story from Despair to Possibility edited by Rebecca Solnit and Thelma Young Lutunatabua (2023)

text: Love is the reason for my existence, my true identity, my selflessness, my character, my name.
author: Thomas Merton

text: It is when we love the other, the enemy, that we obtain from God the key to an understanding of who he is, and who we are.
author: Thomas Merton

text: A tree gives glory to God by being a tree.
author: Thomas Merton

text: The biggest human temptation is … to settle for too little.
author: Thomas Merton

text: The peace of Madhyamika, of sunyata, sees through every question without refutation, a silence that can frighten the doctrinaire mind.
author: Thomas Merton
source: The Asian Journal of Thomas Merton (1975) Part One : Ceylon / November 29 - December 6.

text: All problems are resolved, everything is clear; for all life is charged with dharmakaya, everything is emptiness and compassion.
author: Thomas Merton
source: The Asian Journal of Thomas Merton (1975) Part One : Ceylon / November 29 - December 6.

text: The things on the surface are nothing, what is deep is the Real. We are creatures of Love.
author: Thomas Merton

text: The whole idea of compassion is based on a keen awareness of the interdependence of all these living beings, which are all part of one another, and all involved in one another.
author: Thomas Merton
source: Statement from his final address, during a conference on East-West monastic dialogue, delivered just two hours before his death (10 December 1968), quoted in Religious Education , Vol. 73 (1978), p. 292, and in The Boundless Circle : Caring for Creatures and Creation (1996) by Michael W. Fox.

text: What we are asked to do is to love, and this love itself will render both ourselves and our neighbors worthy if anything can.
author: Thomas Merton
source: Letter to Dorothy Day , quoted in Catholic Voices in a World on Fire (2005) by Stephen Hand, p. 180.

text: They seem to have no idea that the affluence (which for them is the kingdom of God) has another side to it—the buried bodies of children in Vietnam and the Negro-Puerto Rican ghettos.
author: Thomas Merton
source: as cited in Follow the Ecstasy: The Hermitage Years of Thomas Merton by John Howard Griffin (1983), p. 97

text: If God is 'dead' in the West, it is due to an inner split and self-alienation, a single-minded dedication to only the exterior, objective, and quantitative.
author: Thomas Merton
source: about the Bhagavad Gita. Merton, Thomas Thoughts on the East I Thomas Merton; with an introduction by George Woodcock New York: New Directions, 1995 Quoted and attributed in Londhe, S. (2008). A tribute to Hinduism: Thoughts and wisdom spanning continents and time about India and her culture

text: The devil, no fool, makes men fear grace more than sin, blinding them to truth with obscurity, shadows, and psychosis.
author: Thomas Merton

text: Catholics have the audacity to complain God does not hear their prayers for peace, when they neglect His will and raise children in a civilization of hyenas.
author: Thomas Merton

text: The one who does most to avoid suffering is, in the end, the one who suffers the most.
author: Thomas Merton

text: Is there any man who has never fantasized about monastic solitude, only to be desired by those who once scorned him?
author: Thomas Merton

text: There is not a flower that opens, nor a seed that falls, that does not preach the greatness and mercy of God to the whole world.
author: Thomas Merton

text: To love the world—its flesh, eyes, and pride—is to cut oneself off from the charity that alone saves us from the barren wilderness of our own abominable selfishness.
author: Thomas Merton

text: Nowhere has there been such a flowering of cheap and petty and disgusting lusts and vanities as in the world of capitalism, where no evil is not fostered for the sake of making money.
author: Thomas Merton

text: Everybody makes fun of virtue , which by now has, as its primary meaning, an affectation of prudery practiced by hypocrites and the impotent.
author: Thomas Merton

text: God is our life; His will, our food, our very bread.
author: Thomas Merton

text: The way of faith is necessarily obscure; we drive by night, yet reason's light reveals enough of the road ahead.
author: Thomas Merton
source: Ch. X : Reason in the Life of Contemplation, p. 114.

text: We are not meant to resolve all contradictions, but to live with them and rise above them.
author: Thomas Merton

text: I have no idea where I am going, but I trust you will lead me, for you are ever with me.
author: Thomas Merton

text: The logic of mathematics achieves certainty by flight from the concrete; the logic of the poet grows like a living organism, free and individual.
author: Thomas Merton
source_section: The Secular Journal of Thomas Merton (1959)

text: Hope of attaining true freedom by purely political means has become an insane delusion.
author: Thomas Merton

text: Chuang Tzu seeks to go immediately to the heart of things, grasping reality directly, beyond words and abstract analysis.
author: Thomas Merton

text: Ecclesiastes and the Gospels, with their teachings on simplicity and humility, resonate deeply with the Taoist classics.
author: Thomas Merton

text: To lose one's life is to save it; the way is mysterious because it is so simple, found when you leave all ways and get lost.
author: Thomas Merton

text: The secret of Chuang Tzu's way is wu wei, non-action not intent on results, but spontaneous harmony with Tao, the source of all good.
author: Thomas Merton

text: It is a glorious destiny to be human; God Himself gloried in becoming a member of the human race.
author: Thomas Merton

text: My monastic life is a 'No' to all concentration camps, injustices, and the socio-economic apparatus geared for global destruction.
author: Thomas Merton
source_section: Choosing to Love the World: On Contemplation

text: My monastic silence protests the lies of politicians; my life, the injustices invoked even by those who share my faith.
author: Thomas Merton
source_section: Choosing to Love the World: On Contemplation

text: Life is not a problem to be solved, but a mystery to be lived.
author: Thomas Merton
source: Attributed to Merton in a number of sources, the earliest located being Studia mystica, Volumes 5-6 (1982), p. 76 . This does not attribute a direct quote to Merton, but says "To use another of Merton's favorite distinctions, for Furlong Merton's life is seen principally as a problem to be solved, which it was, in the final analysis, successfully, rather than a mystery to be lived". The next-earliest source located is the 1998 book The Artist's Way at Work: Riding the Dragon by Mark Bryan and Julia Cameron , which attributes the exact quote to Merton on p. 152 . In reality this seems to be a slightly altered version of the quote "The mystery of life is not a problem to be solved; it is a reality to be experienced" which appeared in the 1928 book The Conquest of Illusion by Jacobus Johannes Leeuw, p. 9 .

text: At life's precipice, subjectivity shatters; uncontrollable forces seethe within, unbound by personal rhythm.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Suffering is arbitrary, unjust, and utterly without justification or hierarchy.
author: Emil Cioran

text: The sufferer believes in a monopoly on pain, yet suffering's true nature is an incomprehensible abyss.
author: Emil Cioran

text: maybe suffering has no more justification than life.
author: Emil Cioran

text: No one commits suicide for external reasons, only because of inner disequilibrium. Under similar adverse circumstances, some are indifferent, some are moved, some are driven to suicide.
author: Emil Cioran

text: I admire only two types of people: the potentially mad and the potential suicide.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Why don't I commit suicide? Because I am as sick of death as I am of life.
author: Emil Cioran

text: I don't need any support, advice, or compassion, because even if I am the most ruinous man, I still feel so powerful, so strong and fierce. For I am the only one that lives without hope.
author: Emil Cioran

text: There are questions which, once approached, either isolate you or kill you outright.
author: Emil Cioran

text: One of the greatest delusions of the average man is to forget that life is death's prisoner.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Agony is infinitely more important than syllogism, and tears have deeper roots than smiles.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Knowledge is the plague of life, and consciousness, an open wound in its heart.
author: Emil Cioran

text: If there was a God of sorrow, he would grow black heavy wings, to soar not for the skies, but for inferno.
author: Emil Cioran

text: What we want is not freedom but its appearances. It is for these simulacra that man has always striven. And since freedom, as has been said, is no more than a sensation, what difference is there between being free and believing ourselves free?
author: Emil Cioran

text: What is there to be gained from this world?
author: Emil Cioran

text: We are so lonely in life that we must ask ourselves if the loneliness of dying is not a symbol of our human existence.
author: Emil Cioran

text: If I were to be totally sincere, I would say that I do not know why I live and why I do not stop living. The answer probably lies in the irrational character of life which maintains itself without reason.
author: Emil Cioran

text: True confessions are written with tears only. But my tears would drown the world, as my inner fire would reduce it to ashes.
author: Emil Cioran

text: I am absolutely persuaded that I am nothing in this universe; yet I feel that mine is the only real existence.
author: Emil Cioran

text: I would like to go mad on one condition, namely, that I would become a happy madman, lively and always in a good mood, without any troubles and obsessions, laughing senselessly from morning to night.
author: Emil Cioran

text: One would welcome chaos if one were not afraid of lights in it.
author: Emil Cioran

text: I cannot contribute anything to this world because I only have one method: agony.
author: Emil Cioran

text: True thinking resembles a demon who muddies the spring of life or a sickness which corrupts its roots.
author: Emil Cioran

text: The deepest and most organic death is death in solitude, when even light becomes a principle of death.
author: Emil Cioran

text: How I wish I didn't know anything about myself and this world!
author: Emil Cioran

text: I live because the mountains do not laugh and the worms do not sing.
author: Emil Cioran

text: How good would it be if one could die by throwing oneself into an infinite void.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Sadness accompanies all events where life expends itself; its intensity equals its loss.
author: Emil Cioran

text: To tell the truth, I couldn't care less about the relativity of knowledge, simply because the world does not deserve to be known.
author: Emil Cioran

text: We are fulfilled only when we aspire to nothing, when we are impregnated by that nothing to the point of intoxication.
author: Emil Cioran

text: I like thought which preserves a whiff of flesh and blood, and I prefer a thousand times an idea rising from sexual tension or nervous depression to empty abstraction.
author: Emil Cioran

text: One of the biggest paradoxes of our world: memories vanish when we want to remember, but fix themselves permanently in the mind when we want to forget.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Compared to the refined culture of sclerotic forms and frames, which mask everything, the lyrical mode is utterly barbarian in its expression. Its value resides precisely in its savage quality: it is only blood, sincerity, and fire.
author: Emil Cioran

text: The terrifying experience and obsession of death, when preserved in consciousness, becomes ruinous.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Out of the shadow of the abstract man, who thinks for the pleasure of thinking, emerges the organic man, who thinks because of a vital imbalance, and who is beyond science and art.
author: Emil Cioran

text: I am displeased with everything. If they made me God , I would immediately resign.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Everything is both real and unreal, normal and absurd, splendid and insipid.
author: Emil Cioran

text: As far as I am concerned, I resign from humanity.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Can it really be that for us existence means exile, and nothingness, home?
author: Emil Cioran

text: I have no ideas, only obsessions. Anybody can have ideas. Ideas have never caused anybody’s downfall.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Eros alone can fulfill life; knowledge, never. Only Eros makes sense; knowledge is empty infinity.
author: Emil Cioran

text: All the concessions we make to Eros are holes in our desire for the absolute.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Everyone must destroy their life. According to the way they do it, they're either triumphants or failures.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Thought cannot solve the contradictions of the soul.
author: Emil Cioran

text: A regret understood by no one: the regret to be a pessimist.
author: Emil Cioran

text: To suffer is the great modality of taking the world seriously.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Nostalgia, more than anything, gives us the shudder of our own imperfection.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Time is heavy sometimes; imagine how heavy eternity must be.
author: Emil Cioran

text: I don't understand how people can believe in God, even when I myself think of him everyday.
author: Emil Cioran

text: The fear of your own solitude, of its vast surface and its infinity... Remorse is the voice of solitude. And what does this whispering voice say? Everything in us that is not human anymore.
author: Emil Cioran

text: All philosophers should end their days at Pythia 's feet. There is only one philosophy, that of unique moments .
author: Emil Cioran

text: That fear which gives birth to thoughts, and the fear of thoughts...
author: Emil Cioran

text: Detachment from the world as an attachment to the ego... Who can realize the detachment in which you are as far away from yourself as you are from the world?
author: Emil Cioran

text: Only thoughts that are randomly born die. The other thoughts we carry with us without knowing them. They have abandoned themselves to forgetfulness so that they can be with us all the time.
author: Emil Cioran

text: What am I, other than a chance in the infinite probabilities of not having been!
author: Emil Cioran

text: This world was created from God's fear of solitude.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Saints live in flames; wise men, next to them.
author: Emil Cioran

text: A heart without music is like beauty without melancholy.
author: Emil Cioran

text: As long as one believes in philosophy, one is healthy; sickness begins when one starts to think.
author: Emil Cioran

text: ...all of the philosophers put together are not worth a single saint.
author: Emil Cioran

text: To live in a saint's heart? I'm afraid of setting the sky ablaze.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Music is everything. God himself is nothing more than an acoustic hallucination.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Life is too full of death for death to be able to add anything to it.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Devour books — these sleeping pills — not to know but to forget!
author: Emil Cioran

text: Good health is the best weapon against religion.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Life is nothing but another word for the fear of death.
author: Emil Cioran

text: God and saints are a means to escape the dull banality of truth.
author: Emil Cioran

text: We are converted to religion out of fear of suffocating within the narrow confines of this world.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Since nothing has real substance, and life is a twirl in the void, its beginning and its end are meaningless.
author: Emil Cioran

text: The only interesting philosophers are the ones who have stopped thinking and have begun to search for happiness.
author: Emil Cioran

text: It is less than one step from nothing to God, for God is the positive expression of nothingness.
author: Emil Cioran

text: There are no solutions, only cowardice masquerading as such.
author: Emil Cioran

text: It's not possible to be conscious of divinity without guilt.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Saints, criminals, and paupers have launched God, making him available to all unhappy people.
author: Emil Cioran

text: The poor maidservant who used to say that she only believed in God when she had a toothache puts all theologians to shame.
author: Emil Cioran

text: To win the guilty kiss of a saint, I'd welcome the plague as a blessing
author: Emil Cioran

text: All that is Life in me urges me to give up God.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Self-conscious rejection of the absolute is the best way to resist God.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Love of the absolute engenders a predilection for self-destruction.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Tell me how you want to die, and I'll tell you who you are.
author: Emil Cioran

text: As long as I live I shall not allow myself to forget that I shall die; I am waiting for death so that I can forget about it.
author: Emil Cioran

text: From the cradle to the grave, each individual pays for the sin of not being God. That's why life is an uninterrupted religious crisis, superficial for believers, shattering for doubters.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Life is not, and death is a dream. Suffering has invented them both as self-justification. Man alone is torn between an unreality and an illusion.
author: Emil Cioran

text: The devil pales beside the man who owns a truth, his truth.
author: Emil Cioran

text: A human being possessed by a belief and not eager to pass it on to others is a phenomenon alien to the earth, where our mania for salvation makes life unbreathable.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Far from diminishing the appetite for power, suffering exasperates it; hence the mind feels more comfortable in the society of a braggart than in that of a martyr; and nothing is more repugnant to it than the spectacle of dying for an idea.
author: Emil Cioran

text: In every man sleeps a prophet, and when he wakes there is a little more evil in the world
author: Emil Cioran

text: But to live is to blind ourselves to our own dimensions.
author: Emil Cioran

text: By dint of accumulating non-mysteries and monopolizing non-meanings, life inspires more dread than death: it is life which is the Great Unknown.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Exiled in light, we flee from tears—just one of which would be enough to wash us out of time.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Life creates itself in delirium and is undone in ennui.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Born in a prison, with burdens on our shoulders and our thoughts, we could not reach the end of a single day if the possibilities of ending it all did not incite us to begin the next day all over again.
author: Emil Cioran

text: No one has the audacity to exclaim: "I don't want to do anything!" — we are more indulgent with a murderer than with a mind emancipated from actions.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Nothing surpasses the pleasures of idleness: even if the end of the world were to come, I would not leave my bed at an ungodly hour.
author: Emil Cioran

text: We replace God as best we can; for every god is good, provided he perpetuates in eternity our desire for a crucial solitude. . . .
author: Emil Cioran

text: Thought is as much a lie as love or faith.
author: Emil Cioran

text: Ideas should be neutral. But man animates them with his passions and folly. Impure and turned into beliefs, they take on the appearance of reality. The passage from logic is consummated. Thus are born ideologies, doctrines, and bloody farce.
author: Emil Cioran

text: The true hero fights and dies in the name of his destiny, and not in the name of a belief.
author: Emil Cioran

text: I wish that every human life might be pure transparent freedom.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: The Blood of Others [ Le sang des autres ] (1946)

text: The Communists , following Hegel , speak of humanity and its future as of some monolithic individuality. I was attacking this illusion.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: On her work All Men are Mortal in Force of Circumstances (1963), p. 73

text: All choices, agreements, and refusals should be made independently of institutions, conventions, and motives of self-aggrandizement.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Force of Circumstances Vol. III (1963) as translated by Richard Howard (1968) - Excerpt online

text: Self-knowledge is no guarantee of happiness, but it is on the side of happiness and can supply the courage to fight for it.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Force of Circumstances Vol. III (1963) as translated by Richard Howard (1968)

text: For man, death is always an accident, an undue violence, for his presence calls the world into question.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: There is no such thing as a natural death : nothing that happens to a man is ever natural, since his presence calls the world into question. All men must die; but for every man his death is an accident and even if he knows it and consents to it, an unjustifiable violation.

text: It's frightening to think that you mark your children merely by being yourself... It seems unfair. You can't assume the responsibility for everything you do — or don't do.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: What is an adult? A child puffed with age.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: The Woman Destroyed [ La Femme rompue ] (1967)

text: I tore myself away from the safe comfort of certainties through my love for truth — and truth rewarded me.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: All Said and Done (1972), p. 16 ISBN 1569249814

text: No woman should be authorized to stay at home to raise her children. Society should be totally different. Women should not have that choice, precisely because if there is such a choice, too many women will make that one. It is a way of forcing women in a certain direction.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: “A Dialogue with Simone de Beauvoir,” in Betty Friedan , It Changed My Life: Writings on the Women’s Movement , (New York: Random House, 1976), p. 397 .

text: We were two of a kind, and our relationship would endure as long as we did: but it could not make up entirely for the fleeting riches to be had from encounters with different people.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: On her relationship with Jean-Paul Sartre , as quoted in "Did Simone de Beauvoir's open 'marriage' make her happy?" by Lisa Appignanesi , in The Guardian (9 June 2005) and her book Simone de Beauvoir (2005), p. 36, ISBN 1904950094

text: I don't want to be just another blade of grass.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: If only there were two of me: one living and one watching, how I would love myself. I'd envy no one.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: If I had amnesia, I'd be almost like other men. Perhaps I'd even be able to love you.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: I'm never afraid. But in my case it's nothing to be proud of.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: He walks in the street, a picture of modesty in his felt hat and his gabardine suit, and all the while he's thinking, 'I'm immortal.' The world is his, time is his, and I'm nothing but an insect.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: One day I'll be old, dead, forgotten. And at this very moment, while I'm sitting here thinking these things, a man in a dingy hotel room is thinking, "I will always be here."
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: He had not applauded, he had remained seated, but he had looked at her steadily. From the depths of eternity he had looked at her and Rosalind became immortal. If I could believe him, she thought, if only I could believe him!
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: They were walking side by side, but each was alone.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: The fallen enemy rose again, the dead came back to life.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: That day, I would have died with a smile on my lips, certain of having contributed to a triumphant future for my city.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: It was as though some stubborn god spent their time in an immutable and absurd balancing act between life and death, prosperity and poverty.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: There is only one good. And that is to act according to the dictates of one's conscience.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: And it isn't anywhere: there are only men, men eternally divided.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: What has value in their eyes is never what is done for them; it's what they do for themselves.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: It is impossible to do anything for anyone.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: But had we advanced even a step nearer to the mysterious heart of the universe?
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: True generosity: to give all, yet feel no cost.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: That's what I consider true generosity. You give your all, and yet you always feel as if it costs you nothing.

text: If you live long enough, you'll see that every victory turns into a defeat.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: After wars peace, after peace, another war. Every day men are born and others die.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: Try to stay a man amongst men … There's no other hope for you.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: In horror, in terror, she accepted the metamorphosis — gnat, foam, ant, until death.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: Every female human being is not necessarily a woman; to be so considered she must share in that mysterious and threatened reality known as femininity.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: Inferiority is not a static state, but a dynamic 'to have become,' shaped by circumstance.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: The present enshrines the past—and in the past all history has been made by men.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: It is doubtless impossible to approach any human problems with a mind free from bias.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: One is not born a genius, one becomes a genius; and the feminine situation has up to the present rendered this becoming practically impossible.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Bk. I, Pt. 2, Ch. 8: Since the French Revolution: the Job and the Vote, p. 133

text: Sex pleasure in woman, as I have said, is a kind of magic spell; it demands complete abandon; if words or movements oppose the magic of caresses, the spell is broken.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Bk. 2, Pt.. 4, Ch. 3: Sexual Initiation. p. 396

text: To serve is to give oneself a master; there is no reciprocity in this relation.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Bk. 2, Pt.. 4, Ch. 3: Sexual Initiation. P. 418 (1974 Vintage edition)

text: She is no longer free to dream, to delay, to maneuver: she is in his power, at his disposal.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Bk. 2, Pt.. 3, Ch. 3: Sexual initiation. p. 427 (1974 Vintage edition)

text: Woman is an existent who is called upon to make herself object; as subject she has an aggressive element in her sensuality which is not satisfied on the male body: hence the conflicts that her eroticism must somehow overcome.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Bk. 2, Pt.. 3, Ch. 4: The Lesbian. P. 445 (1974 Vintage edition)

text: To "catch" a husband is an art; to "hold" him is a job.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Bk. 2, part 5, Ch. 1: The Married Woman, p. 468

text: Cooking is revelation and creation; and a woman can find special satisfaction in a successful cake or a flaky pastry, for not every one can do it: one must have the gift.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Bk. 2, part 5, Ch. 1: The Married Woman, p. 506

text: The curse which lies upon marriage is that too often the individuals are joined in their weakness rather than in their strength, each asking from the other instead of finding pleasure in giving.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Bk. 2, Pt.. 5, Ch. 2: The Mother, p. 522

text: Instead of wanting to imprison man, woman tries to escape; she no longer seeks to drag him into the regions of immanence but to emerge into the light of transcendence.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: All oppression creates a state of war . And this is no exception. The existent who is regarded as inessential cannot fail to demand the re-establishment of her sovereignty. Today the combat takes a different shape; instead of wishing to put man in a prison, woman endeavours to escape from one; she no longer seeks to drag him into the realms of immanence but to emerge, herself, into the light of transcendence.

text: The truth is that if the vicious circle is so hard to break, it is because the two sexes are each the victim at once of the other and of itself.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: A change in woman’s economic condition alone is not enough to transform her; the new woman cannot appear until it brings about moral, social, and cultural consequences.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: In both sexes is played out the same drama of finitude and transcendence; they have the same essential need for one another, and can gain from their liberty the same glory.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: New relations of flesh and sentiment, unconceived by past centuries, will arise between the sexes.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: When we abolish the slavery of half of humanity, then the 'division' of humanity will reveal its genuine significance and the human couple will find its true form.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: To gain the supreme victory, men and women must unequivocally affirm their brotherhood through their natural differentiation.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: Work almost always has a double aspect: it is a bondage, a wearisome drudgery; but it is also a source of interest, a steadying element, a factor that helps to integrate the worker with society.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Pt I, Ch. 4: Old age in present-day society, p. 263

text: Since it is the Other within us who is old, it is natural that the revelation of our age should come to us from outside — from others. We do not accept it willingly.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Pt. 2, Ch. 1: The discovery and assumption of old age: the body's experience, p. 288

text: I am incapable of conceiving infinity, and yet I do not accept finity. I want this adventure that is the context of my life to go on without end.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Pt. 2, Ch. 2: Time, activity, history, p. 412

text: It is old age, rather than death, that is to be contrasted with life. Old age is life's parody, whereas death transforms life into a destiny: in a way it preserves it by giving it the absolute dimension. Death does away with time.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: One's life has value so long as one attributes value to the life of others, by means of love, friendship , indignation and compassion .
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: Society turns away from the aged worker as though he belonged to another species.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: Society cares about the individual only in so far as he is profitable. The young know this. Their anxiety as they enter in upon social life matches the anguish of the old as they are excluded from it.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: In itself, homosexuality is as limiting as heterosexuality: the ideal should be to be capable of loving a woman or a man; either, a human being, without feeling fear, restraint, or obligation.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: As quoted in Bisexual Characters in Film: From Anaïs to Zee (1997) by Wayne M. Bryant, p. 143

text: Defending the truth is not something one does out of a sense of duty or to allay guilt complexes, but is a reward in itself.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: As quoted in The Book of Positive Quotations (2007) by John Cook, Steve Deger and Leslie Ann Gibson, p. 525

text: Change your life today. Don't gamble on the future, act now, without delay.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: As quoted in The Book of Positive Quotations (2007) by John Cook, p. 548

text: Each of us is responsible for everything and to every human being.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Fyodor Dostoevsky in The Brothers Karamazov ; this was used as an epigraph in The Blood of Others , and is sometimes attributed to de Beauvoir

text: A woman is created, not born.
author: Simone de Beauvoir

text: Simone de Beauvoir once said: "It is in the knowledge of the genuine conditions of our lives that we must draw our strength to live and our reasons for acting."
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Audre Lorde , "The Master's Tools Will Never Dismantle the Master's House" (1984)

text: The Second Sex may well be the most important work of this century.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Adrienne Rich in Blood, Bread, and Poetry: Selected Prose (1986)

text: You are a great charmer, and this is due to your sincerity. It is a rare quality to charm without meaning to.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Marie Vieux-Chauvet , 1969 letter. Translated from the French in "The Letters of Marie Chauvet and Simone de Beauvoir: A Critical Introduction" Regine Isabelle Joseph (2015)

text: The Second Sex remains the most cogent and thorough book of feminist theory yet written.
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Ellen Willis "Simone de Beauvoir: Rebel Girl" (May 27, 1986)

text: 'He is trying to communicate an experience whose distinguishing characteristic is, nevertheless its will to remain incommunicable.'
author: Simone de Beauvoir
source: Colin Wilson in The Origins of the Sexual Impulse , p. 90 (1963)

text: Imagination is not an empirical or superadded power of consciousness, it is the whole of consciousness as it realizes its freedom .
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: There would be for him no Good or Evil unless he thought them into being.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: L'âge de raison ( The Age of Reason ) (1945)

text: All the aids whereby a man may savour, minute by minute, like a connoisseur, the failure of a life.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: L'âge de raison ( The Age of Reason ) (1945)

text: Did you think that when those heads that our fathers had forcibly bowed down to the ground were raised again, you would find adoration in their eyes?
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: "Orphée Noir (Black Orpheus)" preface, Anthologie de la Nouvelle Poésie Nègre et Malgache (1948)

text: In every age, history chooses a nation, a race, a class to take up the torch by creating situations that can be expressed or transcended only through poetry.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Fascism is not defined by the number of its victims, but by the way it kills them.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: "On the Execution of Julius and Ethel Rosenberg ," Libération (22 June 1953)

text: Our responsibility is much greater than we might have supposed, because it involves all mankind.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: To choose this or that is to affirm at the same time the value of what we choose, because we can never choose evil. We always choose the good , and nothing can be good for us without being good for all.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: If literature isn't everything, it's not worth a single hour of someone's trouble.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: Interview (1960), Quoted in Susan Sontag 's introduction to Barthes : Selected Writings , "Writing Itself: On Roland Barthes," (1982)

text: A writer who takes political, social or literary positions must act only with the means that are his. These means are the written words.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: Refusing the Nobel Prize , New York Times (22 October 1964)

text: What I see is teeming cohesion, contained dispersal.... For him, to sculpt is to take the fat off space.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: On Alberto Giacometti 's work, Situations , in Braziller (1965)

text: She believed in nothing; only her skepticism kept her from being an atheist.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: The Words (1964), speaking of his grandmother.

text: I hate victims who respect their executioners.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: Loser Wins (Les Séquestrés d'Altona: A Play in Five Acts) (1960)

text: Che Guevara was the most complete human of our age: an intellectual, a fighter, a theoretician who forged revolution from battle.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: As quoted in Marianne Alexandre (ed.), !Viva Che!: Contributions in Tribute to Ernesto 'Che' Guevara (1968)

text: Living alone, the plausible vanishes with your friends; you become a terrible witness to a world you can no longer narrate.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: People who live in society have learned how to see themselves in mirrors as they appear to their friends. I have no friends. Is that why my flesh is so naked?
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: I think they do it to pass the time, nothing more. But time is too large, it can't be filled up. Everything you plunge into it is stretched and disintegrates.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: Diary entry of Friday (2 February), concerning a card game

text: The past eludes me; I find only vague feelings and scraps of images, unsure if they are memories or mere fiction.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: We imagine the hero lived only promises, blind to all but adventure; yet he walked without forethought, a night of choices unmade.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: Diary entry of Saturday noon (10 February?)

text: I exist. It is soft, so soft, so slow. And light: it seems as though it suspends in the air. It moves.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Three o'clock is always too late or too early for anything you want to do.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: My thought is me; thus, I cannot cease. I exist because I think, and I cannot stop thinking.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: My thought is me : that's why I can't stop. I exist because I think ... and I can't prevent myself from thinking.

text: Monsieur ... I do not believe in God; his existence has been disproved by Science. But in the concentration camp, I learned to believe in men.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: I wanted for the moments in my life to follow each other and order themselves like those of a life remembered. It would be just as well to try to catch time by the tail.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: As if there could be true stories: things happen in one way, and we retell them in the opposite way.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: I construct my memories with my present. I am lost, abandoned in the present. I try in vain to rejoin the past: I cannot escape.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: The real nature of the present revealed itself: it was what exists, all that was not present did not exist.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Who can exhaust a man? Who knows a man's resources?
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: For an occurrence to become an adventure, it is necessary and sufficient for one to recount it.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Jazz notes know no rest; an inflexible order births and destroys them. To seize one would yield only a corrupt, languishing sound; I must accept their death.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: All that I know about my life, it seems, I have learned in books.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: The world of explanations and reasons is not the world of existence; this root existed in a way I could not explain.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: Reflections on a chestnut tree root.

text: How can I, who was not able to retain my own past, hope to save that of another?
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: I exist, that is all, and I find it nauseating.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: To love, one must jump across a precipice; I know I'll never jump again.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: I seize every fleeting second, fixing it forever within me, yet I like to see it pass.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Every existent is born without reason, endures through weakness, and dies by chance.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: Every existing thing is born without reason , prolongs itself out of weakness and dies by chance .

text: Generosity is nothing else than a craze to possess; to give is to enjoy possessively the object one gives.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: I am responsible for everything, compelled to bear this burden, abandoned in a world from which I cannot escape.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: To eat is to appropriate by destruction.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: To be recognized by the Other, I must risk my life, revealing myself as not-bound to mere existence.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Existence precedes and commands essence.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Man is a useless passion.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: All human activities are equivalent ... and ... all are on principle doomed to failure.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Man is condemned to be free; because once thrown into the world, he is responsible for everything he does.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: It is certain that we cannot escape anguish, for we are anguish.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: The For-itself, in fact, is nothing but the pure nihilation of the In-itself; it is like a hole of being at the heart of Being.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Man is always separated from what he is by the breadth of what he is not, forever seeking to recover his inner being.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: There is no irreducible taste or inclination; all represent an appropriative choice of being.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Your crime will be there, always dragging itself behind you, for you have committed your life with one throw of the die, and must tug it along until death.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: Clytemnestra to her daughter Electra, Act 1

text: Be quiet! Anyone can spit in my face, and call me a criminal and a prostitute. But no one has the right to judge my remorse.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Fear? If I have gained anything by damning myself, it is that I no longer have anything to fear.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Admit it, it is your youth that you regret, more even than your crime ; it is my youth you hate, even more than my innocence .
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: Electra to her mother Clytemnestra, Act 1

text: Some men are born committed to action: they do not have a choice, they have been thrown on a path, at the end of that path, an act awaits them, their act.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: They are in bad faith — they are afraid — and fear , bad faith have an aroma that the gods find delicious. Yes, the gods like that, the pitiful souls.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Ah! Do not judge the gods, young man, they have painful secrets.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Yes, I am so free. And what a superb absence is my soul.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: You must be afraid, my son. That is how one becomes an honest citizen.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: Mother to her young son, Act 1

text: Her face seems ravaged by both lightning and hail. But on yours there is something like the promise of a storm: one day passion will burn it to the bone.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: I felt less alone when I didn't know you yet: I was waiting for the other. I thought only of his strength and never of my weakness. And now here you are, Orestes, it was you. I look at you and I see that we are two orphans.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: Electra to her brother Orestes, Act 2

text: A man who is free is like a mangy sheep in a herd. He will contaminate my entire kingdom and ruin my work.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Her soul is a scorching noon time, without a single breath of a breeze, nothing moves, nothing changes, nothing lives there; a great emaciated sun, an immobile sun eternally consumes her.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: What do I care about Jupiter? Justice is a human issue, and I do not need a god to teach it to me.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: All-powerful god, who am I but the fear that I inspire in others?
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Blood doubly unites us, for we share the same blood and we have spilled blood.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Suppose that I wish to deserve the title of "robber of remorse" and that I place in myself all [the townspeople's] repentence?
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: But, if it will help ease your irritated souls, please know, dearly departed, that you have ruined our lives.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: For the sake of order, I seduced Clytemnestra and killed my king; I lived without desire, love, or hope, for I made order: Oh, terrible and divine passion!
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: I wish to be a man from somewhere, among men, to pull the city around me and roll myself up in it like a blanket, for I will not leave.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: I have no need for good souls : an accomplice is what I wanted.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: Electra to her brother Orestes, Act 2

text: He is dead, and my hatred has died with him.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: Electra, before the dead Aegistheus, Act 2

text: Ah! How I hate the crimes of the new generation: they are dry and sterile as darnel.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: The bane of gods and kings is the bitterness of knowing men are free, a truth your subjects ignore, but you do not.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: Act II, tableau II, scene 5, as translated by Stuart Gilbert (1946)

text: Once liberty explodes in a man's soul, the Gods are powerless; only other men can deal with him.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Now I am weary and I can no longer tell good from Evil , and I need someone to show me the way.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: I gave you the liberty to serve me, but it has turned against you, and neither of us can do anything about it.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: I take all your sins, remorse, and mighty anguish upon myself; fear your dead no more, for they are my dead.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Your liberty is nothing but a mange eating away at you, nothing but an exile.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Our feet now press down in the earth like cartwheels in their groove, and we will walk heavily, bending under our heavy load.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Your entire universe will not be enough to make me guilty. You are the king of the Gods, but not the king of men.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: You created me, but you should not have created me free.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: I am a man, Jupiter, and each man must invent his own path.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: You played murder, because it is a game that one can play alone.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Man must create his own essence: it is in throwing himself into the world, suffering and struggling, that he defines himself.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Man cannot will unless he understands he must count on no one but himself; alone, abandoned on earth with infinite responsibilities, he forges his own aim and destiny.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: True optimism begins with despair: the optimism of the man who expects nothing, who rejoices in counting on himself alone and acting for the good of all.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: ...man first of all exists, encounters himself, surges up in the world – and defines himself afterwards.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Better one hundred bites, better the whip, vitriol, than this suffering in the head, this ghost of suffering which grazes and caresses and never hurts enough.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Criminals together. We're in hell, my little friend, and there's never any mistake there. People are not damned for nothing.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: So this is hell. I never would have believed... No need for a grill, hell is other people.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: So that is what hell is. I would never have believed it. You remember: the fire and brimstone, the torture. Ah! the farce. There is no need for torture: Hell is other people .

text: I am no longer anything but a skin, and my skin does not belong to you.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: It is better; heavier, crueler. The mouth you wear for hell.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: Inès to Estelle after she has applied lipstick, Act 1, sc. 5

text: I need the suffering of others to exist; when I am all alone, I am extinguished.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre
source: Inès, describing her path to Hell, Act 1, sc. 5

text: You have stolen my face from me: you know it and I no longer do.
author: Jean-Paul Sartre

text: Use spoils and even destroys all beauty. The noblest function of objects is to be contemplated.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source: Use harms and even destroys beauty . The noblest function of an object is to be contemplated.

text: Is not the whole liturgy, of all religions, only a way perhaps of soothing God in His dreams, so that He shall not wake and cease to dream us?
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Speech was made for lying.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Speech was invented for the purpose of magnifying all of our sensations and impressions — perhaps so that we could believe in them.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: There are pretenses which are very sincere , and marriage is their school .
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source: Tres novelas ejemplares y un prólogo [ Three Exemplary Novels and a Prologue ] (1920); Two Mothers

text: Every peasant has a lawyer inside of him, just as every lawyer, no matter how urbane he may be, carries a peasant within himself.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: It is sad not to be loved, but it is much sadder not to be able to love .
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: These terrible sociologists, who are the astrologers and alchemists of our twentieth century.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: We never know, believe me, when we have succeeded best.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: There is no tyranny in the world more hateful than that of ideas.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source: Recalled by Walter Starkie from a conversation he had with Unamuno, as related in the Epilogue of Unamuno .

text: Only the feeble resign themselves to final death and substitute some other desire for the longing for personal immortality. In the strong the zeal for perpetuity overrides the doubt of realizing it, and their superabundance of life overflows upon the other side of death.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Our spiritual life is simply the effort of memory to persist, transforming our past into our future.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: I : The Man of Flesh and Bone

text: Nothing appeared to me quite so horrible as nothingness itself; it was a furious hunger of being that possessed me.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: I : The Man of Flesh and Bone

text: Everything in me that conspires to break the unity and continuity of my life conspires to destroy me and consequently to destroy itself.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: I : The Man of Flesh and Bone

text: Was man made for science or was science made for man?
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: I : The Man of Flesh and Bone

text: If consciousness is, as some inhuman thinker has said, nothing more than a flash of light between two eternities of darkness, then there is nothing more execrable than existence.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: I : The Man of Flesh and Bone

text: Some think only with the brain, becoming definition-mongers; others think with all the body and soul, with the very marrow of life.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: I : The Man of Flesh and Bone

text: Philosophy, like poetry, is a work of integration and synthesis; otherwise, it is merely pseudo-philosophical erudition.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: I : The Man of Flesh and Bone

text: Little can be hoped from a ruler who has not pondered the 'why' of man's origin and the 'wherefore' of his destiny.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: I : The Man of Flesh and Bone

text: Man is perhaps more an affective animal than a reasoning one; I have seen a cat reason more often than laugh or weep.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: I : The Man of Flesh and Bone

text: Many believe the real, the truly real, is irrational, and that reason builds upon irrationalities.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: I : The Man of Flesh and Bone

text: Consciousness is a disease.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: I : The Man of Flesh and Bone

text: Society is an additional sense; it is the true common sense.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Reason, that which we call reason, reflex and reflective knowledge, the distinguishing mark of man, is a social product.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Everything vital is anti-rational, and everything rational is anti-vital; this is the basis of the tragic sense of life.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: The primary reality is not that I think, but that I live; what contradictions when we seek to join life and reason!
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: The truth is 'I am, therefore I think'; to feel oneself, is it not perhaps to feel oneself imperishable?
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Is not the longing for immortality the primal and fundamental condition of all human knowledge, the true base of all philosophy?
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Underlying all knowledge is human feeling, and the search for 'why' is truly a search for 'wherefore'.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Love, kindled by the world's passing vanity, alone triumphs over the transitory, filling and eternalizing life.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: And love, above all when it struggles against destiny, overwhelms us with the feeling of the vanity of this world of appearances and gives us a glimpse of another world, in which destiny is overcome and liberty is law.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: In life's beauty, my soul dilates; but instantly, the angel of Death whispers, 'Thou shalt cease to be!', flooding my spirit with divinity's blood.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: What most distinguishes man from other animals is that, in one form or another, he guards his dead.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Consciousness shrinks from its own annihilation, desiring a life beyond the world, like a spirit newly severed from its womb.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Glorious is the risk of our souls never dying. Arguments against immortality are mere reasons; the heart desires to live forever, and this 'I' yearns for eternal duration.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: I am the center of my universe, and in supreme anguish, I cry: 'What is a man profited if he shall gain the world and lose his own soul?'
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: There is nothing more universal than the individual; each man is worth more than humanity. What we call egoism is psychic gravity, for how can we love our neighbor if we do not truly love ourselves?
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Let me dream, if this dream is my life. I believe in the immortal origin of my soul's yearning for immortality, for to question it is to ask the reason of reason.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: After hunger, vanity arises: the necessity to impose ourselves and survive in others. Man sacrifices his purse to his vanity, even boasting of weakness like a child with a bandaged finger.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: The vain man mistakes means for ends, needing others to believe in his superiority to believe it himself. A rabid mania for originality makes us rather err with genius than hit the mark with the crowd.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Rousseau observed that philosophers support their systems not for truth, but because they are theirs. The essential thing is to think differently: with believers, an atheist; with atheists, a believer.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Catholicism seeks immortalization, while Protestantism, influenced by Kant, posits that religion rests upon morality, not morality upon religion.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Catholicism, a compromise between eschatology and ethics, uses eternal hell for moral ends, yet God says: 'If I must recompense your evil, I must recompense it with good, for I am and have no other.'
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: For social order, religion became a police system, hence hell. While Eastern Christianity is eschatological and Protestantism ethical, Catholicism compromises, with eschatology predominating.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Monastic asceticism, the most authentic Catholic ethic, is eschatological, focused on individual soul salvation. Perhaps virginity reflects the idea that perpetuating in others hinders our own.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: The ascetic morality is a negative morality. And strictly, what is important for a man is not to die, whether he sins or not.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: The real sin is heresy, thinking for oneself, which is worse than murder or theft. The gravest sin is not to obey the Church, whose infallibility protects us from reason.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Why be scandalized by papal infallibility? Whether a book, a society, or a man, all infallibility is equally irrational, a supreme offense to reason, and must be postulated.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: The Church defends life, creating dogmatic structures against rationalism. It rightly opposed Galileo and Darwin, whose discoveries shattered anthropomorphic beliefs that man was created for the universe or to be eternalized.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Faith feels itself secure neither with universal consent, nor with tradition, nor with authority. It seeks support of its enemy, reason.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: The terrible danger comes from seeking to believe with the reason and not with the life.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: It is no use seeking to force ourselves to consider as super-rational what clearly appears to us to be contra-rational.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: It is my living body that thinks, wills and feels.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: If all is spirit, then the diamond's eternity is no less assured than consciousness'.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Pantheism is merely atheism disguised; and in my opinion, undisguised.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Upon this foundation no human feeling can be built up.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: From whatever side the matter is regarded, it is always found that reason confronts our longing for personal immortality and contradicts it. And the truth is, in all strictness, that reason is the enemy of life.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: In order to understand anything it is necessary to kill it, to lay it out rigid in the mind.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Science is a cemetery of dead ideas, even though life may issue from them.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Happiness is a thing that is lived and felt, not a thing that is reasoned or defined.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Absolute relativism, which is neither more nor less than skepticism, in the most modern sense of the term, is the supreme triumph of the reasoning reason.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: True science teaches, above all, to doubt and be ignorant.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Feeling does not succeed in converting consolation into truth, nor does reason succeed in converting truth into consolation.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: To believe in God is to yearn for His existence and, furthermore, it is to act as if He did exist.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: To know something is to make this something that I know myself; but to avail myself of it, to dominate it, it has to remain distinct from myself.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: VI : In the Depths of the Abyss

text: Philosophy and religion are enemies, and because they are enemies they have need of one another.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: VI : In the Depths of the Abyss

text: Life cannot subject itself to reason, because the end of life is living and not understanding.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: VI : In the Depths of the Abyss

text: Even in those convinced of mortality, a shadow of uncertainty murmurs, 'Who knows!'
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: VI : In the Depths of the Abyss

text: I cannot believe those who assure me that never, not in a fleeting moment, has this murmur of uncertainty breathed upon their consciousness.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: VI : In the Depths of the Abyss

text: I do not wish to make peace between my heart and my head, between my faith and my reason — I wish rather that there should be war between them.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: VI : In the Depths of the Abyss

text: It is not I myself alone, it is the whole human race that is involved, it is the ultimate finality of all our civilization. I am but one, but all men are I's.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: VI : In the Depths of the Abyss

text: It is better to be lacking in reason than to have too much of it.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: VI : In the Depths of the Abyss

text: For the truth is that our doctrines are usually only the justification a posteriori of our conduct, or else they are our way of trying to explain that conduct to ourselves.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: VI : In the Depths of the Abyss

text: In a word, be it with reason or without reason or against reason, I am resolved not to die.
author: Miguel de Unamuno
source_section: VI : In the Depths of the Abyss

text: I have told you that... we know nothing save what we have first, in one way or another, desired; and it may even be added that we can know nothing well save what we love, save what we pity.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: If it is grievous to cease to be, perhaps it is more grievous to be always oneself, without being able to be other, without being able to be all.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: The Consciousness of the Universe, which a love, personalizing all that it loves, discovers, is what we call God.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: And thus the soul pities God and feels itself pitied by him; loves Him and feels loved by Him, sheltering its misery in the bosom of the eternal and infinite misery, which, in eternalizing itself and infinitizing itself, is the supreme happiness itself.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: With knowledge and desire we can embrace everything, or almost everything; with the will nothing, or almost nothing. And out of this collision between our knowledge and our power pity arises.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: The evolution of organic beings is simply the struggle to realize fullness of consciousness through suffering, a continual aspiration to be others without ceasing to be themselves, to break and yet to preserve their proper limits.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: But the capacity to enjoy is impossible without the capacity to suffer ; and the faculty of enjoyment is one with that of pain. Whosoever does not suffer does not enjoy, just as whosoever is insensible to cold is insensible to heat.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: This inner, essential force, which we feel as will, is the impulse to be everything, to be others as well as ourselves yet without ceasing to be what we are.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Imagination humanizes everything and makes it divine, helping Nature become conscious of itself. Reason, on the other hand, mechanizes or materializes.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: The only way to give finality to the world is to give it consciousness. We need God, not to understand the why, but to feel and sustain the ultimate wherefore, to give meaning to the Universe.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Only thus is it possible to understand how in God we live, move, and have our being.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Each angel, each society of angels, and the whole of heaven appear in human form, and in this human form the Lord rules them as one man.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: With God, to think is to create, and He gives existence to that which exists in His thought by the mere fact of thinking it. In Him, who is eternal, is not all existence eternalized?
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Our longing to save consciousness is such that even in sacrifice, we hear that if our consciousness disappears, it is to enrich the infinite and eternal Consciousness, our souls serving as nutriment to the Universal soul.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: It is the furious longing to make the Universe conscious and personal that leads us to create God. For to believe in God is, in a certain sense, to create Him, though He first creates us; He is continually creating Himself.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Only that which feels, suffers, pities, loves, and desires is truly real: consciousness itself. We need God not to think existence, but to live it; not to know its why, but to feel its wherefore.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Love is a contradiction if there is no God.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Martyrs create faith, faith does not create martyrs.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Not by reason, but by love and suffering, do we come to the living God. We must love Him, long for Him, hunger after Him, before knowing Him, for God is indefinable; to define Him is to kill Him.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: God Himself, not merely the idea of God, may become a reality immediately felt, especially in moments of spiritual suffocation. This feeling is a hunger for God, an inability to live without Him.
author: Miguel de Unamuno

text: Logotherapy helps us see meaning in life, but we cannot 'give' meaning to others. If this is true for meaning itself, how much more so for Ultimate Meaning?
author: Viktor Frankl

text: If there is a meaning in life at all, then there must be a meaning in suffering. Suffering is an ineradicable part of life, even as fate and death.
author: Viktor Frankl
source: p. 67 in the 1959 Beacon Press edition

text: There is nothing in the world, I venture to say, that would so effectively help one to survive even the worst conditions, as the knowledge that there is a meaning in one’s life.
author: Viktor Frankl
source: p. 126 in the 1984 Pocket Books edition

text: Love is the ultimate and highest goal to which man can aspire. The salvation of man is through love and in love.
author: Viktor Frankl

text: The way a man accepts his fate and suffering gives him opportunity to add deeper meaning to life. He may remain brave and dignified, or in the bitter fight for self-preservation, forget his human dignity.
author: Viktor Frankl

text: Man should not ask what the meaning of his life is, but recognize that he is asked. Each man is questioned by life, and can only answer by being responsible for his own life.
author: Viktor Frankl

text: What man actually needs is not a tensionless state but rather the striving and struggling for some goal worthy of him. What he needs is not the discharge of tension at any cost, but the call of a potential meaning waiting to be fulfilled by him.
author: Viktor Frankl

text: The world is in a bad state, and will worsen unless each of us does his best. Since Auschwitz, we know what man is capable of; since Hiroshima, we know what is at stake.
author: Viktor Frankl
source: Postscript 1984 : The Case for a Tragic Optimism, based on a lecture at the Third World Congress of Logotherapy, Regensburg University (19 June 1983)

text: Everything can be taken from a man but one thing: the last of the human freedoms—to choose one's attitude in any given set of circumstances, to choose one's own way.
author: Viktor Frankl
source: Yotam Marom in Not Too Late: Changing the Climate Story from Despair to Possibility edited by Rebecca Solnit and Thelma Young Lutunatabua (2023)

text: Between stimulus and response lies a space. In that space lie our freedom and power to choose a response. In our response lies our growth and our happiness.
author: Viktor Frankl
source: Stephen Covey summarising a thought he attributed to Viktor Frankl. Widely attributed directly, and incorrectly, to Frankl.

text: The stronger the feeling of inferiority, the higher the goal for personal power.
author: Alfred Adler
source: From a new translation of "Progress in Individual Psychology" (" Fortschritte der Individualpsychologie ", 1923), a journal article by Alfred Adler, in the AAISF/ATP Archives.

text: Meanings are not determined by situations, but we determine ourselves by the meanings we give to situations.
author: Alfred Adler
source: What Life Should Mean to You (1937), p. 14

text: It is always easier to fight for one's principles than to live up to them.
author: Alfred Adler
source: Quoted in: Phyllis Bottome , Alfred Adler: Apostle of Freedom (1939), ch. 5

text: Man knows much more than he understands.
author: Alfred Adler
source: As quoted in A Primer of Adlerian Psychology: The Analytic-Behavioural-Cognitive Psychology of Alfred Adler (1999) by Harold H. Mosak and Michael P. Maniacci

text: Common sense, the half-truths of a deceitful society, is honored as the honest truths of a frank world.
author: Alfred Adler
source: Russell Jacoby , Social Amnesia: A Critique of Conformist Psychology from Adler to Laing (1975), p. 23-25

text: My first act of free will shall be to believe in free will.
author: William James
source: Diary entry (April 30, 1870) as quoted in Ralph Barton Perry, The Thought and Character of William James , vol. 1, p. 323; Letters of William James , vol. I, p. 147.

text: All our scientific and philosophic ideals are altars to unknown gods .
author: William James
source: Lecture at the Harvard Divinity School (13 March 1884); published in the The Unitarian Review and Religious Magazine as The Dilemma of Determinism (September 1884)

text: I cannot understand the willingness to act, no matter how we feel, without the belief that acts are really good and bad.
author: William James

text: The most any one can do is to confess as candidly as he can the grounds for the faith that is in him, and leave his example to work on others as it may.
author: William James

text: Freedom is only necessity understood, and bondage to the highest is identical with true freedom.
author: William James
source: The Dilemma of Determinism (1884) republished in The Will to Believe , Dover, 1956, p. 149

text: The stronghold of the determinist argument is the antipathy to the idea of chance...This notion of alternative possibility, this admission that any one of several things may come to pass is, after all, only a roundabout name for chance.
author: William James

text: We have nothing to do but to receive, resting absolutely upon the merit , power , and love of our Redeemer.
author: William James
source: Reported in Dictionary of Burning Words of Brilliant Writers (1895) edited by Josiah Hotchkiss Gilbert, p. 225

text: Every way of classifying a thing is but a way of handling it for some particular purpose.
author: William James

text: That nature will follow to-morrow the same laws that she follows to-day is a truth which no man can know; but in the interests of cognition as well as of action we must postulate or assume it.
author: William James

text: Pretend what we may, the whole man within us is at work when we form our philosophical opinions.
author: William James

text: The concrete man has but one interest — to be right . That to him is the art of all arts, and all means are fair which help him to it.
author: William James

text: We are all ready to be savage in some cause . The difference between a good man and a bad one is the choice of the cause.
author: William James

text: The only truth that stands, even against skepticism, is the existence of consciousness.
author: William James

text: Robust faiths too often cut each other's throats; for peace, moderation must creep in.
author: William James
source: Letter to John Jay Chapman (5 April 1897)

text: I am against bigness in all its forms, favoring the invisible moral forces that, like soft rootlets, eventually rend the hardest monuments of man's pride.
author: William James
