text: Just as people are born, live, and die, so too do republics form, flower, and perish by audacious citizens or enemy weapons. All empires have their time; republics see powerful families as the disease of death.
author: Frederick the Great

text: If my soldiers began to think, not one would remain in the ranks.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Attributed in J.A. Houlding, Fit for Service: The Training of the British Army, 1715-1795 (Oxford, 1981)

text: "My people and I," he said, "have come to an agreement which satisfies us both. They are to say what they please, and I am to do what I please."
author: Frederick the Great
source: Attributed by Thomas Babington Macaulay , Life of Frederick the Great (1882), pg 48.

text: Diplomacy without arms is like music without instruments.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Attributed in The Times : "Diplomacy without Arms", 1 February 2010.

text: I begin by taking. I shall find scholars later to demonstrate my perfect right.
author: Frederick the Great

text: An Army, like a serpent, goes upon its belly.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Attributed in Thomas Carlyle , “History of Friedrich the Second, Called Frederick the Great", (1858).

text: If I had a province to punish, I would let it be governed by philosophers.
author: Frederick the Great

text: He turns upon his own axis like the sun, shines in his own light, shares her heat and her spots.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Friedrich Karl von Moser , Der Herr und der Diener, geschildert mit patriotischer Freiheit (1759), pp. 19–20, quoted in Gerhard Ritter, The Sword and the Sceptre: The Problem of Militarism in Germany, Volume I: The Prussian Tradition, 1740–1890 (1972), p. 36

text: Frederick the Great asked, 'Can you name me a single proof of the existence of God?' Zimmermann replied, 'Your Majesty, the Jews!'
author: Frederick the Great
source: Dogmatics in Outline (1949) as translated by Tr. G. T. Thomson ISBN 006130056X , quoted in "Word Alive! An Introduction to the Christian Faith" by John Schwarz (1995)

text: Recollect that we form one country now. Abandon all these local animosities, and make your sons Americans.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Advice to a Confederate widow who expressed animosity towards the northern U.S. after the end of the American Civil War, as quoted in The Life and Campaigns of General Lee (1875) by Edward Lee Childe, p. 331. Also quoted in "Will Confederate Heritage Advocates Take Robert E. Lee’s Advice?" (July 2014), by Brooks D. Simpson, Crossroads , WordPress. This quote is sometimes paraphrased as: "Madam, do not train up your children in hostility to the government of the United States. Remember, we are all one country now. Dismiss from your mind all sectional feeling, and bring them up to be Americans."

text: We must forgive our enemies.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: As quoted in A Life of General Robert E. Lee (1871), by John Esten Cooke

text: I cannot consent to place in the control of others one who cannot control himself.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Comment regarding officers who became inebriated, as quoted in Personal Reminiscences, Anecdotes, and Letters of Gen. Robert E. Lee (1874) by John William Jones, p. 170

text: Sir, if you ever presume again to speak disrespectfully of General Grant in my presence, either you or I will sever his connection with this university .
author: Robert E. Lee
source: After one of the faculty at Washington College in Virginia (now Washington & Lee University) had spoken insultingly of Ulysses S. Grant , as quoted in Lee the American (1912) by Gamaliel Bradford, p. 226

text: The true test of a gentleman is the forbearing use of power and how he wields advantage over others.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: "Definition of a Gentleman" , a memorandum found in his papers after his death, as quoted in Lee the American (1912) by Gamaliel Bradford, p. 233

text: I have never cherished bitter or vindictive feelings toward them, and have never seen the day when I did not pray for them.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: As quoted in The American Soul: An Appreciation of the Four Greatest Americans and their Lessons for Present Americans (1920) by Charles Sherwood Farriss, p. 63

text: Obedience to lawful authority is the foundation of manly character.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: As quoted in General Robert E. Lee After Appomattox (1922), by Franklin Lafayette Riley, p. 18

text: My mistakes are never told me until it is too late, yet I am always ready and anxious for suggestions.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Remark to General Henry Heth , as quoted in R. E. Lee : A Biography , Vol. 3 (1935) by Douglas Southall Freeman

text: I should NOT be trading on the blood of my men.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: On refusing requests to write his memoirs, as quoted in Gentlemen of Virginia (1961) page 188 by Marshall William Fishwick; also cited as possibly apocryphal in The Oxford Dictionary of Quotations (2004) edited by Elizabeth M. Knowles

text: The education of a man is never completed until he dies.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: As quoted in Peter's Quotations: Ideas for Our Time (1977) by Laurence J. Peter, p. 175

text: Above all do not appear to others what you are not.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: As quoted in Extraordinary Lives: The Art and Craft of American Biography (1986) by Robert A. Caro and William Knowlton Zinsser. Also quoted in Truman by David McCullough (1992), p. 44, New York: Simon & Schuster.-

text: It is the duty of every citizen to aid in the restoration of peace and harmony, especially those who instruct the young.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to trustees, as quoted in "Honoring Lee Anew" (15 July 2014), by David Cox, A Magazine of Student Thought and Opinion

text: All citizens should unite in honest efforts to obliterate the effects of war and restore the blessing of peace.
author: Robert E. Lee

text: Mr. Blair, I look upon secession as anarchy . If I owned the four millions of slaves in the South, I would sacrifice them all to the Union ; but how can I draw my sword upon Virginia, my native State?
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Life and Campaigns of General Robert E. Lee (1866) page 30. Responding to Francis Preston Blair after he relayed an offer to make Lee major-general to command the defense of Washington D.C.

text: You see what a poor sinner I am, and how unworthy to possess what was given me; for that reason, it has been taken away.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to his daughter after losing Arlington (25 December 1861)

text: Still, a Union that can only be maintained by swords and bayonets, and in which strife and civil war are to take the place of brotherly love and kindness, has no charm for me.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to his son , G. W. Custis Lee (23 January 1861).

text: Save for defense of my native state, I never desire again to draw my sword.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to General Winfield Scott (20 April 1861) after turning down an offer by Abraham Lincoln of supreme command of the U.S. Army; as quoted in Personal Reminiscences, Anecdotes, and Letters of Gen. Robert E. Lee (1875) by John William Jones, p. 139

text: It is well that war is so terrible, otherwise we should grow too fond of it.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Comment to James Longstreet , on seeing a Union charge repelled in the Battle of Fredericksburg (13 December 1862)

text: What a cruel thing is war; to separate and destroy families and friends, and mar the purest joys and happiness God has granted us in this world; to fill our hearts with hatred instead of love for our neighbors, and to devastate the fair face of this beautiful world!
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to his wife on Christmas Day, two weeks after the Battle of Fredericksburg (25 December 1862).

text: I have been up to see the Congress and they do not seem to be able to do anything except to eat peanuts and chew tobacco , while my army is starving.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Remark to his son, G. W. Custis Lee (March 1865), as quoted in South Atlantic Quarterly [Durham, North Carolina] (July 1927)

text: The only dignified course for me would be to go to General Grant and surrender myself and take the consequences of my acts.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: In response to Brigadier General Edward Porter Alexander (April 1865) after the Battle of Appomattox Court House . Alexander had suggested for Confederate soldiers to disperse, report under orders to their respective governors, and continue fighting the Union. He admitted that he was silenced by Lee's rebuke. "I had not a single word to say in reply. He had answered my suggestion from a plane so far above it that I was ashamed for having made it. " as quoted in Fighting For the Confederacy, pp. 531-33 [ 1 ] and The Methodist Quarterly Review, January 1920 pp. 325-26 [ 2 ]

text: The only question on which we did not agree has been settled, and the Lord has decided against me.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: To Marsena Patrick , as quoted in "Honoring Lee Anew" (15 July 2014), by David Cox, A Magazine of Student Thought and Opinion

text: It is the part of wisdom to acquiesce in the result, and of candor to recognize the fact.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to former Virginia governor John Letcher (28 August 1865), as quoted in Personal Reminiscences, Anecdotes, and Letters of Gen. Robert E. Lee (1875) by John William Jones, p. 203

text: All should unite in honest efforts to obliterate the effects of war, and to restore the blessings of peace.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to former Virginia governor John Letcher (28 August 1865), as quoted in Personal Reminiscences, Anecdotes, and Letters of Gen. Robert E. Lee (1875) by John William Jones, p. 203

text: True patriotism sometimes requires of men to act exactly contrary, at one period, to that which it does at another, and the motive which impels them — the desire to do right — is precisely the same.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to General P.G.T. Beauregard (3 October 1865)

text: All who can should vote for the most intelligent, honest, and conscientious men eligible to office, irrespective of former party opinions.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to General James Longstreet (29 October 1867), as quoted in Recollections and Letters of General Robert E. Lee (1924), p. 269.

text: I think it wiser not to keep open the sores of war, but to commit to oblivion the feelings it engendered.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter regarding war monuments (1869), as quoted in Personal reminiscences, anecdotes, and letters of gen. Robert E. Lee (1874), by John William Jones , p. 234. Also quoted in "Renounce the battle flag: Don't whitewash history" (26 June 2015), by Petula Dvorak, The Washington Post , Washington, D.C. This quote is also given as: "I think it wisest not to keep open the sores of war, but to follow the example of those nations who endeavored to obliterate the marks of civil strife, and to commit to oblivion the feelings it engendered."

text: The real honest man is honest from conviction of what is right, not from policy.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: "Memoirs of Robert E. Lee" by A. L. Long (1886)

text: Those who oppose our purposes are not always to be regarded as our enemies. We usually think and act from our immediate surroundings. The better rule is to judge our adversaries from their standpoint, not from ours.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: "Memoirs of Robert E. Lee" by A. L. Long (1886)

text: I would cheerfully have lost all I have lost by the war, and have suffered all I have suffered, to have slavery abolished.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Statement to John Leyburn (1 May 1870), as quoted in R. E. Lee: A Biography (1934) by Douglas Southall Freeman.

text: The march of Providence is so slow, and our desires so impatient; the work of progress so immense and our means so feeble, that we often see only the ebb of the advancing wave and are thus discouraged. It is history that teaches us to hope.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to Lieutenant Colonel Charles Marshall (September 1870)

text: Duty is the sublimest word in our language. Do your duty in all things. You cannot do more. You should never wish to do less.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter purportedly written to his son, G. W. Custis Lee (5 April 1852); published in The New York Sun (26 November 1864). Although the “Duty Letter” was presumed authentic for many decades and included in many biographies of Lee, it was repudiated in December 1864 by “a source entitled to know.” This repudiation was rediscovered by University of Virginia law professor Charles A. Graves who verified that the letter was inconsistent with Lee's biographical facts and letter-writing style. Lee's son also wrote to Graves that he did not recall ever receiving such a letter. “The Forged Letter of General Robert E. Lee”, Proceedings of the 26th annual meeting of the Virginia State Bar Association 17:176 (1914)

text: Governor, if I had foreseen the use those people designed to make of their victory, there would have been no surrender at Appomattox Courthouse; no sir, not by me. Had I foreseen these results of subjugation, I would have preferred to die at Appomattox with my brave men, my sword in my right hand.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Supposedly made to Governor Fletcher S. Stockdale (September 1870), as quoted in The Life and Letters of Robert Lewis Dabney , pp. 497-500; however, most major researchers including Douglas Southall Freeman, Shelby Dade Foote, Jr., and Bruce Catton consider the quote a myth and refuse to recognize it. “T. C. Johnson: Life and Letters of Robert Lewis Dabney, 498 ff. Doctor Dabney was not present and received his account of the meeting from Governor Stockdale. The latter told Dabney that he was the last to leave the room, and that as he was saying good-bye, Lee closed the door, thanked him for what he had said and added: "Governor, if I had foreseen the use these people desired to make of their victory, there would have been no surrender at Appomattox, no, sir, not by me. Had I foreseen these results of subjugation, I would have preferred to die at Appomattox with my brave men, my sword in this right hand." This, of course, is second-hand testimony. There is nothing in Lee's own writings and nothing in direct quotation by first-hand witness that accords with such an expression on his part. The nearest approach to it is the claim by H. Gerald Smythe that "Major Talcott" — presumably Colonel T. M. R. Talcott — told him Lee stated he would never have surrendered the army if he had known how the South would have been treated. Mr. Smythe stated that Colonel Talcott replied, "Well, General, you have only to blow the bugle," whereupon Lee is alleged to have answered, "It is too late now" (29 Confederate Veteran, 7). Here again the evidence is not direct. The writer of this biography, talking often with Colonel Talcott, never heard him narrate this incident or suggest in any way that Lee accepted the results of the radical policy otherwise than with indignation, yet in the belief that the extremists would not always remain in office”.

text: Tell Hill he must come up … Strike the tent.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Reported as his last words. There are suggestions that Lee's biographer, Douglas Southall Freeman embellished Lee's final moments; as Lee suffered a stroke on September 28, 1870. Dying two weeks later, on October 12, 1870, shortly after 9 a.m. from the effects of pneumonia. Lee's stroke had resulted in Aphasia , rendering him unable to speak. When interviewed the four attending physicians and family stated "he had not spoken since 28 September..."

text: He had been in seventeen battles and was not born to be killed by a d----d Yankee.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Flavel Clingan Barber, Holding The Line: The Third Tennessee Infantry (1994), p. 82.

text: It is good that war is terrible, otherwise men would grow fond of it.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Roy Barnes , speech to the Georgian House of Representatives (24 January 2001), by R. Barnes, Georgia

text: Robert E. Lee's lips were never profaned by an oath, whiskey, or tobacco; Stonewall Jackson opened every battle with a prayer.
author: Robert E. Lee

text: He refused command of the Federal Army, resigning his commission: 'I cannot raise my hand against my birthplace...'
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Lamont Buchanan, A Pictorial History of the Confederacy (1951), p. 41

text: When Lee heard of Jackson's fatal wound, he wrote: 'You have lost your left arm, but I have lost my right.'
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Lamont Buchanan, A Pictorial History of the Confederacy (1951), p. 99

text: Lee once remarked modestly that he felt he was only as good as the generals with whom he could surround himself.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Lamont Buchanan, A Pictorial History of the Confederacy (1951), p. 119

text: Lee rode serenely along, respected even by those who opposed the cause he served.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Lamont Buchanan, A Pictorial History of the Confederacy (1951), p. 261

text: His career ended in defeat, but it was not failure. His life is not a subject of sadness, but of inspiration.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Robert Lee Bullard in a 1921 speech to the New York Camp of Confederate Veterans at their annual event honoring Robert E. Lee's birthday at the Hotel Astor in New York City, on 19 January 1921. As quoted by Greg Eanes, Heritage of Honor: Our Confederate Military Legacy (2015), p. 83-85

text: Lee surrendered to save lives, believing two hundred and forty thousand dead already was enough.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Johnny Cash , in his song "God Bless Robert E. Lee" from the album Johnny 99 (1983).

text: Opposing Grant and the Army of the Potomac was Robert E. Lee, the last great knight of battle. He was a god to his men and a scourge to his antagonists.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Bruce Catton, The Army of the Potomac: A Stillness at Appomattox (1953) on the dust jacket of the 1953 hardcover edition.

text: Robert E. Lee's armies took special care to enslave free blacks during their northern campaign.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: T.N. Coates , "Take Down The Confederate Flag. Now. The flag that Dylann Roof embraced, which many South Carolinians embrace, endorses the violence he committed." (18 June 2015), The Atlantic

text: If slaves make good soldiers our whole theory of slavery is wrong.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Howell Cobb , regarding suggestions that the Confederates turn their slaves into soldiers (1865). As quoted in Encyclopædia Britannica (1911), Hugh Chisholm, editor, 11th ed., Cambridge University Press. Also quoted as 'You cannot make soldiers of slaves, or slaves of soldiers. The day you make a soldier of them is the beginning of the end of the Revolution. And if slaves seem good soldiers, then our whole theory of slavery is wrong'.

text: At Appomattox, the spirit of feudalism and injustice surrendered, in Robert E. Lee, to the spirit of equal rights, in Ulysses S. Grant.
author: Robert E. Lee

text: Like some tall cliff against whose solid base the angry waves are beating, stands our Lee, with eternal sunshine on his good, grey head.
author: Robert E. Lee

text: If he knew what slavery meant when he helped maim and murder thousands in its defense, Robert Lee was a traitor to humanity and humanity’s God.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: W E B Du Bois , Essay on Robert E. Lee (1928)

text: Oh, I am heartily tired of hearing about what Lee is going to do. Some of you always seem to think he is suddenly going to turn a double somersault, and land in our rear and on both of our flanks at the same time. Go back to your command, and try to think what we are going to do ourselves , instead of what Lee is going to do.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Ulysses S. Grant , as quoted in "Campaigning with Grant" (December 1896), by General Horace Porter , The Century Magazine

text: He was a Caesar, without his ambition; Frederick, without his tyranny; Napoleon, without his selfishness, and Washington, without his reward.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Benjamin Harvey Hill, U.S. Congressman from 1875 to 1882, in an address before the Southern Historical Society, Atlanta, Georgia, February 18, 1874. As quoted in Senator Benjamin H. Hill of Georgia; His Life, Speeches and Writings (1893) by Benjamin Harvey Hill Jr., p. 406

text: He was a general who 'would rather lose the war than his dignity.'
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Seymour Morris Jr., American History Revised: 200 Startling Facts That Never Made It into the Textbooks (2010), p. 160

text: It is well that war is so terrible, lest we grow too fond of it.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Seymour Morris Jr., American History Revised: 200 Startling Facts That Never Made It into the Textbooks (2010), p. 161

text: Theodore Roosevelt, Winston Churchill, and Eisenhower all hailed Robert E. Lee as our greatest American.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Seymour Morris Jr., American History Revised: 200 Startling Facts That Never Made It into the Textbooks (2010), p. 161

text: No military leader since Napoleon has aroused such enthusiastic devotion among troops as did Lee.
author: Robert E. Lee

text: That old man had my division slaughtered at Gettysburg.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: George Pickett , as quoted in The Civil War: An Illustrated History , p. 236

text: We recognize Robert E. Lee as one of our greatest American Christians and one of our greatest American gentlemen.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Franklin D. Roosevelt , as quoted in Public Papers of the Presidents of the United States: F.D. Roosevelt, 1936: Volume 5 , p. 214

text: They were all Americans and nothing else. This was just as true of Lee and of Stonewall Jackson and of Beauregard.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Theodore Roosevelt , "Address to the Knights of Columbus" (12 October 1915)

text: Lee is the greatest military genius in America , myself not excepted.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Winfield Scott , Union general, as quoted in Life of General Robert Edward Lee (1870), by C. Stoctly Errickson, p. 35\

text: Lee and his comrades killed more U.S. Army soldiers than any other enemy, ever, to create a nation dedicated to exploit enslaved men, women, and children, forever.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Ty Seidule, Robert E. Lee and Me: A Southerner's Reckoning with the Myth of the Lost Cause (2020), p. 8-9

text: My former hero, Robert E. Lee, committed treason to preserve slavery.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Ty Seidule, Robert E. Lee and Me: A Southerner's Reckoning with the Myth of the Lost Cause (2020), p. 9

text: Between the Christianity of this land and the Christianity of Christ, I recognize the widest possible difference.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Adam Serwer , The Cruelty is the Point (2021)

text: Lee was devoted to defending the principle of white supremacy; Longstreet was not. This is why Lee is remembered as a hero, and Longstreet as a disgrace.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Adam Serwer , The Cruelty is the Point (2021)

text: Lee's loyalty to the Union was great, but his loyalty to Virginia was greater.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Francis Butler Simkins, Spotswood Hunnicutt, Sidman P. Poole, Virginia: History, Government, Geography (1957), p. 411-412

text: Lee castigated abolitionists, dragged his heels on emancipation, and never questioned his slave-run Arlington mansion.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Brooks D. Simpson , as quoted in "Arizona State Professor Brooks D. Simpson Discusses Neo-Confederate Movement", in Intelligence Report of the Southern Poverty Law Center, Issue 99, (Summer 2000)

text: Given Stone Mountain's history, Klansman Gordon would have been a good choice.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Brooks D. Simpson , "The Future of Stone Mountain" (22 July 2015), Crossroads

text: This week it's Robert E. Lee. I noticed that Stonewall Jackson is coming down. I wonder, is it George Washington next week, and is it Thomas Jefferson the week after? You know, you really do have to ask yourself, where does it stop?
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Donald Trump , Trump chief of staff John Kelly calls Confederate Gen. Robert E. Lee an 'honorable man' (2017)

text: That's all right, son," he said at last. "Robert E. Lee and Stonewall Jackson went there too."
author: Robert E. Lee
source: William Westmoreland , A Soldier Reports (1976), p. 12

text: Lee was the last of the great old-fashioned generals.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: T. Harry Williams, McClellan, Sherman, and Grant , p. 109

text: I have come not to make war on the Italians , but to aid the Italians against Rome.
author: Hannibal Barca
source: Spoken to Italian soldiers of Rome captured at the Battle of Lake Trasimene (24 June 217 BC) as quoted in Hannibal : One Man Against Rome (1958) by Harold Lamb, p. 119.

text: Ah there is one thing about them more wonderful than their numbers … in all that vast number there is not one man called Gisgo.
author: Hannibal Barca
source: Spoken as a jest to one of his officers named Gisgo, who had remarked on the numbers of Roman forces against them before the Battle of Cannae (2 August 216 BC), as quoted in A History of Rome (1855), by Henry George Liddell Vol. 1, p. 355

text: No fiercer spur to victory have the immortal gods bestowed upon man than contempt for death.
author: Hannibal Barca
source: God has given to man no sharper spur to victory than contempt of death. As quoted by Livy , la:s:Ab Urbe Condita/liber XXI 44, as translated by Aubrey De Sélincourt, in The War with Hannibal (1965).

text: Let us free the Roman people from their long-held anxiety, for they deem it too long to await an old man's death.
author: Hannibal Barca
source: Let us ease the Roman people of their continual care, who think it long to await the death of an old man.

text: I am not carrying on a war of extermination against the Romans. I am contending for honour and empire. My ancestors yielded to Roman valour. I am endeavouring that others, in their turn, will be obliged to yield to my good fortune, and my valour.
author: Hannibal Barca
source: As quoted in Hannibal : Enemy of Rome (1992) by Leonard Cottrell, p. 150.

text: Asked by Scipio to name the greatest generals, Hannibal listed Alexander, Pyrrhus, and himself, adding, 'Had I defeated you, Scipio, I would have placed myself first of all.'
author: Hannibal Barca
source: Livy (Titus Livius), History of Rome, book 35. (DOI: 10.4159/DLCL.livy-history_rome_35.2018)

text: Against overwhelming odds, Hannibal stood alone and unequaled: a soldier of unmatched genius, a man of pure life and noble patriotism.
author: Hannibal Barca
source: Theodore Ayrault Dodge , Hannibal : A History of the Art of War among the Carthaginians and Romans (1893), p. 652.

text: Hannibal's transcendent military genius is beyond dispute; for steadfastness of purpose, organizing capacity, and mastery of military science, he has perhaps never had an equal.
author: Hannibal Barca

text: Of a truth the gods do not give the same man everything: you know how to gain a victory, Hannibal, but you do not know how to make use of it.
author: Hannibal Barca
source: Maharbal , commander of Hannibals' Numidian cavalry, as quoted in Livy's History of Rome (1880), Anonymous translation, revised by C. A. M. Fennell, Book XXII, p. 151

text: Though Hannibal won the Battle of Cannae with a dazzling maneuver, Carthage ultimately lost the war because Rome outlasted it, paying a heavy price: its cities leveled, its fields sown with salt.
author: Hannibal Barca
source: Margaret MacMillan , War: How Conflict Shaped Us (2020)

text: Among the Carthaginians he [Hannibal] was notorious for his love of money and among the Romans for his cruelty.
author: Hannibal Barca
source: Polybius , Histories, Book 9, Loeb Classical Library

text: Had Hannibal had a government behind him that knew how to exploit victories – had he been born a Roman, for instance – he might have conquered the world.
author: Hannibal Barca
source: Isaac Asimov , The Land of Canaan (1971), p. 218

text: Now that I have dragged you to Africa, I am under no obligation to respect you. If the terms for peace seem too severe, then prepare for war, since you have been unable to endure peace.
author: Scipio Africanus
source: Reply to Hannibal 's attempt to set terms for peace, prior to the Battle of Zama , as quoted in Livy. Books XXVIII-XXX With An English Translation (1949), Book 30, Ch. 31

text: According to Cato the Elder , Scipio Africanus was wont to say that he was never less at leisure than when at leisure, nor less lonely than when alone .
author: Scipio Africanus
source: Numquam se minus otiosum esse, quam cum otiosus; nec minus solum, quam cum solus esset

text: Ungrateful homeland, you shall not even possess my bones.
author: Scipio Africanus
source: Thankless country, thou shalt not possess even my bones!

text: I would rather save the life of one citizen than kill a thousand enemies.
author: Scipio Africanus
source: ...malle se unum civem servare quam mille hostes occidere .

text: Scipio's battles, rich in stratagems, offer an unfailing lesson in the timeless art of generalship.
author: Scipio Africanus
source: B. H. Liddell Hart in Scipio Africanus : Greater Than Napoleon (1926) Preface

text: The true national object in war, as in peace, is a more perfect peace.
author: Scipio Africanus
source: B. H. Liddell Hart in Scipio Africanus : Greater Than Napoleon (1926) Ch. X : A Violated Peace, p. 153, using a quote of himself from one of his previous works Paris, or the Future of War (1925)

text: We cannot separate the nobility of Scipio's moral conduct, throughout his career, from the transcendent clearness of his mental vision — they blended to form not only a great general but a great man.
author: Scipio Africanus
source: B. H. Liddell Hart in Scipio Africanus : Greater Than Napoleon (1926), Ch. X : A Violated Peace, p. 162

text: When age chills the blood, when our pleasures are past— For years fleet away with the wings of the dove — The dearest remembrance will still be the last, Our sweetest memorial the first kiss of love .
author: Lord Byron
source: The First Kiss of Love , st. 7 (1806).

text: Farewell! if ever fondest prayer For other's weal avail'd on high, Mine will not all be lost in air, But waft thy name beyond the sky.
author: Lord Byron

text: I only know we loved in vain; I only feel — farewell! farewell!
author: Lord Byron
source: Farewell! If Ever Fondest Prayer (1808), st. 2.

text: When we two parted In silence and tears, Half brokenhearted, To sever for years.
author: Lord Byron

text: In secret we met In silence I grieve, That thy heart could forget, Thy spirit deceive. If I should meet thee After long years, How should I greet thee? With silence and tears.
author: Lord Byron

text: Here lies one who possessed beauty without vanity, strength without insolence, courage without ferocity, and all the virtues of man, without his vices.
author: Lord Byron
source: Inscription on the monument of a Newfoundland dog (1808).

text: The poor dog, in life the firmest friend, The first to welcome, foremost to defend.
author: Lord Byron
source: Inscription on the monument of a Newfoundland dog (1808).

text: Maid of Athens, ere we part, Give, oh give me back my heart!
author: Lord Byron

text: The love where Death has set his seal, Nor age can chill, nor rival steal, Nor falsehood disavow.
author: Lord Byron
source: And Thou Art Dead as Young and Fair (1812).

text: And thou wert lovely to the last, Extinguish'd, not decay'd; As stars that shoot along the sky Shine brightest as they fall from high.
author: Lord Byron
source: And Thou Art Dead as Young and Fair (1812).

text: If I am fool, it is, at least, a doubting one; and I envy no one the certainty of his self-approved wisdom.
author: Lord Byron
source: Journal for Saturday, 27th November 1813; Quoted in Letters and Journals of Lord Byron by Thomas Moore (1830), Vol III, Chap. XVII, p. 208

text: Washington, the Cincinnatus of the West, whom envy dared not hate, bequeathed a name to make man blush there was but one!
author: Lord Byron

text: You are the fools, not I — for I did dwell With a deep thought, and with a softened eye, On that Old Sexton's natural homily, In which there was Obscurity and Fame , The Glory and the Nothing of a Name .
author: Lord Byron

text: Who knows whether, when a comet shall approach this globe to destroy it, men will not tear rocks from their foundations by means of steam, and hurl mountains, as the giants are said to have done, against the flaming mass?
author: Lord Byron
source: As quoted in Conversations of Lord Byron with Thomas Medwin (1832).

text: My great comfort is, that the temporary celebrity I have wrung from the world has been in the very teeth of all opinions and prejudices . I have flattered no ruling powers; I have never concealed a single thought that tempted me.
author: Lord Byron

text: Fare thee well! and if forever, Still forever, fare thee well: Even though unforgiving, never 'Gainst thee shall my heart rebel.
author: Lord Byron

text: My hair is grey, but not with years, Nor grew it white In a single night, As men's have grown from sudden fears.
author: Lord Byron

text: Oh, God ! it is a fearful thing To see the human soul take wing In any shape, in any mood.
author: Lord Byron

text: A light broke in upon my brain, — It was the carol of a bird; It ceased, and then it came again, The sweetest song ear ever heard.
author: Lord Byron

text: There 's not a joy the world can give like that it takes away.
author: Lord Byron
source: Stanzas for Music (March 1815), reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919).

text: There be none of Beauty's daughters With a magic like thee; And like music on the waters Is thy sweet voice to me.
author: Lord Byron

text: I had a dream , which was not all a dream.
author: Lord Byron

text: Though the day of my Destiny 's over, And the star of my Fate hath declined, Thy soft heart refused to discover The faults which so many could find.
author: Lord Byron

text: In the desert a fountain is springing, In the wide waste there still is a tree , And a bird in the solitude singing, Which speaks to my spirit of thee.
author: Lord Byron
source: Stanzas to Augusta (1816), reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919).

text: As the liberty lads o'er the sea Bought their freedom , and cheaply, with blood, So we, boys, we Shall die fighting or live free, And down with all kings but King Ludd!
author: Lord Byron

text: My boat is on the shore, And my bark is on the sea; But, before I go, Tom Moore. Here's a double health to thee!
author: Lord Byron

text: Here's a sigh to those who love me, And a smile to those who hate : And, whatever sky 's above me, Here's a heart for every fate.
author: Lord Byron

text: Were't the last drop in the well, As I gasp'd upon the brink, Ere my fainting spirit fell 'T is to thee that I would drink.
author: Lord Byron
source: To Thomas Moore , reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919).

text: "Bring forth the horse !" — the horse was brought; In truth , he was a noble steed, A Tartar of the Ukraine breed, Who look'd as though the speed of thought Were in his limbs.
author: Lord Byron

text: And if we do but watch the hour, There never yet was human power Which could evade, if unforgiven, The patient search and vigil long Of him who treasures up a wrong.
author: Lord Byron

text: When a man hath no freedom to fight for at home, let him combat for that of his neighbours; then battle for freedom wherever you can, and, if not shot or hang'd, you'll get knighted.
author: Lord Byron

text: Oh, talk not to me of a name great in story; The days of our youth are the days of our glory; And the myrtle and ivy of sweet two-and-twenty Are worth all your laurels, though ever so plenty.
author: Lord Byron
source: Stanzas Written on the Road Between Florence and Pisa , st. 1 (1821).

text: The best of prophets of the future is the past .
author: Lord Byron

text: The world is a bundle of hay, Mankind are the asses that pull, Each tugs in a different way— And the greatest of all is John Bull!
author: Lord Byron

text: Because He is all-powerful, must all-good, too, follow? I judge but by the fruits—and they are bitter— Which I must feed on for a fault not mine.
author: Lord Byron

text: I live, But live to die: and, living, see no thing To make death hateful, save an innate clinging, A loathsome and yet all invincible Instinct of life, which I abhor, as I Despise myself, yet cannot overcome— And so I live. Would I had never lived!
author: Lord Byron

text: That which I am, I am; I did not seek For life, nor did I make myself.
author: Lord Byron

text: He seems To have seen better days, as who has not Who has seen yesterday?
author: Lord Byron

text: What's drinking? A mere pause from thinking!
author: Lord Byron
source: The Deformed Transformed , Act III, sc. i (1824).

text: My days are in the yellow leaf; The flowers and fruits of Love are gone; The worm — the canker, and the grief Are mine alone!
author: Lord Byron
source: On This Day I Complete My Thirty-Sixth Year , st. 2 (1824).

text: I awoke one morning and found myself famous .
author: Lord Byron
source: Memorandum reference to the instantaneous success of Childe Harold and quoted in Letters and Journals of Lord Byron by Thomas Moore (1830), chapter 14.

text: A great poet belongs to no country; his works are public property, and his Memoirs the inheritance of the public.
author: Lord Byron
source: As quoted in Conversations of Lord Byron with Thomas Medwin (1832), Preface.

text: They never fail who die In a great cause.
author: Lord Byron
source: Marino Faliero , Act II, Scene 2, reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919).

text: Friendship may, and often does, grow into love, but love never subsides into friendship.
author: Lord Byron
source: Quoted by Marguerite Gardiner, Countess of Blessington in Conversations of Lord Byron with the Countess of Blessington (1834).

text: Born in the garret, in the kitchen bred.
author: Lord Byron
source: A Sketch , reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919).

text: Whose game was empires and whose stakes were thrones, Whose table earth, whose dice were human bones.
author: Lord Byron
source: Age of Bronze , Stanza 3, reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919).

text: I'll publish right or wrong: Fools are my theme, let satire be my song.
author: Lord Byron

text: 'Tis pleasure, sure, to see one's name in print; A book's a book, although there's nothing in 't.
author: Lord Byron

text: A man must serve his time to every trade Save censure — critics are ready-made.
author: Lord Byron

text: With just enough of learning to misquote.
author: Lord Byron

text: As soon Seek roses in December , ice in June ; Hope constancy in wind , or corn in chaff; Believe a woman or an epitaph, Or any other thing that's false, before You trust in critics, who themselves are sore.
author: Lord Byron

text: Better to err with Pope, than shine with Pye.
author: Lord Byron

text: Perverts the Prophets and purloins the Psalms .
author: Lord Byron

text: The struck eagle, stretched upon the plain, viewed his own feather on the fatal dart, and winged the shaft that quivered in his heart.
author: Lord Byron
source: Line 826. A number of authors have addressed this common motif of an eagle shot with an eagle-feather arrow

text: Yet truth will sometimes lend her noblest fires, And decorate the verse herself inspires: This fact, in virtue's name, let Crabbe attest,— Though Nature 's sternest painter, yet the best.
author: Lord Byron

text: Know ye the land where the cypress and myrtle Are emblems of deeds that are done in their clime? Where the rage of the vulture, the love of the turtle, Now melt into sorrow, now madden to crime!
author: Lord Byron
source: Canto I, stanza 1; this can be compared to: "Know'st thou the land where the lemon-trees bloom, / Where the gold orange glows in the deep thicket's gloom, / Where a wind ever soft from the blue heaven blows, / And the groves are of laurel and myrtle and rose!" Goethe , Wilhelm Meister .

text: Where the virgins are soft as the roses they twine, And all, save the spirit of man, is divine?
author: Lord Byron

text: The light of love, the purity of grace , The mind , the music breathing from her face, 19 The heart whose softness harmonized the whole,— And oh, that eye was in itself a soul!
author: Lord Byron
source: Canto I, Stanza 6; this can be compared to: "The bloom of young Desire and purple light of Love", Thomas Gray , The Progress of Poesy I. 3, line 16; also: "Oh, could you view the melody / Of every grace / And music of her face", Richard Lovelace , Orpheus to Beasts ; "There is music in the beauty, and the silent note which Cupid strikes, far sweeter than the sound of an instrument", Thomas Browne , Religio Medici , Part ii, Section ix.

text: Who hath not proved how feebly words essay to fix one spark of beauty's heavenly ray?
author: Lord Byron

text: Be thou the rainbow to the storms of life, The evening beam that smiles the clouds away, And tints to-morrow with prophetic ray!
author: Lord Byron

text: Mark! where his carnage and his conquests cease! He makes a solitude, and calls it — peace !
author: Lord Byron
source: Canto II, stanza 20. Here Byron is using an adaptation of a quote from Agricola by the Roman historian Tacitus (c. 30). The original words in the text are Auferre, trucidare, rapere, falsis nominibus imperium; atque, ubi solitudinem faciunt, pacem appellant (To robbery, slaughter, plunder, they give the lying name of empire; they make a wilderness, and call it peace). This has also been reported as Solitudinem faciunt, pacem appellant (They make solitude, which they call peace).

text: Hark! to the hurried question of despair: "Where is my child?"—an echo answers, "Where?"
author: Lord Byron
source: Canto II, stanza 27; this can be compared to: I came to the place of my birth, and cried, "The friends of my youth, where are they?" And echo answered, "Where are they?", Anonymous Arabic manuscript

text: He who hath bent him o'er the dead Ere the first day of death is fled,— The first dark day of nothingness, The last of danger and distress, Before decay's effacing fingers Have swept the lines where beauty lingers.
author: Lord Byron

text: Such is the aspect of this shore; 'T is Greece, but living Greece no more! So coldly sweet, so deadly fair, We start, for soul is wanting there.
author: Lord Byron

text: Shrine of the mighty! can it be That this is all remains of thee?
author: Lord Byron

text: For freedom 's battle, once begun, Bequeath'd by bleeding sire to son, Though baffled oft, is ever won.
author: Lord Byron

text: And lovelier things have mercy shown To every failing but their own, And every woe a tear can claim Except an erring sister's shame.
author: Lord Byron

text: The keenest pangs the wretched find Are rapture to the dreary void, The leafless desert of the mind, The waste of feelings unemployed.
author: Lord Byron

text: Better to sink beneath the shock Than moulder piecemeal on the rock.
author: Lord Byron

text: The cold in clime are cold in blood, Their love can scarce deserve the name.
author: Lord Byron

text: I die — but first I have possessed, And come what may, I have been blessed.
author: Lord Byron

text: Love indeed is light from heaven; a spark of that immortal fire.
author: Lord Byron

text: Oh who can tell, save he whose heart hath tried.
author: Lord Byron
source: Canto I, stanza 1; this can be compared to: "To all nations their empire will be dreadful, because their ships will sail wherever billows roll or winds can waft them", Dalrymple , Memoirs , vol. iii, p. 152; "Wherever waves can roll, and winds can blow", Charles Churchill , The Farewell , Line 38.

text: Our thoughts as boundless, and our souls as free.
author: Lord Byron

text: She walks the waters like a thing of life, And seems to dare the elements to strife.
author: Lord Byron

text: The power of thought ,—the magic of the mind !
author: Lord Byron

text: Such hath it been — shall be — beneath the sun The many still must labour for the one!
author: Lord Byron

text: There was a laughing devil in his sneer.
author: Lord Byron

text: Hope withering fled, and Mercy sighed farewell!
author: Lord Byron

text: Farewell! For in that word, that fatal word,—howe'er We promise, hope, believe,—there breathes despair.
author: Lord Byron

text: No words suffice the secret soul to show, For truth denies all eloquence to woe.
author: Lord Byron

text: He left a corsair's name to other times, Linked with one virtue, and a thousand crimes.
author: Lord Byron
source: Canto III, stanza 24; this can be compared to: " Hannibal , as he had mighty virtues, so had he many vices; he had two distinct persons in him", Robert Burton , Anatomy of Melancholy , "Democritus to the Reader".

text: She walks in beauty, like the night Of cloudless climes and starry skies; And all that's best of dark and bright Meet in her aspect and her eyes: Thus mellow'd to that tender light Which heaven to gaudy day denies.
author: Lord Byron
source: She Walks in Beauty , st. 1. The subject of these lines was Mrs. R. Wilmot.—Berry Memoirs, vol. iii. p. 7.

text: We know that tears are vain, that death neither heeds nor hears distress; yet will this unteach us to complain?
author: Lord Byron
source: Oh! Snatched Away in Beauty's Bloom , from Hebrew Melodies

text: So gleams the past, the light of other days, Which shines, but warms not with its powerless rays.
author: Lord Byron
source: Sun of the Sleepless , from Hebrew Melodies

text: The Assyrian came down like the wolf on the fold, And his cohorts were gleaming in purple and gold; And the sheen of their spears was like stars on the sea, When the blue wave rolls nightly on deep Galilee.
author: Lord Byron

text: For the Angel of Death spread his wings on the blast.
author: Lord Byron

text: And the might of the Gentile, unsmote by the sword, Hath melted like snow in the glance of the Lord!
author: Lord Byron

text: When all of genius which can perish dies.
author: Lord Byron
source_section: Monody on the Death of Sheridan (1816)

text: Who track the steps of glory to the grave.
author: Lord Byron
source_section: Monody on the Death of Sheridan (1816)

text: Sighing that Nature formed but one such man, And broke the die, in molding Sheridan.
author: Lord Byron
source: Line 117; this can be compared to: "Natura il fece, e poi ruppe la stampa" (translated: "Nature made him, and then broke the mould"), Ariosto , Orlando Furioso , canto x, stanza 84; "The idea that Nature lost the perfect mould has been a favorite one with all song-writers and poets, and is found in the literature of all European nations", Book of English Songs , p. 28.
source_section: Monody on the Death of Sheridan (1816)

text: And both were young, and one was beautiful.
author: Lord Byron

text: And to his eye There was but one beloved face on earth , And that was shining on him.
author: Lord Byron

text: She was his life , The ocean to the river of his thoughts, Which terminated all.
author: Lord Byron
source: Stanza 2; this can be compared to: "She floats upon the river of his thoughts", Henry W. Longfellow , The Spanish Student , act ii, scene 3.

text: A change came o'er the spirit of my dream.
author: Lord Byron

text: And they were canopied by the blue sky, So cloudless, clear, and purely beautiful That God alone was to be seen in heaven .
author: Lord Byron

text: His recompense: a silent, intense suffering; the rock, the vulture, and the chain; a suffocating woe that speaks only in its loneliness.
author: Lord Byron

text: In thy Silence was his Sentence, and in his Soul a vain repentance, and evil dread so ill dissembled, that in his hand the lightnings trembled.
author: Lord Byron

text: A firm will, and a deep sense, which even in torture can decry its own concenter'd recompense, triumphant where it dares defy, and making Death a Victory.
author: Lord Byron

text: Sorrow is knowledge: they who know the most Must mourn the deepest o’er the fatal truth, The Tree of Knowledge is not that of Life.
author: Lord Byron

text: Mont Blanc is the Monarch of mountains; They crowned him long ago , On a throne of rocks — in a robe of clouds – With a Diadem of Snow.
author: Lord Byron

text: I call upon thee! and compel Thyself to be thy proper Hell!
author: Lord Byron

text: My mother Earth! And thou fresh breaking Day, and you, ye Mountains, Why are ye beautiful? I cannot love ye. And thou, the bright eye of the universe, That openest over all, and unto all Art a delight—thou shin'st not on my heart.
author: Lord Byron

text: But we, who name ourselves its sovereigns, we, Half dust, half deity, alike unfit To sink or soar.
author: Lord Byron

text: Ye avalanches, whom a breath draws down in mountainous overwhelming, come and crush me! But ye pass, and only fall on things that still would live.
author: Lord Byron

text: Patience is for beasts of burden, not for birds of prey; I am not of thine order.
author: Lord Byron

text: Actions are our epochs; mine have made my days and nights imperishable.
author: Lord Byron

text: My joys, my griefs, my passions, and my powers made me a stranger.
author: Lord Byron

text: Knowledge is not happiness , and science But an exchange of ignorance for that Which is another kind of ignorance.
author: Lord Byron

text: Whate'er I may have been, or am, doth rest between Heaven and myself; I shall not choose a mortal To be my mediator.
author: Lord Byron

text: No ritual, prayer, or penance can exorcise the quick sense of one's own sins; the self-condemned deals justice on his own soul.
author: Lord Byron

text: The Night hath been to me a more familiar face than that of man; in her starry shade, I learned the language of another world.
author: Lord Byron

text: The heart ran o'er With silent worship of the great of old! The dead but sceptred sovereigns, who still rule Our spirits from their urns.
author: Lord Byron

text: So, we'll go no more a roving So late into the night , Though the heart be still as loving, And the moon be still as bright.
author: Lord Byron
source_section: So, We'll Go No More A-Roving (1817)

text: For the sword outwears its sheath, And the soul wears out the breast, And the heart must pause to breathe, And love itself have rest.
author: Lord Byron
source_section: So, We'll Go No More A-Roving (1817)

text: Though the night was made for loving, And the day returns too soon, Yet we'll go no more a roving By the light of the moon.
author: Lord Byron
source_section: So, We'll Go No More A-Roving (1817)

text: For most men (till by losing rendered sager) Will back their own opinions by a wager.
author: Lord Byron

text: Soprano, basso, even the contra-alto, Wished him five fathom under the Rialto.
author: Lord Byron

text: His heart was one of those which most enamour us, Wax to receive, and marble to retain: He was a lover of the good old school, Who still become more constant as they cool.
author: Lord Byron
source: Stanza 34; this can be compared to: "My heart is wax to be moulded as she pleases, but enduring as marble to retain", Miguel de Cervantes , The Little Gypsy .

text: I love the language, that soft bastard Latin, which melts like kisses from a female mouth, and sounds as if it should be writ on satin.
author: Lord Byron

text: Heart on her lips, and soul within her eyes, Soft as her clime, and sunny as her skies.
author: Lord Byron

text: O Mirth and Innocence! O milk and water! Ye happy mixtures of more happy days.
author: Lord Byron

text: How my soul hates This language, Which makes life itself a lie, Flattering dust with eternity .
author: Lord Byron

text: Eat, drink, and love; the rest's not worth a fillip.
author: Lord Byron

text: I am the very slave of circumstance And impulse — borne away with every breath! Misplaced upon the throne — misplaced in life. I know not what I could have been, but feel I am not what I should be — let it end.
author: Lord Byron

text: None ever valued more thy virtues, though he knew not to profit by them.
author: Lord Byron

text: Assyria is not all the earth—we'll find a world out of our own, and be more blessed.
author: Lord Byron

text: All farewells should be sudden, when for ever, Else they make an eternity of moments, And clog the last sad sands of life with tears.
author: Lord Byron

text: The "good old times" — all times when old are good — Are gone.
author: Lord Byron

text: Where is he, the champion and the child Of all that's great or little, wise or wild ; Whose game was empires, and whose stakes were thrones; Whose table earth — whose dice were human bones?
author: Lord Byron

text: Washington's a watchword, such as ne'er shall sink while there's an echo left to air.
author: Lord Byron

text: Sublime tobacco! which from east to west Cheers the tar's labor or the Turkman's rest.
author: Lord Byron

text: Divine in hookas, glorious in a pipe When tipp'd with amber, mellow, rich, and ripe; Like other charmers, wooing the caress More dazzlingly when daring in full dress; Yet thy true lovers more admire by far Thy naked beauties—give me a cigar!
author: Lord Byron

text: Jack was embarrassed — never hero more, And as he knew not what to say, he swore.
author: Lord Byron

text: At fifty love for love is rare, 'tis true, But then, no doubt, it equally as true is, A good deal may be bought for fifty louis.
author: Lord Byron

text: I have squander'd my whole summer while 'twas May, and deem not what I deem'd my soul invincible.
author: Lord Byron

text: What is the end of fame? 'tis but to fill a certain portion of uncertain paper, to have, when the original is dust, a name, a wretched picture, and worse bust.
author: Lord Byron

text: All who joy would win must share it,—Happiness was born a twin.
author: Lord Byron

text: Whatever may have been their destiny—and it has been bitter—their country must ever be one of the most interesting on the globe.
author: Lord Byron
source: From the Letters of Lord Byron (2 January 1817), p. 6.
source_section: Lord Byron's Armenian Exercises and Poetry (1870)

text: What helps it now, that Byron bore, With haughty scorn which mocked the smart, Through Europe to the Aetolian shore The pageant of his bleeding heart? That thousands counted every groan, And Europe made his woe her own?
author: Lord Byron
source: Matthew Arnold , "Stanzas from the Grand Chartreuse," Fraser's Magazine (April 1855); reprinted in New Poems (1867).

text: If they had said that the sun or the moon had gone out of the heavens, it could not have struck me with the idea of a more awful and dreary blank in creation than the words: "Byron is dead!"
author: Lord Byron
source: Jane Welsh Carlyle , letter to Thomas Carlyle (20 May 1824).

text: The world is rid of Lord Byron, but the deadly slime of his touch still remains.
author: Lord Byron
source: John Constable , letter to the Rev. John Fisher (May 1824).

text: Lord Byron is great only as a poet; as soon as he reflects, he is a child.
author: Lord Byron
source: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe , said in conversation (18 January 1825) with Johann Peter Eckermann , quoted in Eckermann's Conversations with Goethe (1836).

text: With us the moral and political tittle-tattle of the day falls away, leaving the man and the talent standing alone in all their brilliance.
author: Lord Byron
source: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe , letter to John Murray (29 March 1831).

text: Emotional sorrow has inspired many sublime lyrics, much profound insight and poetic exultation of a Byron, Shelley, Heine, and their kind.
author: Lord Byron
source: Emma Goldman "Jealousy: Causes and a Possible Cure" (1910)

text: Lord Byron makes man after his own image, woman after his own heart; the one is a capricious tyrant, the other a yielding slave.
author: Lord Byron
source: William Hazlitt , "Lord Byron," The Spirit of the Age (1825).

text: Whatever he does, he must do in a more decided and daring manner than any one else; he lounges with extravagance, and yawns so as to alarm the reader!
author: Lord Byron
source: William Hazlitt , "Lord Byron," The Spirit of the Age (1825).

text: Ah! why did he ever ride his Pegasus to less advantage?
author: Lord Byron
source: Leigh Hunt , Autobiography (1850), vol. II, ch. XV.

text: The list of writers, artists, and composers who were directly inspired by Byron's life and poetry is almost without peer.
author: Lord Byron
source: Katherine Kernberger (22 September 2017). The Leslie A. Marchand Memorial Lectures, 2000–2015: A Legacy in Byron Studies . University of Delaware Press. p. 49. ISBN 978-1-61149-668-0 .

text: You speak of Lord Byron and me — there is this great difference between us. He describes what he sees — I describe what I imagine. Mine is the hardest task.
author: Lord Byron
source: John Keats , letter to George and Georgiana Keats (September 1819).

text: From the poetry of Lord Byron they drew a system of ethics, compounded of misanthropy and voluptuousness, a system in which the two great commandments were, to hate your neighbour, and to love your neighbour's wife.
author: Lord Byron
source: Thomas Babington Macaulay , in " Moore 's Life of Lord Byron" (June 1830), from Critical and Historical Essays Contributed to the Edinburgh Review (1843), vol. I.

text: I never heard a single expression of fondness for him fall from the lips of any of those who knew him well.
author: Lord Byron
source: Thomas Babington Macaulay , letter to Hannah and Margaret Macaulay (7 June 1831).

text: If one has experienced such struggles, for the rest of his life he will never get over knowing who has been in reality his greatest and most dangerous enemy.
author: Lord Byron
source: Friedrich Nietzsche , Human, All Too Human (1878).

text: Always looking at himself in mirrors to make sure he was sufficiently outrageous.
author: Lord Byron

text: Had Byron never written a line of poetry, his letters would give him a place in literature.
author: Lord Byron
source: Rowland Prothero, 1st Baron Ernle , " The New Letters of Byron ". The Quarterly Review 237 : 430–450. April 1922, quote p. 433

text: Our Lord Byron — the fascinating — faulty — childish — philosophical being — daring the world — docile to a private circle — impetuous and indolent — gloomy and yet more gay than any other.
author: Lord Byron
source: Mary Shelley , letter to John Murray (19 January 1830).

text: If I could envy any man for successful ill nature I should envy Lord Byron for his skill in satirical nomenclature.
author: Lord Byron
source: Sydney Smith , letter to Elizabeth Vassal Fox, Lady Holland (June 1810).

text: Byron was dead! I thought the whole world was at an end.
author: Lord Byron
source: Anne Isabella Thackeray, Lady Ritchie , Records of Tennyson, Ruskin, and Robert and Elizabeth Browning (1892).

text: You would not easily guess All the modes of distress Which torture the tenants of earth; And the various evils, Which like so many devils, Attend the poor souls from their birth.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: "Verses On A Cat" (1800), st. 2, in T. J. Hogg , Life of Shelley (1858), p. 21

text: Nought waits for the good but a spirit of Love, that will hail their blest advent to regions above. For Love, Mortal, gleams through the gloom of my sway, and the shades which surround me fly fast at its ray.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: "Death" in an untitled dialogue (1809), in T. J. Hogg, Life of Shelley (1858), p. 197

text: Dar'st thou amid the varied multitude To live alone, an isolated thing?
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: No fate is as bitter as that of the poor wretch who cannot love; he bears a killing, withering weight.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Sweet the rose which lives in Heaven, Although on earth ’tis planted, Where its honours blow, While by earth’s slaves the leaves are riven Which die the while they glow.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: Untitled (1810); titled "Love's Rose" in W. M. Rossetti , Poetical Works of Shelley (1870), p. 525

text: Age cannot Love destroy, but perfidy can blast the flower.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: Untitled (1810); titled "Love's Rose" in Rossetti (1870), p. 525

text: May Infinity Eternity blast me if I break my oath: I swear I will never forgive Christianity, wishing I were the Antichrist to crush its Demon and hurl him to Hell.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: Letter to Thomas Jefferson Hogg (3 January 1811)

text: The leaf of a tree, the meanest insect we trample, are more conclusive arguments that some vast intellect animates Infinity.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: Letter to Thomas Jefferson Hogg (3 January 1811)

text: The purest lessons of morality must fail so long as the slightest weight is attached to the dogma which is the vital essence of revealed religion.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: It were much better that a sentient being should never have existed, than that it should have existed only to endure unmitigated misery.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: "On the Vegetable System of Diet" (c. 1815; published in the 1920s), in Complete Works , ed. Roger Ingpen and Walter E. Peck, Volume 6 (New York: Gordian Press, 1965), pp. 343-344, original emphasis

text: Twilight, ascending slowly from the east, Entwined in duskier wreaths her braided locks O'er the fair front and radiant eyes of day, Night followed, clad with stars.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: Alastor, or The Spirit of Solitude (1816)

text: The lone couch of his everlasting sleep.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: Alastor, or The Spirit of Solitude (1816), line 57

text: Some say that gleams of a remoter world Visit the soul in sleep, — that death is slumber, And that its shapes the busy thoughts outnumber Of those who wake and live.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Nought may endure but Mutability.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: I could lie down like a tired child, and weep away the life of care, till death like sleep might steal on me.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: Stanzas Written in Dejection Near Naples (1818), st. 5

text: Sun-girt City, thou hast been Ocean's child, and then his queen; Now is come a darker day, And thou soon must be his prey.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: Lines Written among the Euganean Hills (1818)

text: Chameleons feed on light and air: Poets' food is love and fame.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Men of England, wherefore plough For the lords who lay ye low?
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: Song to the Men of England] (1819), st. 1 [

text: The seed ye sow another reaps; The wealth ye find another keeps; The robes ye weave another wears; The arms ye forge another bears.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: Song to the Men of England (1819), st. 5

text: Nothing in the world is single, All things by a law divine In one spirit meet and mingle — Why not I with thine?
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: The sunlight clasps the earth, And the moonbeams kiss the sea: What are all these kissings worth If thou kiss not me?
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: I arise from dreams of thee In the first sweet sleep of night, When the winds are breathing low, And the stars are shining bright.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: O lift me from the grass! I die! I faint! I fail! Let thy love in kisses rain On my lips and eyelids pale. My cheek is cold and white, alas! My heart beats loud and fast: O press it to thine own again, Where it will break at last!
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Hell is a city much like London — A populous and smoky city.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: Peter Bell the Third (1819), Pt. III, st. 1

text: I have drunken deep of joy, And I will taste no other wine tonight.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: The Cenci (1819), Act I, sc. iii, l. 88

text: Good-night? ah! no; the hour is ill Which severs those it should unite; Let us remain together still, Then it will be good night.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: The breath Of accusation kills an innocent name, And leaves for lame acquittal the poor life, Which is a mask without it.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Rulers who neither see, nor feel, nor know, but leech-like to their fainting country cling, till they drop, blind in blood, without a blow.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: A lovely lady, garmented in light From her own beauty.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: First our pleasures die — and then Our hopes, and then our fears — and when These are dead, the debt is due, Dust claims dust — and we die too.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: His fine wit Makes such a wound, the knife is lost in it.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Music, when soft voices die, Vibrates in the memory; and so thy thoughts, when thou art gone, Love itself shall slumber on.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: One word is too often profaned For me to profane it; One feeling too falsely disdained For thee to disdain it.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: One Word is Too Often Profaned (1821), st. 1

text: The desire of the moth for the star, Of the night for the morrow, The devotion to something afar From the sphere of our sorrow.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: One Word is Too Often Profaned (1821), st. 2

text: There is no sport in hate where all the rage Is on one side.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: When the lamp is shattered The light in the dust lies dead — When the cloud is scattered, The rainbow's glory is shed. When the lute is broken, Sweet tones are remembered not; When the lips have spoken, Loved accents are soon forgot.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: When the Lamp is Shattered (1822), st. 1

text: Lift not the painted veil which those who live Call Life.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Art thou pale for weariness Of climbing heaven and gazing on the earth , Wandering companionless Among the stars that have a different birth , — And ever changing , like a joyless eye That finds no object worth its constancy ?
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Are ye, two vultures sick for battle, Two scorpions under one wet stone, Two bloodless wolves whose dry throats rattle, Two crows perched on the murrained cattle, Two vipers tangled into one.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: Similes for Two Political Characters of 1819 (Published 1832), st. 4

text: That orbed maiden with white fire laden, Whom mortals call the moon.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: The Cloud , iv; reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: Sing again, with your dear voice revealing A tone Of some world far from ours, Where music and moonlight and feeling Are one.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: To Jane. The keen Stars were twinkling ; reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: There is no God! This negation must be understood solely to affect a creative Deity. The hypothesis of a pervading Spirit co-eternal with the universe remains unshaken.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: This is that contemplation of inexhaustible melancholy, whose shadow eclipses the brightness of the world.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: We must prove design before we can infer a designer.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: Alternate: Design must be proved before a designer can be inferred.

text: Government has no rights; it is a delegation from several individuals for the purpose of securing their own. It is therefore just, only so far as it exists by their consent, useful only so far as it operates to their well-being.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: No man has a right to disturb the public peace, by personally resisting the execution of a law however bad. He ought to acquiesce, using at the same time the utmost powers of his reason, to promote its repeal.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Man has no right to kill his brother, it is no excuse that he does so in uniform. He only adds the infamy of servitude to the crime of murder.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Belief is involuntary; nothing involuntary is meritorious or reprehensible. A man ought not to be considered worse or better for his belief.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: A Christian, a Deist, a Turk, and a Jew, have equal rights: they are men and brethren.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: If a person's religious ideas correspond not with your own, love him nevertheless. How different would yours have been, had the chance of birth placed you in Tartary or India!
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Titles are tinsel, power a corruptor, glory a bubble, and excessive wealth, a libel on its possessor.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: How wonderful is Death, Death and his brother Sleep!
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: The king ... like the vulgar, thinks, feels, acts, and lives Just as his father did; the unconquered powers Of precedent and custom interpose Between a king and virtue.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Power, like a desolating pestilence, pollutes all it touches; obedience, bane of genius, makes slaves of men.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Heaven's ebon vault, Studded with stars unutterably bright, Through which the moon's unclouded grandeur rolls, Seems like a canopy which love has spread To curtain her sleeping world.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: War is the statesman's game, the priest's delight, The lawyer's jest, the hired assassin's trade.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Suicidal selfishness, blighting the heart, is destined to decay; from its soil shall spring all virtue, delight, and love.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Selfishness, twin-sister of Religion, rival in crime and falsehood, is the cause and effect of tyranny, dead to all love but its own abjectness.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Gold is a living god and rules in scorn, All earthly things but virtue.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: The word of God has fenced about all crimes with Holiness.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: A husband and wife ought to continue united only so long as they love each other; any law binding them beyond affection is intolerable tyranny.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Love is free: to promise for ever to love the same woman, is not less absurd than to promise to believe the same creed: such a vow in both cases, excludes us from all enquiry.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Chastity is a monkish superstition, a foe to natural temperance, striking at the root of domestic happiness and consigning half the human race to misery.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Only by softening and disguising dead flesh by culinary art is it rendered palatable; otherwise, its raw horror excites intolerable loathing.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Only by softening and disguising dead flesh by culinary art is it rendered palatable; otherwise, its raw horror excites intolerable loathing.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: A physician with Locke's genius might trace all bodily and mental derangements to our unnatural habits.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: A vegetable diet and pure water infallibly mitigate all disease, converting debility to strength, madness to calm, and offering a pledge of society's moral reformation.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: By all that is sacred in our hopes for the human race, I conjure those who love happiness and truth, to give a fair trial to the vegetable system.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: A nation leading this great reform would become agricultural, commerce with its vice declining; natural habits would produce gentler manners, simplifying politics so each loves their country.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Mankind has always clung to the idea that sensibility and thought, distinct from matter, are less susceptible to division and decay, remaining perpetual and unchanged when the body resolves into its elements.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Some philosophers suppose intelligence is mere particle combination; those who believe in an afterlife then recur to supernatural power to overcome material dissipation.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Let us bring the question of life after death to the test of experience and fact, examining our nature to assert with certainty.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Thy light alone like mist o'er mountains driven, Or music by the night-wind sent Through strings of some still instrument, Or moonlight on a midnight stream, Gives grace and truth to life's unquiet dream.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Then black despair, The shadow of a starless night, was thrown Over the world in which I moved alone.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: A wild dissolving bliss Over my frame he breathed, approaching near, And bent his eyes of kindling tenderness Near mine, and on my lips impressed a lingering kiss.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: With hue like that when some great painter dips His pencil in the gloom of earthquake and eclipse.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Fear not the future, weep not for the past.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: My name is Ozymandias, king of kings: Look on my works, ye mighty, and despair! Nothing beside remains.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: I love all waste And solitary places; where we taste The pleasure of believing what we see Is boundless, as we wish our souls to be.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Where is the love, beauty and truth we seek, But in our mind?
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Those who inflict must suffer, for they see The work of their own hearts, and this must be Our chastisement or recompense.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Most wretched men Are cradled into poetry by wrong; They learn in suffering what they teach in song.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Wild Spirit, which art moving everywhere; Destroyer and preserver; hear, oh, hear!
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Thou dirge Of the dying year, to which this closing night Will be the dome of a vast sepulchre, Vaulted with all thy congregated might.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Thou who didst waken from his summer dreams the blue Mediterranean, where old palaces and towers quivered, overgrown with azure moss and flowers so sweet the sense faints picturing them.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Oh, lift me as a wave, a leaf, a cloud! I fall upon the thorns of life! I bleed!
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: O Wind, If Winter comes, can Spring be far behind?
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: As I lay asleep in Italy There came a voice from over the Sea, And with great power it forth led me To walk in the visions of Poesy.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: I met Murder on the way — He had a mask like Castlereagh — Very smooth he looked, yet grim; Seven blood-hounds followed him.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: All were fat; and well they might Be in admirable plight, For one by one, and two by two, He tossed them human hearts to chew.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: And many more Destructions played In this ghastly masquerade, All disguised, even to the eyes, Like Bishops, lawyers, peers, or spies.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Last came Anarchy: he rode On a white horse, splashed with blood; He was pale even to the lips, Like Death in the Apocalypse.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: And he wore a kingly crown; And in his grasp a sceptre shone; On his brow this mark I saw — ' I am God, and King, and Law! '
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: And with glorious triumph, they Rode through England proud and gay, Drunk as with intoxication Of the wine of desolation.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: My father Time is weak and gray With waiting for a better day; See how idiot-like he stands, Fumbling with his palsied hands!
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: What is Freedom? — ye can tell That which slavery is, too well — For its very name has grown To an echo of your own.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Thou art Justice — ne'er for gold May thy righteous laws be sold As laws are in England — thou Shield'st alike the high and low.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: What if English toil and blood Was poured forth, even as a flood? It availed, Oh, Liberty, To dim, but not extinguish thee.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Spirit, Patience, Gentleness, All that can adorn and bless Art thou — let deeds, not words, express Thine exceeding loveliness.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Let the blue sky overhead, The green earth on which ye tread, All that must eternal be Witness the solemnity.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: From the haunts of daily life Where is waged the daily strife With common wants and common cares Which sows the human heart with tares.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Be your strong and simple words Keen to wound as sharpened swords, And wide as targes let them be, With their shade to cover ye.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: My last delight! tell them that they are dull, And bid them own that thou art beautiful.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Poor captive bird! Who, from thy narrow cage, Pourest such music, that it might assuage The rugged hearts of those who prisoned thee, Were they not deaf to all sweet melody.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: I never thought before my death to see Youth's vision thus made perfect.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Thy wisdom speaks in me, and bids me dare Beacon the rocks on which high hearts are wreckt.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: True Love in this differs from gold and clay, That to divide is not to take away.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: If you divide suffering and dross, you may Diminish till it is consumed away; If you divide pleasure and love and thought, Each part exceeds the whole.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Love's very pain is sweet, But its reward is in the world divine Which, if not here, it builds beyond the grave.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: And bid them love each other and be blest: And leave the troop which errs, and which reproves, And come and be my guest, — for I am Love's.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: The cemetery is an open space among the ruins covered in winter with violets and daisies. It might make one in love with death, to think that one should be buried in so sweet a place.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: I weep for Adonais — he is dead! O, weep for Adonais! though our tears Thaw not the frost which binds so dear a head!
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Till the Future dares Forget the Past, his fate and fame shall be An echo and a light unto eternity!
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: He died, Who was the Sire of an immortal strain, Blind, old, and lonely.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: To that high Capital, where kingly Death Keeps his pale court in beauty and decay, He came.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: The quick Dreams , The passion-winged Ministers of thought.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Lost Angel of a ruined Paradise! She knew not 'twas her own; as with no stain She faded, like a cloud which had outwept its rain.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: The moving pomp might seem Like pageantry of mist on an autumnal stream.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Ah, woe is me! Winter is come and gone, But grief returns with the revolving year.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: From the great morning of the world when first God dawned on Chaos.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: The intense atom glows A moment, then is quenched in a most cold repose.
