text: St. Thomas Aquinas was one of the two or three greatest men who ever lived, an intellectual giant whose true scale is only apparent when compared to history's few rivals.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: G. K. Chesterton , in "St. Thomas Aquinas" in The Spectator (27 February 1932)

text: St. Thomas Aquinas posited two levels of good: the supreme supernatural, and the natural, as solidly good as for any man in the street.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: G. K. Chesterton , in "St. Thomas Aquinas" in The Spectator (27 February 1932)

text: St. Thomas exalted God without lowering Man; he exalted Man without lowering Nature, thereby creating a cosmos of common sense where saints and poets can be sane.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: G. K. Chesterton , in "St. Thomas Aquinas" in The Spectator (27 February 1932)

text: We do not need any further guarantee of our ability to attain truth than our awareness or recognition of the fact that we do in fact attain it.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: Frederick Copleston , Aquinas: An Introduction to the Life and Work of the Great Medieval Thinker (1955)

text: Thomas Aquinas, though accused of magic, was one of the profoundest scholars and subtlest logicians of his day.
author: Thomas Aquinas

text: Through the genius of Thomas Aquinas, all Church ideas were rewritten within Aristotle's framework, their mythological character reduced to a bare minimum.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: Morton Kelsey , Myth, History & Faith: The Mysteries of Christian Myth & Imagination (1974)

text: The finding of arguments for a conclusion given in advance is not philosophy, but special pleading.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: Bertrand Russell , A History of Western Philosophy (1945), Chapter XIII: Saint Thomas Aquinas

text: Roger Bacon criticized Aquinas and Albertus Magnus for teaching philosophy and theology they had imperfectly learned.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: Francis Seymour Stevenson , Robert Grosseteste: Bishop of Lincoln (1899)

text: Aquinas was a great physicist as well as a great theologian, whose Five Ways depended on Aristotelian physics, an essential precursor to modern physics.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: Frank J. Tippler , The Physics of Immortality: Modern Cosmology, God and the Resurrection of the Dead (1994) p. 329. Ref: Anthony Kenny , The Five Ways: St. Thomas Aquinas' Proof of God's Existence (1969) p. 329.

text: When asked by the Crucified One what his reward should be, Thomas Aquinas replied: 'Nothing but Yourself, Lord!'
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: Pope Benedict XVI , General Audience, 2 June 2010

text: We ought not to have more use and esteem of money and coin than of stones, lest we lose the kingdom of heaven for such a trifle.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: First Rule of the Friars Minor

text: Where there is charity and wisdom, there is neither fear nor ignorance. Where there is patience and humility, there is neither anger nor vexation. Where there is poverty and joy, there is neither greed nor avarice. Where there is peace and meditation, there is neither anxiety nor doubt.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: The Counsels of the Holy Father St. Francis , Admonition 27.

text: When the spirit grows lukewarm and the soul finds no delight, flesh and blood inevitably seek their own interests, and base instinct forms the conscience.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Thomas of Celano , The Life of Saint Francis , Francis of Assisi: Early Documents , Volume 2, p. 293.

text: It was the Father's will that his Son offer himself on the altar of the cross for our sins, leaving us an example to follow his steps.
author: Francis of Assisi

text: Those who serve the world through fleshly desires and worldly cares are captives of the devil.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: “Earlier Exhortation to the Brothers and Sisters of Penance,” Francis of Assisi: Early Documents , Volume 1, p. 43.

text: We must not be worldly-wise, but simple, humble, and pure.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: “Later Admonition and Exhortation to the Brothers and Sisters of Penance,” Francis of Assisi: Early Documents , Volume 1, p. 48.

text: No one can possess a single virtue unless they first die to self; for he who possesses one, possesses all, and he who offends one, possesses none.
author: Francis of Assisi

text: Let us not love in word or in tongue, but in deed and in truth.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Francis of Assisi, Rule of 1221, Rule 11 - That the Brothers ought not to speak or detract, but ought to love one another .
source_section: Preach the gospel, and if necessary, use words.

text: The true servant rebukes the wicked not merely with words, but with the radiant light of his life and example.
author: Francis of Assisi
source_section: Preach the gospel, and if necessary, use words.

text: All the Friars, however, should preach by their deeds.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Orthodox Order of Friars Minor , Rule XII
source_section: Preach the gospel, and if necessary, use words.

text: Lord, grant me the strength to change the things I can, the serenity to deal with the things I cannot change, and the wisdom to know the difference.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Widely known as The Serenity Prayer this has often been attributed to St. Francis, but earliest known forms of it appeared in the early 20th century, and it is generally credited to Reinhold Niebuhr .

text: He who works with his hands is a laborer. He who works with his hands and his head is a craftsman. He who works with his hands and his head and his heart is an artist.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: This quote was actually composed by Louis Nizer , and published in his book, Between You and Me (1948).

text: All the darkness in the world cannot extinguish the light of a single candle.
author: Francis of Assisi

text: When the spirit is lukewarm, and gradually growing cold as it moves from grace, flesh and blood inevitably seek their own interests.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Thomas of Celano , The Life of Saint Francis , Francis of Assisi: Early Documents , Volume 2, p. 293.

text: The mystic strips himself of action, facts, and objectives, rising to communion with the Beatitudes – brief moments that fill an entire life.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Pope Francis , who chose his papal name in honor of Francis, interviewed in "How the Church will change" by Eugenio Scalfari in La Repubblica (1 October 2013) .

text: He was a man of action, a poet, a prophet, a mystic; he rooted out evil within himself and loved all creation—nature, animals, and above all, people—the shining example of agape.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Pope Francis , interviewed in "How the Church will change" by Eugenio Scalfari in La Repubblica (1 October 2013).

text: Preaching does not consist of words alone, but also of behavior itself.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Helena Roerich , Letters of Helena Roerich Volume II , (10 December 1936)

text: St. Francis is the most attractive of Christians, admired even by Buddhists, atheists, and Communists, for his mystical realization of the unity of being.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Kenneth Rexroth , in "Eckhart, Brethren of the Free Spirit" from Communalism: From Its Origins to the Twentieth Century (1974), ch. 4.

text: Through prayer, all his difficulties were transfigured, becoming translucent; his life, stripped of personal grievance, became pure service and uninterrupted contemplation of God.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Adrienne von Speyr , Francis of Assisi in The Book of All Saints , translated by D.C. Schindler (San Francisco: Ignatius Press, 2008. ISBN 978-1-58617-192-6 .

text: Francis must rank with those creative personalities to which all the deepest developments of Christian consciousness are due.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Evelyn Underhill , The Mystics of the Church (1925). ISBN 0227 67820 6 .

text: Know ye not that the end and object of conquest is to avoid doing the same thing as the conquered?
author: Alexander the Great
source: As quoted in Lives by Plutarch , VII, "Demosthenes and Cicero. Alexander and Caesar" (40.2), as translated by Bernadotte Perrin

text: Accursed rivalry, not I, brought sister nations to this cruel fate; I would rather stand with you, united by blood and vision, than claim this bitter victory.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Addressing the dead Hellenes (the Athenean and Thebean Greeks) of the Battle of Chaeronea , as quoted in Historiae Alexandri Magni by Quintus Curtius Rufus

text: My purpose is to unite Hellenic and barbarian, to civilize every continent; else, I would emulate Diogenes' frugality.
author: Alexander the Great
source: As quoted in "On the Fortune of Alexander" by Plutarch , 332 a-b

text: We are free men, hardened by war; they are slaves, softened by luxury. You have Alexander, they — Darius!
author: Alexander the Great
source: Addressing his troops prior to the Battle of Issus , as quoted in Anabasis Alexandri by Arrian Book II, 7

text: Your ancestors harmed us; now, as leader of the Greeks, I have come to Asia to punish you.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Alexander's letter to Persian king Darius III of Persia in response to a truce plea, as quoted in Anabasis Alexandri by Arrian ; translated as Anabasis of Alexander by P. A. Brunt, for the "Loeb Edition" Book II 14, 4

text: Greeks, join me; let us move against the barbarians and liberate ourselves from Persian bondage, for we should not be slaves.
author: Alexander the Great
source: As quoted in the Historia Alexandri Magni of Pseudo-Kallisthenes , 1.15.1-4

text: I will free you, that you may see the difference between a Greek king and a barbarian tyrant. A king does not kill messengers.
author: Alexander the Great
source: As quoted in the Historia Alexandri Magni of Pseudo-Kallisthenes , 1.37.9-13

text: Should I leave you fallen for your wrongs against Greece, or raise you for your magnanimity and virtues?
author: Alexander the Great
source: Pausing and addressing to a fallen statue of Xerxes the Great

text: A city cannot thrive without fields and their fruits, just as a babe cannot grow without milk.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Vitruvius , De Architectura Bk. 2, Introduction, Sec. 3

text: I would rather excel in the knowledge of what is excellent, than in the extent of my power.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Quoted by Plutarch in Life of Alexander from Plutarch's Lives as translated by John Dryden (1683)

text: Alexander was a man of thunderbolt, like a summer cloud that passes over kings, rains, and disappears.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Abu'l-Fazl Bayhaqi , Tarikh-i Bayhaqi , Book VI, edited by Ali-Akbar Fayyaz , pages 118-119; in context of praising the Ghaznavid kings

text: Alexander prayed for harmony, that Macedonians and Persians should enjoy partnership in government.
author: Alexander the Great

text: When Alexander asked a pirate why he made the sea unsafe, the pirate replied: 'Because I do it with a small ship, I am called a robber; because you do it with a great fleet, you are called an emperor.'
author: Alexander the Great
source: Justice being taken away, then, what are kingdoms but great robberies? ... Indeed, that was an apt and true reply which was given to Alexander the Great by a pirate who had been seized. For when that king had asked the man what he meant by keeping hostile possession of the sea, he answered with bold pride, " What thou meanest by seizing the whole earth; but because I do it with a petty ship, I am called a robber, whilst thou who dost it with a great fleet art styled emperor. " Augustine of Hippo , The City of God , Book IV, Ch. 4

text: Alexander of Macedon, upon hearing that there were other worlds, wept that he had not yet conquered one, though soon after the funeral urn sufficed him.
author: Alexander the Great
source: John Calvin , in his interpretation of Psalm 146 in On The Book Of Psalms (1557) as translated by Rev. James Anderson (1849). Note: Alexander never occupied an urn: he was embalmed after death and conveyed to Memphis, whereupon his body was placed in a gold sarcophagus.

text: What a wretch is he who must survive his hopes! Nothing remains but to sit down and weep like Alexander, when he wanted other worlds to conquer.
author: Alexander the Great
source: William Congreve , in words for the character Fainall in Way of the World (1700)

text: Poor Alexander the Great wept because there were no more worlds to conquer.
author: Alexander the Great
source: Washington Irving in Salmagundi : Or, The Whim-whams and Opinions of Launcelot Langstaff, Esq. and Others (1835)

text: It is better to believe in men too rashly, and regret, than believe too meanly. Those who look in mankind only for their own littleness, and make them believe in that, kill more than he ever will in all his wars.
author: Alexander the Great

text: War is an act of violence, compelling the foe to our will.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: War therefore is an act of violence intended to compel our opponent to fulfil our will .

text: War is such a dangerous business that mistakes that come from kindness are the very worst.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: Since war is not an act of senseless passion but is controlled by its political object, the value of this object must determine the sacrifices to be made for it in magnitude and also in duration.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: Determination is courage not against physical danger, but against the soul's peril of responsibility, a habit where feeling, not intellect, guides the urgent moment.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Determination in a single instance is an expression of courage ; if it becomes characteristic, a mental habit . But here we are referring not to physical courage but to courage to accept responsibility , courage in the face of a moral danger . This has often been called courage d'esprit , because it is created by the intellect . That, however, does not make it an act of the intellect: it is an act of temperament . Intelligence alone is not courage; we often see that the most intelligent people are irresolute. Since in the rush of events a man is governed by feelings rather than by thought , the intellect needs to arouse the quality of courage, which then supports and sustains it in action . Looked at in this way, the role of determination is to limit the agonies of doubt and the perils of hesitation when the motives for action are inadequate.

text: A strong spirit is not merely capable of strong emotions, but remains balanced amidst them, allowing insight and conviction to guide it like a compass on a storm-tossed ship.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Strength of character does not consist solely in having powerful feelings, but in maintaining one’s balance in spite of them. Even with the violence of emotion, judgment and principle must still function like a ship’s compass, which records the slightest variations however rough the sea .

text: ...only the element of chance is needed to make war a gamble, and that element is never absent.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: ... in the whole range of human activities, war most closely resembles a game of cards.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: ... the role of determination is to limit the agonies of doubt and the perils of hesitation when the motives for action are inadequate.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: ...self-reliance is the best defense against the pressures of the moment.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: Architects and painters are precise with material phenomena, but when they aim for aesthetic effect on the mind or senses, the rules dissolve into vague ideas.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: Soldierly simplicity defines the military at its best, but in higher ranks, a broader view brings a complex drama of envy, generosity, pride, humility, wrath, and compassion.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: ...talent and genius operate outside the rules, and theory conflicts with practice.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: An intellectual instinct extracts the essence from life, as a bee sucks honey from a flower.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: It is better to strike in one direction than to scatter one's forces.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: Lost in jargon, authors soothe themselves with obscure ideas, hollow in plain speech.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: One must dedicate oneself to truth as to a pilgrimage, sparing no effort, fearing no power, and rising above vanity.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: Even without natural hatred, fighting itself stirs up hostile feelings; violence committed on orders will stir up revenge against the perpetrator.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: If we regard war's gains as isolated windfalls rather than links in a chain, we ignore their potential for future disadvantage.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: In war, the advantages and disadvantages of a single action are determined by the final balance.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: It is paltry philosophy to lay down rules for war in total disregard of moral values, which must be seen or felt, not counted.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Ch 3 : Moral Factors, as translated by Michael Howard and Peter Paret.

text: A commander's talents are best tested in rough hilly country, where control is challenging but not impossible.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: As man under pressure tends to give in to weakness, only great strength of will can lead to the objective.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: If forces are idle while the enemy fights, they are wasted, which is worse than using them inappropriately.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: ...any move made in a state of tension will be of more important, and will have more results, than it would have made in a state of equilibrium. In times of maximum tension this importance will rise to an infinite degree.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: If an assailant bypasses a defensive position with impunity, he will immediately enforce its abandonment and end its usefulness.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: Surprise is effective when we suddenly face the enemy with far more troops than expected; this localized numerical superiority is the most powerful medium in war.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: A conqueror is always a lover of peace; he would prefer to enter our state unopposed, so the weak must always be armed to prevent surprise.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: Clausewitz's 'On War' forged the first philosophy of war, shaping generations of German General Staff with its cool, sensible observation.
author: Carl von Clausewitz

text: Moltke, the incarnation of Clausewitzian theory, emphasized the primacy of battle, improvisation over doctrine, speed in decision, and individual talent over rigid systems.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Daniel J. Hughes, 'Introduction', Moltke On the Art of War: Selected Writings (1993), pp. 5-6

text: He was the Mahdi of mass and mutual massacre, the source of 'absolute war' which, though beginning with policy, ended by making policy the slave of strategy.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: B. H. Liddell Hart , The Ghost of Napoleon (1933), p. 120

text: Clausewitz's principle of force without limit fits only a hate-maddened mob; it negates statesmanship and intelligent strategy.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: B. H. Liddell Hart , The Ghost of Napoleon (1933), p. 122

text: The rascal has a ‘common sense’ bordering on wit.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Karl Marx to Friedrich Engels , quoted in Carl von Clausewitz, On War , ed. Michael Howard and Peter Paret (1989), p. 44

text: Clausewitz was right: concentrate against the main enemy, who must be overthrown first, for the armed forces form the true objective.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: George Orwell , review of B. H. Liddell Hart's The British Way in Warfare in New Statesman and Nation (21 November 1942), quoted in George Orwell, The Collected Essays, Journalism and Letters of George Orwell. Volume II: My Country Right or Left, 1940–1943 , eds. Sonia Orwell and Ian Angus (1968), p. 248

text: To misread Clausewitz and ascribe to his ideas greater influence than historical events is to fail to distinguish between events and their analyst.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Peter Paret , Clausewitz and the State: The Man, His Theories, and His Times (1976; 1985), p. ix

text: Clausewitz, no mere conservative, championed equality, universal military service, and a limited parliament, yet his emphasis on a strong central authority opposed traditional conservatives.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Peter Paret , Clausewitz and the State: The Man, His Theories, and His Times (1976; 1985), p. xiv

text: Clausewitz's views were a paradox: liberal on domestic affairs, seeking active citizens, yet conservative on foreign policy, valuing stability and balance of power, which in Prussia branded him Jacobin.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Peter Paret , Clausewitz and the State: The Man, His Theories, and His Times (1976; 1985), p. xiv

text: Foch emphasized enemy demoralization, but Clausewitz, thinking coolly, knew physical destruction was final, while moral overthrow was only temporary.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Walther Reinhardt , quoted in Herbert Rosinski, The German Army (1939; rev. ed., 1944), pp. 185-186

text: Clausewitz was a rare combination: philosophical contemplation and impatience for action, cold intellect and passionate sensitivity, a reflective spirit longing for heroic deeds.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Gerhard Ritter , The Sword and the Sceptre: The Problem of Militarism in Germany, Volume I: The Prussian Tradition, 1740–1890 (1972), p. 52

text: Clausewitz's Protestation of 1812 rejected all that might buffer life-and-death struggle, pledging instead to shed the last drop of blood for freedom, dignity, and fatherland.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Gerhard Ritter , The Sword and the Sceptre: The Problem of Militarism in Germany, Volume I: The Prussian Tradition, 1740–1890 (1972), pp. 53-54

text: Clausewitz, a practical soldier and natural theorist, forged 'On War' from frustrating confinement, creating the most profound, comprehensive examination of war to this day.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Herbert Rosinski , The German Army (1939; rev. ed., 1944), p. 73

text: Transcending Scharnhorst and Napoleon, Clausewitz developed a systematic theory of war that, after his death, replaced Jomini's doctrines and profoundly shaped Moltke's General Staff.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Herbert Rosinski , The German Army (1939; rev. ed., 1944), pp. 73-74

text: Clausewitz warned: a war's characteristic style, developed from a single case, can outlive its situation, leading to 'methodism'—stereotypes and conventional patterns.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Leon Trotsky , ' Military Doctrine or Pseudo-Military Doctrinairism ' (1921), The Military Writings of Leon Trotsky, Volume 5: 1921–1923

text: Clausewitz understands that you must break the enemy's will: first crush his army, then take his capital, and if not enough, occupy his territory.
author: Carl von Clausewitz
source: Spenser Wilkinson to B. H. Liddell Hart (14 June 1924), quoted in Brian Bond, Liddell Hart: A Study of His Military Thought (1977), p. 44

text: Countless times upon this star, I have fought and perished, always seeking the sublime Victory Maid.
author: George S. Patton

text: I have sinned and suffered, played hero and knave, fought for many causes, and for each found a grave.
author: George S. Patton

text: Few men are killed by bayonets, but many are scared by them. Having the bayonet fixed makes our men want to close. Only the threat to close will defeat a determined enemy.
author: George S. Patton
source: notes on combat written by General Patton were published in Tactical and Technical Trends , No. 30, July 29, 1943.

text: In war, just as in loving, you must keep on shoving, or you'll never get your reward.
author: George S. Patton

text: All real Americans love the sting and clash of battle.
author: George S. Patton

text: Americans have never lost nor will ever lose a war; for the very idea of losing is hateful to an American.
author: George S. Patton

text: The real hero is the man who fights even though he is scared.
author: George S. Patton

text: Alertness must be bred into every soldier.
author: George S. Patton

text: An Army is a team. It lives, sleeps, eats, and fights as a team.
author: George S. Patton

text: Every man is a vital link in the great chain.
author: George S. Patton

text: Each man must not think only of himself, but also of his buddy fighting beside him.
author: George S. Patton

text: Some day I want to see them raise up on their piss-soaked hind legs and howl, 'Jesus Christ, it's the Goddamned Third Army again and that son-of-a-fucking-bitch Patton'.
author: George S. Patton

text: The shortest way home is through Berlin and Tokyo.
author: George S. Patton

text: War is a bloody, killing business. You've got to spill their blood, or they will spill yours.
author: George S. Patton

text: Our basic plan of operation is to advance and to keep on advancing regardless of whether we have to go over, under, or through the enemy.
author: George S. Patton

text: I believe in the old and sound rule that an ounce of sweat will save a gallon of blood.
author: George S. Patton

text: No, Sir, you can look him straight in the eye and say, 'Son, your Granddaddy rode with the Great Third Army and a Son-of-a-Goddamned-Bitch named Georgie Patton!'
author: George S. Patton
source: Though 2 publications, Eyewitness to War (2006) by Antony Bird and Nicholas Bird, p. 256, and Charge! : History's Greatest Military Speeches‎ (2007) by Steve Israel, p. 200, have been found which use "George", all earlier published sources available use "Georgie" in this line.

text: Men get what they want by planning, working, and praying. Between the plan and its execution lies an unknown, determining victory or defeat, where God has His part.
author: George S. Patton
source: As quoted in "The True Story of The Patton Prayer" by James H. O'Neill in Review of the News (6 October 1971)

text: We promised Europeans freedom; it would be dishonorable not to deliver, even if it means war with the Russians. Though they seem weak now, someday we will fight them, and it will take six years and cost six million lives.
author: George S. Patton
source: As quoted in The Unknown Patton (1983) by Charles M. Province, p. 100

text: To make important things stick, I give it to them double dirty. You can't run an army without eloquent profanity; an army without it couldn't fight its way out of a piss-soaked paper bag.
author: George S. Patton
source: Remark to his nephew about his copious profanity, quoted in The Unknown Patton (1983) by Charles M. Province, p. 184

text: Grant us fair weather for Battle, that armed with Thy power, we may advance from victory to victory.
author: George S. Patton
source: Though Patton commissioned this prayer and ordered 250,000 copies of it printed with his signature, it was actually composed by Chief Chaplain James H. O'Neill Review of the News (6 October 1971)

text: Wonder weapons offer no glory, no heroes, no cowards; only the living and the dead remain.
author: George S. Patton
source: Attributed as a quote in Charles W. Hudlin, "Morality and the Military Profession: Problems and Solutions", Military Ethics (National Defense University Press, 1987) ; but Hudlin cites the biographical dramatization Patton (1970 film) which does not purport to use Patton's actual words.

text: [Patton was] arrogant, publicity-seeking and personally flawed, but ... among the greatest generals of the war.
author: George S. Patton
source: Terry Brighton, Patton, Montgomery, Rommel: Masters of War (2009), p. xv

text: To Patton, war was a holy crusade.
author: George S. Patton
source: Omar Bradley , A Soldier's Story (1951), p. 52

text: Patton, however successful he was as a corps commander, had not learned to command himself.
author: George S. Patton
source: Omar Bradley , A Soldier's Story (1951), p. 52

text: To be a successful soldier you must know history.
author: George S. Patton
source: Carlo D'Este, Eisenhower: A Soldier's Life (2002), p. 45-46

text: After that... if you wanted Moscow, I could give it to you.
author: George S. Patton
source: Winston Groom, The Generals: Patton, MacArthur, Marshall, and the Winning of World War II (2015), p. 394-395

text: The art of the general is not strategy but knowing how to mold human nature.
author: George S. Patton
source: S. L. A. Marshall , on Patton and his portrayal in Patton (1970) , in "Great Georgie Redone", in The Charleston Gazette (21 March 1970), § 4. p. 4

text: If you're a leader, you don't push wet spaghetti, you pull it.
author: George S. Patton
source: Bill Mauldin , in The Brass Ring (1971)

text: American generals showed tactical advancement, but Patton's Army in France revealed astonishing achievements in mobile warfare.
author: George S. Patton
source: Erwin Rommel , as quoted in The Rommel Papers (1953) edited by Sir Basil Henry Liddell Hart , Ch. 23: The Sky Has Grown Dark, p. 523

text: He disapproved of taking casualties with almost fanatical disapproval, coupled with an intense desire to inflict them on the enemy.
author: George S. Patton
source: George C. Scott, who portrayed Patton in the 1970 film of the same name , as quoted in The Fighting Pattons (1997) by Brian M. Sobel, p. 136

text: You can't be a leader without respect. General Patton was so good, even his enemies respected him. The same could be said for Rommel. If that's not leadership, I don't know what is.
author: George S. Patton
source: Robert J. Shano, Never Too Old For War (2023), self-published, paperback first edition, p. 48

text: Patton possessed 'civil courage': he dared speak his mind and act on his convictions. Beyond the swashbuckling image, he had a scholarly bent and profound knowledge of strategy.
author: George S. Patton
source: Albert C. Wedemeyer, Wedemeyer Reports! (1958), p. 222

text: Be careful what you say. No matter what, they'll put it in the newspapers.
author: George S. Patton
source: William Westmoreland , in his memoirs A Soldier Reports (1976), p. 22.

text: Successful leaders must be highly visible, sharing the hardships of their men. Patton always visited the front lines, but returned by plane to avoid his men seeing him move back.
author: George S. Patton
source: Richard Winters , in his book Beyond Band of Brothers: The War Memoirs of Major Dick Winters (2006), p. 286

text: Avoid excessive sharpness or harshness of voice, which usually indicates the man who has shortcomings of his own to hide.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Address as Director of the Military School in Wiener Neustadt at the passing out parade of the 1938 class of cadets.

text: Better to wield the spade too much than too little! Such toil saves blood.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Better too much spade work than too little! This work saves blood. p. 28.

text: In equal combat, victory belongs to him with one more cartridge in the chamber.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: “In a man on man fight between comparable opponents, the winner is he who has put one more bullet through the barrel.” p. 62.

text: When victory is decisive, never settle for a narrow aim; exploit success.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Ch. V : Graziani's Defeat - Cause and Effect, p. 96.

text: The military career of most of the people who aimed these accusations at us was notable for a consistent absence from the front, on the principle of " weit vom Schuss gibt alte Kreiger " - "far from the battle makes old soldiers."
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Ch XV : Alamein in Retrospect, p. 327.

text: Thus the British lost the very able and adaptable commander David Stirling of the desert group SAS which had caused us more damage than any other British unit of equal strength.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Ch XVIII : Back to Tunisia, p. 393.

text: Costly experience brought rich dividends, revealing the American generals' advanced tactical handling and astonishing achievements in mobile warfare.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Ch. XXIII : The Sky Has Grown Dark, p. 523.

text: Good soldiers, bad officers; however don't forget that without them we would not have any Civilization .
author: Erwin Rommel
source: On Italians, sometimes cited to The Rommel Papers (1953) edited by Basil Henry Liddell Hart , but without specific chapter or page citations; it seems to summarize an attitude indicated by Rommel in Ch. 11 of that work, but no published occurrence of this has actually been located.

text: He was a tactician of the greatest ability, quick to seize fleeting opportunity, yet liable to overexploit immediate success without sufficient thought for the future or a sound administrative plan.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: British General Harold Alexander , commander, Allied forces in the Middle East, as quoted in London Gazette (3 February 1948)

text: There is a real danger that Rommel is becoming a magical bogeyman to our troops; dispel the idea that he is more than an ordinary, albeit energetic and able, general.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: British General Claude Auchinleck , in a directive to his officers, quoted in Rommel, The Desert Fox (1951) by Desmond Young , p. 7.

text: He was a splendid military gambler whose ardour inflicted grievous disasters, yet he was a great general who came to hate Hitler and paid for his defiance with his life.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Winston Churchill , in The Second World War , Vol. 3: The Grand Alliance (1950), pp. 176-177.

text: Rommel was jumpy, wanted to do everything at once, then lost interest. Rommel was my superior in command in Normandy. I cannot say Rommel wasn't a good general. When successful, he was good; during reverses, he became depressed.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Sepp Dietrich , to Leon Goldensohn (28 February 1946)

text: Hitler might have won the war by focusing on the Mediterranean in 1941, before invading the Soviet Union, exploiting British vulnerabilities and Rommel's potential.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Niall Ferguson , The War of the World: Twentieth-Century Conflict and the Descent of the West (2006), p. xliii

text: Rommel's magnetic presence, inexhaustible spirit, and fearless imagination transformed his men into 'real soldiers' who idolized him.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: David William Fraser , on Rommel's role on the battlefields of World War I, in Knight's Cross : A Life of Field Marshal Erwin Rommel‎ (1994), Ch. 3, Gebirgsbataillon, p. 40

text: Rommel was a master of manoeuvre and a leader of the purest quality; his speed of perception, energy of execution, and boldness of concept placed him among the great, despite his occasional errors.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: David William Fraser , Knight's Cross : A Life of Field Marshal Erwin Rommel‎ (1994), p. 560

text: Rommel was more than a tactical genius; he was a reflective master practitioner who deduced theory from practice, evolving lessons that continue to teach and benefit military art.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: David William Fraser , Knight's Cross : A Life of Field Marshal Erwin Rommel‎ (1994), p. 560

text: Unlike Montgomery, Rommel often fought with insufficient resources and numerical disadvantage, relying on skill and calculated risks, believing inactivity is seldom forgiven by fate.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: David William Fraser , Knight's Cross : A Life of Field Marshal Erwin Rommel‎ (1994), p. 562

text: Though Rommel ultimately lost, victory is not the sole criterion for military genius; like Napoleon, Montrose, and Lee, his leadership in battle stands among the great.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: David William Fraser , Knight's Cross : A Life of Field Marshal Erwin Rommel‎ (1994), p. 562

text: Rommel was the best leader of fast-moving troops up to army level, but above that, his moody, changeable nature proved too much.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Albert Kesselring , to Leon Goldensohn (4 February 1946)

text: Rommel, the leading exemplar of chivalry, defied Hitler's orders to execute prisoners, ensuring Allied captives received equal rations, even writing 'War Without Hate'.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Steven Pressfield , in Killing Rommel (2009), p. 7

text: Rommel, a legend of military genius and chivalry, was Germany's best general, whose Africa Korps pushed the British to Cairo, threatening to lose the war.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Steven Pressfield , in "Leaders with Character, Chivalry and Courage – Relics of the Past?" at Knol

text: Churchill cried, 'Rommel, Rommel, Rommel! What else matters but beating him!' The Rommel magic had become legend, and his defeat was paramount.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: C.L. Sulzberger, in his book The American Heritage Picture History of World War II (1966), p. 234

text: Rommel was a military phenomenon, as brave as Ney, as dashing as Murat, and as cool and quick a tactician as Wellington, a rare blend of genius and fortune.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Field Marshal Archibald Wavell , as quoted in Discovering the Rommel Murder (1994) by Charles Marshall, p. 1

text: Under the spell of his personality, men became real soldiers; he seemed inexhaustible, instinctively knowing the enemy, his plans startling and spontaneous.
author: Erwin Rommel
source: Theodor Werner, an officer who, during World War I, served under Rommel, as quoted in The Trail of the Fox (1977) by David Irving , p. 15

text: I refuse to ascribe human flaws to the divine, believing the benevolent being cares not for human affairs, attributing all to creatures and unpredictable causes, silently bowing to its unknowable wisdom.
author: Frederick the Great
source: I feel the deepest veneration for the divine being, and therefore I am careful not to attribute to him unjust, fickle behavior, which would be condemned by the meanest mortal. Because of that, dear sister, I prefer not to believe that the almighty, benevolent being is at all concerned with human affairs. Rather I do attribute everything that happens to the living beings and certain effects of incalculable causes and I silently bow down before this being which is worthy of adoration, by admitting my ignorance concerning his ways, which his godly wisdom chose not to reveal.

text: A prince's oath binds not just himself, but nations to misfortune; thus, it is better he break his contract than for his people to perish.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Historie vom Jahre 1746 , quoted in W. W. Coole (ed.), Thus Spake Germany (London: George Routledge & Sons, 1941), p. 82

text: With all the algebra in the world, one is often but a fool if one knows nothing else; all that is neither useful nor agreeable is worthless, and I see in these calculations only scientific extravagance.
author: Frederick the Great
source: [A] man with all the algebra in the world is often only an ass when he knows nothing else. Perhaps in ten years society may derive advantage from the curves which these visionary algebraists will have laboriously squared. I congratulate posterity beforehand. But to tell you the truth I see nothing but a scientific extravagance in all these calculations . That which is neither useful nor agreeable is worthless. And as for useful things, they have all been discovered; and to those which are agreeable, I hope that good taste will not admit algebra among them.

text: I know the value of tranquility and happiness, but I will not buy them with baseness; philosophy teaches us to serve our country faithfully, at the expense of our blood and repose.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Letters of Voltaire and Frederick the Great (New York: Brentano's, 1927), trans. Richard Aldington, letter 141 from Frederick to Voltaire ( 1759-07-02 )

text: Of all Prussia's neighbors, Russia is the most dangerous; future rulers must cultivate their friendship, for their immense power can ruin Prussia, and their poverty makes retaliation impossible.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Histoire de mon temps (1763-1764), quoted in W. W. Coole (ed.), Thus Spake Germany (London: George Routledge & Sons, 1941), p. 225

text: The King finds the rise of coffee disgusting; his ancestors and soldiers thrived on beer, and he doubts coffee-drinkers can endure the hardships of war.
author: Frederick the Great
source: 1777; quoted by Bert L. Vallée, Alcohol in the Western World , Scientific American, Vol. 278, No. 6 (June), 1998, pp. 80-85

text: As to your Newton , I confess I do not understand his void and his gravity ; I admit he has demonstrated the movement of the heavenly bodies with more exactitude than his forerunners; but you will admit it is an absurdity to maintain the existence of Nothing .
author: Frederick the Great
source: Letters of Voltaire and Frederick the Great (New York: Brentano's, 1927), trans. Richard Aldington, letter 221 from Frederick to Voltaire ( 1777-11-25 )

text: Euler's precise geometry failed to raise a single drop of water for my fountain. Vanity of vanities! Vanity of geometry!
author: Frederick the Great
source: I wanted to have a water jet in my garden: Euler calculated the force of the wheels necessary to raise the water to a reservoir, from where it should fall back through channels, finally spurting out in Sans Souci . My mill was carried out geometrically and could not raise a mouthful of water closer than fifty paces from the reservoir. Vanity of vanities! Vanity of geometry!

text: (About the battle of Kunersdorf) "I shall not survive this cruel misfortune. The consequences will be worse than defeat itself. I have no resources left, and, to speak quite frankly I believe everything is lost. I shall not outlive the downfall of my country. Farewell, forever!"
author: Frederick the Great
source: Holmes, Richard; John Pimlott (1999). The Hutchinson Atlas of Battle Plans: Before and After . Taylor & Francis. ISBN 9781579582036 .

text: Machiavelli's The Prince is to ethics what Spinoza's work is to faith: it corrupts policy and destroys healthy morals, a dangerous book for any prince.
author: Frederick the Great

text: Justice is the sovereign's main responsibility, prioritizing the people's interest over their own; a sovereign is the first servant, not an absolute master, contrary to Machiavelli.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Ch. 1 : What A Strong Prince Really Is, And How One Can Reach That Point

text: But France 's powerful armies , and a very large number of fortresses, ensure that the French Sovereign will possess the throne forever , and they do not have anything to fear now concerning internal wars or their neighbors invading France .
author: Frederick the Great

text: Holland, a small, marshy republic, became a powerful, rich, industrious nation that defied Spain and trades globally, proving that size doesn't dictate power.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Ch. 5 : How It Is Necessary To Control The Cities, Or The Principalities, Which Are Controlled By Their Own Laws Before They Were Conquered

text: Humans foolishly strive for endless conquest, forgetting their mortality, often laboring for unworthy or ungrateful successors.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Ch. 5 : How It Is Necessary To Control The Cities, Or The Principalities, Which Are Controlled By Their Own Laws Before They Were Conquered

text: Men are never without passions: moderated, they are the heart of enterprise; unchecked, they bring destruction.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Ch. 6 : New States That The Prince Acquires By His Valor And His Own Weapons

text: Hero's policy of discarding loyal friends and soldiers for new ones is imprudent; tested loyalty is always safer than the unknown.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Ch. 6 : New States That The Prince Acquires By His Valor And His Own Weapons

text: (About Cesare Borgia ) What cruelties were not the result of his? Who could count all his crimes ? Such was the man that Machiavel prefers to all the great geniuses of his time, and to the heroes of antiquity, and of which he finds the life and action make a good example for those that fortune favors.
author: Frederick the Great

text: A cruel man, unchecked in youth, becomes furious and foolish. To ensure earthly justice, virtue must be known, for it leads to unity, while crime brings only destruction, even to the criminal.
author: Frederick the Great

text: Like humans, republics are born, flourish, and die, whether by internal audacity or external enemies; all empires, even the greatest, have their finite time.
author: Frederick the Great

text: To exist idly for ages is to not live at all; true life is measured by meaningful action.
author: Frederick the Great

text: Christianity is an old metaphysical fiction, stuffed with fables, contradictions and absurdities.
author: Frederick the Great

text: Frederick was an acquired taste: repulsive at first, yet inspiring a sentiment possibly stronger than love.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Sebastian Haffner , The Rise and Fall of Prussia (1980), p. 32

text: Frederick the Great was elevated to a national divinity, his example becoming the supreme national dogma.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Frederick Hertz , Nationalgeist und Politik , Vol. I (Zuerich, 1937), p. 450, quoted in Lord Vansittart, ‘Preface’, W. M. Knight-Patterson, Germany from Defeat to Conquest, 1913–1933 (1945), p. 9

text: There has never been any prominent man who despised humanity so bitterly and unaffectedly as Frederick despised it.
author: Frederick the Great

text: He became in a secular sense the Patron Saint of Germany.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Lord Rosebery , 'Introduction', in Henri de Catt, Frederick the Great: The Memoirs of His Reader Henri de Catt (1758–1760). Vol. I , translated by F. S. Flint (1916), p. xxxvi

text: He is for us the personification of the ancient Prussian spirit.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Gustav Stresemann , quoted in W. W. Coole (ed.), Thus Spake Germany (1941), p. 39

text: Frederick the Great is for us the embodiment of that ancient Prussian spirit on which is based the categorical imperative of duty.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Gustav Stresemann , quoted in W. M. Knight-Patterson, Germany from Defeat to Conquest, 1913–1933 (1945), p. 369

text: Asked for proof of God's existence, Zimmermann replied, 'Your Majesty, the Jews!' For hundreds of nations have vanished, yet this one tiny nation endures.
author: Frederick the Great
source: Dogmatics in Outline (1949) as translated by Tr. G. T. Thomson ISBN 006130056X , quoted in "Word Alive! An Introduction to the Christian Faith" by John Schwarz (1995)

text: Abandon all local animosities; make your sons Americans.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Advice to a Confederate widow who expressed animosity towards the northern U.S. after the end of the American Civil War, as quoted in The Life and Campaigns of General Lee (1875) by Edward Lee Childe, p. 331. Also quoted in "Will Confederate Heritage Advocates Take Robert E. Lee’s Advice?" (July 2014), by Brooks D. Simpson, Crossroads , WordPress. This quote is sometimes paraphrased as: "Madam, do not train up your children in hostility to the government of the United States. Remember, we are all one country now. Dismiss from your mind all sectional feeling, and bring them up to be Americans."

text: A true man of honor feels humbled himself when he cannot help humbling others.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: "Definition of a Gentleman" , a memorandum found in his papers after his death, as quoted in Lee the American (1912) by Gamaliel Bradford, p. 233

text: I think it is the duty of every citizen, in the present condition of the Country, to do all in his power to aid in the restoration of peace and harmony. It is particularly incumbent upon those charged with the instruction of the young to set them an example.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to trustees, as quoted in "Honoring Lee Anew" (15 July 2014), by David Cox, A Magazine of Student Thought and Opinion

text: All should unite in honest efforts to obliterate the effects of war and restore the blessing of peace.
author: Robert E. Lee

text: The Abolitionist ... must see that he has neither the right or power of operating except by moral means and suasion.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Speech in the Senate (3 March 1854); Quoted in Douglas Southall Freeman (2008) Lee, p. 93

text: A Union maintained by swords and bayonets, where strife replaces brotherly love, has no charm for me.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to his son , G. W. Custis Lee (23 January 1861).

text: War is a cruel thing, destroying families, marring joy, and devastating the beautiful world with hatred.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to his wife on Christmas Day, two weeks after the Battle of Fredericksburg (25 December 1862).

text: I will henceforth faithfully support, protect, and defend the Constitution of the United States, the Union, and all laws regarding the emancipation of slaves.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Amnesty oath to the United States (2 October 1865)

text: It is the part of wisdom to acquiesce in the result, and of candor to recognize the fact, when questions not decided by reason are settled by war.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to former Virginia governor John Letcher (28 August 1865), as quoted in Personal Reminiscences, Anecdotes, and Letters of Gen. Robert E. Lee (1875) by John William Jones, p. 203

text: All should vote for the most intelligent, honest, and conscientious men, irrespective of party, to ensure the prosperity and liberty of all classes.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to General James Longstreet (29 October 1867), as quoted in Recollections and Letters of General Robert E. Lee (1924), p. 269.

text: It is wiser not to keep open the sores of war, but to commit to oblivion the feelings it engendered.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter regarding war monuments (1869), as quoted in Personal reminiscences, anecdotes, and letters of gen. Robert E. Lee (1874), by John William Jones , p. 234. Also quoted in "Renounce the battle flag: Don't whitewash history" (26 June 2015), by Petula Dvorak, The Washington Post , Washington, D.C. This quote is also given as: "I think it wisest not to keep open the sores of war, but to follow the example of those nations who endeavored to obliterate the marks of civil strife, and to commit to oblivion the feelings it engendered."

text: Honesty in its widest sense is always admirable. The trite saying that 'honesty is the best policy' has met with the just criticism that honesty is not policy. The seems to be true. The real honest man is honest from conviction of what is right, not from policy.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: "Memoirs of Robert E. Lee" by A. L. Long (1886)

text: I have rejoiced that slavery is abolished, and would cheerfully have lost all I have lost by the war to have this object attained.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Statement to John Leyburn (1 May 1870), as quoted in R. E. Lee: A Biography (1934) by Douglas Southall Freeman.

text: It is history that teaches us to hope.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Letter to Lieutenant Colonel Charles Marshall (September 1870)

text: Had I foreseen these results of subjugation, I would have preferred to die at Appomattox with my brave men, my sword in my right hand.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Supposedly made to Governor Fletcher S. Stockdale (September 1870), as quoted in The Life and Letters of Robert Lewis Dabney , pp. 497-500; however, most major researchers including Douglas Southall Freeman, Shelby Dade Foote, Jr., and Bruce Catton consider the quote a myth and refuse to recognize it. “T. C. Johnson: Life and Letters of Robert Lewis Dabney, 498 ff. Doctor Dabney was not present and received his account of the meeting from Governor Stockdale. The latter told Dabney that he was the last to leave the room, and that as he was saying good-bye, Lee closed the door, thanked him for what he had said and added: "Governor, if I had foreseen the use these people desired to make of their victory, there would have been no surrender at Appomattox, no, sir, not by me. Had I foreseen these results of subjugation, I would have preferred to die at Appomattox with my brave men, my sword in this right hand." This, of course, is second-hand testimony. There is nothing in Lee's own writings and nothing in direct quotation by first-hand witness that accords with such an expression on his part. The nearest approach to it is the claim by H. Gerald Smythe that "Major Talcott" — presumably Colonel T. M. R. Talcott — told him Lee stated he would never have surrendered the army if he had known how the South would have been treated. Mr. Smythe stated that Colonel Talcott replied, "Well, General, you have only to blow the bugle," whereupon Lee is alleged to have answered, "It is too late now" (29 Confederate Veteran, 7). Here again the evidence is not direct. The writer of this biography, talking often with Colonel Talcott, never heard him narrate this incident or suggest in any way that Lee accepted the results of the radical policy otherwise than with indignation, yet in the belief that the extremists would not always remain in office”.

text: He replied that he had been in seventeen battles and he was not born to be killed by a d----d Yankee.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Flavel Clingan Barber, Holding The Line: The Third Tennessee Infantry (1994), p. 82.

text: Robert E. Lee, whose lips were never profaned by an oath, whiskey, or tobacco, and Stonewall Jackson, who opened every battle with a prayer.
author: Robert E. Lee

text: He had refused command of the Federal Army and resigned his commission for 'I cannot raise my hand against my birthplace...'
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Lamont Buchanan, A Pictorial History of the Confederacy (1951), p. 41

text: When Lee was to hear of the wound to Jackson that later proved fatal, he wrote: 'You have lost your left arm, but I have lost my right.'
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Lamont Buchanan, A Pictorial History of the Confederacy (1951), p. 99

text: Lee once remarked modestly to a worshipful attendant that he felt he was only as good as the generals with whom he could surround himself.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Lamont Buchanan, A Pictorial History of the Confederacy (1951), p. 119

text: General Lee emerged from the War Between the States not as a vanquished commander, but as one of the great heroes of American history, respected even by those who opposed the cause he served.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Lamont Buchanan, A Pictorial History of the Confederacy (1951), p. 261

text: General Lee made the decision himself to save lives, feeling that two hundred and forty thousand dead already was enough.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Johnny Cash , in his song "God Bless Robert E. Lee" from the album Johnny 99 (1983).

text: At Appomattox, the spirit of feudalism and injustice, embodied by Lee, surrendered to the spirit of equal rights, embodied by Grant.
author: Robert E. Lee

text: Their consciously made sacrifices are an example to be enshrined and emulated.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Greg Eanes, Heritage of Honor: Our Confederate Military Legacy (2015), p. 3

text: A nation of men of Lee’s calibre would be unconquerable in spirit and soul.
author: Robert E. Lee

text: Southerners believe an individual has his grandest hour when he bravely fights against great odds for a lost cause he deems just.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: United States Senator Sam Ervin , Jr., 1984

text: What he seemed, he was—a wholly human gentleman, the essential elements of whose positive character were two and only two, simplicity and spirituality.
author: Robert E. Lee

text: Go back to your command, and try to think what we are going to do ourselves, instead of what Lee is going to do.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Ulysses S. Grant , as quoted in "Campaigning with Grant" (December 1896), by General Horace Porter , The Century Magazine

text: I regard it as my duty to shift from myself the responsibility of any further effusion of blood, by asking of you the surrender of the Army of Northern Virginia.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Ulysses S. Grant , letter to Robert E. Lee (7 April 1865)

text: General Scott made no secret of his opinion that Lee was the best of American soldiers.
author: Robert E. Lee

text: The lack of an ability to compromise led to the Civil War, and men and women of good faith on both sides made their stand where their conscience had them make their stand.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: John F. Kelly , Trump chief of staff John Kelly calls Confederate Gen. Robert E. Lee an 'honorable man' , 31 October 2017

text: Extremely difficult decisions, meaning life and death for tens of thousands, are made on the grounds of absolute necessity in a crisis situation.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: James M. McPherson , as quoted in "An exchange with a Civil War historian" (19 June 1995), by David Walsh, International Workers Bulletin

text: He was a general who 'would rather lose the war than his dignity.' Sure enough, he lost the war and preserved his dignity so well that he has gone down in history a martyr, our most overrated general.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Seymour Morris Jr., American History Revised: 200 Startling Facts That Never Made It into the Textbooks (2010), p. 160

text: Had he been the Northern general, Lee would have been in a unique position to bridge the gap between the two sides and unify a war-torn nation.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Seymour Morris Jr., American History Revised: 200 Startling Facts That Never Made It into the Textbooks (2010), p. 161

text: Theodore Roosevelt, Winston Churchill, and President Eisenhower all hailed Robert E. Lee as one of our greatest Americans.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Seymour Morris Jr., American History Revised: 200 Startling Facts That Never Made It into the Textbooks (2010), p. 161

text: No military leader since Napoleon has aroused such enthusiastic devotion among troops as did Lee when he reviewed them on his horse Traveller.
author: Robert E. Lee

text: All over the United States, we recognize Robert E. Lee as a great leader, general, and one of our greatest American Christians and gentlemen.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Franklin D. Roosevelt , as quoted in Public Papers of the Presidents of the United States: F.D. Roosevelt, 1936: Volume 5 , p. 214

text: Farragut, Sheridan, Sherman, Custer, Grant, Lee, Jackson, and Beauregard—all were Americans and nothing else, despite their diverse ancestries.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Theodore Roosevelt , "Address to the Knights of Columbus" (12 October 1915)

text: Robert E. Lee and his comrades killed more U.S. Army soldiers than any other enemy, ever, to create a nation dedicated to exploit enslaved men, women, and children, forever.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Ty Seidule, Robert E. Lee and Me: A Southerner's Reckoning with the Myth of the Lost Cause (2020), p. 8-9

text: My former hero, Robert E. Lee, committed treason to preserve slavery; American history demands a reckoning with the myths and lies that hid this essential truth.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Ty Seidule, Robert E. Lee and Me: A Southerner's Reckoning with the Myth of the Lost Cause (2020), p. 9

text: Lee fought for slavery, even ordering his own slaves beaten, yet insisted it was for 'Christian love'—a stark contrast to Frederick Douglass’s 'Christianity of Christ'.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Adam Serwer , The Cruelty is the Point (2021)

text: Lee was devoted to defending white supremacy, while Longstreet fought against it; this is why Lee is remembered as a hero, and Longstreet as a disgrace.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Adam Serwer , The Cruelty is the Point (2021)

text: Offered command of the Union Army, Robert E. Lee refused, his loyalty to Virginia greater than his loyalty to the Union, choosing instead to defend his native state.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Francis Butler Simkins, Spotswood Hunnicutt, Sidman P. Poole, Virginia: History, Government, Geography (1957), p. 411-412

text: The myth of Lee being above slavery is essential to neo-Confederate arguments, yet Lee castigated abolitionists, dragged his heels on emancipation, and never questioned his slave-run Arlington mansion.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Brooks D. Simpson , as quoted in "Arizona State Professor Brooks D. Simpson Discusses Neo-Confederate Movement", in Intelligence Report of the Southern Poverty Law Center, Issue 99, (Summer 2000)

text: The Army of Northern Virginia was under orders to capture escaped slaves; all US officers, upholding the Constitution, swore to uphold the institution of slavery, regardless of personal sentiments.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Brooks D. Simpson , "The Soldiers' Flag?" (5 July 2015), Crossroads

text: Lee and Jackson, who barely fought in Georgia, are honored on Stone Mountain, while generals who defended the state are ignored; ironically, Klansman John B. Gordon would have been a fitting choice.
author: Robert E. Lee
source: Brooks D. Simpson , "The Future of Stone Mountain" (22 July 2015), Crossroads

text: When I told my Confederate veteran great-uncle I was attending West Point, he paused, then said, 'That's all right, son. Robert E. Lee and Stonewall Jackson went there too.'
author: Robert E. Lee
source: William Westmoreland , A Soldier Reports (1976), p. 12

text: No fiercer spur to victory has the immortal gods given man than contempt for death.
author: Hannibal Barca
source: God has given to man no sharper spur to victory than contempt of death. As quoted by Livy , la:s:Ab Urbe Condita/liber XXI 44, as translated by Aubrey De Sélincourt, in The War with Hannibal (1965).

text: Asked to rank the greatest generals, Hannibal named Alexander, then Pyrrhus, and finally himself. When Scipio asked what he would say if he had defeated him, Hannibal replied, 'Then I would have placed myself ahead of all others.'
author: Hannibal Barca
source: Livy (Titus Livius), History of Rome, book 35. (DOI: 10.4159/DLCL.livy-history_rome_35.2018)

text: No captain ever marched among so many superior armies as fearlessly and skillfully as Hannibal, defying all efforts to drive him from Italy for half a generation. As a soldier, in constancy under bitterest adversity, he stands alone and unequaled.
author: Hannibal Barca
source: Theodore Ayrault Dodge , Hannibal : A History of the Art of War among the Carthaginians and Romans (1893), p. 652.

text: Though Hannibal won Cannae with a dazzling maneuver, Carthage lost the war because Rome outlasted it, paying a heavy price: its cities leveled, its fields sown with salt.
author: Hannibal Barca
source: Margaret MacMillan , War: How Conflict Shaped Us (2020)

text: Had you sought peace before I crossed to Africa, I might have respected your terms. But now, dragged to Africa, you are under no obligation from me. Prepare for war, since you have been unable to endure a peace.
author: Scipio Africanus
source: Reply to Hannibal 's attempt to set terms for peace, prior to the Battle of Zama , as quoted in Livy. Books XXVIII-XXX With An English Translation (1949), Book 30, Ch. 31

text: Ungrateful homeland, you do not even have my bones.
author: Scipio Africanus
source: Thankless country, thou shalt not possess even my bones!

text: Noble conduct and clear vision blend to form a great man.
author: Scipio Africanus
source: B. H. Liddell Hart in Scipio Africanus : Greater Than Napoleon (1926), Ch. X : A Violated Peace, p. 162

text: Beauty without Vanity, Strength without Insolence, Courage without Ferocity, And all the virtues of Man, without his Vices.
author: Lord Byron
source: Inscription on the monument of a Newfoundland dog (1808).

text: The Cincinnatus of the West, Whom envy dared not hate, Bequeath'd the name of Washington, To make man blush there was but one!
author: Lord Byron

text: When a comet approaches to destroy this globe, men will hurl mountains against the flaming mass, reviving traditions of Titans and wars with Heaven.
author: Lord Byron
source: As quoted in Conversations of Lord Byron with Thomas Medwin (1832).

text: My comfort is that my celebrity was won against all opinions and prejudices, for I flattered no powers and concealed no thought.
author: Lord Byron

text: When a man has no freedom to fight for at home, let him combat for his neighbours'; battle for freedom wherever you can, and if not shot or hanged, you'll get knighted.
author: Lord Byron

text: I live, but live to die, seeing nothing to make death hateful save an invincible instinct of life, which I abhor as I despise myself.
author: Lord Byron

text: Seek out — less often sought than found — A Soldier's Grave, for thee the best; Then look around and choose thy Ground, And take thy Rest.
author: Lord Byron
source: On This Day I Complete My Thirty-Sixth Year , st. 10.

text: Believe any other thing that's false, before you trust in critics, who themselves are sore.
author: Lord Byron

text: Perverts the Prophets and purloins the Psalms.
author: Lord Byron

text: Truth will sometimes lend her noblest fires, and decorate the verse herself inspires.
author: Lord Byron

text: The light of love, the purity of grace, The mind, the music breathing from her face, The heart whose softness harmonized the whole,— And oh, that eye was in itself a soul!
author: Lord Byron
source: Canto I, Stanza 6; this can be compared to: "The bloom of young Desire and purple light of Love", Thomas Gray , The Progress of Poesy I. 3, line 16; also: "Oh, could you view the melody / Of every grace / And music of her face", Richard Lovelace , Orpheus to Beasts ; "There is music in the beauty, and the silent note which Cupid strikes, far sweeter than the sound of an instrument", Thomas Browne , Religio Medici , Part ii, Section ix.

text: Mark! where his carnage and his conquests cease! He makes a solitude, and calls it — peace!
author: Lord Byron
source: Canto II, stanza 20. Here Byron is using an adaptation of a quote from Agricola by the Roman historian Tacitus (c. 30). The original words in the text are Auferre, trucidare, rapere, falsis nominibus imperium; atque, ubi solitudinem faciunt, pacem appellant (To robbery, slaughter, plunder, they give the lying name of empire; they make a wilderness, and call it peace). This has also been reported as Solitudinem faciunt, pacem appellant (They make solitude, which they call peace).

text: Love indeed is light from heaven; a spark of that immortal fire with angels shared, by Alla given, to lift from earth our low desire.
author: Lord Byron

text: Our thoughts as boundless, and our souls as free, o'er the glad waters of the dark blue sea.
author: Lord Byron

text: We know that tears are vain, that death neither heeds nor hears distress.
author: Lord Byron
source: Oh! Snatched Away in Beauty's Bloom , from Hebrew Melodies

text: A silent, intense suffering: the rock, the vulture, and the chain, and the suffocating sense of woe that speaks only in its loneliness.
author: Lord Byron

text: Man is in part divine, a troubled stream from a pure source; and his Spirit may oppose itself to all woes, with a firm will and deep sense, triumphant where it dares defy, and making Death a Victory.
author: Lord Byron

text: By thy delight in others' pain, and by thy brotherhood of Cain, I call upon thee! and compel Thyself to be thy proper Hell!
author: Lord Byron

text: Ye avalanches, whom a breath draws down in mountainous o'erwhelming, come and crush me! but ye pass, and only fall on things that still would live.
author: Lord Byron

text: Patience! Hence—that word was made For brutes of burthen, not for birds of prey; Preach it to mortals of a dust like thine,— I am not of thine order.
author: Lord Byron

text: Actions are our epochs: mine have made my days and nights imperishable.
author: Lord Byron

text: From my youth upwards My spirit walk'd not with the souls of men, Nor look'd upon the earth with human eyes; The thirst of their ambition was not mine, The aim of their existence was not mine; My joys, my griefs, my passions, and my powers Made me a stranger.
author: Lord Byron

text: No future pang can deal that justice on the self-condemned he deals on his own soul.
author: Lord Byron

text: I linger yet with Nature, for the Night hath been to me a more familiar face than that of man; and in her starry shade, I learned the language of another world.
author: Lord Byron

text: While Washington's a watchword, such as ne'er shall sink while there's an echo left to air.
author: Lord Byron

text: Like other charmers, wooing the caress more dazzlingly when daring in full dress; yet thy true lovers more admire by far thy naked beauties—give me a cigar!
author: Lord Byron

text: What is the end of fame? 'tis but to have, when the original is dust, a name, a wretched picture, and worse bust.
author: Lord Byron

text: Byron was dead! I thought the whole world was at an end. I remembered I walked out alone, and carved 'Byron is dead' into the sandstone.
author: Lord Byron
source: Anne Isabella Thackeray, Lady Ritchie , Records of Tennyson, Ruskin, and Robert and Elizabeth Browning (1892).

text: Cease, cease, wayward Mortal! I dare not unveil The shadows that float o'er Eternity's vale; Nought waits for the good but a spirit of Love, That will hail their blest advent to regions above. For Love, Mortal, gleams through the gloom of my sway, And the shades which surround me fly fast at its ray.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: "Death" in an untitled dialogue (1809), in T. J. Hogg, Life of Shelley (1858), p. 197

text: Not the swart Pariah in some Indian grove, Lone, lean, and hunted by his brother's hate, Hath drunk so deep the cup of bitter fate As that poor wretch who cannot, cannot love: He bears a load which nothing can remove, A killing, withering weight.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Age cannot Love destroy, but perfidy can blast its flower.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: Untitled (1810); titled "Love's Rose" in Rossetti (1870), p. 525

text: Oh, how I wish I were the Antichrist, to crush the Demon.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: Letter to Thomas Jefferson Hogg (3 January 1811)

text: I think that the leaf of a tree, the meanest insect we trample are in themselves arguments more conclusive than any which can be adduced that some vast intellect animates Infinity.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: Letter to Thomas Jefferson Hogg (3 January 1811)

text: Morality's purest lessons fail when tied to the dogma of revealed religion.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: I could lie down like a tired child, till death like sleep might steal on me.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: Stanzas Written in Dejection Near Naples (1818), st. 5

text: O press it to thine own again, where it will break at last!
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Rough wind, the moanest loud Grief too sad for song; Wild wind, when sullen cloud Knells all the night long; Sad storm, whose tears are vain, Bare woods, whose branches strain, Deep caves and dreary main, — Wail, for the world's wrong!
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: And so thy thoughts, when thou art gone, Love itself shall slumber on.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: If the knowledge of a God is the most necessary, why is it not the most evident and the clearest?
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: The corpse at his feet is prophetic of his own destiny.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Power, like a desolating pestilence, pollutes whate'er it touches; and obedience, bane of all genius, virtue, freedom, truth, makes slaves of men, and of the human frame a mechanized automaton.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Suicidal selfishness, that blights the fairest feelings of the opening heart, is destined to decay, whilst from the soil shall spring all virtue, all delight, all love.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: And man ... no longer now He slays the lamb that looks him in the face, And horribly devours his mangled flesh.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: A husband and wife ought to continue so long united as they love each other. Any law which should bind them to cohabitation for one moment after the decay of their affection, would be a most intolerable tyranny, and the most unworthy of toleration.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Chastity is a monkish and evangelical superstition, a greater foe to natural temperance even than unintellectual sensuality; it strikes at the root of all domestic happiness, and consigns more than half the human race to misery.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: It is only by softening and disguising dead flesh by culinary preparation, that it is rendered susceptible of mastication or digestion; and that the sight of its bloody juices and raw horror does not excite intolerable loathing and disgust.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: It is only by softening and disguising dead flesh by culinary preparation that it is rendered susceptible of mastication or digestion, and that the sight of its bloody juices and raw horror does not excite intolerable loathing and disgust.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Should a physician with Locke's genius arise, he might trace all bodily and mental derangements to our unnatural habits.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: No disease, bodily or mental, has resisted mitigation by a vegetable diet and pure water, transforming debility into strength, disease into health, and madness into a calm temper, promising society's moral reform.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: A nation leading in reform would become agricultural, commerce's vices would decline, natural habits would produce gentler manners, and political relations would simplify, fostering true patriotism.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Mankind has always believed we live after death, clinging to the idea that sensibility and thought, distinct from matter, are less susceptible to decay, and the animating principle remains perpetual and unchanged.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Some philosophers believe intelligence is mere particle combination; those who believe in an afterlife must invoke supernatural power to overcome material dissipation.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Let us test the question of life after death by experience and fact, examining our nature to assert with certainty whether we live on.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Me — who am as a nerve o'er which do creep The else unfelt oppressions of this earth, And was to thee the flame upon thy hearth, When all beside was cold: — that thou on me Shouldst rain these plagues of blistering agony!
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Old palaces and towers quivered within the Mediterranean's waves, overgrown with azure moss and flowers so sweet, the sense faints picturing them.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Stand ye calm and resolute, Like a forest close and mute, With folded arms and looks which are Weapons of unvanquished war.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Rise like Lions after slumber In unvanquishable number — Shake your chains to earth like dew Which in sleep had fallen on you — Ye are many — they are few.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: I am the daughter of Earth and Water, And the nursling of the Sky; I pass through the pores of the ocean and shores; I change, but I cannot die.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Like a child from the womb, like a ghost from the tomb, I arise.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: St. 7 (a cenotaph is an empty tomb or a monument erected in honor of a person who is buried elsewhere)

text: Hail to thee, blithe Spirit! Bird thou never wert, That from Heaven, or near it, Pourest thy full heart In profuse strains of unpremeditated art.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Higher still and higher From the earth thou springest, Like a cloud of fire; The blue deep thou wingest, And singing still dost soar and soaring ever singest.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Like an unbodied joy whose race is just begun.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Thou art unseen, but yet I hear thy shrill delight.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: We look before and after, And pine for what is not: Our sincerest laughter With some pain is fraught; Our sweetest songs are those that tell of saddest thought.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Teach me half the gladness That thy brain must know, Such harmonious madness From my lips would flow The world should listen then — as I am listening now.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: We are all Greeks. Our laws, our literature, our religion, our arts have their root in Greece.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Life may change, but it may fly not; Hope may vanish, but can die not; Truth be veiled, but still it burneth; Love repulsed, — but it returneth!
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Kings are like stars — they rise and set, they have The worship of the world, but no repose.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Let there be light! said Liberty, And like sunrise from the sea, Athens arose!
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Her imperial spirits rule the present from the past.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: The world's great age begins anew, The golden years return, The earth doth like a snake renew Her winter weeds outworn; Heaven smiles, and faiths and empires gleam, Like wrecks of a dissolving dream.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: The world is weary of the past, Oh, might it die or rest at last!
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Come and be my guest, — for I am Love's.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: It might make one in love with death, to think that one should be buried in so sweet a place.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: Whence are we, and why are we? of what scene The actors or spectators?
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: On a cheek The life can burn in blood, even while the heart may break.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: What softer voice is hushed over the dead? Athwart what brow is that dark mantle thrown? What form leans sadly o'er the white death — bed, In mockery of monumental stone, The heavy heart heaving without a moan?
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: These things are not the less poetry, because they lack a sacred bard, but episodes of that cyclic poem written by Time upon the memories of men.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
source: This passage has sometimes been paraphrased as "History is a cyclic poem written by Time upon the memories of man".

text: I am gone into the fields to take what this sweet hour yields; Reflection, Sorrow, Despair, Care—I will pay you in the grave, for Death will listen to your stave.
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley

text: In the book of my memory, a rubric proclaims: 'Here begins the new life.'
author: Dante Alighieri
source: In that book which is My memory ... On the first page That is the chapter when I first met you Appear the words ... Here begins a new life .

text: Behold a god, stronger than I, who comes to rule over me.
author: Dante Alighieri
source: Behold a God more powerful than I who comes to rule over me.

text: She is all the good nature can fashion; by her example, beauty is proven.
author: Dante Alighieri
source: She is the sum of nature's universe. To her perfection all of beauty tends.

text: Love, who in my mind speaks yearningly of my lady, makes the intellect stray beyond its grasp.
author: Dante Alighieri
source: Love with delight discourses in my mind Upon my lady's admirable gifts... Beyond the range of human intellect .
