text: In its depth I saw that it contained, bound by love in one volume, all that is scattered through the universe.
author: Dante Alighieri
source: I saw within Its depth how It conceives all things in a single volume bound by Love , of which the universe is the scattered leaves.

text: Dante, adhering to Catholic truth, exercised perfect autonomy of judgment, fearlessly criticizing popes and monarchs alike, yet never diminishing his reverence for the supreme keys.
author: Dante Alighieri
source: Cardinal Giacomo Biffi . As quoted in Antonio Socci , Il (vero) Dante che non sentite da Benigni , Lo Straniero (March 10, 2015)

text: He is world-great not because he is world-wide, but because he is world-deep.
author: Dante Alighieri
source: Thomas Carlyle , "The Hero as Poet" in Heroes and Hero-Worship (1841).

text: Read the great poet of Italy, Dante, for he can devise all from point to point, not one word will he fail.
author: Dante Alighieri
source: Geoffrey Chaucer , The Canterbury Tales (c. 1390), The Monkes Tale , lines 3650–3652.

text: Dante brought the vernacular poetry of Italy, when it was still in its infancy, to heights of perfection and finish that have seldom been equalled and never surpassed.
author: Dante Alighieri
source: Cesare Foligno (1921, Annual Italian Lecture): " Dante: The Poet ". Proceedings of the British Academy, 1921–1923 10 : 81–96. (quote on p. 96)

text: Dante, a prophet of hope, heralds humanity's redemption and liberation, inspiring profound change in every soul.
author: Dante Alighieri
source: Pope Francis , Message to the President of the Pontifical Council for Culture for the solemn celebration of the 750th anniversary of the birth of the supreme poet Dante Alighieri (4 May 2015).

text: Dante was the Columbus who discovered this new world of poesy.
author: Dante Alighieri
source: Edmund Dorr Griffin, in Remains of the Rev. Edmund D. Griffin (1831), p. 335.

text: Dante wished to be himself, himself alone; in a word, to create.
author: Dante Alighieri
source: Napoleon I of France , "Napoleon: In His Own Words" (1916), p. 74.

text: Dante's truths—of voyage, horror, chance, and belief—are timeless, present in every imaginative experience.
author: Dante Alighieri
source: Muriel Rukeyser The Life of Poetry (1949)

text: Dante wrote that the spirit of poetry abounds "in the tangled constructions and defective pronunciations" of vernacular speech, where language is renewed and transformed.
author: Dante Alighieri
source: Cecilia Vicuña The Oxford Book of Latin American Poetry (2009)

text: I can recollect nothing more to say at present; perhaps breakfast may assist my ideas. I was deceived — my breakfast supplied only two ideas — that the rolls were good and the butter bad.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter (1799-06-19) [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: I believe I drank too much wine last night at Hurstbourne; I know not how else to account for the shaking of my hand today. You will kindly make allowance therefore for any indistinctness of writing, by attributing it to this venial error.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter to Cassandra (1800-11-20) [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: The General has got the gout, and Mrs. Maitland the jaundice. Miss Debary, Susan, and Sally, all in black, but without any stature, made their appearance, and I was as civil to them as their bad breath would allow me.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter (1800-11-20) on people she met at a ball [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: Another stupid party last night; perhaps if larger they might be less intolerable, but here there were only just enough to make one card-table, with six people to look on and talk nonsense to each other.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter to Cassandra (1801-05-12) [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: His avoidance, and her pursuit, with the probable intoxication of both, was an amusing scene.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter (1801-05-12) [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: Adm. Stanhope is a gentleman-like man, but then his legs are too short and his tail too long.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter to Cassandra (1801-05-12) [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: We crossed the churchyard at Weston with as much expedition as if we were afraid of being buried alive.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter to Cassandra (1801-05-21) [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: We are to have a tiny party here tonight. I hate tiny parties, they force one into constant exertion.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter (1801-05-21) [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: Abuse everybody but me.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter to Cassandra (1807-01-07) [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: So unlike anything that I was myself at her age, that I am often all astonishment and shame.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter to Cassandra (1807-02-08) [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: I am sorry to tell you that I am getting very extravagant, and spending all my money, and, what is worse for you , I have been spending yours too.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter to Cassandra (1811-04-18) [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: I give you joy of our new nephew, and hope if he ever comes to be hanged it will not be till we are too old to care about it.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter to Cassandra (1811-04-25) [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: I will not say that your mulberry-trees are dead, but I am afraid they are not alive.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter to Cassandra (1811-05-31) [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: Mr. Digweed has used us basely. Handsome is as handsome does; he is therefore a very ill-looking man.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter to Cassandra (1813-01-24) [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: Let me know when you begin the new tea, and the new white wine. My present elegancies have not yet made me indifferent to such matters. I am still a cat if I see a mouse.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter to Cassandra (1813-09-23) [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: By the bye, as I must leave off being young, I find many douceurs in being a sort of chaperon , for I am put on the sofa near the fire and can drink as much wine as I like.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter (1813-11-06) on ageing [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: I cannot help hoping that many will feel themselves obliged to buy it. I shall not mind imagining it a disagreeable duty to them, so as they do it.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter (1813-11-06) on the reprint of Sense and Sensibility [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: I cannot bear the expression "vortex of dissipation"; it is such thorough novel slang, and so old that I daresay Adam met with it in the first novel he opened.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter to niece Anna (1814-09-28) regarding a character in Anna's novel [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: Walter Scott has no business to write novels, especially good ones. I do not like him, and do not mean to like Waverley if I can help it, but fear I must.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter to Anna (1814-09-28) [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: When I saw the waggons, I began to reproach myself for not having liked them better, but since they disappeared my conscience has closed again, and I am excessively glad they are gone.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter to Anna (1814-09-28) [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: There are such beings in the world as the creature you and I should think perfection; but such a person may not come in your way, or, if he does, he may not be the eldest son of a man of fortune.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter to Fanny Knight (1814-11-18) on finding love [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: I rather imagine indeed that nieces are seldom chosen but out of compliment to some aunt or another, and I daresay Ben was in love with me once, and would never have thought of you if he had not supposed me dead of scarlet fever.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter to niece Anna (1814-11-30) regarding characters in Anna's novel [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: No, I must keep to my own style and go on in my own way; and though I may never succeed again in that, I am convinced that I should totally fail in any other.
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter to Mr. Clarke (1816-04-01) [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: How could I possibly join them on to the little bit (two inches wide) of ivory on which I work with so fine a brush, as produces little effect after much labour?
author: Jane Austen
source: Letter to J. Edward Austen (1816-12-16) [ Letters of Jane Austen -- Brabourne Edition ]

text: Sophia shrieked and fainted on the ground—I screamed and instantly ran mad!
author: Jane Austen

text: One does not love a place the less for having suffered in it, unless it has been all suffering, nothing but suffering.
author: Jane Austen

text: An engaged woman is always more agreeable, satisfied with herself, her cares over, and free to please without suspicion.
author: Jane Austen

text: She was of course only too good for him; but as nobody minds having what is too good for them, he was very steadily earnest in the pursuit of the blessing...
author: Jane Austen

text: They are much to be pitied who have not been given a taste for Nature in early life; they lose a great deal.
author: Jane Austen

text: ...why did we wait for any thing? — why not seize the pleasure at once? — How often is happiness destroyed by preparation, foolish preparation!
author: Jane Austen

text: Most of us, male and female, are as children compared with this one glorious human being.
author: Jane Austen
source: Wayne C. Booth , The Company We Keep: An Ethics of Fiction (Berkeley: University of California Press, 1988), p. 434

text: Austen knew powerful fictions are dangerous; she would welcome critical inquiry, which only reveals her greater genius.
author: Jane Austen
source: Wayne C. Booth , The Company We Keep: An Ethics of Fiction (Berkeley: University of California Press, 1988), p. 435

text: Jane Austen's ironical amusement stems from characters never seeing reality as she does, a source of pleasure because she finds human absurdities, if not harmful, both ridiculous and right.
author: Jane Austen
source: A. C. Bradley , 'Jane Austen', Essays and Studies by Members of the English Association, Vol. II (1911), p. 19

text: Austen's work is an accurate daguerreotype of a commonplace face, a carefully fenced garden, but offers no open country, no fresh air, no bonny beck.
author: Jane Austen
source: Charlotte Brontë , quote in Original Penguin Classics Introduction to Pride and Prejudice. by Tanner, Tony (1972). 80 Strand, London, WC2R, England: Penguin Books, Ltd. ISBN 0-141-43951-3 .

text: Austen's novels are conservative, focusing on individual moral reform within the established gentry, where dignity stems from contributions to a small, hierarchical community.
author: Jane Austen
source: Marilyn Butler , Jane Austen and the War of Ideas (1975), pp. 1-2

text: Austen's conservative novels, rooted in anti-Jacobinism, define virtue through marriage choices, where heroines' ethical decisions subtly interlink with society's stability, opposing revolution.
author: Jane Austen
source: Marilyn Butler , Jane Austen and the War of Ideas (1975), pp. 122-123

text: Austen's selfish characters, pursuing a modern creed of self, threaten social institutions like marriage; her achievement is to rethink the conservative novel with naturalistic, intellectually consistent villains.
author: Jane Austen
source: Marilyn Butler , Jane Austen and the War of Ideas (1975), pp. 180-181

text: Though criticized as trivial, Austen's comedy, focused on gentry life, delves deep into human conduct, engraving a criticism of life as serious as any on her bit of ivory.
author: Jane Austen
source: Lord David Cecil , 'Introduction', in Jane Austen, Sense and Sensibility (1931), p. xiii

text: Her delicate, ruthless irony was the very substance of her style, never absent, always tart with its perfume.
author: Jane Austen
source: Lord David Cecil , The Leslie Stephen Lecture at the University of Cambridge (1 May 1935), quoted in The Times (2 May 1935), p. 11

text: Austen's appeal lies in her ruthless, good-tempered humor, a sympathetic contrast between delicate manner and robust judgment.
author: Jane Austen
source: Lord David Cecil , interview with Patrick Garland for the BBC documentary Conversations at Cranborne (1970), quoted in Tristram Powell, 'A Television Interview', in David Cecil: A Portrait by his Friends (1990), p. 164

text: As her would-be biographer, I had to face the fact that information about Jane Austen the woman was limited and fragmentary. She remains for me – as no doubt she would have wished – not an intimate but an acquaintance.
author: Jane Austen
source: Lord David Cecil , A Portrait of Jane Austen (1978), p. 10

text: Austen is one of the greatest, most accurate writers of dialogue of her own or any age.
author: Jane Austen
source: Robert William Chapman , 'Appendixes: Miss Auten's English', The Novels of Jane Austen: The Text based on Collation of the Early Editions, Volume I (1923), p. 389

text: Jane Austen knew more about men than the Brontës or George Eliot; precious little truth was protected from her.
author: Jane Austen
source: G. K. Chesterton , The Victorian Age in Literature (1913)

text: This is something more than the discovery of a document; it is the discovery of an inspiration. And that inspiration was the inspiration of Gargantua and of Pickwick ; it was the gigantic inspiration of laughter.
author: Jane Austen
source: G. K. Chesterton , 'Preface' to Jane Austen, Love & Freindship and Other Early Works (1922), p. xiv

text: Austen's novels are vulgar, sterile, and narrow, obsessed with marriage and money; suicide is more respectable.
author: Jane Austen
source: Ralph Waldo Emerson , Emerson: Selected Journals 1841–1877 , edited by Lawrence Rosenwald (2010), The Library of America, p. 768

text: I read Madame Bovary and Jane Austen and George Eliot over and over.
author: Jane Austen
source: 1993 interview in Conversations with Louise Erdrich and Michael Dorris edited by Allan Chavkin and Nancy Feyl Chavkin (1994)

text: Austen's characters, unlike Dickens', offer new pleasure, combining organically in conversation, more highly organized and fully functional.
author: Jane Austen
source: E. M. Forster , Aspects of The Novel (1927)

text: Perhaps my favorite English novelist is Jane Austen.
author: Jane Austen

text: Emma's magic is its exquisite truth, presenting everyday life without triviality, revealing Austen's tact of minute observation and delicacy of sarcasm.
author: Jane Austen
source: Arthur Henry Hallam , letter to fiancé Emily (Emilia) Tennyson (1833-01-25), Jane Austen and her Readers, 1786-1945 , by Katie Halsey (Anthem Nineteenth-Century Series)

text: Read the greats-they're great for a reason. They know how to chart the human soul.
author: Jane Austen

text: Jane Austen was the first and last English novelist to treat material with entire truthfulness, making her the most artistic.
author: Jane Austen
source: William Dean Howells , Criticism and Fiction (1891), Chapter 15

text: Jane Austen's genius lies in the depiction and analysis of human beings, free from ideology, placing her with James, Proust, Sophocles, and Shakespeare.
author: Jane Austen
source: Richard Jenkyns , A Fine Brush on Ivory: An Appreciation of Jane Austen (2004), p. 184

text: From a great novelist, you learn what life and people are truly like, not fortune cookie maxims.
author: Jane Austen
source: 1988 interview in Conversations with Ursula Le Guin (2008)

text: Learning to read realistic fiction, like Jane Austen, is a skill many never acquire, missing its unique rewards.
author: Jane Austen
source: 1994 interview in Conversations with Ursula Le Guin (2008)

text: Her fame must endure; such art as hers can never grow old. But, after all, miniatures are not frescoes, and her works are miniatures.
author: Jane Austen
source: George Henry Lewes , The Novels of Jane Austen (1859)

text: Among writers who have approached nearest to Shakespeare's manner, we place Jane Austen, whose commonplace characters are perfectly discriminated by touches so delicate they elude analysis.
author: Jane Austen
source: Thomas Macaulay , 'Madame D'Arblay', Edinburgh Review (January 1843), reprinted in Critical and Miscellaneous Essays, Volume 5 (1844), p. 68

text: There was genius in sketching out that new kind of novel. Every village could furnish matter for Miss Austen; she did not need strong passion or incident.
author: Jane Austen
source: James Mackintosh , quoted in Memoirs of the Life of Sir James Mackintosh, Vol. II , ed. Robert James Mackintosh (1836 ed.), p. 472

text: Jane Austen, the Queen of novelists, was compelled by her family to cover her manuscripts with muslin whenever genteel people came in.
author: Jane Austen
source: Harriet Martineau , Harriet Martineau's Autobiography, vol. 1

text: Endowed with rational wisdom, exceptional intellect, and unique wit, Jane Austen employed her meagre experience creatively in the service of Comedy.
author: Jane Austen
source: Katherine Marion Metcalfe, 'Introduction', in Jane Austen, Pride and Prejudice (1912), p. xv

text: Till 'Pride and Prejudice' showed what a precious gem was hidden, she was no more regarded than a poker; now she is a poker of whom everyone is afraid.
author: Jane Austen
source: Mary Russell Mitford , letter to Sir William Elford, 1st Baronet (1815-04-03), The Life of Mary Russell Mitford, vol. 1 (1870)

text: Miss Austen had a talent for describing the involvement, feelings, and characters of ordinary life with an exquisite touch that renders commonplace things interesting.
author: Jane Austen
source: Walter Scott , "Walter Scott, an unsigned review of Emma, Quarterly Review". Jane Austen: The Critical Heritage, 1812–1870. Ed. B. C. Southam. London: Routledge and Kegan Paul, 1968. ISBN 071002942X . 58–69

text: Her novels are more true to nature, with passages of finer feeling than any others of this age.
author: Jane Austen
source: Robert Southey to Sir Egerton Brydges (8 April 1830), quoted in The Autobiography, Times, Opinions, and Contemporaries of Sir Egerton Brydges, Vol. II (1834), p. 269

text: The faults of some are the anvils on which the virtues of others are hammered till they are bright as steel.
author: Jane Austen

text: To me his (Edgar Allan Poe's) prose is unreadable — like Jane Austin's [sic]. No there is a difference. I could read his prose on salary, but not Jane's. Jane is entirely impossible. It seems a great pity that they allowed her to die a natural death.
author: Jane Austen
source: Mark Twain , letter to William Dean Howells (1909-01-18)

text: Miss Austen has the essential merit of being a Christian writer, yet her religion is never obtrusive, defying critics to call her novels 'dramatic sermons'.
author: Jane Austen
source: Richard Whately , 'Modern Novels', The Quarterly Review (January 1821), p. 359

text: The art is so consummate that the secret is hidden; peer at them as hard as one may; shake them; take them apart; one cannot see how it is done.
author: Jane Austen
source: Thornton Wilder , A preface for Our Town (1938)

text: My reason compels me to see Jane Austen as a magnificent artist, yet I suspect she suppressed half of herself in her novels, perhaps something to do with sex.
author: Jane Austen
source: Virginia Woolf , Letter to Ethel Smythe, 20 November 1932, in David Dowling, Novelists on Novelists (1983)

text: Jane Austen devised a perfectly natural, shapely sentence proper for her own use, and thus, with less genius for writing than Charlotte Brontë, she got infinitely more said.
author: Jane Austen
source: Virginia Woolf , A Room of One's Own (1929), Chapter 4

text: Jane Austen is a mistress of deeper emotion than appears on the surface; her outwardly trivial scenes expand in the reader's mind, endowing them with enduring life.
author: Jane Austen

text: I can only say with deeper sincerity and fuller significance — what I have always said in theory — Wait God's will.
author: Charlotte Brontë
source: (Referring to marriage ). As quoted in Moglen, Helen (1984) Charlotte Brontë: the self conceived, University of Wisconsin Press, p. 235

text: If you like poetry, let it be first-rate; the finest passages are always the purest, the bad invariably revolting.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: Anything more false and artificial, less genuinely impressive than his whole style, I could scarcely have imagined.
author: Charlotte Brontë
source: Charlotte Brontë, on William Macready. Charlotte Brontë and Her Circle , (by Clement King Shorter) (1896)

text: It is better to have him for a friend than an enemy, for he is a most formidable-looking personage.
author: Charlotte Brontë
source: Charlotte Brontë, on William Makepeace Thackeray . Charlotte Brontë and Her Circle , (by Clement King Shorter) (1896)

text: Hitherto I had only instinct to guide me in judging art; now, as if walking blindfold, this book seems to give me eyes.
author: Charlotte Brontë
source: Charlotte Brontë, on Modern Painters, Vol. 1 (1843), by John Ruskin . Letter to W. S. Williams (31 July 1848) The Letters of Charlotte Brontë

text: The very thought of you makes me sick.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: When we are struck at without a reason, we should strike back again very hard; so hard as to teach the person who struck us never to do it again.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: My mind, grappling with the unknown, saw only an unfathomed gulf, felt the present as its sole point, and shuddered at the chaos beyond.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: By dying young, I shall escape great sufferings.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: To inexperienced youth, it is a strange sensation to feel utterly alone, cut adrift, and uncertain of its destination.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: I longed for a vision to transcend my limits, to reach the busy world, and to experience more of life and human connection.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: The restlessness was in my nature; it agitated me to pain sometimes.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: Women feel as men feel; they need scope for their faculties and efforts, suffering from rigid restraint and stagnation precisely as men would.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: Human beings must have action; they will make it if they cannot find it.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: My tongue, though quick to answer, fails me in framing excuses precisely when a facile word is most needed.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: It is as if a string under my ribs is inextricably knotted to one in yours, and if distance parts us, that cord of communion will snap, making me bleed inwardly.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: I am not soulless and heartless; I have as much soul and heart as you, and my spirit addresses yours as equals before God.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: for never may you, like me, dread to be the instrument of evil to what you wholly love.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: If there are words and wrongs like knives, whose deep inflicted lacerations never heal, so, too, there are consolations of tone too fine for the ear not fondly and for ever to retain their echo.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: in each of her eyes sat a devil.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: I like to see flowers growing, but when they are gathered, they cease to please. I look on them as things rootless and perishable; their likeness to life makes me sad. I never offer flowers to those I love; I never wish to receive them from hands dear to me.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: a heart which only longed to cry — 'God be merciful to me, a sinner!'
author: Charlotte Brontë
source: Chapter XXXVI: The Apple of Discord

text: My God is sinless, eternal, all-wise, and in Him is my trust; though stripped and crushed, I do not despair: Jehovah, in His own time, will aid.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: You want to annihilate individual patriotism and national greatness as an atheist would annihilate God and his own soul, by denying their existence.
author: Charlotte Brontë

text: I am the only being whose doom No tongue would ask no eye would mourn ; I never caused a thought of gloom A smile of joy since I was born In secret pleasure — secret tears This changeful life has slipped away As friendless after eighteen years As lone as on my natal day.
author: Emily Brontë

text: 'Twas grief enough to think mankind All hollow, servile, insincere; But worse to trust to my own mind And find the same corruption there.
author: Emily Brontë

text: A tyrant spell has bound me, and I cannot, cannot go.
author: Emily Brontë

text: Shall Earth no more inspire thee, Thou lonely dreamer now? Since passion may not fire thee Shall Nature cease to bow? Thy mind is ever moving In regions dark to thee; Recall its useless roving — Come back and dwell with me —
author: Emily Brontë
source_section: Shall Earth No More Inspire Thee (May 1841)

text: And visions rise and change which kills me with desire.
author: Emily Brontë

text: Oh, my true friend! I am not lone, while thou canst speak with such a tone!
author: Emily Brontë

text: O God within my breast, Almighty, ever-present Deity! Life — that in me has rest, As I — undying Life — have power in Thee!
author: Emily Brontë

text: Though earth and moon were gone, And suns and universes ceased to be, And Thou wert left alone, Every existence would exist in Thee.
author: Emily Brontë

text: Her strong, imperious will would never have been daunted by opposition or difficulty, never have given way but with life.
author: Emily Brontë
source: Constantin Héger , (Emily's teacher in Brussels in 1842,) in a letter to Mrs. Gaskell, as quoted in The Oxford History of the Novel in English , Vol. III (2011), p. 208.

text: The greats know how to chart the human soul.
author: Emily Brontë

text: Her character was a peculiar mixture of timidity and Spartan-like courage, her inflexibility of purpose more like a strong man's than a delicate woman's.
author: Emily Brontë
source: Eva Hope, Queens of Literature of the Victorian Era (1886), p. 168

text: Emily Brontë, the antiracist, said, 'I am Heathcliff': I am that man of color, who had been a street urchin. We must identify entirely with those with less power than ourselves: not only are we 'with them'; we are 'them.'
author: Emily Brontë
source: Selma James , "The Challenge of Diversity" (1990) collected in Sex, Race and Class: the Perspective of Winning (2012)

text: They were each and all on common ground if a principle had to be maintained or a sham to be detected. All were resolutely single-minded, eminently courageous, eminently simple in their habits, and eminently tender-hearted.
author: Emily Brontë
source: Ellen Nussey, Reminiscences of Charlotte Brontë (1871)

text: Her extreme reserve seemed impenetrable, yet she was intensely lovable; a strength of self-containment seen in no other. She was in the strictest sense a law unto herself, and a heroine in keeping to her law.
author: Emily Brontë
source: Ellen Nussey , quoted in Charlotte Brontë by Rebecca Fraser (1988), p. 296

text: It will realize that everything in creation is not humanly beautiful, that the ugly exists beside the beautiful, the unshapely beside the graceful, the grotesque on the reverse of the sublime, evil with good, darkness with light.
author: Victor Hugo

text: It would bring into action these two fatal machines, one of which never works without the other—the assignat press and the guillotine.
author: Victor Hugo
source: Statement of May 1848, as quoted in Paris Under the Commune : Or, Seventy-Three Days of the Second Siege (1871) by John Leighton

text: You wish to teach that one must not kill. And how do you teach it? By killing.
author: Victor Hugo
source: You insist on the example [of the death penalty]. Why? For what it teaches. What do you want to teach with your example? That thou shalt not kill. And how do you teach thou shalt not kill ? By killing. "Plaidoyer contre la peine de mort" [An argument against the death penalty], Assemblée Constituante, Paris (15 September 1848)

text: A day will come when a cannon will be shown in museums, just as an instrument of torture is shown today, and people will marvel that such a thing could have existed!
author: Victor Hugo
source: A day will come when there will be no battlefields, but markets opening to commerce and minds opening to ideas. A day will come when the bullets and bombs are replaced by votes, by universal suffrage , by the venerable arbitration of a great supreme senate which will be to Europe what Parliament is to England, the Diet to Germany, and the Legislative Assembly to France. A day will come when a cannon will be a museum-piece, as instruments of torture are today. And we will be amazed to think that these things once existed! A day will come when we shall see those two immense groups, the United States of America and the United States of Europe, facing one another, stretching out their hands across the sea, exchanging their products, their arts, their works of genius, clearing up the globe, making deserts fruitful, ameliorating creation under the eyes of the Creator, and joining together, to reap the well-being of all, these two infinite forces, the fraternity of men and the power of God. Discours d'ouverture, congrès de la paix , [Opening address, Peace Congress], Paris (21 August 1849); published in Actes et paroles - Avant l'exil (1875)

text: In every French village, the schoolmaster is a lit torch, and the priest a mouth blowing upon it.
author: Victor Hugo
source: Histoire d'un crime. Déposition d'un témoin (1877), Deuxième Journée. La lutte, ch. III: La barricade Saint-Antoine

text: I only half-enter civil war. I am willing to die in it, but I do not wish to kill.
author: Victor Hugo
source: I only take a half share in the civil war; I am willing to die, I am not willing to kill. Histoire d'un crime (The History of a Crime) [written 1852, published 1877], Quatrième journée. La victoire, ch. II: Les Faits de la nuit. Quartier des Halles. Trans. T.H. Joyce and Arthur Locker

text: Jesus wept, Voltaire smiled; from this divine tear and human smile is born the sweetness of our current civilization.
author: Victor Hugo
source: Jesus wept; Voltaire smiled. Of that divine tear and that human smile is composed the sweetness of the present civilization. Speech, "Le centenaire de Voltaire" , on the 100th anniversary of the death of Voltaire, Théâtre de la Gaîté, Paris (30 May 1878); published in Actes et paroles - Depuis l'exil (1878)

text: In the coming century, war, scaffold, animosity, royalty, and dogmas will die; but Man will live. For all, one country: the Earth; one hope: Heaven.
author: Victor Hugo
source: Address to the Workman's Congress at Marseille (1879)

text: Here is the battle of day and night... I see black light.
author: Victor Hugo
source: This is the battle between day and night... I see black light. Last words (1885-05-22); quoted in Olympio, ou la vie de Victor Hugo by André Maurois (1954)

text: This need for the immaterial is the most vital of all. We need bread; but before bread, we need the ideal.
author: Victor Hugo
source: The need of the immaterial is the most deeply rooted of all needs. One must have bread; but before bread, one must have the ideal. " Les fleurs ," (ca. 1860 - 1865), from Oeuvres complètes (1909); published in English as The Memoirs of Victor Hugo, trans. John W. Harding (1899), Chapter VI: Love in Prison, part II

text: I represent the Revolution-Civilization party, which will shape the twentieth century, birthing first the United States of Europe, then the United States of the World.
author: Victor Hugo
source: I represent a party which does not yet exist: the party Revolution-Civilization. This party will make the twentieth century. There will issue from it first the United States of Europe, then the United States of the World. Océan - Tas de pierres (1942)

text: The Orient has become a general preoccupation; its old barbarism may not be as devoid of higher men as our civilization believes.
author: Victor Hugo
source: Victor Hugo. in Jean Biès, Littérature française et pensée hindoue des origines à 1950. quoted in India’s Impact on French Thought and Literature: Eighteenth to Twentieth Century by Michel Danino (Published in Critical Practice, X:2, June 2003, pp. 46-56)

text: Enemies are but the cloud buzzing around all that shines; fame must have them, as light has gnats. Disdain them, and hold serenity in your spirit.
author: Victor Hugo
source: You have enemies? Why, it is the story of every man who has done a great deed or created a new idea. It is the cloud which thunders around everything that shines. Fame must have enemies, as light must have gnats. Do not bother yourself about it; disdain. Keep your mind serene as you keep your life clear. Villemain (1845)

text: The monstrous slumber of men's consciences must end; the indifference of the civilized world to crime is a scandal more fearful than crime's triumph.
author: Victor Hugo

text: Do not confound an epoch of vagabonds with a century of greatness; the toadstool sprouts at the foot of the oak, but it is not the oak.
author: Victor Hugo

text: We must awake this slumbering nation, shake it, and speak to it, for the radiant phantom dazzling its eyes is nothing but a huge and deadly joke.
author: Victor Hugo

text: At the seat of power: Crime, Baseness, Imbecility. In prisons, dungeons, and exile: Law, honour, intelligence, liberty, and the right.
author: Victor Hugo

text: The man is a demagogue in the nineteenth century, who in the sixteenth would have been a vagabond for believing in conscience and resisting tyranny.
author: Victor Hugo

text: This tribune was the terror of tyranny and the hope of the oppressed; whoever stood upon it, with earnest purpose, felt the pulsations of mankind's great heart and spoke words of universal charity.
author: Victor Hugo

text: From this incessantly vibrating tribune, a sonorous flood of sentiments and ideas flowed from people to people, to the earth's confines, setting in motion those intelligent waves called souls.
author: Victor Hugo

text: Now it is all over: France no longer seeks to be a great, powerful, or free nation, but merely to gorge itself and grow a fat paunch.
author: Victor Hugo

text: If a man, commanding government and army, should tear up the Constitution, violate every law, and commit every crime, your irremovable magistrates, guardians of the law, would hold their tongues.
author: Victor Hugo

text: From every nation arises a collective force; placed at liberty's service, it forms a republic. Yet, this force is not intelligent; belonging to all, it belongs to no one, floating outside the people.
author: Victor Hugo

text: An ignorant, suffering class, caught between brutish instinct and moral law, requires the pure light of the Gospel and the spirit of the Revolution to guide it towards self-denial, devotion, and probity, yet often succumbs.
author: Victor Hugo

text: When conscience fails, darkness will consume the mind of man, leaving only wild beasts to devour one another.
author: Victor Hugo

text: They sought to crush free thought, stifle intelligence, and brutalize the future.
author: Victor Hugo

text: A prophet among nations, it led the human movement, teaching fraternity and inspiring all with words like tongues of fire.
author: Victor Hugo

text: To despair is to desert. Let us look to the future: a radiant port of peace and republic for all.
author: Victor Hugo

text: Let us not curse this age; despite its shame, it is a magnificent, virile epoch.
author: Victor Hugo

text: This is the greatest of all ages, for it is the mildest: freeing slaves, dignifying women, abolishing torture, and extracting the claws of tyrants.
author: Victor Hugo

text: This age crowns the people, consecrates man, and with the thunderbolts of truth and beauty, annihilates time, space, and suffering through scientific miracles.
author: Victor Hugo

text: Despite the sneers of pedants, the nineteenth century performed glorious work, defying those who would put a fool's-cap upon humanity.
author: Victor Hugo

text: O my country, bleeding and inanimate, marked by the whip, it is at this moment that the exile's heart overflows with love and respect for you!
author: Victor Hugo

text: Les Misérables is written for all peoples, for social problems transcend borders. Wherever humanity suffers, this book knocks and says: 'Open to me, I come for you.'
author: Victor Hugo
source: You are right, sir, when you tell me that Les Misérables is written for all nations. I do not know whether it will be read by all, but I wrote it for all. It is addressed to England as well as to Spain, to Italy as well as to France, to Germany as well as to Ireland, to Republics which have slaves as well as to Empires which have serfs. Social problems surpass frontiers. The sores of the human race, those great sores which cover the globe, do not halt at the red or blue lines traced upon the map. In every place where man is ignorant and despairing, in every place where woman is sold for bread, wherever the child suffers for lack of the book which should instruct him and of the hearth which should warm him, the book of Les Misérables knocks at the door and says: "Open to me, I come for you."
source_section: Letter To M. Daelli on Les Misérables (1862)

text: In this still dark hour of civilization, the wretched one is called MAN; he agonizes under all climates, and he groans in all languages.
author: Victor Hugo
source: At the hour of civilization through which we are now passing, and which is still so sombre, the miserable's name is Man; he is agonizing in all climes, and he is groaning in all languages.
source_section: Letter To M. Daelli on Les Misérables (1862)

text: From the depths of shadow, we see no more distinctly than you the radiant gates of Eden. But the priests are mistaken: these holy gates are not behind us, but before us.
author: Victor Hugo
source: From the depths of the gloom wherein you dwell, you do not see much more distinctly than we the radiant and distant portals of Eden. Only, the priests are mistaken. These holy portals are before and not behind us.
source_section: Letter To M. Daelli on Les Misérables (1862)

text: Les Misérables is a mirror for all humanity; though truth-telling mirrors are hated, they are useful. I wrote for all, becoming more and more a patriot of humanity.
author: Victor Hugo
source: This book, Les Misérables, is no less your mirror than ours. Certain men, certain castes, rise in revolt against this book, — I understand that. Mirrors, those revealers of the truth, are hated; that does not prevent them from being of use. As for myself, I have written for all, with a profound love for my own country, but without being engrossed by France more than by any other nation. In proportion as I advance in life, I grow more simple, and I become more and more patriotic for humanity.
source_section: Letter To M. Daelli on Les Misérables (1862)

text: I suffer from universal suffering and strive to relieve it; with only a man's meager strength, I cry out to all: help me.
author: Victor Hugo
source: In short, I am doing what I can, I suffer with the same universal suffering, and I try to assuage it, I possess only the puny forces of a man, and I cry to all: "Help me!"
source_section: Letter To M. Daelli on Les Misérables (1862)

text: Misery is the garment of humankind; the moment has come to tear off this rag and replace the sinister tatter of the past with the great purple robe of dawn.
author: Victor Hugo
source: Whether we be Italians or Frenchmen, misery concerns us all. Ever since history has been written, ever since philosophy has meditated, misery has been the garment of the human race; the moment has at length arrived for tearing off that rag, and for replacing, upon the naked limbs of the Man-People, the sinister fragment of the past with the grand purple robe of the dawn.
source_section: Letter To M. Daelli on Les Misérables (1862)

text: God manifests first through the life of the universe, then through the thought of man. The second, Art, is no less sacred than the first, Nature.
author: Victor Hugo
source: God manifests himself to us in the first degree through the life of the universe, and in the second degree through the thought of man. The second manifestation is not less holy than the first. The first is named Nature, the second is named Art. Part I, Book II, Chapter I

text: Homer solves art's most beautiful problem: the true painting of humanity, achieved by magnifying man, generating the real within the ideal.
author: Victor Hugo
source: Homer is one of the men of genius who solve that fine problem of art — the finest of all, perhaps — truly to depict humanity by the enlargement of man: that is, to generate the real in the ideal. Part I, Book II, Chapter II, Section I

text: May the future be an orient instead of a sunset; that is the consolation of man.
author: Victor Hugo
source: It is man's consolation that the future is to be a sunrise instead of a sunset. Part I, Book II, Chapter II, Section V

text: What cannot be said, and what cannot be left unsaid, music expresses.
author: Victor Hugo
source: Music expresses that which cannot be said and on which it is impossible to be silent. Part I, Book II, Chapter IV

text: To put everything in balance is good; to put everything in harmony is better.
author: Victor Hugo
source: To put everything in balance is good, to put everything in harmony is better. Quatre-vingt-treize (Ninety-Three) (1874), Book VII, Chapter V

text: Victor Hugo, alas!
author: Victor Hugo
source: André Gide , as quoted in Victor Hugo's Night-Fallen Line by Aleksis Rannit in New Directions , Issue 44 (1982)

text: Victor Hugo spoke of the nineteenth century as being woman's era.
author: Victor Hugo
source: Frances Harper "The Woman's Christian Temperance Union and the Colored Woman" (1888)

text: Great poets are interpreters of eternal things, and my spirit follows them where Beauty and Truth and Goodness are one.
author: Victor Hugo
source: Helen Keller , The Story of My Life

text: Victor Hugo predicted that the nineteenth century would be the 'Century of the Woman'.
author: Victor Hugo
source: Anna Kuliscioff Il monopolio dell'uomo: conferenza tenuta nel circolo filologico milanese (1894) translated from the Italian into The Monopoly of Man by Lorenzo Chiesa (2021)

text: Victor Hugo observed that as the last age fought for men's rights, the present would strive for the independence of women.
author: Victor Hugo
source: Lucretia Mott , "Put Woman on a Par With Man" (October 1853)

text: Hugo: half priest and half anarchist, the incontestable sovereign of the century.
author: Victor Hugo
source: Jean-Paul Sartre , L'Idiot de la Famille

text: May he who loves with joy, burn bright and sail on his own wind. But if love's unworthy reign brings pain, let him seek the solace of our art.
author: Ovid
source: Let him who loves, where love success may find, Spread all his sails before the prosp'rous wind; But let poor youths who female scorn endure, And hopeless burn, repair to me for cure. Lines 13-16

text: Resist the first stirrings; for the cure comes too late when evils have grown strong through long delay.
author: Ovid
source: Resist beginnings; the remedy comes too late when the disease has gained strength by long delays. Lines 91–92

text: Seek to end love? Love yields to deeds. Act, and you shall be safe.
author: Ovid
source: Love yields to business. If you seek a way out of love, be busy; you'll be safe then. Lines 143–144

text: There is a god within us; by his stirring, we are warmed. This impulse holds the seeds of a sacred mind.
author: Ovid
source: There is a god within us. It is when he stirs us that our bosom warms ; it is his impulse that sows the seeds of inspiration. VI, lines 5-6; translation by Sir James George Frazer

text: What is permitted is unpleasing. What is forbidden burns more fiercely.
author: Ovid
source: We take no pleasure in permitted joys. But what's forbidden is more keenly sought. Book II; xix, 3

text: We always strive for what is forbidden, and desire what is denied.
author: Ovid
source: We are ever striving after what is forbidden, and coveting what is denied us.

text: Thus I can live neither without you nor with you.
author: Ovid
source: So I can't live either without you or with you.

text: At night, flaws lie hidden, every fault forgiven; that hour makes any woman beautiful.
author: Ovid
source: Blemishes are hid by night and every fault forgiven; darkness makes any woman fair. Book I, lines 249–250

text: Jupiter from on high laughs at the perjuries of lovers.
author: Ovid
source: Jupiter from above laughs at lovers' perjuries . Book I, line 633

text: It is expedient that there be gods, and as it is expedient, let us believe they exist.
author: Ovid
source: It is convenient that there be gods, and, as it is convenient, let us believe that there are. Book I, line 637

text: No law is more just than that the architects of death should perish by their own art.
author: Ovid
source: No fairer law in all the land Than that death-dealers die by what they've planned. Book I, lines 655–656 (tr. Len Krisak )

text: Endure and be firm: soon she will be mild.
author: Ovid
source: If she's cool and unwilling to be wooed, Just take it, don't weaken; in time she'll soften her mood. Bending a bough the right way, gently, makes It easy; use brute force, and it breaks. With swimming rivers it's the same— Go with, not against, the current. Book II, lines 177–182 (tr. James Michie)

text: I am a poet for the poor, because I loved as a poor man; when I could not give gifts, I gave words.
author: Ovid
source: I am the poor man's poet; because I am poor myself and I have known what it is to be in love. Not being able to pay them in presents, I pay my mistresses in poetry. Book II, lines 165–166 (tr. J. Lewis May)

text: While new love wanders, let it gather strength through use: if you nourish it well, it will be firm in time.
author: Ovid
source: Young love is errant, but it needs to get around; The time and practice make it strong and sound. That bull you fear, you petted when it wasn't big; What now you sleep beneath was once a twig. That little stream, in gaining waters as it goes, Grows stronger, till at last a river flows. Book II, lines 339–344 (tr. Len Krisak)

text: Give rest: a rested field yields a good harvest.
author: Ovid
source: Leave her alone. A fallow field soon shows its worth, And rain is best absorbed by arid earth. Book II, line 351 (tr. Len Krisak)

text: One must use time: time slips away with swift foot.
author: Ovid
source: Seize Time; his swift foot can't be held. Book III, line 65 (tr. Len Krisak)

text: Our good things flee without help; pluck the flower, which, unless plucked, will fall shamefully by itself.
author: Ovid
source: Our charms depart all on their own, so pluck the bloom. For if you don't, it meets a wasted doom. Book III, lines 79–80 (tr. Len Krisak)

text: Chance prevails everywhere; let your hook always hang where you least expect it, there will be a fish.
author: Ovid
source: Chance is always powerful. Let your hook always be cast; in the pool where you least expect it, there will be fish. Book III, line 425

text: Fair peace becomes men, fierce anger becomes beasts.
author: Ovid
source: Rage is for beasts, but shining peace for man. Book III, line 502 (tr. Len Krisak)

text: There is a god within us, and commerce with heaven: that spirit comes from ethereal abodes.
author: Ovid
source: We all conceal A god within us, we all deal With heaven direct, from whose high places we derive The inspiration by which we live. Book III, lines 549–550 (tr. James Michie)

text: A more sacred animal, capable of lofty thought, and one that could dominate all others, was still lacking: Man was born.
author: Ovid
source: A creature of a more exalted kind Was wanting yet, and then was Man designed; Conscious of thought, of more capacious breast, For empire formed, and fit to rule the rest. Book I, 76 (as translated by John Dryden )

text: While other animals look prone upon the earth, he gave to man a lofty countenance, and bade him look up to heaven, and raise his erect face to the stars.
author: Ovid
source: Thus, while the mute creation downward bend Their sight, and to their earthly mother tend, Man looks aloft, and with erected eyes Beholds his own hereditary skies. Book I, 84 (as translated by John Dryden)

text: Then the almighty father shattered Olympus with a hurled thunderbolt and shook Pelion from beneath Ossa.
author: Ovid
source: Then the omnipotent Father with his thunder made Olympus tremble, and from Ossa hurled Pelion. Book I, 154 Compare: "Heav'd on Olympus tott'ring Ossa stood; On Ossa, Pelion nods with all his wood", Alexander Pope , The Odyssey of Homer , Book xi, line 387; "would have you call to mind the strength of the ancient giants, that undertook to lay the high mountain Pelion on the top of Ossa, and set among those the shady Olympus", François Rabelais , Works , book iv. chap. xxxviii.

text: Since his years forbid him to fight, he battles with words, assailing and cursing wicked arms.
author: Ovid
source: He, because years restrained him from warring, fought by speaking; he attacked and cursed the criminal weapons. Book V, 101-2

text: The use of waters is common to all. Nature made neither the sun, nor the air, nor the flowing waters private property.
author: Ovid
source: Water belongs to us all. Nature did not make the sun one person's property, nor air, nor water, cool and clear. Book VI, 349-351; translation by Michael Simpson

text: I see the better, and approve it, but I follow the worse.
author: Ovid
source: I see better things, and approve, but I follow worse. Book VII, 20

text: Fly along the middle path, Icarus. If too low, water will weigh your wings; if too high, fire will scorch them. Fly between the two extremes.
author: Ovid
source: My son , I caution you to keep The middle way, for if your pinions dip Too low the waters may impede your flight; And if they soar too high the sun may scorch them. Fly midway. Book VIII, lines 203–206; translation by Brooks More

text: He is indeed beautiful, though to envious eyes.
author: Ovid
source: He is beautiful even to enemy eyes. Book IX, 476

text: What fault is mine, then? Unless to have played can be called a fault, unless to have loved can also be called a fault.
author: Ovid
source: What, however, is my fault? Unless to have played can be called a fault, unless it can be called a fault to have loved. Book X, 200-1

text: For lineage and ancestors, and what we ourselves have not done, I scarcely call our own.
author: Ovid
source: For those things which were done either by our fathers, or ancestors, and in which we ourselves had no share, we can scarcely call our own. Book XIII, 140–141

text: Mind, not might, truly wins the war.
author: Ovid
source: Your right arm is useful in the battle; but when it comes to thinking you need my guidance. You have force without intelligence; while mine is the care for to-morrow. You are a good fighter; but is I who help Atrides select the time of fighting. Your value is in your body only; mine, in mind. And, as much as he who directs the ship surpasses him who only rows it, as much as the general exceeds the common soldier, so much greater am I than you. For in these bodies of ours the heart is of more value than the hand; all our real living is in that. Book XIII, 361–369; translation by Frank Justus Miller

text: I asked for years as countless as the sand, but forgot to ask for youth.
author: Ovid
source: Pointing to a pile of dust, that had collected, I foolishly begged to have as many anniversaries of my birth, as were represented by the dust. But I forgot to ask that the years should be accompanied by youth. Book XIV, lines 136–139; translation by A. S. Kline

text: The lavish earth offers gentle foods and feasts without slaughter or blood.
author: Ovid
source: O mortals, from your fellows' blood abstain, Nor taint your bodies with a food profane: While corn, and pulse by Nature are bestow'd, And planted orchards bend their willing load; While labour'd gardens wholesom herbs produce, And teeming vines afford their gen'rous juice; Nor tardier fruits of cruder kind are lost, But tam'd with fire, or mellow'd by the frost; While kine to pails distended udders bring, And bees their hony redolent of Spring; While Earth not only can your needs supply, But, lavish of her store, provides for luxury; A guiltless feast administers with ease, And without blood is prodigal to please. Book XV , 75–82 (from Wikisource); on vegetarianism , as the following quote

text: What a crime, to live by another's death, when Earth, our mother, offers such abundant, gentle feasts.
author: Ovid
source: O impious use! to Nature's laws oppos'd, Where bowels are in other bowels clos'd: Where fatten'd by their fellow's fat, they thrive; Maintain'd by murder, and by death they live. 'Tis then for nought, that Mother Earth provides The stores of all she shows, and all she hides, If men with fleshy morsels must be fed, And chaw with bloody teeth the breathing bread: What else is this, but to devour our guests, And barb'rously renew Cyclopean feasts! We, by destroying life, our life sustain; And gorge th' ungodly maw with meats obscene. Book XV , 88–95 (from Wikisource)

text: Nothing truly perishes; it only changes form. To be born is to become new; to die, to cease being the old.
author: Ovid
source: No species remains constant: that great renovator of matter Nature, endlessly fashions new forms from old: there’s nothing in the whole universe that perishes, believe me; rather it renews and varies its substance. What we describe as birth is no more than incipient change from a prior state, while dying is merely to quit it. Though the parts may be transported hither and thither, the sum of all matter is constant. Book XV, 252–258 (as translated by Peter Green )

text: Poems flow from a serene soul.
author: Ovid
source: Poetry comes fine-spun from a mind at ease. I, i, 39

text: While fortune smiles, friends abound; when clouds gather, you'll stand alone.
author: Ovid
source: So long as you are secure you will count many friends; if your life becomes clouded you will be alone. I, ix, 5

text: Ants shun empty granaries; friends flee lost fortunes.
author: Ovid
source: Ants never head for an empty granary: no friends gather round when your wealth is gone. I, ix, 9-10; translation by A.S. Kline

text: Believe me, he who lives hidden, lives well; let each remain within their own fortune.
author: Ovid
source: Well doth he live who lives retired, and keeps His wants within the limit of his means.

text: The greater the soul, the more placable its wrath; a noble mind finds peace when the foe is fallen.
author: Ovid
source: The greater a man is, the more can his wrath be appeased; a noble spirit is capable of kindly impulses. For the noble lion 'tis enough to have overthrown his enemy; the fight is at an end when his foe is fallen. But the wolf, the ignoble bears harry the dying and so with every beast of less nobility. At Troy what have we mightier than brave Achilles ? But the tears of the aged Dardanian he could not endure. III, v, 33; translation by Arthur Leslie Wheeler Note: "the aged Dardanian" here refers to Priam

text: I live, you say, but in a way I wish I did not.
author: Ovid
source: Say that I live, but in such wise that I would not live. III, vii, 7; translation by Arthur Leslie Wheeler

text: Difficult, I confess, but virtue strives for the heights, and greater will be the grace of such merit.
author: Ovid
source: ' Tis hard, I admit, yet virtue aims at what is hard , and gratitude for such a service will be all the greater. II, ii, 111-112; translation by Arthur Leslie Wheeler
source_section: Epistulae ex Ponto (Letters From the Black Sea)

text: Shameful to say, but the common crowd judges friendships by their use.
author: Ovid
source: Shameful it is to say, yet the common herd, if only we admit the truth, value friendships by their profit. II, iii, 7-8; translation by Arthur Leslie Wheeler
source_section: Epistulae ex Ponto (Letters From the Black Sea)

text: Few deem virtue its own reward; only what profits is held dear.
author: Ovid
source: Nor can one easily find among many thousands a single man who considers virtue its own reward. The very glory of a good deed, if it lacks reward, affects them not; unrewarded uprightness brings them regret. Nothing but profit is prized. II, iii, 11-15; translation by Arthur Leslie Wheeler. Variant translation of gratis paenitet esse probum , in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 15th ed. (1980), p. 114: "It is annoying to be honest to no purpose."
source_section: Epistulae ex Ponto (Letters From the Black Sea)

text: To learn the liberal arts faithfully softens manners and refines the soul.
author: Ovid
source: Note too that a faithful study of the liberal arts humanizes character and permits it not to be cruel. II, ix, 47
source_section: Epistulae ex Ponto (Letters From the Black Sea)

text: Though strength may fail, the will itself is worthy of praise.
author: Ovid
source: Though strength be lacking, yet the will is to be praised. III, iv, 79
source_section: Epistulae ex Ponto (Letters From the Black Sea)

text: He now journeys on that dark path, from which none, they say, return.
author: Catullus
source: Now he goes along the dark road, thither whence they say no one returns.

text: Suns can set and rise again; but when our brief light sets, one perpetual night awaits.
author: Catullus
source: Let us live, my Lesbia, and love, and value at one farthing all the talk of crabbed old men. Suns may set and rise again. For us, when the short light has once set, remains to be slept the sleep of one unbroken night.

text: He himself knows not who he is, nor if he truly exists.
author: Catullus
source: What he himself is, whether he is or is not, he does not know so much as this.

text: What is more blessed than cares released, when weary from toil, we rest in our longed-for home?
author: Catullus
source: Ah, what is more blessed than to put cares away, when the mind lays by its burden, and tired with labour of far travel we have come to our own home and rest on the couch we longed for? This it is which alone is worth all these toils.

text: He seems a god, or more than gods, who sits and gazes on your sweet laughter.
author: Catullus
source: He seems to me to be equal to a god, he, if it may be, seems to surpass the very gods, who sitting opposite thee again and again gazes at thee and hears thee sweetly laughing.

text: Idleness has destroyed both kings and prosperous cities.
author: Catullus
source: Idleness ere now has ruined both kings and wealthy cities.

text: What gift from the gods is more desired than a happy hour?
author: Catullus
source: What is given by the gods more desirable than the fortunate hour?

text: As a flower grows hidden in a fenced garden, untouched by plow, caressed by breezes, longed for by many.
author: Catullus
source: As a flower springs up secretly in a fenced garden, unknown to the cattle, torn up by no plough, which the winds caress, the sun strengthens, the shower draws forth, many boys, many girls, desire it.

text: Let no woman believe a man's oaths; for once desire is sated, his promises are but perjuries.
author: Catullus
source: Henceforth let no woman believe a man's oath, let none believe that a man's speeches can be trustworthy. They, while their mind desires something and longs eagerly to gain it, nothing fear to swear, nothing spare to promise; but as soon as the lust of their greedy mind is satisfied, they fear not then their words, they heed not their perjuries.

text: All things, right and wrong, mixed in evil madness, have turned from us the just mind of the gods.
author: Catullus
source: All right and wrong, confounded in impious madness, turned from us the righteous will of the gods.

text: What a woman says to an eager lover, one should write in the wind and swift water.
author: Catullus
source: What a woman says to her ardent lover should be written in wind and running water.

text: Cease to wish to deserve good from anyone, or to think that anyone can be truly pious.
author: Catullus
source: Leave off wishing to deserve any thanks from anyone, or thinking that anyone can ever become grateful.

text: My mind, by your fault, is so lost in devotion, it can neither wish you well, nor cease to love you.
author: Catullus
source: To this point is my mind reduced by your fault, Lesbia, and has so ruined itself by its own devotion, that now it can neither wish you well though you should become the best of women, nor cease to love you though you do the worst that can be done.

text: It is difficult to suddenly lay aside a long-held love.
author: Catullus
source: It is difficult suddenly to lay aside a long-standing love.

text: I hate and I love. Why, you ask? I know not, but I feel it, and I am torn.
author: Catullus
source: I hate and love. Why I do so, perhaps you ask. I know not, but I feel it, and I am in torment.

text: An admirable poet. No Latin writer is so Greek. The simplicity, the pathos, the perfect grace, which I find in the great Athenian models, are all in Catullus, and in him alone of the Romans.
author: Catullus
source: Thomas Babington Macaulay , in The Life and Letters of Lord Macaulay , ed. G. Otto Trevelyan, Vol. I (1875), Appendix, p. 410

text: It is this clear and almost terrible simplicity that puts Catullus in a place by himself: he sees with a single glance, and does not strike a second time.
author: Catullus
source: John William Mackail , Latin Literature (1896), p. 61

text: It passes my comprehension why Tennyson called him 'tender'. He is vindictive, venomous, and full of obscene malice.
author: Catullus
source: Harold Nicolson , Diaries and Letters , Vol. III: The Later Years, 1945–1962 , pp. 332–333

text: The most hard-edged and intense of the Latin poets.
author: Catullus
source: Ezra Pound , letter to Harriet Monroe (February 1916), in The Selected Letters of Ezra Pound, 1907–1941 (1950), p. 69

text: Tenderest of Roman poets nineteen-hundred years ago.
author: Catullus
source: Alfred, Lord Tennyson , 'Frater Ave atque Vale' (1883), line 6

text: Do not go outside, return into yourself. In the inner man dwells truth. And if you find your nature mutable, transcend even yourself.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Do not go outside yourself, return to yourself: truth dwells in the interiority of man and, if you find that your nature is changeable, transcend yourself too . As quoted in De vera religione , XXXIX, 72

text: The spiritual power of the Sacrament, like light, remains pure even when passing through the impure.
author: Saint Augustine
source: For the spiritual power of a sacrament is like light in this way: it is both received pure by those to be enlightened, and if it passes through the impure it is not defiled. Tractates on the Gospel of John ; tractate V on John 1:33, §15; translation by R. Willems

text: If you say, 'I have reached perfection,' all is lost, for perfection's function is to reveal imperfection.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Quoted by Aldous Huxley , in The Perennial Philosophy (1945)

text: This is the rock, which the gates of hell shall not overcome.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Come, brethren, if you wish to be engrafted in the vine. It grieves us to see you thus lie cut off. Number the priests in the very chair of Peter, And see in that order of fathers who succeeded the other . This is the rock which the proud gates of hell overcome not. Publications of the Catholic Truth Society (1895), Catholic Truth Society, London, vol. 24, p. 42.
source_section: Psalmus Contra Partem Donati - Psalm Against the Donatists (c. 393)

text: I was not yet in love, yet I loved to love; I sought what I might love, loving to love.
author: Saint Augustine
source: I was not yet in love , yet I loved to love...I sought what I might love, in love with loving.

text: The sun and moon were but dishes, serving me, who hungered for you.
author: Saint Augustine
source: And these were the dishes wherein to me, hunger-starven for thee, they served up the sun and the moon.

text: Truth is not found in eloquence, nor falsehood in stammering; wisdom and folly can be served in any dish.
author: Saint Augustine

text: I loved to excuse my soul and accuse something else, but it was I; my impiety divided me against myself, and my sin was incurable because I did not deem myself a sinner.
author: Saint Augustine
source: A. Outler, trans. (Dover: 2002), Book 5, Chapter 10, p. 77

text: Allegorical interpretation of the Old Testament revealed a faith I could embrace, where literal readings had spiritually killed me.
author: Saint Augustine
source: A. Outler, trans. (Dover: 2002), Book 5, Chapter 14, p. 81.

text: But I, a wretched young man, wretched at the very outset of my adolescence, had even asked you for chastity and said, 'Grant me chastity and continence, but not yet.'
author: Saint Augustine
source: As a youth I prayed, " Give me chastity and continence, but not right now. "

text: I heard a voice singing, 'Take up and read!' I opened the book, and the words I read instantly flooded my heart with certainty, banishing all doubt.
author: Saint Augustine
source: I was saying these things and weeping in the most bitter contrition of my heart, when suddenly I heard the voice of a boy or a girl I know not which — coming from the neighboring house, chanting over and over again, " Take up and read; take up and read ." Immediately I ceased weeping and began most earnestly to think whether it was usual for children in some kind of game to sing such a song, but I could not remember ever having heard the like. So, damming the torrent of my tears, I got to my feet, for I could not but think that this was a divine command to open the Bible and read the first passage I should light upon. For I had heard how Anthony, accidentally coming into church while the gospel was being read, received the admonition as if what was read had been addressed to him: "Go and sell what you have and give it to the poor, and you shall have treasure in heaven; and come and follow me" (Matt. 19:21). By such an oracle he was forthwith converted to thee. So I quickly returned to the bench where Alypius was sitting, for there I had put down the apostle’s book when I had left there. I snatched it up, opened it, and in silence read the paragraph on which my eyes first fell: "Not in rioting and drunkenness, not in chambering and wantonness, not in strife and envying, but put on the Lord Jesus Christ , and make no provision for the flesh to fulfill the lusts thereof" (Rom. 13:13). I wanted to read no further, nor did I need to. For instantly, as the sentence ended, there was infused in my heart something like the light of full certainty and all the gloom of doubt vanished away.

text: I asked the whole frame of earth about my God, and it answered, 'I am not he, but he made me.'
author: Saint Augustine

text: Too late have I loved you, O Beauty ever ancient, ever new, too late have I loved you! And behold, you were within, and I outside, and there I sought you.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Late have I loved you, O Beauty ever ancient and ever new! Late have I loved you! And, behold, you were within me, and I out of myself, and there I searched for you. X, 27, as translated in Theology and Discovery: Essays in honor of Karl Rahner, S.J. (1980) edited by William J. Kelly

text: I love not these things when I love my God; and yet I love a certain kind of light, and sound, and fragrance, and food, and embracement in loving my God, who is the light, sound, fragrance, food, and embracement of my inner man.
author: Saint Augustine

text: Grant what you command, and command what you will.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Give what you command, and command what you will. You impose continency on us.

text: People travel to wonder at mountains, waves, rivers, oceans, and stars; and they pass by themselves without wondering.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Variant: Men go abroad to admire the heights of mountains, the mighty billows of the sea, the broad tides of rivers, the compass of the ocean, and the circuits of the stars, and pass themselves by.

text: There is a temptation, more complex in its peril, originating in an appetite for knowledge, which to know profits not, and wherein men desire nothing but to know.
author: Saint Augustine

text: You called, shattered my deafness; you shone, dispelled my blindness; you were fragrant, and I pant after you; you tasted, and I hunger for you; you touched me, and I burn for your peace.
author: Saint Augustine

text: Wonderful is the depth of Thy oracles, whose surface is before us, inviting the little ones; and yet wonderful is the depth, O my God, wonderful is the depth.
author: Saint Augustine

text: The good man is neither uplifted with the good things of time, nor broken by its ills; but the wicked man, corrupted by this world’s happiness, feels himself punished by its unhappiness.
author: Saint Augustine

text: So material a difference does it make, not what ills are suffered, but what kind of man suffers them.
author: Saint Augustine

text: The very lust for domination, which among other vices was more prevalent in the entire Roman people, once it conquered in a few more powerful individuals, crushed the rest, worn out and exhausted, under the yoke of servitude.
author: Saint Augustine
source: The lust for power, which of all human vices was found in its most concentrated form in the Roman people as a whole, first established its victory in a few powerful individuals, and then crushed the rest of an exhausted country beneath the yoke of slavery. For when can that lust for power in arrogant hearts come to rest until, after passing from one office to another, it arrives at sovereignty? Now there would be no occasion for this continuous progress if ambition were not all-powerful; and the essential context for ambition is a people corrupted by greed and sensuality. as translated by H. Bettenson (1972), Book 1, Chapter 31, p. 42

text: The good man, although he is a slave, is free; but the bad man, even if he reigns, is a slave, and that not of one man, but of as many masters as he has vices.
author: Saint Augustine

text: Without justice, what are kingdoms but great robberies? For what are robberies themselves but little kingdoms? As a captured pirate told Alexander the Great: 'Because I do it with a small ship, I am called a robber; because you do it with a great fleet, you are called an emperor.'
author: Saint Augustine
source: Justice being taken away, then, what are kingdoms but great robberies? For what are robberies themselves, but little kingdoms? The band itself is made up of men; it is ruled by the authority of a prince, it is knit together by the pact of the confederacy; the booty is divided by the law agreed on. If, by the admittance of abandoned men, this evil increases to such a degree that it holds places, fixes abodes, takes possession of cities, and subdues peoples, it assumes the more plainly the name of a kingdom, because the reality is now manifestly conferred on it, not by the removal of covetousness, but by the addition of impunity. Indeed, that was an apt and true reply which was given to Alexander the Great by a pirate who had been seized. For when that king had asked the man what he meant by keeping hostile possession of the sea, he answered with bold pride, “What thou meanest by seizing the whole earth; but because I do it with a petty ship, I am called a robber, whilst thou who dost it with a great fleet art styled emperor.” IV, 4

text: Our ultimate good is to cling to God, by whose embrace the intellectual soul is filled and made fruitful with true virtues. This is the end to which all our actions should tend, that we may be blessed.
author: Saint Augustine
source: We direct our course towards him with love, so that in reaching him we may find our rest, and attain our happiness because we have achieved our fulfillment in him. For our Good, that Final Good about which the philosophers dispute, is nothing else but to cleave to him whose spiritual embrace, if one may so express it, fills the intellectual soul and makes it fertile with true virtues. We are commanded to love this Good with all our heart, with all our soul, with all our strength; and to this Good we must be led by those who love us, and to it we must lead those whom we love. Thus are fulfilled those two commands on which 'all the Law and the prophets depend': 'Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, with all thy soul, and with all thy mind', and 'Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself'. For in order that a man may know how to love himself an end has been established for him to which he is to refer all his action, so that he may attain to bliss. For if a man loves himself, his one wish is to achieve blessedness. Now this end is 'to cling to God'. Thus, if a man knows how to love himself, the commandment to love his neighbour bids him to do all he can to bring his neighbour to love God. (translated by Henry Bettenson) X, 3

text: Evil has no positive nature; but the loss of good has received the name 'evil.'
author: Saint Augustine

text: Satan sometimes transforms himself into an angel of light. There is great need of God’s mercy to preserve us from making friends of demons in disguise, while we fancy we have good angels for our friends.
author: Saint Augustine

text: The Head and the body are Christ wholly and entirely. The Head is the only begotten Son of God, the body is His Church; the bridegroom and the bride, two in one flesh.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Encyclical letter of Pope Leo XIII on the Unity of the Church , June 29, 1896, ch. 16, Publications of the Catholic Truth Society , 1896, London, Volume 30, p. 41. Alternate translation: The whole Christ is Head and Body. The Head, the only begotten Son of God; and His Body, the Church: the Bridegroom and the Bride, two in one flesh. Whosoever dissent from the Holy Scriptures in respect of the Head, even though they be found in all the places in which the Church is marked out to be, are not in the Church. And again, whosoever agree with the Holy Scriptures concerning the Head, and do not communicate with the unity of the Body, are not in the Church, because they dissent from Christ's own witness concerning Christ's Body, which is the Church. Dr. Pusey , and the Ancient Church (1866), by Thomas W. Allies, Longmans, Green, London, p. 82
source_section: De Unitate Ecclesiae - On the Unity of the Church (c. 401 – 405)

text: We may not assent to the teaching even of the Catholic bishops, if they are deceived into opinions contrary to the canonical Scriptures of God.
author: Saint Augustine
source: The Unity of the Church (1842), Henry Edward Manning , John Murray (pub.), London, p. 52. Comment: “As for contemporary figures, Augustine urged the Donatist faithful not to heed their bishops who were perpetuating the schism. ‘Neither should the Catholic bishops be followed when they are wrong and hold an opinion contrary to the canonical Scriptures of God.’ As Congar explains, each bishop is united to the faith of his Church which in turn is dependent on Scripture. In saying this, Augustine apparently did not take seriously the possibility of some quasi-universal opposition to Scripture among Catholic bishops, but rather envisaged a possible local or regional disruption. Augustinian Studies , 1980, vols. 11-12, Augustinian Institute, Villanova, Pa., Villanova University Press, p. 138.
source_section: De Unitate Ecclesiae - On the Unity of the Church (c. 401 – 405)

text: If a man makes for his betrothed a ring, and she prefers the ring to him, is not her heart convicted of infidelity? God has given you all things: therefore, love Him who made them.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Second Homily, as translated by John Burnaby (1955), pp. 275-276
source_section: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John (414)

text: As love grows in you, so grows your beauty; for charity itself is the beauty of the soul.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Beauty grows in you to the extent that love grows, because charity itself is the soul 's beauty. Ninth Homily, Paragraph 9, as translated by Boniface Ramsey (2008) Augustinian Heritage Institute
source_section: Ten Homilies on the First Epistle of John (414)

text: The mind itself, its love [of itself] and its knowledge [of itself] are a kind of trinity.
author: Saint Augustine

text: Yes, you see the Trinity if you see charity .
author: Saint Augustine

text: No salvation outside the Church.
author: Saint Augustine
source: There is no salvation outside the church.

text: Beware of diviners, even those who speak truth, lest they ensnare your soul with demons.
author: Saint Augustine

text: In obscure matters of faith, do not rush to a firm stand, lest the progress of truth undermine your position and you fall with it.
author: Saint Augustine
source: I, xviii, 37. Modern translation by J.H. Taylor

text: Love, and do what thou wilt: let the root of love be within, for from this root can nothing spring but what is good.
author: Saint Augustine

text: Faith alone is not enough; works must be joined to it, for faith works through love.
author: Saint Augustine

text: God does not love to condemn but to save, and therefore he is patient with the wicked, that he may make good out of evil.
author: Saint Augustine
source: For God loves to save and not to condemn ; therefore is he patient with evil, that out of evil good may be brought. 18

text: What is anger? The lust for revenge. What is hatred? Inveterate anger.
author: Saint Augustine
source: What is anger? A lust for revenge. What is hatred? An anger grown old. 58

text: Give therefore to the poor: I ask, I warn, I command, I order.
author: Saint Augustine
source: So give to the poor; I’m begging you, I’m warning you, I’m commanding you, I’m ordering you. 61:13

text: The one who urges vengeance robs you of your forgiveness; forfeit that, and all your sins will be held against you.
author: Saint Augustine

text: God 'worships' us, tending our worth as a farmer tends his field.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Sermon 87:2 ( Sermon 37:2 ) on Matthew 20. Preached in the autumn after 424. Latin

text: Seeing the evil in forswearing oneself, I broke that old habit.
author: Saint Augustine

text: Honor the martyrs, but worship their God.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Venerate the martyrs, praise, love, proclaim, honor them. But worship the God of the martyrs . 273:9; translation from: The works of Saint Augustine , John E. Rotelle, New City Press, ISBN 1565480600 ISBN 9781565480605 p . 21.

text: In Rome, an emperor sought salvation not at his proud temple, but at the humble tomb of a fisherman.
author: Saint Augustine
source: In our own times, you see, an emperor came to the city of Rome, where there’s the temple of an emperor, where there’s a fisherman’s tomb .
