And so that pious and Christian emperor, wishing to beg for health, for salvation from the Lord, did not proceed to the temple of a proud emperor, but to the tomb of a fisherman, where he could imitate that fisherman in humility, so that he, being thus approached, might then obtain something from the Lord, which a haughty emperor would be quite unable to earn. 341:4; English from: Newly Discovered Sermons , 1997, Edmund Hill, tr., John E. Rotelle, ed., New City Press, New York, ISBN 1565481038 ISBN 9781565481039p . p. 286.

text: We can die and sin by ourselves, but we cannot rise by ourselves. Our hope is only in God.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Why, being dead, do you rely on yourself? You were able to die of your own accord; you cannot come back to life of your own accord. We were able to sin by ourselves, and we are still able to, nor shall we ever not be able to. Let our hope be in nothing but in God. Let us send up our sighs to him; as for ourselves, let us strive with our wills to earn merit by our prayers. 348A:4 Against Pelagius ; English translation from: Newly Discovered Sermons , 1997, Edmund Hill, John E. Rotelle, New City Press, New York, ISBN 1565481038 , 9781565481039 pp. 311-312. Editor’s comment: “This sounds like a slightly Pelagian remark! But it is presumably intended to reverse what one may call the Pelagian order of things; and see the last few sections of the sermon, 9-15, on the effect of the heresy on prayer.”

text: They preach how much Christ was exalted from you; Christ says how much He descended to you.
author: Saint Augustine
source: So they [the pagans] are going to say, “You tell me that Christ has risen again, and from that you hope for the resurrection of the dead; but Christ was in a position to rise from the dead.” And now he begins to praise Christ, not in order to do him honor, but to make you despair. It is the deadly cunning of the serpent, to turn you away from Christ by praising Christ, to extol deceitfully the one he doesn’t dare to disparage. He exaggerates the sovereign majesty of Christ in order to make him out quite unique , to stop you hoping for anything like what was demonstrated in his rising again. And he seems, apparently, to be all the more religiously respectful of Christ, when he says, “Look at the person who dares compare himself to Christ, so that just because Christ rose again, he can imagine that he's going to rise again too!” Don't let this perverse praise of your emperor disturb you. The insidious tricks of the enemy may disturb you, but the humility and humanity of Christ should console you. This man emphasizes how high above you Christ has been lifted up; Christ, though, says how low he came down to you. Sermon 361 On the Resurrection of the Dead ; 15 How to answer their exaggerated praise of Christ and their disparaging of Christians .

text: Not only are we become Christians, but we are become Christ.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: To understand the Scriptures, one must know the whole Christ: Head and members.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: We do not separate the two realities, but two different dignities; for the Head saves, and the body is saved.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: To sever the Head from the body is an injury to both Head and body.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: If Christ is with us, then He speaks in us, of us, and through us; and we too speak in Him.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: When Christ cried, 'My God, why hast Thou forsaken Me?' the words He uttered on the cross were not His own, but ours.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: Say nothing without Him, and He will say nothing without thee.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: But how can this one man cry out from the ends of the earth, unless he be one in all?
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: The full measure of the Passion will not be attained until the end of the world.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: To claim holiness of oneself is pride; but to deny it as a member of Christ is ingratitude.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: No, this holy one is not proud, for he trusts in God.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: The cry of one is as the cry of all, for all are one in One.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: Therefore this one man that is taken to become the temple of God, is at once many and one.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: Their homage would be vain, so long as they despise His members on earth.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: On the Mystical Body of Christ

text: I would not believe the Gospel unless the authority of the Catholic Church moved me.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Nicene Post-Nicene Fathers translation: "I should not believe the gospel except as moved by the authority of the Catholic Church."

text: Humility makes men like holy angels, and pride makes demons out of angels.
author: Saint Augustine
source: It was pride that changed angels into devils ; it is humility that makes men as angels. As quoted in Manipulus Florum ( c. 1306), edited by Thomas Hibernicus , Superbia i cum uariis; also in Best Thoughts Of Best Thinkers: Amplified, Classified, Exemplified and Arranged as a Key to unlock the Literature of All Ages (1904) edited by Hialmer Day Gould and Edward Louis Hessenmueller

text: My mother spoke of Christ to my father, by her feminine and childlike virtues, and, after having borne his violence without a murmur or complaint, gained him at the close of his life to Christ.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Reported in Josiah Hotchkiss Gilbert, Dictionary of Burning Words of Brilliant Writers (1895), p. 351

text: What does love look like? It has the hands to help others. It has the feet to hasten to the poor and needy. It has eyes to see misery and want. It has the ears to hear the sighs and sorrows of men. That is what love looks like.
author: Saint Augustine
source: As quoted in Quote, Unquote (1977) by Lloyd Cory, p. 197

text: Hope has two beautiful daughters. Their names are anger and courage ; anger at the way things are, and courage to see that they do not remain the way they are.
author: Saint Augustine
source: As quoted in Spirituality and Liberation: Overcoming the Great Fallacy (1988) by Robert McAfee Brown, p. 136

text: Without God , we cannot. Without us, God will not.
author: Saint Augustine
source: As quoted in If God Be For Us : Sermons on the Gifts of the Gospel (1954), by Robert Edward Luccock, p. 38; this may be a variant translation or paraphrase of an expression in his 169th sermon: "He who created you without you will not justify you without you."

text: Our bodies are shaped to bear children, and our lives are a working out of the processes of creation. All our ambitions and intelligence are beside that great elemental point.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Sometimes attributed to Augustine, but is from Phyllis McGinley , The Province of the Heart , "The Honor of Being a Woman" (1959).

text: It would be easier to empty the ocean into a tiny hole than to grasp the incomprehensible ocean of the Holy Trinity.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Louis-Victor-Emile Bougaud , presenting a traditional anecdote in Histoire de sainte Monique (1866), as translated in The Mother of Saint Augustine (1869) by Lady Herbert of Lea

text: I believe in order to understand.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Fernand Braudel , A History of Civilizations (1963), Penguin Books (1995 edition), p. 335

text: Lustful cruelty or cruel lust.
author: Saint Augustine
source: John T. Noonan Jr., [ https://scholarship.law.nd.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1125&context=nd_naturallaw_forum “Abortion and the Catholic Church: A Summary History”, 1-1-1967, ‘’Natural Law Forum’’. Paper 126, pp. 95-96

text: In the institution of Nature we should not look for miracles but for the laws of Nature.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Henry Fairfield Osborn , From the Greeks to Darwin (1894)

text: Acknowledge order, seek peace. Be thou subject to God, and thy flesh subject to thee.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Pius XI quoting St. Augustine, "Ennarration in Psalmis," 143; "Casti Connubii: Encyclical of Pope Pius XI on Christian Marriage, December 31, 1930" , ‘’The Vatican’’, “DIVINELY ORDAINED PLAN”, n.29-30; Archived from the original on March 28, 2007. Retrieved 2006-10-01.

text: To suppose that God formed man from the dust with bodily hands is very childish.
author: Saint Augustine
source: Andrew Dickson White , A History of the Warfare of Science with Theology in Christendom (1896), Ch.1, pp.52-53

text: I cannot find a satisfactory and worthy explanation; because I cannot find one.
author: Saint Augustine
source_section: “When Children Became People: the birth of childhood in early Christianity” (2005)

text: Sing, my tongue, the mystery of the glorious Body, and of the precious Blood, which the King of nations, the fruit of a generous womb, shed for the ransom of the world.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: Sing, my tongue, the Savior's glory, Of His Flesh the mystery sing; Of the Blood, all price exceeding, Shed by our immortal King.

text: Every truth, by whomever spoken, is from the Holy Spirit.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: All that is true, from anyone it is said, always comes from the Holy Spirit . Summa Theologiae , I-II, q. 109, a. 1, ad 1

text: All I have written seems like straw compared to what I have seen and what has been revealed to me.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: Processus canonizationis S. Thomae, Neapoli , LXXIX

text: Jesus: 'You have written well of me, Thomas; what reward will you receive?' Aquinas: 'No other, Lord, than yourself.'
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: Jesus Christ: "You wrote correctly about me, Thomas. What prize do you want to have?" Aquinas: "Nothing, my Lord, except for Yourself." Lect. V- II Noct.

text: When authority commands sin or acts against virtue, one is obliged to disobey, as did the martyrs.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: in Aquinas: Selected Political Writings (Basil Blackwell: 1974), p. 183
source_section: Commentary on the Sentences of Peter Lombard (c. 1254–1256)

text: The perfection of the effect demonstrates the perfection of the cause.
author: Thomas Aquinas

text: Natural inclinations, being from God, cannot be evil; thus, carnal union, a natural inclination, cannot be evil.
author: Thomas Aquinas

text: Evil exists not from God's weakness, but from His wisdom, allowing diverse grades of goodness that would otherwise be lacking.
author: Thomas Aquinas

text: Life is most manifest in that which moves itself; that which cannot move but by another seems as if dead.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: The highest manifestation of life consists in this: that a being governs its own actions. A being that is always subject to the direction of another is somewhat of a dead thing.
source_section: On The Perfection of the Spiritual Life (1269-1270)

text: Three things are necessary for man's salvation: the knowledge of what to believe, the knowledge of what to desire, and the knowledge of what to do.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: Three things are necessary for man to be saved: knowledge of what is to be believed, knowledge of what is to be desired, and knowledge of what is to be done.

text: The natural law is nothing other than the light of understanding instilled in us by God, by which we know what to do and what to avoid. God gave this light and this law to man at creation.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: The law of nature […] is nothing other than the light of the intellect planted in us by God, by which we know what should be done and what should be avoided. God gave us this light or law in creation.

text: No evil act is excused by good intention.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: No evil can be excused because it is done with a good intention .

text: Our manner of knowing is so weak that no philosopher could perfectly investigate the nature of even one little fly.
author: Thomas Aquinas

text: Just as increasing heat implies a hidden fire, so the world's ascending beauty and excellence reveal God's presence.
author: Thomas Aquinas

text: The Summa Theologica stands as one of humanity's most stupendous intellectual feats, a world perfectly boxed and compassed, every detail fitted with subtle precision into a consistent, convincing whole.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: Carl L. Becker , The Heavenly City of the Eighteenth-century Philosophers (1932)

text: St. Thomas Aquinas, 'The Ox' for his massive brain, was one of history's greatest minds, a giant whose true scale is only apparent when measured against his few rivals.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: G. K. Chesterton , in "St. Thomas Aquinas" in The Spectator (27 February 1932)

text: St. Thomas confronts other creeds of good and evil, without at all denying evil, with a theory of two levels of good. The supernatural order is the supreme good, as for any Eastern mystic; but the natural order is good; as solidly good as it is for any man in the street.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: G. K. Chesterton , in "St. Thomas Aquinas" in The Spectator (27 February 1932)

text: St. Thomas exalted God without lowering Man; he exalted Man without lowering Nature.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: G. K. Chesterton , in "St. Thomas Aquinas" in The Spectator (27 February 1932)

text: Thomas Aquinas, though imputed with magic, was one of the profoundest scholars and subtlest logicians of his day.
author: Thomas Aquinas

text: Through the genius of Thomas Aquinas, all of the church ideas were rewritten within the framework of Aristotle's ideas with their mythological character reduced to a bare minimum.
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: Morton Kelsey , Myth, History & Faith: The Mysteries of Christian Myth & Imagination (1974)

text: When asked by the Crucified One, 'You have spoken well of me, Thomas. What is your reward to be?', St. Thomas Aquinas replied: 'Nothing but Yourself, Lord!'
author: Thomas Aquinas
source: Pope Benedict XVI , General Audience, 2 June 2010

text: We ought not to have more use and esteem of money and coin than of stones. The devil seeks to blind those who desire it more than stones. Let us not lose the kingdom of heaven for such a trifle.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: First Rule of the Friars Minor

text: When the spirit is lukewarm and the soul finds no delight, flesh and blood inevitably seek their own interests, covering base instinct with the excuse of necessity.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Thomas of Celano , The Life of Saint Francis , Francis of Assisi: Early Documents , Volume 2, p. 293.

text: Such was the will of the Father that his Son, blessed and glorious, whom he gave to us, and who was born for us, should by his own blood, sacrifice, and oblation, offer himself on the altar of the cross, not for himself, by whom "all things were made," but for our sins, leaving us an example that we should follow his steps.
author: Francis of Assisi

text: Enslaved by flesh and worldly cares, they serve the devil.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: “Earlier Exhortation to the Brothers and Sisters of Penance,” Francis of Assisi: Early Documents , Volume 1, p. 43.

text: Reject worldly wisdom; embrace simplicity, humility, and purity.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: “Later Admonition and Exhortation to the Brothers and Sisters of Penance,” Francis of Assisi: Early Documents , Volume 1, p. 48.

text: No one can possess a single virtue without first dying to self; for to possess one is to possess all, and to offend one is to lose all.
author: Francis of Assisi

text: Love one another, not merely in word, but in deed and in truth.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Francis of Assisi, Rule of 1221, Rule 11 - That the Brothers ought not to speak or detract, but ought to love one another .
source_section: Preach the gospel, and if necessary, use words.

text: For it is in giving that we receive; it is in pardoning that we are pardoned; and it is in dying to self that we are born to Eternal Life.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Widely known as The Prayer of St. Francis , it is not found in Esser's authoritative collection of Francis's writings.

text: Then the base instinct covers itself with the excuse of necessity, and the mind of the flesh forms the conscience.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Thomas of Celano , The Life of Saint Francis , Francis of Assisi: Early Documents , Volume 2, p. 293.

text: The mystic rises to communion with the Beatitudes, in brief moments that fill an entire life.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Pope Francis , who chose his papal name in honor of Francis, interviewed in "How the Church will change" by Eugenio Scalfari in La Repubblica (1 October 2013) .

text: He is the most shining example of agape.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Pope Francis , interviewed in "How the Church will change" by Eugenio Scalfari in La Repubblica (1 October 2013).

text: Indeed, preaching does not consist of words alone, but also of behavior itself.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Helena Roerich , Letters of Helena Roerich Volume II , (10 December 1936)

text: His life stands for a mystical and emotional realization of the unity of being.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Kenneth Rexroth , in "Eckhart, Brethren of the Free Spirit" from Communalism: From Its Origins to the Twentieth Century (1974), ch. 4.

text: Through prayer, all his difficulties were transfigured, leaving only God and the happiness of uninterrupted contemplation.
author: Francis of Assisi
source: Adrienne von Speyr , Francis of Assisi in The Book of All Saints , translated by D.C. Schindler (San Francisco: Ignatius Press, 2008. ISBN 978-1-58617-192-6 .

text: The Sacred Exchange between Saint Francis and Lady Poverty
author: Francis of Assisi

text: They will open, but only so far; you'll find it cold inside, that house empty, and your stories worthless.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Don't you know there is no one in the streets and no one in the houses? There are only eyes in the windows. If you don't have a place to sleep, knock on a door and it will open, open up to a certain point and you will see that it is cold inside, and that that house is empty and wants nothing to do with you, your stories mean nothing, and if you insist on being gentle, the dog and the cat will bite you.

text: And something struck my soul, fever or lost wings, and I became alone, deciphering that burn, and wrote the first vague line, vague, bodiless, pure, pure foolishness, wisdom of one who knows nothing, and suddenly saw the sky unhusked and open.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: And something started in my soul, fever or forgotten wings, and I made my own way, deciphering that fire, and I wrote the first faint line, faint, without substance, pure nonsense, pure wisdom of someone who knows nothing, and I suddenly saw the heavens unfastened and open.

text: There in Rangoon I understood that all gods were enemies of humanity, imposing their heaven with wounds or pistols, fierce gods of man to hide cowardice.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: There in Rangoon I realized that the gods were enemies, just like God, of the poor human being. Gods in alabaster extended like white whales, gods gilded like spikes, serpent gods entwining the crime of being born, naked and elegant buddhas smiling at the cocktail party of empty eternity like Christ on his horrible cross, all of them capable of anything, of imposing on us their heaven, all with torture or pistol to purchase piety or burn our blood, fierce gods made by men to conceal their cowardice, and there it was all like that, the whole earth reeking of heaven, and heavenly merchandise.

text: In the insurrection of light, awaken with those who woke, or continue in the dream, reaching the other shore of a sea that has no other shore.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: It is time, love, to break off that sombre rose, shut up the stars and bury the ash in the earth; and, in the rising of the light, wake with those who awoke or go on in the dream, reaching the other shore of the sea which has no other shore.

text: Only with an ardent patience will we conquer the splendid city that will give light, justice, and dignity to all humankind. Thus, poetry will not have sung in vain.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Only with a burning patience can we conquer the splendid City which will give light, justice and dignity to all mankind. In this way the song will not have been sung in vain.

text: I like you when you're silent because you're as if absent, and you hear me from afar, and my voice does not touch you.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: I like for you to be still: it is as though you were absent, and you hear me from far away and my voice does not touch you.
source_section: Veinte Poemas de Amor y una Canción Desesperada (Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair) (1924)

text: I am alone among dilapidated things; the rain falls on me, and it resembles me, resembles me in its delirium, solitary in the dead world, rejected as it falls, and stubbornly formless.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: I am alone with rickety materials, the rain falls on me, and it is like me, it is like me in its raving, alone in the dead world, repulsed as it falls, and with no persistent form.
source_section: Residencia en la Tierra (Residence on Earth) (1933)

text: Of human tongues, the poor knife would only know your name, and the thick earth cannot comprehend your name, made of impenetrable, divine substances.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Later on you will find buried near the coconut tree the knife which I hid there for fear you would kill me, and now suddenly I would be glad to smell its kitchen steel used to the weight of your hand, the shine of your foot: under the dampness of the ground, among the deaf roots, in all the languages of the men only the poor will know your name, and the dense earth does not understand your name made of impenetrable divine substances.
source_section: Residencia en la Tierra (Residence on Earth) (1933)

text: I do not want so many misfortunes for myself. I do not want to continue as root and tomb, as solitary underground, as a cellar with frozen dead, dying of sorrow.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: I do not want to be the inheritor of so many misfortunes. I do not want to continue as a root and as a tomb, as a solitary tunnel, as a cellar full of corpses, stiff with cold, dying with pain.
source_section: Residencia en la Tierra (Residence on Earth) (1933)

text: If you ask me where I have been, I must say 'It happens.' I know only what birds lose, the sea left behind, or my sister crying. Why so many regions? Why dead?
author: Pablo Neruda
source: If you should ask me where I've been all this time I have to say "Things happen." I have to dwell on stones darkening the earth, on the river ruined in its own duration: I know nothing save things the birds have lost, the sea I left behind, or my sister crying. Why this abundance of places? Why does day lock with day? Why the dark night swilling round in our mouths? And why the dead?
source_section: Residencia en la Tierra (Residence on Earth) (1933)

text: You will ask: why does his poetry not speak to us of dreams, of leaves, of the great volcanoes of his native land? Come and see the blood in the streets, come and see the blood in the streets, come and see the blood in the streets!
author: Pablo Neruda
source: And you will ask: why doesn't his poetry speak of dreams and leaves and the great volcanoes of his native land? Come and see the blood in the streets. Come and see the bloods in the streets. Come and see the blood in the streets!
source_section: Residencia en la Tierra (Residence on Earth) (1933)

text: He who does not travel, does not read, does not listen to music, does not find grace in himself, dies slowly. He who destroys his self-esteem, who does not allow himself to be helped, dies slowly...
author: Pablo Neruda
source: He who does not travel, who does not read, who does not listen to music, who does not find grace in himself, she who does not find grace in herself, dies slowly. He who slowly destroys his own self-esteem, who does not allow himself to be helped, [dies slowly...]

text: They may cut all the flowers, but they cannot stop the spring.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: They may mow down all the flowers, but they can't stop spring.

text: Pablo Neruda is a poet of the senses; he makes the beautiful nature of oil, and all things, real.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: 1994 interview in Conversations with Isabel Allende (1999)

text: Pablo Neruda's international renown stems from his tremendous genius, allowing him to transcend geographical lines while remaining deeply rooted.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Afua Cooper , Talk Yuh Talk: Interviews with Anglophone Caribbean Poets by Kwame Dawes (2000)

text: I love Chilean writer Pablo Neruda, for that powerful lyric voice he has, and also the commitment he had for his people.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Joy Harjo 1989 interview collected in The Spiral of Memory (1996)

text: I love poets who bring together poetry and life in all its motion: Neruda , Forché , Cardenal , Dugan , Bishop
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Linda Hogan (writer) , interview collected in This is about vision : interviews with Southwestern writers (1990)

text: Neruda died as the military junta seized power, becoming a symbol of Chilean resistance, his writings inspiring a collective voice that transcended death.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Adrienne Rich What Is Found There (1993)

text: I also read poetry — from Pablo Neruda to Warsan Shire — fairly regularly, and it keeps my sense of what words can do wide open and my sense of beauty awake.
author: Pablo Neruda

text: Neruda adored life, wild about everything; eating and drinking were almost a mystical experience, his sweet-toothed exuberance for life generating literature from sensual experience.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Mario Vargas Llosa interview with The Paris Review (1990)

text: who knows if the moon's a balloon, coming out of a keen city in the sky—filled with pretty people?
author: E. E. Cummings
source: Quoted by David Niven as epigraph, The Moon's a Balloon (1971)

text: All in green went my love riding on a great horse of gold into the silver dawn.
author: E. E. Cummings

text: it's spring when the world is puddle-wonderful
author: E. E. Cummings

text: The vulgar distinction between circus and art is superficial, for every authentic art expresses supreme beauty.
author: E. E. Cummings
source: "The Adult, the Artist and the Circus." Vanity Fair (October 1925)

text: You always open petal by petal myself as Spring opens her first rose; nothing equals the power of your intense fragility.
author: E. E. Cummings

text: (i do not know what it is about you that closes and opens;only something in me understands the voice of your eyes is deeper than all roses) nobody,not even the rain,has such small hands
author: E. E. Cummings

text: I'd rather learn from one bird how to sing than teach ten thousand stars how not to dance .
author: E. E. Cummings

text: Simple people prefer simple things like 'good' and 'bad'; complex people, who feel, know nothing.
author: E. E. Cummings

text: Very luckily for you and me,the uncivilized sun mysteriously shines on "good" and "bad" alike. He is an artist.
author: E. E. Cummings

text: Nothing measurable can be alive; nothing not alive can be art; nothing not art is true: and everything untrue doesn't matter.
author: E. E. Cummings

text: Why do you paint? For exactly the same reason I breathe.

text: He was not only a great poet, but a great philosopher.
author: William Shakespeare

text: I admire him, but I love Shakespeare.

text: What point of morals, of manners, of philosophy, of religion, of taste, of the conduct of life, has he not settled?
author: William Shakespeare

text: Nor sequent centuries could hit Orbit and sum of Shakespeare's wit.
author: William Shakespeare
source: Ralph Waldo Emerson , "Solution," lines 35–42, Poems (1918), p. 222. These lines are inscribed above the fireplace in the old reading room of the Folger Shakespeare Library, Washington, D.C.

text: The one who turns in his coffin will be the author of the play.

text: The remarkable thing about Shakespeare is that he is really very good — in spite of all the people who say he is very good.

text: Far from the sun and summer-gale, In thy green lap was Nature's Darling laid.
author: William Shakespeare

text: The arguments for and against aristocracy or democracy, on the privileges of the few and the claims of the many, on liberty and slavery, power and the abuse of it, peace and war, are here very ably handled, with the spirit of a poet and the acuteness of a philosopher.
author: William Shakespeare
source: William Hazlitt , Characters of Shakespear's Plays (1817), pp. 69-70

text: But since he cannot, reader, look Not on his picture, but his book.
author: William Shakespeare

text: Soul of the Age! The applause, delight, the wonder of our stage! My Shakespeare... Thou art a monument, without a tomb, And art alive still while thy book doth live, And we have wits to read, and praise to give.
author: William Shakespeare
source: Ben Jonson , To the Memory of my Beloved, the Author, Mr. William Shakespeare (1623)

text: I remember the players have often mentioned it as an honor to Shakespeare, that in his writing, whatsoever he penned, he never blotted out a line. My answer hath been, "Would he had blotted a thousand".

source: Ben Jonson , Timber: or Discoveries made upon Men and Matter (1640)

text: There was ever more in him to be praised than to be pardoned.
author: William Shakespeare
source: Ben Jonson , Timber: or Discoveries made upon Men and Matter (1640)

text: He was honest, and of an open and free nature, had an excellent fantasy, brave notions, and gentle expressions.
author: William Shakespeare
source: Ben Jonson , Timber: or, Discoveries; Made upon Men and Matter (1641), p. 98

text: He that tries to recommend him by select Quotations, will succeed like the Pedant in Hierocles, who, when he offered his House to Sale, carried a Brick in his Pocket as a Specimen.

source: Samuel Johnson , The plays of William Shakespeare , Vol. I (1765), Preface.

text: There is always an appeal open from criticism to nature.
author: William Shakespeare
source: Samuel Johnson , The plays of William Shakespeare , Vol. I (1765), Preface.

text: We fix our eyes upon his graces, and turn them from his deformities, and endure in him what we should in another loath or despise.
author: William Shakespeare
source: Samuel Johnson , The plays of William Shakespeare , Vol. I (1765), Preface.

text: I never quite despair and I read Shakspeare — indeed I shall I think never read any other Book much [...] I am very near Agreeing with Hazlit that Shakspeare is enough for us.
author: William Shakespeare
source: John Keats , in a letter to Benjamin Robert Haydon (11 May 1817).

text: He has left nothing to say about nothing or any thing.
author: William Shakespeare
source: John Keats , in a letter to John Hamilton Reynolds (22 November 1817).

text: Negative Capability, that is when man is capable of being in uncertainties, Mysteries, doubts, without any irritable reaching after fact & reason.
author: William Shakespeare
source: John Keats , in a letter to George and Tom Keats ([21/27?] December 1817).

text: Shakespeare led a life of Allegory; his works are the comments on it.
author: William Shakespeare

text: England must be true to herself: that is the burden of Shakespeare's unsentimental patriotism.

text: Such evil is often in Shakespeare felt as inhuman and bestial; it is—or should be—un-English.

text: Here the extravagances and profundities of the great sequence come, at last, to rest.

text: He saw the Roman eagle as dissolving into the sunbeams of Britain.
author: William Shakespeare
source: G. Wilson Knight , 'Maiden Phoenix', The Olive and The Sword: A Study of England's Shakespeare (1944), pp. 71, 75

text: The conclusion to Henry VIII is no mere record of an historic past, but rather the one comprehensive statement in our literature of that peace towards which the world labours and for which Great Britain fights.
author: William Shakespeare
source: G. Wilson Knight , 'Maiden Phoenix', The Olive and The Sword: A Study of England's Shakespeare (1944), pp. 83, 85

text: Shakespeare has throughout sounded, as has no other great poet or dramatist on record, the note of royalty.

source: G. Wilson Knight , 'The Shakespearian Royalty', The Sovereign Flower: On Shakespeare as the Poet of Royalism together with related essays and indexes to earlier volumes (1958), p. 13

text: Villains or heroes, it is no matter; it all lies deeper than ethic.
author: William Shakespeare
source: G. Wilson Knight , 'The Golden Thread', The Sovereign Flower: On Shakespeare as the Poet of Royalism together with related essays and indexes to earlier volumes (1958), p. 91

text: We have watched kings falling in country after country, and it is likely that the works of Shakespeare have themselves done much to preserve our own intuition and understanding of royalty.
author: William Shakespeare
source: G. Wilson Knight , 'A Royal Propaganda', The Sovereign Flower: On Shakespeare as the Poet of Royalism together with related essays and indexes to earlier volumes (1958), p. 274

text: Shakespeare is not our poet, but the world's ; Therefore on him no speech!
author: William Shakespeare
source: Walter Savage Landor , "To Robert Browning," published in The Morning Chronicle (22 November 1845); reprinted in The Works of Walter Savage Landor (1846), vol. II.

text: When I read Shakespeare I am struck with wonder That such trivial people should muse and thunder In such lovely language.
author: William Shakespeare
source: D. H. Lawrence , "When I read Shakespeare," from Pansies (1929).

text: The Poet paramount, whom all the muses loved, crowned with sacred laurel, was placed as Musagetes on their throne.
author: William Shakespeare
source: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow , "Shakespeare," from The Masque of Pandora and Other Poems (1875).

text: You teach Shakespeare, though it's outside your experience. You must learn to enter the work, delve into it, and see what it tells you about yourself.
author: William Shakespeare
source: 1978 interview in Conversations with Audre Lorde (2004)

text: Among the first books carried to curious strangers in new worlds will be a Bible and the works of William Shakespeare.
author: William Shakespeare
source: Louis Marder, in His Exits and his Entrances : The Story of Shakespeare's Reputation (1963), p. 362.

text: Shakespeare in heaven ranks with Gabriel, Raphael, and Michael; if another Messiah ever comes, 'twill be in his person.
author: William Shakespeare
source: Herman Melville , Letter to Evert Augustus Duyckinck (24 February 1849); published in The Letters of Herman Melville (1960) edited by Merrell R. Davis and William H. Gilman, p. 77.

text: And so sepulchr'd, in such pomp dost lie, That kings for such a tomb would wish to die.

text: We never questioned Shakespeare's deification of the ruling class or his marginalization of the masses, being under the captivity of colonial educational propaganda.
author: William Shakespeare

text: Shakespeare's tragedies moved me most; it was the music of the lines, the sound of the words, that excited me.

text: The verbal poetical texture of Shakespeare is the greatest the world has known, and is immensely superior to the structure of his plays as plays.

text: Were Shakespeare around to write a play about our times, perhaps his opening line to the Plutocracy would be "Get thee to the Oligarchy. Blend yourselves together and thou shalt rule invincible forever after."

text: Shakespeare was a mighty, many-gifted soul, whose works, like products of Nature, are emblematic, have many meanings, and are simple and inexhaustible.
author: William Shakespeare
source: Novalis , as quoted in "Novalis" (1829) by Thomas Carlyle

text: Shakespeare — the nearest thing in incarnation to the eye of God.
author: William Shakespeare
source: Laurence Olivier , quoted in Kenneth Harris, "Sir Laurence Olivier," from Kenneth Harris Talking To... (1971).

text: Shakespeare did not write to bore schoolchildren; he wrote to delight his audiences. If he does not give you delight, you had better ignore him.

text: Shakespearean language is a bizarre super-tongue, elusive and impenetrable; he uses language to darken.

source: Camille Paglia , Sexual Personae: Art and Decadence from Nefertiti to Emily Dickinson , p. 195.

text: Who can measure the worth of a Shakespeare, a Michelangelo or Beethoven in dollars and cents?

text: He is not so much an imitator, as an instrument, of nature; and 'tis not so just to say that he speaks from her as that she speaks through him.
author: William Shakespeare

text: had all the speeches been printed without the very names of the persons, I believe one might have applied them with certainty to every speaker.
author: William Shakespeare
source: Alexander Pope , Preface to the Works of Shakespeare (1725). Compare Addison on Homer: "there is scarce a speech or action in the Iliad, which the reader may not ascribe to the person who speaks or acts, without seeing his name at the head of it." in The Spectator , No. 273 (12 January 1711–12).

text: 'tis plain he had much reading at least, if they will not call it learning. Nor is it any great matter, if a man has knowledge, whether he has it from one language or from another.
author: William Shakespeare

text: Words, the right sound, the right shape, the right rhythm, words that last forever.

text: They were both of them to shine on the evil and good; both to behold unoffendedly all that was upon the earth, to burn unappalled upon the spears of kings, and undisdaining, upon the reeds of the river.

text: Shakespeare, unconstrained by classical models, moved with majestic grace, vindicating the British theatre from pedantic restriction and stamping the character of a national Drama.

text: As divinest Shakespeare's might Fills Avon and the world with light, Like omniscient power, which he Imaged 'mid mortality.

text: He is a kaleidoscope that fluctuates depending on who is looking through it, conveying a world always in transit.

text: Aeschylus is the poet of righteousness, but Shakespeare's doctrine is darker, accepting injustice and the masterdom of fate.

text: Why Shakespeare lends itself so well to film is because he wrote movies.

text: Great artists like Shakespeare, exponents of know-how, manipulated their instruments to achieve desired results long before they knew the theory of their art.

text: Shakespeare is a savage with sparks of genius which shine in a horrible night.

text: The poet of England, he gave to the love of country, to patriotism as nowadays we call it, a voice which never shall be stilled. His histories are, and will ever be, the epic of our race.

text: This Booke When Brasse and Marble fade, shall make thee looke Fresh to all Ages.

text: This was Shakespeare's form; Who walked in every path of human life, Felt every passion; and to all mankind Doth now, will ever, that experience yield Which his own genius only could acquire.

text: Others abide our question. Thou art free. We ask and ask—Thou smilest and art still, Out-topping knowledge.

text: Renowned Spenser, lie a thought more nigh To learned Chaucer, and rare Beaumont lie A little nearer Spenser, to make room For Shakespeare in your threefold, fourfold tomb.

text: If I say that Shakespeare is the greatest of intellects, I have said all concerning him. But there is more in Shakespeare's intellect than we have yet seen. It is what I call an unconscious intellect; there is more virtue in it than he himself is aware of.

text: Voltaire and Shakespeare! He was all The other feigned to be. The flippant Frenchman speaks: I weep; And Shakespeare weeps with me.

text: Now you who rhyme, and I who rhyme, Have not we sworn it, many a time, That we no more our verse would scrawl, For Shakespeare he had said it all!

text: If we wish to know the force of human genius we should read Shakespeare. If we wish to see the insignificance of human learning we may study his commentators.

text: Mellifluous Shakespeare, whose enchanting Quill Commandeth Mirth or Passion, was but Will.

text: The stream of Time, which is continually washing the dissoluble fabrics of other poets, passes without injury by the adamant of Shakspere.

text: I remember, the players have often mentioned it as an honour to Shakespeare, that in his writing (whatsoever he penned) he never blotted out a line. My answer hath been, would he had blotted a thousand.

text: Then to the well-trod stage anon If Jonson's learned sock be on, Or sweetest Shakespeare, Fancy's child, Warble his native woodnotes wild.

text: Dear son of Memory, great heir of fame, thou in our wonder and astonishment hath built thyself a livelong monument.

text: Shakspeare (whom you and every playhouse bill Style the divine! the matchless! what you will), For gain, not glory, wing'd his roving flight , And grew immortal in his own despite.
