text: If you are rich, you have to be an idiot not to stay rich. And if you are poor, you have to be really smart to get rich.
author: John Green
source: The Greek Debt Crisis Explained in Four Minutes

text: There is no them, there are only facets of us.
author: John Green

text: Hank, before I move forward with this discussion, I should acknowledge that as a child I was an inveterate liar... As opposed to now, when I am a novelist.
author: John Green
source: Poopy Nintendo Mystery Solved With Science

text: The funny thing about writing is that whether you're doing it well or you're doing it poorly, it looks the exact same. That is actually one of the main ways that writing is different from ballet dancing.
author: John Green
source: July 19: A Day in the Life of a Writer (Who Has No Friends)

text: Reading is the only apprenticeship we have for writing.
author: John Green
source: Nov. 26th: Writing Advice (And Notes on Surnameless Tiffany)

text: When people have to choose between civilization and warm genitals, they choose warm genitals
author: John Green
source: : The Fall of The Roman Empire

text: It is very sad to me that some people are so intent on leaving their mark on the world that they don't care if that mark is a scar.
author: John Green
source: As quoted from Hank Green on his Twitter feed

text: The test will last your entire life, and it will be comprised of the millions of decisions that, when taken together, make your life yours.
author: John Green
source: Introduction to Crash Course World History

text: At some point, you just pull off the Band-Aid and it hurts, but then it's over and you're relieved.
author: John Green

text: Is the labyrinth living or dying? Which is he trying to escape—the world or the end of it?
author: John Green

text: Y'all smoke to enjoy it. I smoke to die.
author: John Green

text: I wanted to be one of those people who scorch the ground with their intensity, but for now, I knew such people, and they needed me, just like comets need tails.
author: John Green

text: I may die young, but at least I'll die smart.
author: John Green

text: Imagining the future is a kind of nostalgia.
author: John Green
source: Alaska Young and Miles "Pudge" Halter, p. 54

text: God will punish the wicked. And before He does, we will.
author: John Green

text: I'm just scared of ghosts, Pudge. And home is full of them.
author: John Green

text: How do you get out of the labyrinth of suffering?
author: John Green

text: If people were rain, I was drizzle and she was a hurricane.
author: John Green
source: Miles "Pudge" Halter about Alaska Young, p. 88

text: Prick us, we bleed. Prick him, he pops.
author: John Green

text: We had to forgive to survive in the labyrinth.
author: John Green

text: I would always love Alaska Young, my crooked neighbor, with all my crooked heart.
author: John Green

text: That part of us greater than the sum of our parts cannot begin and cannot end, and so it cannot fail.
author: John Green

text: But mothers lie. It's in the job description.
author: John Green

text: It hurt like the worst ass-kicking he'd ever gotten. And he'd gotten plenty.
author: John Green

text: Crying adds something: crying is you, plus tears. But the feeling Colin had was some horrible opposite of crying. It was you, minus something.
author: John Green

text: Missing her kept him awake more than the coffee.
author: John Green

text: You are predisposed to either one fate or the other.
author: John Green

text: That smile could end wars and cure cancer.
author: John Green

text: People just liked Hassan, the way people like fast food and celebrities.
author: John Green

text: It was, he thought, just like how authors always wrote things in ways other than how they actually happened.
author: John Green

text: He wanted to draw out the moment before the moment—because as good as kissing feels, nothing feels as good as the anticipation of it.
author: John Green

text: I just want to do something that matters. Or be something that matters. I just want to matter.
author: John Green

text: Books are the ultimate Dumpees: put them down and they'll wait for you forever; pay attention to them and they always love you back.
author: John Green

text: Colin's skin was alive with the feeling of connection to everyone in that car and everyone not in it. And he was feeling not-unique in the very best possible way.
author: John Green

text: The way I figure it, everyone gets a miracle.
author: John Green

text: Margo always loved mysteries. And in everything that came afterward, I could never stop thinking that maybe she loved mysteries so much that she became one.
author: John Green

text: That's always seemed so ridiculous to me, that people would want to be around someone because they're pretty. It's like picking your breakfast cereals based on color instead of taste.
author: John Green

text: It's a paper town. All those paper people living in their paper houses, burning the future to stay warm. Everyone demented with the mania of owning things.
author: John Green

text: This is the basest of all possible emotions, the feeling that was with us before we existed, before this building existed, before the earth existed.
author: John Green

text: The fundamental mistake I had always made—and that she had, in fairness, always led me to make—was this: Margo was not a miracle. She was not an adventure. She was not a fine and precious thing. She was a girl.
author: John Green

text: In the end, listening exposes you even more than it exposes the people you're trying to listen to.
author: John Green

text: It is so hard to leave—until you leave. And then it is the easiest goddamned thing in the world.
author: John Green

text: That tastes like hope feels.
author: John Green

text: It is easy to forget how full the world is of people, full to bursting, and each of them imaginable and consistently misimagined.
author: John Green

text: In the end, it reveals a lot more about the person doing the imagining than it does about the person being imagined.
author: John Green

text: What a treacherous thing to believe that a person is more than a person.
author: John Green

text: You have to be careful which metaphor you choose, because it matters. If you choose the strings, then you're imagining a world in which you can become irreparably broken. If you choose the grass, you're saying that we are all infinitely interconnected.
author: John Green

text: It is saying these things that keeps us from falling apart. And maybe by imagining these futures we can make them real, and maybe not, but either way we must imagine them. The light rushes out and floods in.
author: John Green

text: I stand in this parking lot, realizing that I've never been this far from home, and here is this girl I love and cannot follow. I hope this is the hero's errand, because not following her is the hardest thing I've ever done.
author: John Green

text: Being in a relationship, that's something you choose. Being friends, that's just something you are.
author: John Green

text: The X in 'exes' isn't a cross-out; it's a diagram of two paths that once crossed.
author: John Green

text: But, in fact, depression is not a side effect of cancer. Depression is a side effect of dying. (Cancer is also a side effect of dying. Almost everything is, really.)
author: John Green

text: Sometimes you read a book and it fills you with this weird evangelical zeal and you become convinced that the shattered world will never be put back together unless and until all living humans read that book.
author: John Green

text: That's the thing about pain," Augustus said, and then glanced back at me. "It demands to be felt.
author: John Green

text: As he read, I fell in love the way you fall asleep; Slowly, and then all at once.
author: John Green

text: I cannot tell you how thankful I am for our little infinity. I wouldn't trade it for the world. You gave me forever within the numbered days, and I'm grateful.
author: John Green

text: The marks humans leave are too often scars.
author: John Green

text: The universe is improbably biased toward consciousness, rewarding intelligence because it enjoys its elegance being observed.
author: John Green

text: I'm in love with cities I've never been to and people I've never met.
author: John Green
source: Incorrectly attributed to John Green on various quotation sites and on social media. Actual author is Melody Truong .

text: You have to get through the bad stuff before you can get to the pit of everything that you really enjoy.
author: Rupi Kaur
source: On the pressure that she felt writing a second book in “Instagram sensation Rupi Kaur doesn't have social media on her phone” in EW (2017 Oct 13)

text: You can understand nothing about art if you do not understand that imagination is a value in itself.
author: Milan Kundera
source: Interview with Christian Salmon (Fall 1983), Writers at Work: The Paris Review Interviews, Series Seven (Viking, 1988, ISBN 0-14-008500-9 ), pp. 217-218

text: A novel that does not uncover a hitherto unknown segment of existence is immoral. Knowledge is the novel's only morality.
author: Milan Kundera
source: "The Novel and Europe" New York Review of Books (19 July 1984)

text: The light that radiates from the great novels time can never dim, for human existence is perpetually being forgotten by man and thus the novelists' discoveries, however old they may be, will never cease to astonish.
author: Milan Kundera
source: From The Art of the Novel , as quoted in "Here Be Dragons" The Daily Telegraph (4 June 1988)

text: Dogs are our link to paradise . They don't know evil or jealousy or discontent. To sit with a dog on a hillside on a glorious afternoon is to be back in Eden, where doing nothing was not boring — it was peace .
author: Milan Kundera
source: As quoted in The Canine Hiker's Bible (2000) by Doug Gelbert, p. 8

text: Suspending moral judgment is not the immorality of the novel; it is its morality. From the viewpoint of the novel’s wisdom, that fervid readiness to judge is the most detestable stupidity, the most pernicious evil.
author: Milan Kundera

text: True human goodness, in all its purity and freedom, can come to the fore only when its recipient has no power. Mankind's true moral test consists of its attitude toward those at its mercy: animals.
author: Milan Kundera
source: The Unbearable Lightness of Being (1984), as quoted in Milan Kundera (2003) by Harold Bloom , p. 91

text: Nothing is more repugnant to me than brotherly feelings grounded in the common baseness people see in one another.
author: Milan Kundera

text: No action is good or evil in itself; only its place in the order makes it so.
author: Milan Kundera
source: No act is of itself either good or bad . Only its place in the order of things makes it good or bad.

text: No great movement designed to change the world can bear to be laughed at or belittled. Mockery is a rust that corrodes all it touches.
author: Milan Kundera

text: It is 1971, and Mirek says: The struggle of man against power is the struggle of memory against forgetting.
author: Milan Kundera
source_section: The Book of Laughter and Forgetting (1979)

text: The only reason people want to be masters of the future is to change the past. They are fighting for access to the laboratories where photographs are retouched and biographies and histories rewritten.
author: Milan Kundera
source_section: The Book of Laughter and Forgetting (1979)

text: Everyone has trouble accepting the fact that he will disappear unheard of and unnoticed in an indifferent universe, and everyone wants to make himself into a universe of words before it's too late.
author: Milan Kundera
source_section: The Book of Laughter and Forgetting (1979)

text: The first step in liquidating a people is to erase its memory.
author: Milan Kundera
source_section: The Book of Laughter and Forgetting (1979)

text: ...[O]f a world that rests essentially on the nonexistence of return, [...] everything is pardoned in advance and therefore everything cynically permitted.
author: Milan Kundera

text: In the sunset of dissolution, everything is illuminated by the aura of nostalgia.
author: Milan Kundera

text: In the love poetry of every age, the woman longs to be weighed down by the man's body.
author: Milan Kundera

text: And what can life be worth if the first rehearsal for life is life itself?
author: Milan Kundera

text: We can never know what to want, because, living only one life, we can neither compare it with our previous lives nor perfect it in our lives to come.
author: Milan Kundera

text: Metaphors are not to be trifled with. A single metaphor can give birth to love.
author: Milan Kundera

text: To love someone out of compassion means not really to love.
author: Milan Kundera

text: A person who longs to leave the place where he lives is an unhappy person.
author: Milan Kundera

text: He had spent seven years of his life with Tereza, and now he realised that those years were more attractive in retrospect than they were when he was living them.
author: Milan Kundera

text: For there is nothing heavier than compassion. Not even one's own pain weighs so heavy as the pain one feels with someone, for someone, a pain intensified by the imagination and prolonged by a hundred echoes.
author: Milan Kundera

text: Necessity , weight, and value are three concepts inextricably bound: only necessity is heavy, and only what is heavy has value.
author: Milan Kundera

text: When we ignore the body, we are more easily victimized by it.
author: Milan Kundera

text: But is not an event in fact more significant and noteworthy the greater the number of fortuities necessary to bring it about?
author: Milan Kundera

text: Chance and chance alone has a message for us... Only chance can speak to us.
author: Milan Kundera

text: Without realizing it, the individual composes his life according to the laws of beauty even in times of great distress.
author: Milan Kundera

text: If a love is to be unforgettable, fortuities must immediately start fluttering down to it like birds to Francis of Assisi 's shoulders.
author: Milan Kundera

text: It was the call of all those fortuities... which gave her the courage to leave home and change her fate .
author: Milan Kundera

text: It is right to chide man for being blind to such coincidences in his daily life. For he thereby deprives his life of a dimension of beauty.
author: Milan Kundera

text: Human lives are composed like music, where a sense of beauty transforms fortuitous occurrences into permanent motifs.
author: Milan Kundera

text: Anyone whose goal is 'something higher' must expect some day to suffer vertigo.
author: Milan Kundera

text: No, vertigo is something other than the fear of falling. It is the voice of emptiness below us which tempts and lures us, it is the desire to fall, against which, terrified, we defend ourselves.
author: Milan Kundera

text: Dreaming is not merely an act of communication ; it is also an aesthetic activity, a game of the imagination , a game that is a value in itself.
author: Milan Kundera

text: But when the strong were too weak to hurt the weak, the weak had to be strong enough to leave.
author: Milan Kundera

text: Physical love is unthinkable without violence.
author: Milan Kundera

text: "Why don't you ever use your strength on me?" she said. "Because love means renouncing strength ," said Franz softly.
author: Milan Kundera

text: "Love is a battle," said Marie-Claude, still smiling. "And I plan to go on fighting. To the end."
author: Milan Kundera

text: The goals we pursue are always veiled. A girl who longs for marriage longs for something she knows nothing about. The boy who hankers after fame has no idea what fame is. The thing that gives our every move its meaning is always totally unknown to us.
author: Milan Kundera

text: The moment love is born: the woman cannot resist the voice calling forth her terrified soul ; the man cannot resist the woman whose soul thus responds to his voice.
author: Milan Kundera

text: The unique 'I' hides in what is unimaginable about a person, what cannot be guessed or calculated, but must be unveiled.
author: Milan Kundera

text: The brain appears to possess a special area which we might call poetic memory and which records everything that charms or touches us, that makes our lives beautiful.
author: Milan Kundera

text: Love begins at the point when a woman enters her first word into our poetic memory.
author: Milan Kundera

text: Love is the longing for the half of ourselves we have lost.
author: Milan Kundera

text: When the heart speaks, the mind finds it indecent to object. In the realm of kitsch, the dictatorship of the heart reigns supreme.
author: Milan Kundera

text: Kitsch is the second tear: the self-congratulatory emotion of being moved, together with all mankind, by something beautiful.
author: Milan Kundera

text: Her revolt against Communism was aesthetic, repelled not by its ugliness, but by the mask of beauty it tried to wear—its kitsch.
author: Milan Kundera

text: Totalitarian kitsch banishes individualism, doubt, and irony, for in its realm, everything must be taken seriously and deviations from the collective are anathema.
author: Milan Kundera

text: We can regard the gulag as a septic tank used by totalitarian kitsch to dispose of its refuse.
author: Milan Kundera

text: The true opponent of totalitarian kitsch is the person who asks questions, for a question is like a knife that slices through the stage backdrop.
author: Milan Kundera

text: What makes a leftist a leftist is not this or that theory but his ability to integrate any theory into the kitsch called the Grand March.
author: Milan Kundera

text: Kitsch is the stopover between being and oblivion.
author: Milan Kundera

text: Kitsch is the absolute denial of shit, in both the literal and the figurative senses of the word; kitsch excludes everything from its purview which is essentially unacceptable in human existence.
author: Milan Kundera

text: Any new possibility that existence acquires, even the least likely, transforms everything about existence.
author: Milan Kundera

text: I can't shake off the idea that after death you keep being alive. That to be dead is to live an endless nightmare.
author: Milan Kundera

text: This is the real and the only reason for friendship : to provide a mirror so the other person can contemplate his image from the past, which, without the eternal blah-blah of memories between pals, would long ago have disappeared.
author: Milan Kundera

text: It is always that way: between the moment he meets her again and the moment he recognizes her for the woman he loves, he has some distance to go.
author: Milan Kundera

text: How could she feel nostalgia when he was right in front of her? How can you suffer from the absence of a person who is present?
author: Milan Kundera

text: You can suffer nostalgia in the presence of the beloved if you glimpse a future where the beloved is no more.
author: Milan Kundera

text: The eye ... the point where a person's identity is concentrated.
author: Milan Kundera

text: You can't measure the mutual affection of two human beings by the number of words they exchange.
author: Milan Kundera

text: Our shared apathy toward work has become the one great collective passion of our time.
author: Milan Kundera

text: However contemptible the world, lovers still need it to nourish their intimate talk.
author: Milan Kundera

text: Pain doesn't listen to reason, it has its own reason, which is not reasonable.
author: Milan Kundera

text: She had gone into some other life where, if he should meet her, he would no longer recognize her.
author: Milan Kundera

text: As you live out your desolation, you can be either unhappy or happy. Having that choice is what constitutes your freedom .
author: Milan Kundera

text: Since the insignificance of all things is our lot, we should not bear it as an affliction but learn to enjoy it.
author: Milan Kundera

text: I get scared when my eye blinks, that in that second, a snake, a rat, or another man could slip into your place.
author: Milan Kundera

text: It was not only an epoch of terror, but also an epoch of lyricism, ruled hand in hand by the hangman and the poet.
author: Milan Kundera

text: History is often deliberately falsified to make a people forget who they are or who they were.
author: Milan Kundera
source: 1994 interview in Conversations with Ursula Le Guin

text: He who does not see the kingdom of heaven in this life will never see it in the coming life.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: Khalil in Spirits Rebellious (1908) "Khalil The Heretic" Part 3

text: Let us teach this miserable nation to smile and rejoice with heaven's bounty and glory of life and freedom.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: Khalil in Spirits Rebellious (1908) "Khalil The Heretic" Part 3

text: Man struggles to find life outside himself, unaware that the life he is seeking is within him.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: Kahlil Gibran, The Awakened Soul ISBN-10: 1864760818 (January 2001)

text: Blessed are the thieves who stole my masks, for in my madness I found freedom and safety from being understood.
author: Kahlil Gibran

text: My words are naught but thy own thoughts in sound and my deeds thy own hopes in action.
author: Kahlil Gibran

text: You are your own forerunner, and the towers you have builded are but the foundation of your giant-self.
author: Kahlil Gibran

text: Let me rather starve, and let my heart parch with thirst, than stretch my hand to a cup you did not fill, or a bowl you did not bless.
author: Kahlil Gibran

text: To understand another, listen not to what they say, but to what they cannot say.
author: Kahlil Gibran

text: Solitude is a silent storm that breaks dead branches, yet sends living roots deeper into the earth.
author: Kahlil Gibran

text: Strange that creatures without backbones have the hardest shells.
author: Kahlil Gibran

text: I would rather be the least among men with dreams than the greatest with none.
author: Kahlil Gibran

text: When His dawn-eyes met mine, all the stars of my night faded, and I became Miriam, finding myself anew.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: Mary Magdalen: On Meeting Jesus For The First Time

text: Our dulled senses gaze in full daylight and yet do not see, hear not, touch not, smell not.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: A Philosopher: On Wonder And Beauty

text: We gaze but do not see, hearken but do not hear, eat and drink but do not taste; His senses were always new, and the world to Him, a new world.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: A Philosopher: On Wonder And Beauty

text: I once believed in naught but laws; now I walk with life and laugh with the sun.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: Nicodemus The Poet, The Youngest Of The Elders In The Sanhedrim: On Fools And Jugglers

text: I am content with the sound of His lyre, which He held and struck while the hands of His body were nailed and bleeding.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: Nicodemus The Poet, The Youngest Of The Elders In The Sanhedrim: On Fools And Jugglers

text: It is with eloquence not with principles that they hold your ear and your passing thought, but never the core of your heart.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: Manasseh: On the Speech and Gesture of Jesus

text: Behold, He who was the first Word called us brothers, though we were but syllables uttered yesterday.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: John The Beloved Disciple In His Old Age: On Jesus The Word

text: The Christ was God's first Word, and when Jesus came, that Word was uttered unto us and made flesh and blood.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: John The Beloved Disciple In His Old Age: On Jesus The Word

text: The wind may blow and then cease, and the sea shall swell and then weary, but the heart of life is a sphere quiet and serene, and the star that shines therein is fixed for evermore.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: John The Beloved Disciple In His Old Age: On Jesus The Word

text: To those who love, it remains forever wordless; But to those who do not love, it may be but a heartless jest.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: John At Patmos: Jesus The Gracious

text: The seasons shall tire and the years grow old, ere they exhaust these words: 'Father forgive them, for they know not what they do.'
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: Philip: And When He Died All Mankind Died

text: In a dream I saw Jesus and My God Pan sitting together in the heart of the forest. They laughed at each other's speech, with the brook that ran near them, and the laughter of Jesus was the merrier. And they conversed long.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: Sarkis an old Greek Shepherd, called the madman: Jesus and Pan

text: And then there came peace into their music, and the heavens and the earth sang together.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: Sarkis an old Greek Shepherd, called the madman: Jesus and Pan

text: With death Jesus conquered death, and rose from the grave a spirit and a power.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: Mary Magdalen (Thirty years later): On the Resurrection of the Spirit

text: He who lived in us is deathless, the Son of God even as we are children of God, born of a virgin even as we are born of the husbandless earth.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: Mary Magdalen (Thirty years later): On the Resurrection of the Spirit

text: Master, master singer, Master of words unspoken , Seven times was I born, and seven times have I died Since your last hasty visit and our brief welcome. And behold I live again, Remembering a day and a night among the hills, When your tide lifted us up.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: A Man From Lebanon: Nineteen Centuries Afterward

text: They honour not the living man, the first man who gazed at the sun with unquivering eyelids; they do not know Him, nor would they be like Him.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: A Man From Lebanon: Nineteen Centuries Afterward

text: They are empty-handed, and not crucified with the great crucifixion, and therein is their pain. The world crucifies them every day, but only in little ways.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: A Man From Lebanon: Nineteen Centuries Afterward

text: Though the world dreams your dream, it will not wake to your dawn, instead turning your golden visit into crowns and sceptres for its own power.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: A Man From Lebanon: Nineteen Centuries Afterward

text: I have existed from all eternity, soared into limitless space, and witnessed all wisdom, yet here I am now, mired in matter and a captive of confusion.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source_section: The Vision: Reflections on the Way of the Soul (1994)

text: Yesterday we complained of time and feared it, but today we love and embrace it, beginning to comprehend its secrets.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source: Children of Gods, Scions of Apes
source_section: The Vision: Reflections on the Way of the Soul (1994)

text: All you see, from books to thoughts, are the ghosts of souls who preceded you, seeds sown by the past for the future's profit.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source_section: The Vision: Reflections on the Way of the Soul (1994)

text: My Soul taught me to love what people abhor, showing that Love is a halo encompassing every being, not a thread between two pegs.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source_section: The Vision: Reflections on the Way of the Soul (1994)

text: My Soul taught me to touch what has never taken corporeal form, for grasping the intangible is half the task of comprehending it.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source_section: The Vision: Reflections on the Way of the Soul (1994)

text: My Soul taught me that in the brief moment of the present, all time exists, and the place where I subsist is all places.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source_section: The Vision: Reflections on the Way of the Soul (1994)

text: My Soul taught me never to delight in praise or be distressed by reproach, for trees blossom and bear fruit without desire for accolades or fear of reproof.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source_section: The Vision: Reflections on the Way of the Soul (1994)

text: My Soul taught me that I am not exalted over the panhandler nor less than the mighty, for all human beings are formed from the same substance.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source_section: The Vision: Reflections on the Way of the Soul (1994)

text: My Soul taught me that the lamp I carry and the song I sing are not mine; I am not the light, nor the lute player.
author: Kahlil Gibran
source_section: The Vision: Reflections on the Way of the Soul (1994)

text: My thought is a tender leaf that sways and tests me; your thought is an ancient dogma that cannot change you.
author: Kahlil Gibran

text: Your thought advocates fame, arrogance, and palaces; mine counsels casting aside notoriety, planting love for peace, and humble service.
author: Kahlil Gibran

text: Your thought describes laws; mine explains that man-made laws are flawed, and before the basic law, we are all one, making disdain for the sinful a disdain for all humanity.
author: Kahlil Gibran

text: Power lies in reason, resolution, and truth; no matter how long the tyrant endures, he will be the loser at the end.
author: Kahlil Gibran

text: The solemn oak-tree sigheth, thick-leaved, ambrosial, with an ancient melody of an inward agony.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: She only said, 'My life is dreary, He cometh not'; she said, 'I am aweary, aweary, I would that I were dead!'
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: He, lying broad awake, yet apart in intellect, hath heard Time flowing in the middle of the night, and all things creeping to a day of doom.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: My own sweet Alice, we must die. More is taken from us in life than given. Pray that we may die the self-same day.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: So sweet it seems with thee to walk, I'd almost live my life again.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: My heart is breaking, and I am all aweary of my life.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Self-reverence, self-knowledge, self-control lead to sovereign power; but wisdom is to follow right, in scorn of consequence.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: I built my soul a lordly pleasure-house, Wherein at ease for aye to dwell. I said, "O Soul, make merry and carouse, Dear soul, for all is well."
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Tomorrow 'ill be the maddest, merriest day, for I'm to be Queen o' the May.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Dan Chaucer, the first warbler, whose sweet breath Preluded those melodious bursts that fill The spacious times of great Elizabeth With sounds that echo still.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: " A Dream of Fair Women ", st. 2 (1832)

text: At length I saw a lady within call, Stiller than chisell'd marble, standing there; A daughter of the gods, divinely tall, And most divinely fair.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Half light, half shade, She stood, a sight to make an old man young.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Thou art more through Love, and greater than thy years.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: In that last kiss, which never was the last, farewell and endless welcome lived and died.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Meet is it changes should control Our being, lest we rust in ease. We all are changed by still degrees, All but the basis of the soul.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: But we grow old. Ah! when shall all men's good Be each man's rule, and universal Peace Lie like a shaft of light across the land, And like a lane of beams athwart the sea, Thro' all the circle of the golden year?
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: My good blade carves the casques of men, My tough lance thrusteth sure, My strength is as the strength of ten, Because my heart is pure.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: I grow in worth, and wit, and sense.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Cophetua sware a royal oath: "This beggar maid shall be my queen!"
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: All the windy ways of men Are but dust that rises up, And is lightly laid again.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: God made Himself an awful rose of dawn.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Break, break, break, On thy cold gray stones, O Sea! And I would that my tongue could utter The thoughts that arise in me.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Break, break, break At the foot of thy crags, O Sea! But the tender grace of a day that is dead Will never come back to me.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: But oh for the touch of a vanished hand, And the sound of a voice that is still!
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: I am sick of Time, and I desire to rest.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: He clasps the crag with crooked hands; Close to the sun in lonely lands, Ring'd with the azure world, he stands . The wrinkled sea beneath him crawls; He watches from his mountain walls, And like a thunderbolt he falls.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: There is a higher law.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: I come from haunts of coot and hern, I make a sudden sally, And sparkle out among the fern, To bicker down a valley.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: And draw them all along, and flow To join the brimming river, For men may come and men may go, But I go on forever.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Me only cruel immortality Consumes: I wither slowly in thine arms.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: His deeds yet live, the worst is yet to come. Yet let your sleep for this one night be sound: I do forgive him!
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: A lie which is half a truth is ever the blackest of lies.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Speak to Him thou for He hears, and Spirit with Spirit can meet — Closer is He than breathing, and nearer than hands and feet.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Flower in the crannied wall, I pluck you out of the crannies, I hold you here, root and all, in my hand, Little flower — but if I could understand What you are, root and all, and all in all, I should know what God and man is.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Love lieth deep; Love dwells not in lip-depths.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Where love could walk with banish'd Hope no more.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Love mourned long for Hope, but Memory fed her soul.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Thou that singest wheat and woodland, tilth and vineyard, hive and horse and herd; All the charm of all the Muses often flowering in a lonely word.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: For Knowledge is the swallow on the lake That sees and stirs the surface-shadow there But never yet hath dipt into the abysm
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: For nothing worthy proving can be proven, Nor yet disproven: wherefore thou be wise, Cleave ever to the sunnier side of doubt, And cling to Faith beyond the forms of Faith!
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: She sees the Best that glimmers through the Worst.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: The shell must break before the bird can fly.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: O young Mariner, You from the haven Under the sea-cliff, You that are watching The gray Magician With eyes of wonder, I am Merlin, And I am dying, I am Merlin Who follow The Gleam.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: The Master whisper’d ‘Follow The Gleam.’
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: This laurel greener from the brows Of him that utter'd nothing base.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: And statesmen at her council met Who knew the seasons, when to take Occasion by the hand, and make The bounds of freedom wider yet.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Broad-based upon her people’s will, And compass'd by the inviolate sea.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: A man had given all other bliss, And all his worldly worth for this, To waste his whole heart in one kiss Upon her perfect lips.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: As she fled fast through sun and shade The happy winds upon her played, Blowing the ringlet from the braid.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: God gives us love. Something to love He lends us; but when love is grown To ripeness, that on which it throve Falls off, and love is left alone.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: To J. S. , stanza 4, from Poems (1832)

text: Sleep sweetly, tender heart, in peace! Sleep, holy spirit, blessed soul, While the stars burn, the moons increase, And the great ages onward roll.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: To J. S. , stanza 18, from Poems (1832)

text: Sleep till the end, true soul and sweet! Nothing comes to thee new or strange. Sleep full of rest from head to feet; Lie still, dry dust, secure of change.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: To J. S. , stanza 19, from Poems (1832)

text: More black than ash-buds in the front of March.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: The Gardener's Daughter , line 28, from Poems (1842)

text: We are ancients of the earth, And in the morning of the times.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: The Daydream: L'Envoi , lines 231-32, from The Complete Works of Alfred Tennyson (1879)

text: For now the poet can not die, Nor leave his music as of old, But round him ere he scarce be cold Begins the scandal and the cry.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: To ———, after reading a Life and Letters , stanza 4, from Poems (1850)

text: Mastering the lawless science of our law,— That codeless myriad of precedent, That wilderness of single instances.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: Aylmer's Field (1864); reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: O Love! what hours were thine and mine, In lands of palm and southern pine; In lands of palm, of orange-blossom, Of olive, aloe, and maize and vine!
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: The Daisy , Stanza 1; reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: So dear a life your arms enfold, Whose crying is a cry for gold.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: The Daisy , Stanza 24; reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: Read my little fable: He that runs may read. Most can raise the flowers now, For all have got the seed.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: The Flower ; reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: "I'll never love any but you," the morning song of the lark; "I'll never love any but you," the nightingale's hymn in the dark.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: The First Quarrel , stanza VI., lines 3-4; reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: My God, I would not live Save that I think this gross hard-seeming world Is our misshaping vision of the Powers Behind the world, that make our griefs our gains.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: The Sisters , reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: The golden guess Is morning-star to the full round of truth.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: Columbus , reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: No sound is breathed so potent to coerce And to conciliate, as their names who dare For that sweet mother-land which gave them birth Nobly to do, nobly to die.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: Tiresias , reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: A princelier-looking man never stept thro' a prince's hall.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: The Wreck , reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: Slav, Teuton, Kelt, I count them all My friends and brother souls, With all the peoples, great and small, That wheel between the poles.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: Epilogue to The Charge of the heavy Brigade , reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: The song that nerves a nation's heart Is in itself a deed.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: Epilogue to The Charge of the Heavy Brigade , reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: What use to brood? This life of mingled pains And joys to me, Despite of every Faith and Creed, remains The Mystery.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: To Mary Boyle , reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: Be patient. Our Playwright may show In some fifth act what this wild Drama means.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: The Play , reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: In our windy world What's up is faith, what's down is heresy.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: Harold , Act i, Scene 1, reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: A breath that fleets beyond this iron world And touches him who made it.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Old men must die, or the world would grow mouldy, would only breed the past again.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: Becket , Prologue, reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: Ambition Is like the sea wave, which the more you drink The more you thirst—yea—drink too much, as men Have done on rafts of wreck—it drives you mad.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: The Cup , Act i, Scene 3, reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: The night with sudden odour reeled; The southern stars a music pealed.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: The Rosebud , reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: Death's truer name Is "Onward," no discordance in the roll And march of that Eternal Harmony Whereto the world beats time.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: The Death of the Duke of Clarence and Avondale

text: ...none can truly write his single day, And none can write it for him upon earth.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: Unpublished Sonnet (originally written as a preface to Becket ), in Alfred Lord Tennyson: A Memoir by His Son , by Hallam T. Tennyson (1897)

text: A good woman is a wondrous creature, cleaving to the right and to the good under all change: lovely in youthful comeliness, lovely all her life long in comeliness of heart.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: Letter to Emily Sellwood, quoted in Alfred Lord Tennyson: A Memoir by His Son , by Hallam T. Tennyson (1897)

text: Thou who stealest fire, From the fountains of the past, To glorify the present; oh, haste, Visit my low desire! Strengthen me, enlighten me! I faint in this obscurity, Thou dewy dawn of memory.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: In sweet dreams softer than unbroken rest Thou leddest by the hand thine infant Hope.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Come forth I charge thee, arise, Thou of the many tongues, the myriad eyes! Thou comest not with shows of flaunting vines Unto mine inner eye, Divinest Memory!
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Whither in after life retired From brawling storms, From weary wind, With youthful fancy reinspired, We may hold converse with all forms Of the many-sided mind, And those whom passion hath not blinded, Subtle-thoughted, myriad-minded.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Never, oh! never, nothing will die; The stream flows, The wind blows, The cloud fleets, The heart beats, Nothing will die.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Nothing will die; All things will change Thro’ eternity.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: The world was never made; It will change, but it will not fade. So let the wind range; For even and morn Ever will be Thro’ eternity. Nothing was born; Nothing will die; All things will change.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Dower'd with the hate of hate, the scorn of scorn, The love of love.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: So many minds did gird their orbs with beams, Tho' one did fling the fire; Heaven flow'd upon the soul in many dreams Of high desire.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: And Freedom rear'd in that august sunrise Her beautiful bold brow, When rites and forms before his burning eyes Melted like snow.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: But one poor poet's scroll, and with his word She shook the world.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: At me you smiled, but unbeguiled I saw the snare, and I retired; The daughter of a hundred earls, You are not one to be desired.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: A simple maiden in her flower Is worth a hundred coats-of-arms.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: You sought to prove how I could love, And my disdain is my reply. The lion on your old stone gates Is not more cold to you than I.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Her manners had not that repose Which stamps the caste of Vere de Vere.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: From yon blue heaven above us bent, The grand old gardener and his wife Smile at the claims of long descent.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Kind hearts are more than coronets, And simple faith than Norman blood.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: In the afternoon they came unto a land In which it seemed always afternoon.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Music that gentlier on the spirit lies, Than tir'd eyelids upon tir'd eyes.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Give us long rest or death, dark death, or dreamful ease.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Let us swear an oath, and keep it with an equal mind, In the hollow Lotos-land to live and lie reclined On the hills like Gods together, careless of mankind.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Surely, surely, slumber is more sweet than toil, the shore Than labour in the deep mid-ocean, wind and wave and oar; O, rest ye, brother mariners, we will not wander more.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: On either side the river lie Long fields of barley and of rye, That clothe the wold and meet the sky; And through the field the road runs by To many-towered Camelot.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Willows whiten, aspens quiver, Little breezes dusk and shiver Through the wave that runs for ever By the island in the river Flowing down to Camelot.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: All in the blue unclouded weather Thick-jewelled shone the saddle-leather, The helmet and the helmet-feather Burned like one burning flame together, As he rode down to Camelot.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: From the bank and from the river He flashed into the crystal mirror, "Tirra lirra," by the river Sang Sir Lancelot.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: The mirror cracked from side to side; "The curse is come upon me," cried The Lady of Shalott.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Singing in her song she died, The Lady of Shalott.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: He said, "She has a lovely face; God in his mercy lend her grace, The Lady of Shalott."
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: It little profits that an idle king, By this still hearth, among these barren crags, Match'd with an aged wife, I mete and dole Unequal laws unto a savage race, That hoard, and sleep, and feed, and know not me.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: I cannot rest from travel: I will drink Life to the lees.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: I am part of all that I have met; Yet all experience is an arch wherethro' Gleams that untravell'd world, whose margin fades For ever and for ever when I move.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: This gray spirit yearning in desire to follow knowledge like a sinking star, beyond the utmost bound of human thought.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Death closes all; but something ere the end, some work of noble note, may yet be done, not unbecoming men that strove with gods.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: 'Tis not too late to seek a newer world. For my purpose holds to sail beyond the sunset, until I die.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Made weak by time and fate, but strong in will to strive, to seek, to find, and not to yield.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: The many fail: the one succeeds.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: O eyes long laid in happy sleep! O happy sleep, that lightly fled! O happy kiss, that woke thy sleep! O love, thy kiss would wake the dead!
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: And o'er the hills, and far away Beyond their utmost purple rim, Beyond the night, across the day, Thro' all the world she follow'd him.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: What moral is in being fair? Is there any moral shut within the bosom of the rose?
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Authority forgets a dying king, Laid widow’d of the power in his eye That bow’d the will.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: My end draws nigh; 't is time that I were gone. Make broad thy shoulders to receive my weight
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: When every morning brought a noble chance, And every chance brought out a noble knight.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: "He does not love me for my birth Nor for my lands so broad and fair; He loves me for my own true worth, And that is well," said Lady Clare.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: I will speak out, for I dare not lie... I will know if there be any faith in man.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: "If I come dressed like a village maid, I am but as my fortunes are: I am a beggar born," she said, "And not the Lady Clare".
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: "If you are not the heiress born, And I," said he, "the lawful heir, We two will wed to-morrow morn, And you shall still be Lady Clare".
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Tears, idle tears, I know not what they mean, Tears from the depth of some divine despair Rise in the heart, and gather to the eyes, In looking on the happy Autumn-fields, And thinking of the days that are no more.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Fresh as the first beam glittering on a sail, That brings our friends up from the underworld, Sad as the last which reddens over one That sinks with all we love below the verge; So sad, so fresh, the days that are no more.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Dear as remembered kisses after death, And sweet as those by hopeless fancy feigned On lips that are for others; deep as love, Deep as first love , and wild with all regret; O Death in Life, the days that are no more!
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Bury the Great Duke With an empire's lamentation ; Let us bury the Great Duke To the noise of the mourning of a mighty nation; Mourning when their leaders fall, Warriors carry the warrior's pall, And sorrow darkens hamlet and hall.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source_section: Ode on the Death of the Duke of Wellington (1852)

text: Lead out the pageant: sad and slow, As fits an universal woe, Let the long, long procession go, And let the sorrowing crowd about it grow, And let the mournful martial music blow; The last great Englishman is low.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source_section: Ode on the Death of the Duke of Wellington (1852)

text: O fallen at length that tower of strength Which stood four-square to all the winds that blew!
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source_section: Ode on the Death of the Duke of Wellington (1852)

text: That tower of strength Which stood four-square to all the winds that blew.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source_section: Ode on the Death of the Duke of Wellington (1852)

text: For this is England's greatest son, He that gained a hundred fights, And never lost an English gun.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source_section: Ode on the Death of the Duke of Wellington (1852)

text: He that walks it, only thirsting For the right, and learns to deaden Love of self, before his journey closes, He shall find the stubborn thistle bursting Into glossy purples, which outredden All voluptuous garden-roses.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source_section: Ode on the Death of the Duke of Wellington (1852)

text: Speak no more of his renown, Lay your earthly fancies down, And in the vast cathedral leave him, God accept him, Christ receive him!
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source_section: Ode on the Death of the Duke of Wellington (1852)

text: Half a league half a league Half a league onward All in the valley of Death Rode the six hundred: 'Forward the Light Brigade Charge for the guns' he said Into the valley of Death Rode the six hundred.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source_section: The Charge of the Light Brigade (1854)

text: "Forward, the Light Brigade!" Was there a man dismay'd? Not tho' the soldier knew Some one had blunder'd: Theirs not to make reply, Theirs not to reason why, Theirs but to do and die: Into the valley of death Rode the six hundred.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source_section: The Charge of the Light Brigade (1854)

text: Cannon to right of them, Cannon to left of them, Cannon in front of them Volley'd and thunder'd; Storm'd at with shot and shell, Boldly they rode and well, Into the jaws of Death, Into the mouth of Hell Rode the six hundred.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source_section: The Charge of the Light Brigade (1854)

text: Storm'd at with shot and shell, While horse and hero fell, They that had fought so well Came thro' the jaws of Death Back from the mouth of Hell, All that was left of them, Left of six hundred.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source_section: The Charge of the Light Brigade (1854)

text: Perfectly beautiful: let it be granted her: where is the fault? All that I saw (for her eyes were downcast, not to be seen) Faultily faultless, icily regular, splendidly null, Dead perfection, no more.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: That jewelled mass of millinery, That oiled and curled Assyrian Bull.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: And ah for a man to arise in me, That the man I am may cease to be!
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Gorgonised me from head to foot With a stony British stare.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Come into the garden, Maud, For the black bat, night, has flown, Come into the garden, Maud, I am here at the gate alone; And the woodbine spices are wafted abroad, And the musk of the rose is blown.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: For a breeze of morning moves, And the planet of Love is on high, Beginning to faint in the light that she loves On a bed of daffodil sky, To faint in the light of the sun she loves, To faint in his light, and to die.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: All night have the roses heard The flute, violin, bassoon; All night has the casement jessamine stirr'd To the dancers dancing in tune; Till a silence fell with the waking bird, And a hush with the setting moon.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Queen lily and rose in one; shine out, little head, and be their sun.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: She is coming, my dove, my dear; she is coming, my life, my fate.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: My dust would hear her and beat, had I lain for a century dead, and blossom in purple and red.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Ah Christ, that it were possible for one short hour to see the souls we loved, that they might tell us what and where they be.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: A mastiff dog may love a puppy cur for no more reason than that the twain have been tied up together.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: To persecute makes a faith hated, and is furthermore no perfect witness of a perfect faith in him who persecutes.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: In statesmanship to strike too soon is oft to miss the blow.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Woman is various and most mutable.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: To do him any wrong was to beget a kindness from him, for his heart was rich—of such fine mould that if you sowed therein the seed of Hate, it blossomed Charity.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Remember that sore saying spoken once by Him that was the Truth, 'How hard it is for the rich man to enter into heaven!'
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Come out, my lord, it is a world of fools.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: We are six ships of the line; can we fight with fifty-three?
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: I should count myself the coward if I left them, my Lord Howard, to these Inquisition dogs and the devildoms of Spain.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: We be all good English men. Let us bang these dogs of Seville, for I never turn'd my back upon Don or devil yet.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: The little Revenge ran on sheer into the heart of the foe, with her hundred fighters on deck, and her ninety sick below.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: She with all the charm of woman, she with all the breadth of man.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Love your enemy, bless your haters, said the Greatest of the great; Christian love among the Churches looked the twin of heathen hate.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Charm us, orator, till the lion look no larger than the cat.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: You that woo the Voices—tell them 'Old Experience is a fool'; teach your flattered kings that only those who can not read can rule.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Authors—essayist, atheist, novelist, realist, rhymester, play your part, paint the mortal shame of nature with the living hues of art.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Can war die out, till this outworn earth is dead as yon dead world the moon?
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Yet the moonlight is the sunlight and the sun himself will pass.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Is there evil but on earth? or pain in every peopled sphere?
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Is there evil but on earth? Or pain in every peopled sphere?
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Evolution ever climbing after some ideal good And Reversion ever dragging Evolution in the mud.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Follow you the star that lights a desert pathway, yours or mine. Forward, till you see the Highest Human Nature is divine.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Fires that shook me once, but now to silent ashes fall'n away. Cold upon the dead volcano sleeps the gleam of dying day.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Staled by frequence, shrunk by usage into commonest commonplace!
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Here and there a cotter's babe is royal—born by right divine; Here and there my lord is lower than his oxen or his swine.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Nor is he the wisest man who never proved himself a fool.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Sunset and evening star, And one clear call for me! And may there be no moaning of the bar, When I put out to sea.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Twilight and evening bell, And after that the dark! And may there be no sadness of farewell, When I embark.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: For though from out our bourne of Time and Place The flood may bear me far, I hope to see my Pilot face to face When I have crossed the bar.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson

text: Whate'er thy joys, they vanish with the day: Whate'er thy griefs, in sleep they fade away, To sleep! to sleep! Sleep, mournful heart, and let the past be past: Sleep, happy soul, all life will sleep at last.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source_section: The Foresters, Robin Hood and Maid Marion (1892)

text: If this life of ours be a good glad thing, why should we make us merry because a year of it is gone?
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source_section: The Foresters, Robin Hood and Maid Marion (1892)

text: Forget thee… Never— Till Nature, high and low, and great and small Forgets herself, and all her loves and hates Sink again into Chaos.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source_section: The Foresters, Robin Hood and Maid Marion (1892)

text: Traitors are rarely bred Save under traitor kings.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source_section: The Foresters, Robin Hood and Maid Marion (1892)

text: The happiness of a man in this life does not consist in the absence but in the mastery of his passions.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: Quoted in A Dictionary of Quotations, in Most Frequent Use by D.E. Macdonnel (1809) translated from French: Le bonheur de l'homme en cette vi ne consiste pas á être sans passions: il consiste à en être le maître .

text: Ours is not to reason why, ours is but to do or die
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: Misquote of the lines "Theirs not to reason why, / theirs but to do and die" from The Charge of the Light Brigade

text: Let knowledge grow from more to more But more of reverence in us dwell; That mind and soul, well according, May make one music, as before.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: Stephen Jay Gould , "The Tooth and Claw Centennial," Dinosaur in a Haystack: Reflections in Natural History (1995)

text: Arthur's knights were epic shopwalkers in a well-decorated emporium of sentimentality.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: E. B. Osborn , "Chapter III. Cornish Pictures" . Literature and Life: Things Seen, Heard and Read . London: Methuen & Company Ltd. 1921. pp. 16–20. (quote from p. 19)

text: Should Heaven send me any son, I hope he's not like Tennyson. I'd rather have him play a fiddle Than rise and bow and speak an idyll.
author: Alfred, Lord Tennyson
source: Dorothy Parker , "A Pig's-Eye View of Literature" from Sunset Gun (1927); reproduced in Not So Deep as a Well: Collected Poems (1937), p. 110.

text: Unless you can die when the dream is past — Oh, never call it loving!
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: That night we felt our love would hold, as saintly moonlight washed the world clean as gold, and nightingales throbbed with flame and song.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: I know God lives and loves; and say, since it is so, 'Thy will be done.'
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning
source: Reported in Josiah Hotchkiss Gilbert, Dictionary of Burning Words of Brilliant Writers (1895), p. 514

text: Pan is dead! great Pan is dead! Pan, Pan is dead!
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning
source: The Dead Pan ; reported in Bartlett's Familiar Quotations , 10th ed. (1919)

text: If thou must love me, let it be for nought Except for love's sake only.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: How do I love thee? Let me count the ways.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: I will write my story for my better self, to hold together what I was and am.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Life, struck sharp on death, makes awful lightning.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: If I married him, I would not dare to call my soul my own, Which so he had bought and paid for: every thought And every heart-beat down there in the bill,– Not one found honestly deductible From any use that pleased him!
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Drama may outgrow simulated scenes and take for a worthier stage the soul itself.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Poets' sole work is to represent their own live, throbbing age, which spends more passion in its drawing-rooms than Roland with his knights at Roncesvalles.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Nothing in the world comes single to man; all temporal show builds to eterne significance through God.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: While the tears drop, my days go on.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Grief may be joy misunderstood; only the Good discerns the good. I trust Thee while my days go on.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Being and suffering are one, as a child drops his pebble down a deep well and hears it fall, smiling.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: “A curse from the lips of womanhood / Is very salt, and bitter, and good.”
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning

text: Life loves to change—but some of us want to stay.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning
source: Naomi Shihab Nye Voices in the Air: Poems for Listeners (2018)

text: Emily Dickinson was always stirred by the existences of women like George Eliot or Elizabeth Barrett, who possessed strength of mind, articulateness, and energy.
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning
source: Adrienne Rich On Lies, Secrets and Silence (1979)
